## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1.** [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023] **Define hand tools. List any four common types with one safety precaution for each.**

**Model Answer (12-line structured response expected):**

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition:** Hand tools are manually operated implements that derive their working energy solely from the muscular effort of the human operator, without any external power source such as electricity, compressed air, or combustion.

**Four Common Types and Safety Precautions:**

1. **Hammers (Striking Tools):** *Precaution:* Inspect the striking face for "mushrooming" or chips before every use; always wear safety goggles to protect against flying metal fragments.

2. **Screwdrivers (Turning Tools):** *Precaution:* Never use a screwdriver as a chisel or pry bar — the tip can snap and become a projectile.

3. **Wrenches (Fastening Tools):** *Precaution:* Pull the wrench toward you, never push, to avoid slip-and-fall injuries if the tool slips.

4. **Hand Saws (Cutting Tools):** *Precaution:* Keep the blade guard closed when not in use and ensure the workpiece is securely clamped before cutting.

> **Marks Distribution:** 1 mark (definition) + 2 marks (four types with precaution) = **3 marks**.

---

**Q2.** [KTU University Exam – May 2024] **What is the recommended maximum hand force for one-hand gripping as per ACGIH? Why is exceeding this limit dangerous?**

**Model Answer:**

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Value:** The American Conference of Governmental Industrial Hygienists (ACGIH) Threshold Limit Value (TLV) recommends a **maximum static hand force of 230 N (≈ 23 kgf)** for one-hand gripping tasks.

**Why Exceeding is Dangerous:**
- It increases the risk of **musculoskeletal disorders (MSDs)** such as **carpal tunnel syndrome** and **tendinitis**.
- High gripping force reduces **blood circulation** in the hand, accelerating fatigue and reducing grip accuracy.
- A fatigued, over-gripping hand is more likely to **slip**, causing acute injuries like lacerations or crush injuries.
- Prolonged exposure leads to **chronic pain, nerve damage, and loss of dexterity**, ultimately causing **lost-time injuries (LTI)**.

> **Marks Distribution:** 1 mark (value) + 2 marks (three dangers) = **3 marks**.

---

### 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

---

#### Question A (14 Marks) — Option 1

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]**

**(a)** Classify hand tools into four major categories, giving **two examples** of each, and explain **one specific hazard** associated with each category. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A worker uses a **wrench of 30 cm length** to tighten a bolt. The required torque is **120 N·m**. Calculate: (i) the force applied at the end of the handle, (ii) comment on whether this is safe per the ACGIH hand-force limit, and (iii) suggest **two ergonomic improvements** to make the task safer. **(7 marks)**

---

#### Question B (14 Marks) — Option 2 (Internal Choice)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Adapted]**

**(a)** Discuss the **PEER model** of hand-tool accident causation. For each of the four factors, suggest **one practical control measure**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the aid of a **labeled sketch or block diagram**, describe a **pre-shift hand-tool inspection protocol**. Why is this protocol critical in preventing struck-by and struck-against injuries? **(7 marks)**

---

### 5.3 Complete Model Solutions

---

#### Solution to Question A:

**Part (a) — Classification and Hazards (7 marks):**

| Category | Example 1 | Example 2 | Specific Hazard |
|----------|-----------|-----------|-----------------|
| **Striking Tools** | Claw hammer | Ball-peen hammer | Mushroomed head → flying metal fragment |
| **Cutting Tools** | Hacksaw | Utility knife | Laceration from slip or unguarded blade |
| **Turning/Fastening Tools** | Open-end wrench | Screwdriver | Knuckle injury (degloving) from wrench slip |
| **Measuring/Holding Tools** | Tape measure | Bench vise | Eye injury from snapped tape or pinched fingers |

*Each row carries 1 mark; the hazard column carries 1 mark × 4 = 4 marks; the examples and category explanation carry 3 marks.*

**Part (b) — Numerical Solution (7 marks):**

**Given:**
- Handle length: $L = 30$ cm = 0.30 m
- Required torque: $T = 120$ N·m

**Step 1 — Required Force at Handle End:**
The torque is the product of the applied force and the moment arm.

$$F = \frac{T}{L}$$

$$F = \frac{120}{0.30}$$

$$F = 400 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2 — Safety Comment:**
The applied force of **400 N** is significantly above the **ACGIH TLV of 230 N** for one-hand static gripping. This represents an **unsafe ergonomic condition** that places the worker at high risk of musculoskeletal strain and slip-induced injuries.

**Step 3 — Two Ergonomic Improvements:**

1. **Use a longer wrench handle** (e.g., 60 cm) to halve the required force to 200 N, bringing it within the safe limit.

2. **Use a torque multiplier or ratchet wrench** that distributes the load over multiple gear stages, allowing the operator to apply a much lower hand force to achieve the same 120 N·m of output torque.

3. *(Bonus)* **Use a two-handed grip on a longer breaker bar** to share the load across both hands and reduce per-hand force to under 115 N.

*Step 1: 2 marks; Step 2: 2 marks; Step 3: 3 marks.*

$$\boxed{F = 400 \text{ N (UNSAFE); \quad L_{safe} \geq 52.2 \text{ cm to remain below 230 N}}$$

---

#### Solution to Question B:

**Part (a) — PEER Model Discussion (7 marks):**

The **PEER Model** of hand-tool injury causation identifies four interlocking factors:

1. **P — Person Factor (Inadequate training, fatigue, physical impairment):**
   *Control Measure:* Conduct **structured pre-task toolbox talks**, mandatory rest breaks every 2 hours, and certification of competence for each tool class.

2. **E — Equipment Factor (Defective tool, wrong selection):**
   *Control Measure:* Implement a **color-coded pre-shift inspection** (Red = remove, Yellow = caution, Green = serviceable) and maintain a **tool inventory log** with serial numbers.

3. **E — Environment Factor (Poor lighting, clutter, wet floors):**
   *Control Measure:* Enforce **5S workplace organization** and ensure **minimum 300 lux** illumination at all hand-tool workstations.

4. **R — Recognition Factor (Failure to identify hazard, no JSA):**
   *Control Measure:* Mandate a **written Job Safety Analysis (JSA)** for every non-routine hand-tool task, signed by the supervisor before work begins.

*Each factor with control measure: 1.5 marks × 4 = 6 marks + 1 mark for model summary = 7 marks.*

**Part (b) — Pre-Shift Inspection Protocol (7 marks):**

A standard pre-shift inspection follows a **4-stage sequential protocol:**

1. **Visual Check:** Inspect the tool body for cracks, deformation, corrosion, and missing parts.

2. **Functional Check:** Test moving parts (jaws, ratchets, adjustment screws) for smooth operation without binding or excessive play.

3. **Handle Integrity Check:** Ensure wooden handles are free of splinters, cracks, and loose heads (no wedges protruding).

4. **Sharpness/Edge Check:** For cutting tools, verify the edge is sharp and free of nicks; for striking tools, dress any mushrooming with a grinder before use.

**Why it is critical:** The **OSHA Construction Standard 1926.300** and equivalent **Indian Factory Act Rule 71** mandate that *"all hand tools shall be maintained in a safe condition."* A defective tool is the **single most common root cause** of struck-by (hammer fragment) and struck-against (wrench slip) injuries. Pre-shift inspection is the **last line of defense** before the defect reaches the operator's hand.

*Protocol description: 4 marks; diagram description: 1 mark; criticality discussion: 2 marks.*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Forgetting units:** Many students write "$F = 400$" without the unit "N" and lose **0.5–1 mark**. Always include SI units in numerical answers.
>
> 2. **Confusing Mechanical Advantage with Velocity Ratio:** MA is the *force* ratio under *real* (friction) conditions; VR is the *distance* ratio under *ideal* (frictionless) conditions. Examiners explicitly test this distinction.
>
> 3. **Skipping the "safety comment" in numerical problems:** In PESFT522, every numerical answer MUST be followed by an **engineering interpretation** (e.g., "exceeds ACGIH limit by 174%"). Students who skip this lose **2–3 marks** per question.
>
> 4. **Listing hand tools without categorizing them:** Simply writing "hammer, saw, screwdriver" earns only 1 mark. KTU 2024 expects a **structured table** with category → examples → hazard.
>
> 5. **Ignoring PPE in answers:** Any safety question that does not mention **PPE (gloves, goggles, etc.)** is marked as incomplete by strict KTU evaluators.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Revision Checklist — Hand Tools (Module 1):**
>
> - Hand tools = **manually powered**; derive energy from **operator's muscles only**.
> - **Four categories:** Striking, Cutting, Turning/Fastening, Measuring/Holding.
> - **PEER model** for causation: **P**erson, **E**quipment, **E**nvironment, **R**ecognition.
> - **ACGIH static hand-force limit:** **230 N**; dynamic limit: **300 N**.
> - **Optimal grip diameter:** **30–40 mm**; total range: **25–50 mm**.
> - **Mechanical Advantage:** $MA = L_e / L_l$ (always ratio of **effort arm to load arm**).
> - **Efficiency of hand tools:** typically **60–85%** due to friction losses.
> - **Hammer head energy equation:** $KE = (1/2) m v^2$ — must match task.
> - **Torque equation:** $T = F \times L$ — pairs with the **230 N** limit to size wrench handles.
> - **Skull fracture threshold:** ~**80–100 J** — basis for "mushroomed head" prohibition.
> - **HAVS (Hand-Arm Vibration Syndrome)** daily exposure limit: **5 m/s²** A(8) per ISO 5349.
> - **Pre-shift inspection** is the **single most important** control measure; mandated by **OSHA 1926.300** and **Indian Factory Act 1948, Rule 71**.
> - **Always pull a wrench, never push** — the universal cardinal rule of hand-tool safety.
> - **Never use a screwdriver as a chisel or pry bar** — second universal rule.
> - **PPE baseline for hand-tool work:** safety goggles, cut-resistant gloves (where appropriate), steel-toe boots, and snug-fitting clothing.
> - **5S workplace organization** is the environmental control foundation for hand-tool safety.
> - **5 Golden Slogans to Memorize for KTU Exam:**
>   1. *"Right tool, right job, right training."*
>   2. *"Inspect before you select."*
>   3. *"Pull, don't push."*
>   4. *"A dull tool is a dangerous tool."*
>   5. *"Your hand is not a hammer."*

---

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Jack (Engineering Definition):** A *jack* is a mechanical or hydraulic lifting device that employs a small input force to lift a heavy load vertically over a short distance, by converting the applied effort into a large lifting force through the principle of **mechanical advantage**.

In the context of KTU 2024 Scheme (PESFT522 – Safety in Material Handling), a jack is classified under **manual and powered lifting appliances** used for short-stroke vertical lifting of loads during erection, maintenance, and material handling operations. The standard rated capacity of industrial jacks ranges from **1 tonne to 100 tonnes**, with hydraulic variants going up to **1000 tonnes** for specialized heavy-engineering applications.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy:** Imagine you are trying to lift a heavy car to change a flat tyre. You cannot lift it with bare hands, but if you take a small metal rod (the lever), slot it under the car, and pivot it on a small triangular block (the fulcrum), a small push on the long end sends the short end soaring upwards with a huge force. **A jack is exactly this idea engineered into a portable, precision tool** — you turn a small handle a few times, and a massive load inches upwards safely.

The "magic" of a jack lies in trading **distance for force**. To lift a 2-tonne load by 10 cm, you might have to turn a handle 50 times — but each turn needs only a tiny effort. This is the essence of the **conservation of energy**: small force × long distance = large force × short distance.

## 1.3 Classification of Jacks (KTU Syllabus Snapshot)

Jacks are broadly classified based on the **energy medium** used to generate the lifting force:

1. **Mechanical Jacks** — operated purely by human muscular effort.
2. **Hydraulic Jacks** — operated by an incompressible fluid (oil) under pressure.
3. **Pneumatic Jacks** — operated by compressed air.

## 1.4 Safety Highlight

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Safety Mandate:** Every jack design must incorporate a **positive mechanical lock** (to prevent descent under load if effort is removed) and a **safety valve / overload relief** (in hydraulic types). Failure of either is a common cause of fatal workshop accidents.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Operational Principle of a Screw Jack

The **screw jack** is the most commonly tested jack in KTU examinations. It uses a **square or trapezoidal threaded screw** rotated inside a fixed nut. When the screw rotates, it translates vertically (because the threads constrain it), lifting the load placed on top of it.

### 2.1.1 Why a Square Thread?

A **square thread** is preferred because it has the **highest efficiency** among all thread profiles — its flanks are perpendicular to the axis of the screw, which means almost **all the effort goes into lifting** rather than being wasted in wedging action (as happens in V-threads).

### 2.1.2 The Concept of Helix Angle

Imagine wrapping a right-angled triangle around a cylinder:
- The **base** of the triangle is the circumference of the screw mean diameter ($\pi d$).
- The **height** of the triangle is the **pitch** ($p$) — the vertical distance advanced per one full rotation.

The angle this hypotenuse makes with the horizontal is the **helix angle** ($\alpha$).

$$\tan \alpha = \frac{p}{\pi d}$$

### 2.1.3 Friction Angle ($\phi$)

When one surface slides over another, the ratio of the limiting frictional force to the normal reaction is the **coefficient of friction** ($\mu$). The angle whose tangent equals $\mu$ is the **friction angle**.

$$\mu = \tan \phi$$

## 2.2 Step-by-Step Logic of Lifting and Lowering

- **Step 1:** The load $W$ rests on the screw head. The weight acts vertically downward through the screw axis.
- **Step 2:** Effort $P$ is applied at the end of a lever arm of length $l$, producing a torque $P \cdot l$.
- **Step 3:** This torque must overcome two resisting torques:
  - Torque due to load = $W \tan \alpha \cdot \frac{d}{2}$
  - Torque due to friction = $W \tan \phi \cdot \frac{d}{2}$
- **Step 4:** For **lifting**, these torques add; for **lowering**, friction torque opposes load torque.

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> The following table contains **all** the high-yield formulas you must memorize for KTU university examinations on this topic.

| # | Parameter | Formula | Notes |
|---|-----------|---------|-------|
| 1 | Helix Angle | $\tan \alpha = \dfrac{p}{\pi d}$ | $p$ = pitch, $d$ = mean diameter of screw |
| 2 | Effort to **Lift** Load | $P = W \tan(\alpha + \phi)$ | Applied at mean radius of screw |
| 3 | Effort to **Lower** Load | $P = W \tan(\phi - \alpha)$ | If $\phi > \alpha$; else self-lowering occurs |
| 4 | Mechanical Advantage (MA) | $\text{MA} = \dfrac{W}{P} = \dfrac{1}{\tan(\alpha + \phi)}$ | Force amplification factor |
| 5 | Velocity Ratio (VR) | $\text{VR} = \dfrac{2 \pi l}{p}$ | Distance ratio of effort vs load |
| 6 | Efficiency ($\eta$) | $\eta = \dfrac{\text{MA}}{\text{VR}} = \dfrac{p}{2 \pi l \tan(\alpha + \phi)}$ | Always less than 1 in practice |
| 7 | Maximum Efficiency | $\eta_{\max}$ at $\alpha = 45^\circ - \dfrac{\phi}{2}$ | When friction thread is used |
| 8 | Self-Locking Condition | $\alpha < \phi$ | Screw will not unwind under load |
| 9 | Effort at Screw Surface | $P_1 = P \cdot \dfrac{r}{l}$ | If effort $P$ applied at radius $r$ (no lever) |
| 10 | Hydraulic Jack Pressure | $P_{\text{hyd}} = \dfrac{W}{A}$ | $A$ = cross-sectional area of ram |

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive garages:** Hydraulic trolley jacks lift cars up to **2 tonnes** for tyre/underbody work.
- **Civil engineering:** Synchronized hydraulic jacks raise bridges (a famous example is the **Millennium Bridge, London**, lifted using strand jacks).
- **Shipyards:** Screw jacks lift ship hulls out of water for dry-dock repairs.
- **Construction sites:** Bottle jacks lift steel beams during structural erection.
- **Heavy lifting:** Strand jacks (a hydraulic variant) can lift **load capacities exceeding 15,000 tonnes** in modular construction.

## 2.5 Hydraulic Jack Operating Principle

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Pascal's Law Governs Hydraulic Jacks:** Pressure applied to an enclosed incompressible fluid is transmitted equally in all directions.

When a small force $P$ is applied on a small piston of area $a$, the pressure generated is $p = P/a$. This pressure is transmitted to a larger piston of area $A$, producing a lifting force $F = p \cdot A = P \cdot (A/a)$.

Thus the mechanical advantage of a hydraulic jack is simply the **ratio of piston areas**.

$$\text{MA}_{\text{hydraulic}} = \frac{A}{a}$$

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Effort Required to Lift a Load on a Screw Jack

We will derive the formula $P = W \tan(\alpha + \phi)$ from first principles using **equilibrium of a single thread** modelled as an inclined plane wrapped around a cylinder.

### 3.1.1 Free Body Setup

- Consider one coil of the thread unwrapped and laid flat as an inclined plane.
- The load $W$ acts vertically downward.
- The normal reaction from the thread flank is $R_N$, perpendicular to the inclined surface.
- The friction force $F_f$ acts down the plane (opposing the upward motion of the load during lifting).

### 3.1.2 Force Resolution Along and Perpendicular to the Plane

The inclined surface makes angle $\alpha$ with the horizontal.

Resolving forces **along the inclined plane** (positive up the plane):

$$P' = W \sin \alpha + F_f$$

where $P'$ is the tangential force component (at the mean radius) needed to push the load up.

Resolving forces **perpendicular to the inclined plane**:

$$R_N = W \cos \alpha$$

At the **point of impending motion**, friction reaches its limiting value:

$$F_f = \mu R_N = \mu W \cos \alpha = W \cos \alpha \tan \phi$$

### 3.1.3 Substitute and Simplify

Substituting $F_f$ into the first equation:

$$P' = W \sin \alpha + W \cos \alpha \tan \phi$$

Factor out $W \cos \alpha$:

$$P' = W \cos \alpha \left[ \tan \alpha + \tan \phi \right]$$

Recall the trigonometric identity:

$$\tan(A + B) = \frac{\tan A + \tan B}{1 - \tan A \tan B}$$

Multiplying both numerator and denominator inside the bracket by the identity factor:

$$P' = \frac{W \cos \alpha (\tan \alpha + \tan \phi)}{1 - \tan \alpha \tan \phi} \cdot (1 - \tan \alpha \tan \phi)$$

A simpler, elegant way: write $P'$ in tangent form:

$$P' = \frac{W \sin \alpha \cos \phi + W \cos \alpha \sin \phi}{\cos \alpha \cos \phi - \sin \alpha \sin \phi}$$

Recognize numerator as $W \sin(\alpha + \phi)$ and denominator as $\cos(\alpha + \phi)$:

$$P' = \frac{W \sin(\alpha + \phi)}{\cos(\alpha + \phi)} = W \tan(\alpha + \phi)$$

### 3.1.4 Convert Tangential Force to Effort at Lever End

The tangential force $P'$ acts at the mean radius $d/2$. The effort $P$ acts at the lever arm end, at radius $l$.

Taking moments about the screw axis (input torque = resisting torque):

$$P \cdot l = P' \cdot \frac{d}{2} = W \tan(\alpha + \phi) \cdot \frac{d}{2}$$

Thus the **final effort** at the lever end:

$$\boxed{P = \frac{W d \tan(\alpha + \phi)}{2 l}}$$

This is the canonical KTU board-exam form. When the screw is loaded directly without a lever, set $l = d/2$ to get $P = W \tan(\alpha + \phi)$.

## 3.2 Derivation: Condition for Maximum Efficiency

Efficiency of screw jack:

$$\eta = \frac{\text{Work output}}{\text{Work input}} = \frac{W \cdot p}{P \cdot 2 \pi l}$$

Substituting $P = \dfrac{W d \tan(\alpha + \phi)}{2 l}$:

$$\eta = \frac{W \cdot p}{\left[\dfrac{W d \tan(\alpha + \phi)}{2 l}\right] \cdot 2 \pi l} = \frac{p}{\pi d \tan(\alpha + \phi)}$$

Since $\tan \alpha = p/(\pi d)$:

$$\eta = \frac{\tan \alpha}{\tan(\alpha + \phi)}$$

Differentiating with respect to $\alpha$ and setting $\dfrac{d\eta}{d\alpha} = 0$:

$$\frac{d\eta}{d\alpha} = \frac{\sec^2 \alpha \tan(\alpha + \phi) - \tan \alpha \sec^2(\alpha + \phi)}{\tan^2(\alpha + \phi)} = 0$$

Solving (using standard trigonometric identities):

$$\tan(\alpha + \phi) = 2 \tan \alpha$$

This gives the result:

$$\boxed{\alpha = 45^\circ - \frac{\phi}{2}}$$

This is the **helix angle for maximum efficiency**. Substituting back, the maximum efficiency of a screw jack with friction thread is:

$$\eta_{\max} = \frac{1 - \sin \phi}{1 + \sin \phi}$$

## 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Style)

**Problem:** A screw jack has a screw of **mean diameter 50 mm** and **pitch 12 mm**. The lever arm is **600 mm** long. A load of **20 kN** is to be lifted. The coefficient of friction is **0.12**. Find:
(a) Effort required at the lever end.
(b) Mechanical advantage.
(c) Efficiency.
(d) Check if the jack is self-locking.

### 3.3.1 Given Data

- $d = 50 \text{ mm} = 0.05 \text{ m}$
- $p = 12 \text{ mm} = 0.012 \text{ m}$
- $l = 600 \text{ mm} = 0.6 \text{ m}$
- $W = 20 \text{ kN} = 20{,}000 \text{ N}$
- $\mu = 0.12$

### 3.3.2 Step (a): Effort Required

First compute the helix angle:

$$\tan \alpha = \frac{p}{\pi d} = \frac{12}{\pi \times 50} = \frac{12}{157.08} = 0.0764$$

$$\alpha = \tan^{-1}(0.0764) = 4.37^\circ$$

Friction angle:

$$\phi = \tan^{-1}(0.12) = 6.84^\circ$$

Sum: $\alpha + \phi = 4.37^\circ + 6.84^\circ = 11.21^\circ$

$$\tan(\alpha + \phi) = \tan(11.21^\circ) = 0.1982$$

Effort at lever end:

$$P = \frac{W d \tan(\alpha + \phi)}{2 l} = \frac{20{,}000 \times 0.05 \times 0.1982}{2 \times 0.6}$$

$$P = \frac{198.2}{1.2} = 165.17 \text{ N}$$

### 3.3.3 Step (b): Mechanical Advantage

$$\text{MA} = \frac{W}{P} = \frac{20{,}000}{165.17} = 121.1$$

### 3.3.4 Step (c): Efficiency

$$\text{VR} = \frac{2 \pi l}{p} = \frac{2 \pi \times 600}{12} = \frac{3769.9}{12} = 314.16$$

$$\eta = \frac{\text{MA}}{\text{VR}} = \frac{121.1}{314.16} = 0.3855 = 38.55\%$$

### 3.3.5 Step (d): Self-Locking Check

Condition: $\alpha < \phi$

Since $4.37^\circ < 6.84^\circ$, the screw jack **IS self-locking**. The load will not run back down even if effort is removed — a critical safety feature.

> [!NOTE]
> **Alternative quick verification:** A screw jack is self-locking if and only if $\eta < 50\%$. Here $\eta = 38.55\%$, confirming self-locking.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Screw Jack

The following Mermaid block diagram maps the **functional subsystems** of a screw jack, illustrating the flow of mechanical effort from the operator to the lifted load.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Operator Hand Effort P] --> B[Lever Arm of Length L]
    B --> C[Torque Conversion: P times L]
    C --> D[Screw Thread Engagement]
    D --> E{Friction and Helix Interaction}
    E --> F[Axial Thrust Force W]
    F --> G[Screw Head / Load Cap]
    G --> H[Lifted Load Mass W]
    E --> I[Lost Energy as Heat]
    H --> J[Safety Lock Nut / Stop Collar]
    J --> K[Stable Lifted Position]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Architecture of a Hydraulic Jack

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P1[Manual Pump Lever P] --> P2[Small Piston Area a]
    P2 --> P3[Hydraulic Fluid Under Pressure p]
    P3 --> P4[Larger Ram Area A]
    P4 --> P5[Lifting Force F equals p times A]
    P5 --> P6[Saddle / Load Platform]
    P6 --> P7[Heavy Load W]
    P3 --> P8[Relief Valve Set at Rated Load]
    P8 --> P9[Safety Overflow to Reservoir]
    P3 --> P10[Check Valve prevents Load Drop]
```

## 4.3 Comparative Block Diagram: Mechanical vs Hydraulic Jacks

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph Mechanical_Jacks
        M1[Screw Jack]
        M2[Rack and Pinion Jack]
        M3[Lever Jack]
    end
    subgraph Hydraulic_Jacks
        H1[Bottle Jack]
        H2[Floor Trolley Jack]
        H3[Long Stroke Ram Jack]
    end
    subgraph Pneumatic_Jacks
        PN1[Air Bag Jack]
        PN2[Compressed Air Ram]
    end
    M1 --> App[Application: Vehicle Workshop, Structural Erection]
    M2 --> App
    M3 --> App
    H1 --> App
    H2 --> App
    H3 --> App
    PN1 --> App
    PN2 --> App
```

## 4.4 Schematic Layout of a Typical Screw Jack

```
                  ┌──────────────┐
                  │   LOAD W     │  ← Top cap / saddle
                  └──────┬───────┘
                         │
                  ┌──────┴───────┐
                  │  SCREW HEAD  │  ← Carries the load
                  └──────┬───────┘
                         │
            ╔════════════╧════════════╗
            ║   THREADED SHAFT        ║  ← Rotates inside
            ║   (Mean Diameter d)     ║     fixed nut
            ║   Pitch = p             ║
            ╚════════════╤════════════╝
                         │
                  ┌──────┴───────┐
                  │  FIXED NUT   │  ← Welded to frame
                  └──────┬───────┘
                         │
            ┌────────────┴────────────┐
            │   BASE / FRAME           │  ← Anchored to ground
            └──────────────────────────┘

   Lever Arm (length l) ──────► attached to screw head
                                  Operator applies P here
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define a Jack and List Its Main Types. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**

A jack is a portable lifting device used to raise heavy loads through a small applied effort, exploiting mechanical advantage. The main types are:

1. **Mechanical jacks** — screw jack, rack and pinion jack, lever jack.
2. **Hydraulic jacks** — bottle jack, floor trolley jack, long-stroke ram.
3. **Pneumatic jacks** — air bag jack, pneumatic ram.

### Q2. State the Condition for Self-Locking of a Screw Jack. `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**

A screw jack is **self-locking** when the helix angle $\alpha$ is **less than** the friction angle $\phi$, i.e., $\alpha < \phi$. Equivalently, the efficiency is **less than 50%**. Under this condition, the screw will not unwind even if the applied effort is removed — an essential safety property.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]`

**A screw jack is used to lift a load of 30 kN. The screw has a mean diameter of 60 mm and a pitch of 15 mm. The lever arm is 500 mm long, and the coefficient of friction between the screw and nut is 0.10.**

**(a)** Determine the effort required at the end of the lever to (i) raise the load and (ii) lower the load. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** Calculate the mechanical advantage, velocity ratio, and efficiency of the jack during the lifting operation. State whether the jack is self-locking. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Given:** $W = 30 \text{ kN}$, $d = 60 \text{ mm} = 0.06 \text{ m}$, $p = 15 \text{ mm} = 0.015 \text{ m}$, $l = 500 \text{ mm} = 0.5 \text{ m}$, $\mu = 0.10$.

Helix angle:

$$\tan \alpha = \frac{p}{\pi d} = \frac{15}{\pi \times 60} = \frac{15}{188.5} = 0.0796$$

$$\alpha = 4.55^\circ$$

Friction angle:

$$\phi = \tan^{-1}(0.10) = 5.71^\circ$$

**(i) Effort to Raise:**

$$\alpha + \phi = 4.55 + 5.71 = 10.26^\circ$$

$$\tan(\alpha + \phi) = 0.1810$$

$$P_{\text{raise}} = \frac{W d \tan(\alpha + \phi)}{2 l} = \frac{30{,}000 \times 0.06 \times 0.1810}{2 \times 0.5}$$

$$P_{\text{raise}} = \frac{325.8}{1.0} = 325.8 \text{ N}$$

**(ii) Effort to Lower:**

$$\phi - \alpha = 5.71 - 4.55 = 1.16^\circ$$

$$\tan(\phi - \alpha) = 0.0202$$

$$P_{\text{lower}} = \frac{W d \tan(\phi - \alpha)}{2 l} = \frac{30{,}000 \times 0.06 \times 0.0202}{2 \times 0.5}$$

$$P_{\text{lower}} = \frac{36.4}{1.0} = 36.4 \text{ N}$$

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Mechanical Advantage:**

$$\text{MA} = \frac{W}{P_{\text{raise}}} = \frac{30{,}000}{325.8} = 92.08$$

**Velocity Ratio:**

$$\text{VR} = \frac{2 \pi l}{p} = \frac{2 \pi \times 500}{15} = \frac{3141.6}{15} = 209.44$$

**Efficiency:**

$$\eta = \frac{\text{MA}}{\text{VR}} = \frac{92.08}{209.44} = 0.4396 = 43.96\%$$

**Self-Locking Check:**

$\alpha = 4.55^\circ < \phi = 5.71^\circ$. Hence **self-locking** (also confirmed by $\eta < 50\%$).

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning:** Students frequently forget that $\alpha$ and $\phi$ must both be computed in **degrees** before adding, OR both in **radians**. Mixing units causes a 2-3 mark deduction. Also, the lever arm length $l$ is the **full length** from screw axis, not half — common mistake.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) `[KTU University Exam – May 2024]`

**(a)** With the aid of a free-body diagram, derive the expression for the effort required to raise a load by a screw jack with a square thread, considering friction at the screw threads. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A hydraulic jack has a plunger diameter of 20 mm and a ram diameter of 100 mm. The lever arm used to operate the plunger has a mechanical advantage of 10. Determine: (i) the force that must be applied to lift a load of 50 kN, (ii) the pressure developed in the hydraulic fluid. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution to (a)

**Free Body Setup:** A single thread of the screw is unwrapped and represented as a block of weight $W$ on an inclined plane of angle $\alpha$ (helix angle).

**Forces acting on the block:**
- Weight $W$ acting vertically downward.
- Effort force $P'$ acting along the plane (upward).
- Normal reaction $R_N$ acting perpendicular to the plane.
- Friction force $F_f = \mu R_N$ acting down the plane (opposing motion).

**Resolution along plane:**

$$P' = W \sin \alpha + F_f = W \sin \alpha + \mu R_N$$

**Resolution perpendicular to plane:**

$$R_N = W \cos \alpha$$

**Substitute $\mu = \tan \phi$:**

$$P' = W \sin \alpha + W \cos \alpha \tan \phi$$

**Convert to tangent form using trigonometric identities:**

$$P' = \frac{W \sin(\alpha + \phi)}{\cos(\alpha + \phi)} = W \tan(\alpha + \phi)$$

**Convert tangential force $P'$ to lever end effort $P$** using moment balance:

$$P \cdot l = P' \cdot \frac{d}{2}$$

$$\boxed{P = \frac{W d}{2 l} \tan(\alpha + \phi)}$$

#### Model Solution to (b)

**Given:** Plunger diameter $d_p = 20 \text{ mm}$, so $a = \pi d_p^2 / 4 = \pi (20)^2 / 4 = 314.16 \text{ mm}^2$.

Ram diameter $d_r = 100 \text{ mm}$, so $A = \pi d_r^2 / 4 = \pi (100)^2 / 4 = 7853.98 \text{ mm}^2$.

Load $W = 50 \text{ kN} = 50{,}000 \text{ N}$.

**(i) Force at lever:**

The hydraulic jack delivers a force on the ram equal to pressure × ram area. The lever's mechanical advantage of 10 means the operator's effort is amplified by 10 at the plunger.

Total mechanical advantage of jack = $\dfrac{A}{a} \times 10$

$$\text{Total MA} = \frac{7853.98}{314.16} \times 10 = 25 \times 10 = 250$$

$$F = \frac{W}{\text{Total MA}} = \frac{50{,}000}{250} = 200 \text{ N}$$

**(ii) Pressure in fluid:**

$$p = \frac{W}{A} = \frac{50{,}000}{7853.98 \times 10^{-6}} = 6.366 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 6.366 \text{ MPa}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students compute the force at the plunger end (ignoring the lever MA) and forget to multiply by 10 — leading to an answer of 2000 N instead of the correct **200 N**. Also, do **not** confuse mm² with m² when computing pressure; use SI base units (m²) before substituting into the pressure formula.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Must-Memorize for KTU Exams):**

- A **jack** converts a small effort over a long distance into a large force over a short distance.
- The **three primary families** are mechanical, hydraulic, and pneumatic.
- **Screw jack** uses a square thread — most efficient thread form.
- **Helix angle:** $\tan \alpha = p / (\pi d)$
- **Effort to lift:** $P = W \tan(\alpha + \phi)$ at screw surface; $P = \dfrac{W d \tan(\alpha+\phi)}{2l}$ at lever end.
- **Effort to lower:** $P = W \tan(\phi - \alpha)$
- **Self-locking** iff $\alpha < \phi$, equivalently $\eta < 50\%$.
- **Maximum efficiency** occurs when $\alpha = 45^\circ - \phi/2$, giving $\eta_{\max} = (1-\sin\phi)/(1+\sin\phi)$.
- **Mechanical advantage** = $W/P$; **Velocity ratio** = $2\pi l / p$.
- **Efficiency** = MA / VR.
- **Hydraulic jack** obeys **Pascal's Law** — MA = ratio of ram to plunger areas.
- **Safety essentials:** positive mechanical lock, relief valve, check valve, rated capacity plate.
- **Industrial capacities:** screw jacks up to **50 tonnes**, hydraulic jacks up to **1000+ tonnes**, pneumatic jacks up to **30 tonnes**.
- Always work in **consistent SI units** (convert mm to m before substituting into formulas).
- Self-locking is a **safety property** — non-self-locking screws must NEVER be used for vertical lifting without an external brake.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Hand Trucks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT522 - SAFETY IN MATERIAL HANDLING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Hand Trucks # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **Hand Truck** (also called a handcart, sack truck, trolley, or dolly depending on configuration) is a non-powered, manually-propelled wheeled device used for the short-distance transport, lifting, and stacking of unitized or loose materials within a workplace. As per the KTU 2024 Scheme syllabus for **PESFT522 – Safety in Material Handling**, hand trucks are classified as a sub-category of **Non-powered Industrial Trucks** under the broader umbrella of **Manual Material Handling (MMH)** equipment. In technical terms, a hand truck is a *first-class lever with a mechanical advantage*, in which the axle of the wheel acts as the fulcrum, the load provides the effort arm, and the operator applies the force at the handles. The device is governed by the principles of **statics, moment equilibrium, and rolling friction**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** Hand trucks are the most common, lowest-cost, and most frequently used material handling equipment in industries such as warehousing, retail, postal services, manufacturing, and airports. Safety assessment of hand trucks is mandatory under the **Factory Act 1948 (India)**, **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.178**, and the **Indian Standard IS 7251**. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition Imagine you need to move a heavy gas cylinder across your kitchen. You can't lift it because it's too heavy, and dragging it would damage the floor. What do you do? You roll it! A hand truck works on exactly this same idea — instead of **lifting** the weight against gravity, you **tilt** it and let the **wheels carry the load**. Think of a hand truck as a **mechanical seesaw mounted on wheels**: - The **wheel axle** is the pivot (fulcrum). - The **load** sits on the nose plate. - **You (the operator)** push down or pull back on the handles to keep the load balanced. The "magic" is that the operator only needs to provide a *small tilting force* to balance the *large weight* of the load — the **mechanical advantage** comes from the geometry of the handles. > [!NOTE] > **Key Design Metric:** The standard nose plate of a hand truck is **455 mm × 190 mm (18 in × 7.5 in)**, with a typical load rating ranging from **90 kg to 450 kg**. ## 1.3 Classification Snapshot | Hand Truck Class | Typical Capacity | Primary Use | |---|---|---| | Two-wheel hand truck (Sack truck) | 90 – 250 kg | Crates, sacks, cylinders | | Platform hand truck (Four-wheel) | 200 – 450 kg | Boxes, appliances | | Convertible hand truck | 180 – 360 kg | Dual configuration | | Drum hand truck | 200 – 400 kg | 55-gallon drums | | Pallet hand truck (Pallet jack) | 1000 – 2500 kg | Standard pallets | --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Physics of a Hand Truck A two-wheel hand truck is a **first-class lever system**. When the operator tilts the truck back so that the load is supported entirely on the two wheels, the system reaches static equilibrium under three forces: 1. **Weight of the load (W_L)** acting downward at the load's center of gravity (CG). 2. **Tilt reaction force (R)** at the wheel axle, acting along the truck frame. 3. **Operator effort (F)** applied at the handles, acting downward and slightly backward. ### 2.2 Free-Body Logic (Step-by-Step) For a two-wheel hand truck tilted at angle **θ** from the vertical: - **Vertical equilibrium:** The vertical component of the wheel reaction supports the load weight. - **Moment equilibrium about the axle:** The operator's handle force creates a counter-moment to the moment created by the load weight about the axle. - **Rolling resistance:** A small horizontal force is required to overcome rolling friction at the wheels. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Why "Hand Trucks" belong to MMH Safety:** The Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) and the **National Institute of Occupational Safety and Health (NIOSH)** have published the **Lifting Equation (1991)** to evaluate the Recommended Weight Limit (RWL) for manual lifting. Hand trucks are recommended because they reduce the **horizontal and vertical travel distance** of the load, drastically lowering the risk of **musculoskeletal disorders (MSDs)** such as lower-back injuries. ## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Quantity | Formula | Description | Units | |---|---|---|---|---| | 1 | Required Handle Force | $F = W_L \cdot \dfrac{a}{b}$ | Lever principle: $F$ = effort, $W_L$ = load, $a$ = load-arm, $b$ = effort-arm | N (Newtons) | | 2 | Mechanical Advantage | $MA = \dfrac{b}{a} = \dfrac{W_L}{F}$ | Ratio of effort-arm to load-arm | dimensionless | | 3 | Vertical Lift of Load CG | $h = L \cdot (1 - \cos\theta)$ | Height the load CG rises when tilted | m | | 4 | Rolling Resistance Force | $F_r = \mu_r \cdot W_L$ | $\mu_r$ = rolling friction coefficient (rubber tire: 0.015 – 0.035) | N | | 5 | Start-up Push Force | $F_s = F_r + F_a$ | $F_a$ = inertia force = $\dfrac{W_L \cdot v}{g \cdot t}$ | N | | 6 | Required Tilt Angle | $\theta = \tan^{-1}\left(\dfrac{a}{h_t}\right)$ | $h_t$ = effective handle height | degrees | | 7 | Stability Against Tipping | $SF = \dfrac{M_R}{M_O} \geq 1.5$ | $M_R$ = restoring moment, $M_O$ = overturning moment | dimensionless | | 8 | NIOSH RWL | $RWL = LC \cdot HM \cdot VM \cdot DM \cdot AM \cdot FM \cdot CM$ | Recommended Weight Limit (multipliers) | kg | > [!NOTE] > **Critical Pipe-Symbol Note for KTU Board Exams:** When writing **absolute value** in the KTU answer script, use the notation $\vert x \vert$ or $\mid x \mid$, **never** the bare pipe symbol $\vert$ inside a row — it confuses the typist and may lose marks. ## 2.4 Engineering Utility of Hand Truck Mechanics The lever principle applied in hand trucks is a **direct application of statics in occupational ergonomics**. Every workplace safety audit in KTU 2024 Scheme's *Safety Engineering* module evaluates the **moment arm ratio** $(b/a)$ to ensure that the operator's required force $F$ remains below the **NIOSH Action Limit (AL) of 340 N** for prolonged tasks, and the **Maximum Permissible Limit (MPL) of 1130 N** for occasional lifts. The **stability factor (SF) of 1.5** is the universal safety margin used in mechanical handling equipment design — it ensures that even with a **50% overload or sudden jerk**, the truck will not tip backward and crush the operator. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Required Handle Force for Static Equilibrium **Statement:** Derive the expression for the operator's required handle force $F$ for a two-wheel hand truck carrying a vertical load $W_L$ at a horizontal distance $a$ from the wheel axle, with the operator's hands applying force at horizontal distance $b$ from the axle. ### Given: - Load weight: $W_L$ (N), acting vertically downward at horizontal distance $a$ from the axle. - Operator's effort: $F$ (N), acting vertically downward at horizontal distance $b$ from the axle. - Both arms are horizontal (truck tilted such that the frame is approximately horizontal). - The system is in **static equilibrium** (no rotation, no translation). ### Step 1: Free-Body Diagram Setup Three forces act on the hand truck frame: - $W_L$ at distance $a$ (load side). - $F$ at distance $b$ (handle side). - Reaction $R$ at the axle. ### Step 2: Apply Translational Equilibrium Sum of vertical forces equals zero: $$ \sum F_y = 0 $$ $$ R - W_L - F = 0 $$ $$ R = W_L + F $$ This step explains that the ground reaction at the axle must support the **combined** weight of the load and the downward push of the operator. ### Step 3: Apply Rotational Equilibrium Taking moments about the **axle (pivot)** and setting $\sum M_{axle} = 0$: The load $W_L$ creates a moment tending to tilt the truck *forward* (load side down): $$ M_{load} = W_L \cdot a $$ The operator's effort $F$ creates a counter-moment tending to tilt the truck *backward* (handle side down): $$ M_{handle} = F \cdot b $$ For equilibrium: $$ M_{load} = M_{handle} $$ $$ W_L \cdot a = F \cdot b $$ ### Step 4: Solve for the Operator's Force $$ F = W_L \cdot \dfrac{a}{b} $$ ### Step 5: Mechanical Advantage Since the effort-arm $b$ is typically 2 to 4 times longer than the load-arm $a$, the operator applies only a fraction of the load weight: $$ MA = \dfrac{b}{a} $$ For a typical $b/a = 3$, the operator handles only **33% of the load weight** — which is the entire ergonomic rationale for using a hand truck over manual lifting. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Final Result:** > $$\boxed{F = W_L \cdot \dfrac{a}{b} = \dfrac{W_L}{MA}}$$ > This is the most-tested formula on hand trucks in KTU ESE (End Semester Examination). --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Vertical Lift of the Load's Center of Gravity **Statement:** When a hand truck is tilted from vertical to an angle $\theta$, the load's CG (initially at the wheel base) rises vertically. Derive the expression for this vertical lift $h$. ### Given: - Initial position: load CG is on the floor (height = 0). - Frame length from axle to load CG: $L$ (m). - Tilt angle from vertical: $\theta$. ### Step 1: Initial CG Position (Vertical Truck) When the truck is vertical, the load CG is at horizontal distance $L$ from the axle, and at **height 0** (on the floor). ### Step 2: CG Position After Tilting by Angle $\theta$ After tilting, the load CG is at: - Horizontal distance: $L \cdot \sin\theta$ - Vertical height: $L \cdot \cos\theta$ ### Step 3: Calculate the Vertical Rise $$ h = \text{New Height} - \text{Initial Height} $$ $$ h = L \cdot \cos\theta - 0 $$ Wait — the CG is initially *below* the axle (on the floor), so the rise is from $-L$ (when at the bottom of the nose plate) to the projected position. The standard formula is: $$ h = L \cdot (1 - \cos\theta) $$ > [!NOTE] > This expression is critical for **NIOSH Lifting Equation** applications, where the **Vertical Multiplier (VM)** depends on $h$. --- ## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Hand Truck Types vs. Parameters | Parameter | Two-Wheel Sack Truck | Four-Wheel Platform | Pallet Jack | Drum Hand Truck | |---|---|---|---|---| | Load Capacity (kg) | 90 – 250 | 200 – 450 | 1000 – 2500 | 200 – 400 | | Wheels | 2 rubber | 4 swivel | 2 large + 2 small | 2 + 1 cradle | | Effort Arm (m) | 1.1 – 1.3 | 0.9 – 1.1 | 1.0 – 1.2 | 1.0 – 1.2 | | Load Arm (m) | 0.4 – 0.5 | 0.5 – 0.7 | 0.6 – 0.8 | 0.4 – 0.5 | | $MA = b/a$ | 2.5 – 3.0 | 1.5 – 2.0 | 1.5 – 1.8 | 2.4 – 2.8 | | Operator Force @ Max Load (N) | 320 – 400 | 900 – 1100 | 5500 – 6800 | 700 – 850 | | Best For | Sacks, cylinders | Boxes, appliances | Palletized goods | Liquid drums | | Main Hazard | Backward tip-over | Pinch points | Foot crush | Spillage | --- ## 3.4 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Board Style) **Question:** A worker uses a two-wheel hand truck to transport a load of $W_L = 900$ N. The horizontal distance of the load CG from the wheel axle is $a = 0.45$ m, and the operator's handles are at horizontal distance $b = 1.2$ m from the axle. Assuming the truck is in static equilibrium and the rolling friction coefficient is $\mu_r = 0.025$, calculate: (a) The required handle force to hold the load. (b) The mechanical advantage. (c) The horizontal force required to start motion on a flat floor. ### Solution: **(a) Required handle force:** $$ F = W_L \cdot \dfrac{a}{b} = 900 \cdot \dfrac{0.45}{1.2} = 900 \cdot 0.375 $$ $$ \boxed{F = 337.5 \text{ N}} $$ **(b) Mechanical advantage:** $$ MA = \dfrac{b}{a} = \dfrac{1.2}{0.45} = 2.667 $$ $$ \boxed{MA \approx 2.67} $$ **(c) Force to overcome rolling resistance:** $$ F_r = \mu_r \cdot W_L = 0.025 \cdot 900 $$ $$ \boxed{F_r = 22.5 \text{ N}} $$ **Interpretation:** The operator must apply only **337.5 N** vertically at the handle (a manageable effort) to balance a **900 N** load — and just **22.5 N** additional horizontal push to start the wheels rolling. --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Functional Architecture of a Two-Wheel Hand Truck ```mermaid flowchart TB node1["LOAD BLOCK
W_L acts at CG"] node2["NOSE PLATE
455 mm x 190 mm"] node3["VERTICAL FRAME
Steel channel section"] node4["WHEEL AXLE
Fulcrum point"] node5["RUBBER WHEELS
Diameter 200 to 260 mm"] node6["HANDLE GRIP
Ergonomic rubber sleeve"] node7["CROSS BRACE
Load support bar"] node8["OPERATOR
Applies F at distance b"] node1 --> node2 node2 --> node3 node3 --> node4 node4 --> node5 node3 --> node7 node7 --> node2 node3 --> node6 node8 --> node6 ``` ## 4.2 Force & Moment Flow Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR loadA["W_L
Load weight"] -->|arm a| pivotA["AXLE
Fulcrum"] pivotA -->|arm b| effortA["F
Handle force"] pivotA -->|reaction R| groundA["GROUND"] noteA["Moment Equilibrium:
W_L * a = F * b"] -.-> pivotA ``` ## 4.3 Hand Truck Selection Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD startA["START:
Identify load type"] --> q1{"Load shape
regular?"} q1 -->|Yes| q2{"Load > 250 kg?"} q1 -->|No, cylindrical| drA["Use DRUM HAND TRUCK"] q2 -->|Yes| q3{"Palletized?"} q2 -->|No| sackA["Use TWO-WHEEL SACK TRUCK"] q3 -->|Yes| palletA["Use PALLET JACK"] q3 -->|No| platA["Use FOUR-WHEEL PLATFORM TRUCK"] drA --> safetyA["SAFETY:
Wear steel-toe boots
Use load strap"] sackA --> safetyA palletA --> safetyA platA --> safetyA ``` ## 4.4 Block Diagram of Hand Truck Safety Subsystem ```mermaid flowchart TB ssub1["OPERATOR TRAINING
Module per OSHA 1910.178"] --> ssub2["PRE-USE INSPECTION
Wheels, frame, handles"] ssub2 --> ssub3["LOAD ASSESSMENT
Weight less than rated capacity"] ssub3 --> ssub4["PROPER TILT AND BALANCE
Load CG over wheels"] ssub4 --> ssub5["TRANSIT
Maintain clear line of sight"] ssub5 --> ssub6["UNLOADING
Lower load gently; never drop"] ssub6 --> ssub7["POST-USE STORAGE
Park in designated area"] ssub7 --> ssub1 ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Define a hand truck. List any four advantages of using hand trucks for material handling.** **Model Answer:** A hand truck is a non-powered, manually-propelled wheeled device used for short-distance transport of unitized or loose loads. It operates on the lever principle, with the wheel axle acting as the fulcrum. Four advantages: 1. **Low capital cost** — typically ₹2,000 to ₹15,000, accessible to small businesses. 2. **No external power required** — energy-efficient and eco-friendly. 3. **Reduces operator fatigue** — mechanical advantage of 2 to 4 reduces required effort to 25%–50% of the load weight. 4. **Versatile** — usable in narrow aisles, stairs (with stair-climbing wheels), and on uneven surfaces. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **State the NIOSH Recommended Weight Limit (RWL) equation and explain the significance of the Frequency Multiplier (FM).** **Model Answer:** $$ RWL = LC \times HM \times VM \times DM \times AM \times FM \times CM $$ Where $LC = 23$ kg (Load Constant), and the multipliers $HM, VM, DM, AM, FM, CM$ are dimensionless correction factors. **Significance of FM:** The Frequency Multiplier reduces the RWL as the **lifting frequency** increases. For example, $FM = 1.00$ for one lift per 5 minutes, but $FM = 0.25$ for continuous lifting. This reflects the **physiological fatigue** of repetitive motion and is a critical safety parameter in MMH guidelines. --- ## Part B — Full-Length Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2022] **(a) [7 Marks]** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the working principle of a two-wheel hand truck. Derive the expression for the operator's effort required to balance a vertical load. **(b) [7 Marks]** A worker pushes a four-wheel platform hand truck carrying a $W_L = 1500$ N load. The coefficient of rolling friction is $\mu_r = 0.030$ and the wheel diameter is $d = 0.20$ m. Calculate: (i) the rolling resistance, (ii) the work done in moving the truck a distance of $25$ m, and (iii) the minimum coefficient of friction at the operator's shoes to prevent slipping (assume operator mass $m = 70$ kg, $g = 9.81$ m/s²). ### Model Solution — Question A **(a) Working Principle and Derivation:** A two-wheel hand truck consists of a **vertical steel frame** with a **nose plate at the bottom** for placing the load, **two wheels mounted on a common axle** at the rear, and **handles at the top** for the operator. **Working Principle:** When the operator tilts the truck backward, the load lifts off the ground and is balanced on the wheels. The **wheel axle acts as the fulcrum (pivot)** of a first-class lever. The **load weight $W_L$** at distance $a$ from the axle is balanced by the **operator's downward force $F$** applied at the handles at distance $b$ from the axle. **Free-Body Diagram (verbal):** ``` OPERATOR (F downward) | v [ HANDLE ] | | | b | | | | AXLE • | <-- FULCRUM | a | | | [ NOSE PLATE ] ^ | LOAD (W_L downward) ``` **Derivation:** Taking moments about the axle for static equilibrium: $$ \sum M_{axle} = 0 $$ $$ W_L \cdot a - F \cdot b = 0 $$ $$ F = W_L \cdot \dfrac{a}{b} $$ This expression is the **fundamental lever equation** for all manual handling equipment using a first-class lever configuration. **(b) Numerical Solution:** **(i) Rolling Resistance:** $$ F_r = \mu_r \cdot W_L = 0.030 \cdot 1500 $$ $$ \boxed{F_r = 45 \text{ N}} $$ **(ii) Work Done over 25 m:** $$ W = F_r \cdot d = 45 \cdot 25 $$ $$ \boxed{W = 1125 \text{ J} = 1.125 \text{ kJ}} $$ **(iii) Minimum Coefficient of Friction for Operator:** Operator weight: $W_{op} = m \cdot g = 70 \cdot 9.81 = 686.7$ N To prevent slipping, the friction force at the shoes must equal the horizontal push: $$ \mu_{min} \cdot W_{op} \geq F_r $$ $$ \mu_{min} \geq \dfrac{F_r}{W_{op}} = \dfrac{45}{686.7} $$ $$ \boxed{\mu_{min} = 0.0655} $$ This is well within the typical **shoe-floor friction range of 0.4 to 0.9** for dry surfaces, confirming that slipping is not a concern — but on **wet, oily, or contaminated floors**, $\mu$ can drop below 0.0655, and a slip becomes highly probable. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – May 2023] **(a) [7 Marks]** Compare and contrast two-wheel hand trucks and four-wheel platform trucks. Discuss the safety hazards specific to each. **(b) [7 Marks]** A warehouse uses a drum hand truck to transport a $200$-litre steel drum filled with oil. The drum mass is $45$ kg, the oil mass is $180$ kg, and the hand truck mass is $30$ kg. Calculate: (i) the total load, (ii) the operator's required handle force given $a = 0.50$ m and $b = 1.10$ m, and (iii) comment on the operator's risk of musculoskeletal injury if the task is repeated 5 times per hour. ### Model Solution — Question B **(a) Comparative Analysis:** | Parameter | Two-Wheel Hand Truck | Four-Wheel Platform Truck | |---|---|---| | Wheels | 2 (rear axle) | 4 (swivel casters) | | Load Support | Operator must balance | Platform self-supports | | Load Capacity | 90 – 250 kg | 200 – 450 kg | | Maneuverability | High in narrow aisles | Requires wider turning radius | | Operator Skill | Moderate to high | Low | | Stability | Risk of backward tip | Low (4-point contact) | | Best Use | Sacks, cylinders, drums | Boxes, appliances, cases | **Safety Hazards — Two-Wheel:** 1. **Backward tip-over** — leading cause of hand truck injuries. 2. **Operator crush injury** between truck and wall. 3. **Awkward posture** during tilting. **Safety Hazards — Four-Wheel:** 1. **Pinch points** between wheels and frame. 2. **Foot/leg crush** if wheel rolls over foot. 3. **Loss of control on ramps** — no operator balancing input. **(b) Numerical Solution:** **(i) Total Load:** $$ W_L = (45 + 180 + 30) \cdot 9.81 = 255 \cdot 9.81 $$ $$ \boxed{W_L = 2501.55 \text{ N}} $$ **(ii) Required Handle Force:** $$ F = W_L \cdot \dfrac{a}{b} = 2501.55 \cdot \dfrac{0.50}{1.10} $$ $$ \boxed{F = 1137.07 \text{ N}} $$ **(iii) Ergonomic Comment:** The force $F = 1137$ N **exceeds the NIOSH Maximum Permissible Limit (MPL) of 1130 N** for occasional lifts. Performing this task 5 times per hour would push the worker into the **high-risk zone for lower-back injury**. **Recommendation:** Use a **powered drum truck** or a **hydraulic lift table**, or redesign the task so that the operator's CG is closer to the load (reducing $a$). --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Sign Convention Error:** Students often forget to take moments about the *axle* (not the handle). Always specify the pivot in your free-body diagram — **1 mark lost if omitted**. > 2. **Unit Conversion:** Loading values in the problem are sometimes given in **kgf** (kilogram-force) — convert to **Newtons** by multiplying by $g = 9.81$ m/s² before applying formulas. > 3. **Forgetting Rolling Friction:** A purely static analysis gives $F = W_L \cdot (a/b)$. For motion, **add the rolling resistance** $F_r = \mu_r \cdot W_L$ — many students omit this and lose 2 marks. > 4. **Confusing MA with Velocity Ratio:** MA is a *force ratio*; it is dimensionless. Do not write units. > 5. **NIOSH RWL Misuse:** RWL applies to **manual lifting**, not hand-truck operation. State the RWL only if the question explicitly asks about the lifting component. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - **Definition:** A hand truck is a non-powered, wheeled, manually-propelled device that operates as a **first-class lever** with the wheel axle as the fulcrum. - **Governing Equation:** $F = W_L \cdot (a/b)$, where $a$ is the load-arm and $b$ is the effort-arm. The **mechanical advantage** is $MA = b/a$. - **Standard Capacity:** 90 – 250 kg for two-wheel; 200 – 450 kg for four-wheel platform; up to **2500 kg** for pallet jacks. - **Stability Rule:** $SF = M_R / M_O \geq 1.5$ (50% safety margin against tipping). - **Rolling Friction:** Rubber tires on smooth concrete give $\mu_r = 0.015$ to $0.035$. - **NIOSH Force Limits:** Action Limit (AL) = 340 N; Maximum Permissible Limit (MPL) = 1130 N. - **Safety Priorities:** (1) Pre-use inspection, (2) Load within rated capacity, (3) Operator training, (4) Clear line of sight, (5) Housekeeping of travel path. - **Common Hand Truck Types:** Two-wheel sack truck, four-wheel platform, convertible, drum truck, pallet jack. - **Key Standards:** OSHA 29 CFR 1910.178, IS 7251, Factory Act 1948, NIOSH Lifting Equation 1991. - **Most-tested Concept in KTU:** The moment equation and mechanical advantage derivation — memorize $F = W_L \cdot (a/b)$ and the free-body diagram. ---

topics.title: Dollies and Wheelbarrows - Storage of Specific Materials - Problems with Hazardous Materials
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT522 - SAFETY IN MATERIAL HANDLING
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Dollies and Wheelbarrows - Storage of Specific Materials - Problems with Hazardous Materials

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Dollies – Formal Definition
A **dolly** is a low, flat platform mounted on rollers or casters used to transport heavy or bulky loads over short horizontal distances in industrial, warehouse, and workshop environments. Dollies are unpowered material handling devices that rely on manual push/pull force or a towing vehicle for locomotion.

**Key Engineering Sub-Types:**
- **Flat Dolly** – a simple flat platform on four swivel casters.
- **Platform Dolly (Trolley)** – a heavier-duty version with reinforced frame and larger wheels.
- **Hand Truck (Two-Wheel Dolly)** – an L-shaped device with a vertical back and toe plate.
- **Pallet Dolly** – designed specifically to carry standard pallets (typically **1200 mm × 1000 mm** or **48 in × 40 in**).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Verbatim Concept):** Dollies are non-powered, wheeled devices intended for short-distance, intermittent transport of unit loads, primarily relying on **rolling friction** rather than sliding friction to reduce the operator effort (push/pull force).

### 1.2 Wheelbarrows – Formal Definition
A **wheelbarrow** is a single-wheel (or sometimes dual-wheel) hand-propelled carrier, consisting of a shallow tray, a single front wheel, and two rear handles for the operator. It is a Class I lever system used for moving loose, granular, or bagged materials over short distances, especially where ground is uneven.

**Variants Recognized in KTU Curriculum:**
- **Single-Wheel Wheelbarrow** – high maneuverability, lower stability.
- **Two-Wheel Wheelbarrow** – higher stability, lower effort, lower maneuverability.
- **Sack Truck / Barrow Truck** – a hybrid used for cylindrical/bagged loads.

### 1.3 Storage of Specific Materials – Formal Definition
The practice of holding raw, in-process, or finished materials in a designated location under controlled conditions (temperature, humidity, segregation, stacking height) to preserve utility, prevent deterioration, and ensure safety. Materials are classified by **state** (solid, liquid, gas), **hazard class** (flammable, corrosive, toxic, reactive), and **physical form** (powder, bulk, sheet, coil).

### 1.4 Hazardous Materials (HazMat) – Formal Definition
Per the **UN Recommendations on the Transport of Dangerous Goods (UNRTDG)**, hazardous materials are substances that pose an unreasonable risk to health, safety, or property during storage, handling, or transport. They are categorized into **9 UN Classes** (Explosives, Gases, Flammable Liquids, Flammable Solids, Oxidizers, Toxic & Infectious, Radioactive, Corrosives, Miscellaneous).

### 1.5 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of a dolly as a "sneaker for heavy boxes."** When you carry a heavy suitcase, you drag it – this is *sliding friction* (high effort). If you place the same suitcase on a small wheeled platform, it rolls – this is *rolling friction* (low effort, often **1/50 to 1/100** of sliding friction).
> 
> **A wheelbarrow is essentially a "lever on wheels."** Imagine lifting a 50 kg bag directly – your back bears the full 50 kg. Place that same bag in a wheelbarrow with the wheel 60 cm from the bag and handles 120 cm from the wheel – your hands now bear only **~25 kg** because the wheel acts as the fulcrum, giving a **mechanical advantage of ~2:1**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Storage of specific materials** is like organizing a refrigerator: raw meat on the bottom shelf (to prevent drips), dairy in the cold zone, fruits in the crisper. In industry, the "cold zone," "middle shelf," and "bottom" are defined by **chemical compatibility** rather than temperature. Storing an acid above a base is a recipe for a violent reaction if a leak occurs.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Operating Principle of Dollies

A dolly converts a **sliding friction** problem into a **rolling friction** problem. The operator's applied force $F$ must overcome:

1. **Rolling resistance** at the wheel–floor interface.
2. **Internal bearing friction** at the caster axle.
3. **Inertial resistance** when starting from rest.
4. **Gradient resistance** when the floor is not perfectly level.

The fundamental rolling resistance equation is:

$$F_{rr} = \mu_r \cdot W$$

Where:
- $F_{rr}$ = rolling resistance force (N)
- $\mu_r$ = coefficient of rolling friction (dimensionless, typically **0.001 to 0.01** for steel wheel on smooth concrete)
- $W$ = normal load on the wheel (N)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For comparison, the coefficient of **kinetic sliding friction** $\mu_k$ between steel and concrete is approximately **0.45 to 0.60**, making rolling **45 to 600 times more efficient** than sliding.

### 2.2 Operating Principle of Wheelbarrow – Class I Lever

The wheelbarrow is a first-class lever where:
- **Fulcrum** = the front wheel (axle).
- **Effort (E)** = the operator's lifting force on the handles.
- **Load (L)** = the weight of the material in the tray.

Applying the principle of moments about the wheel axle:

$$E \times d_e = L \times d_l$$

Solving for Effort:

$$E = L \times \frac{d_l}{d_e}$$

Where:
- $E$ = effort applied at handles (N)
- $L$ = weight of load (N)
- $d_e$ = distance from wheel axle to handles (m)
- $d_l$ = distance from wheel axle to load center of gravity (m)

The **Mechanical Advantage (MA)** is:

$$MA = \frac{d_e}{d_l}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** A typical builder's wheelbarrow has $d_e \approx 1.2$ m and $d_l \approx 0.6$ m, giving $MA = 2.0$. This means a 50 kg load requires only 25 kg of lift force — but the operator must lift through twice the distance.

### 2.3 Storage of Specific Materials – Categorical Logic

Materials are stored using a **3-tier classification system**:

| Tier | Classification Basis | Examples |
|------|----------------------|----------|
| **Tier 1** | Physical State | Solids, Liquids, Gases |
| **Tier 2** | Hazard Class | Flammable, Corrosive, Toxic, Reactive, Oxidizer |
| **Tier 3** | Compatibility Group | Acid-Base-Solvent-Oxidizer-Water-Reactive |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The "Segregation Matrix"** is the cornerstone of hazardous material storage. The U.S. National Fire Protection Association **NFPA 704** "fire diamond" provides a 0–4 rating system for **Health (Blue), Flammability (Red), Reactivity (Yellow), and Special (White)** hazards. Storage compatibility is determined by cross-referencing these ratings.

### 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Concept | Equation | Variables & Units | Boundary Conditions / Notes |
|---|---------|----------|-------------------|------------------------------|
| 1 | Rolling Resistance Force | $F_{rr} = \mu_r \cdot W$ | $F_{rr}$ in N, $\mu_r$ dimensionless, $W$ in N | $\mu_r$ steel-on-steel ≈ **0.001**; rubber-on-asphalt ≈ **0.04** |
| 2 | Wheelbarrow Effort (Class I Lever) | $E = L \times (d_l / d_e)$ | All in N and m | Static equilibrium; ignores handle weight |
| 3 | Mechanical Advantage (Wheelbarrow) | $MA = d_e / d_l$ | Dimensionless | MA > 1 = force amplification; MA < 1 = speed amplification |
| 4 | Caster Load Rating | $W_{max} = (P_{rated} \times N_{wheels}) / SF$ | $W_{max}$ in kg, $P_{rated}$ in kg/wheel, $N$ dimensionless, $SF$ dimensionless | Typical $SF = 1.5$ to $2.0$ for industrial dollies |
| 5 | Pallet Rack Vertical Load | $\sigma_{column} = W_{total} / A_{column}$ | $\sigma$ in MPa, $W$ in N, $A$ in mm² | Must be $\le \sigma_{yield} / SF$ |
| 6 | Stability Ratio (Toppling) | $SR = T_{width} / (2 \times h_{cg})$ | $T_{width}$ base width (m), $h_{cg}$ CoG height (m) | $SR > 1.0$ = stable; $SR < 1.0$ = topples |
| 7 | Hazardous Material Separation Distance | $d = k \cdot \sqrt{Q}$ | $d$ in m, $Q$ quantity in kg, $k$ empirical constant (m·kg⁻⁰·⁵) | Used in chemical warehouse planning |
| 8 | Vapour Pressure (Antoine Eq. – Storage) | $\log_{10}(P) = A - B / (C + T)$ | $P$ in mmHg, $T$ in °C | A, B, C are material-specific constants |
| 9 | Ventilation Air Changes per Hour | $ACH = Q_{air} / V_{room}$ | $Q_{air}$ in m³/h, $V_{room}$ in m³ | Flammable storage: **ACH ≥ 6** (NFPA 30) |
| 10 | Stack Pressure on Bottom Container | $P_{stack} = (n \cdot m \cdot g) / A$ | $P$ in Pa, $n$ = number of containers, $m$ mass (kg), $A$ base area (m²) | Must be $\le$ crush strength of bottom container |

> [!WARNING]
> **CRITICAL KTU CONSTRAINT:** When writing equations inside the markdown table above, the vertical pipe $\vert$ symbol was replaced with $\mid$ or escaped. Students writing in answer sheets should write $E = L \cdot (d_l / d_e)$ or use the colon-slash notation $d_l : d_e$ to avoid losing marks due to illegible bar symbols.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility
- **Manufacturing plants** use dollies to move coils of steel (each weighing **5,000 to 25,000 kg**) safely.
- **Construction sites** rely on wheelbarrows for concrete pouring, soil transport, and brick handling.
- **Pharmaceutical warehouses** apply the storage matrix to segregate **API powders** (Active Pharmaceutical Ingredients) from solvents, oxidizers, and water-reactive compounds.
- **Hazardous material warehouses** in oil refineries use the **NFPA 30 / OSHA 1910.106** standards for flammable liquid storage cabinets.

## SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Rolling Resistance vs Sliding Friction – Why Dollies are Efficient

**Given:** A wooden crate of mass $m = 100$ kg must be moved **5 m** across a concrete floor. Two methods are compared.

#### Method A – Sliding (No Dolly)
The horizontal force required equals the kinetic friction force:

$$F_{slide} = \mu_k \cdot N = \mu_k \cdot (m \cdot g)$$

Substituting $m = 100$ kg, $g = 9.81$ m/s², $\mu_k$ (wood on concrete) $= 0.50$:

$$F_{slide} = 0.50 \times (100 \times 9.81)$$

$$F_{slide} = 0.50 \times 981 = 490.5 \text{ N}$$

**Work done against friction over 5 m:**

$$W_{slide} = F_{slide} \times d = 490.5 \times 5 = 2452.5 \text{ J}$$

#### Method B – Rolling (On a 4-Wheel Dolly)
The horizontal force required equals the rolling resistance:

$$F_{roll} = \mu_r \cdot W = \mu_r \cdot (m \cdot g)$$

For a steel caster wheel on smooth concrete, $\mu_r = 0.005$:

$$F_{roll} = 0.005 \times 981 = 4.905 \text{ N}$$

**Work done against rolling resistance over 5 m:**

$$W_{roll} = 4.905 \times 5 = 24.525 \text{ J}$$

#### Efficiency Comparison

$$\eta = \frac{W_{roll}}{W_{slide}} = \frac{24.525}{2452.5} = 0.01 = 1\%$$

The dolly reduces the required energy (and effort) to **1% of the sliding value** — a 100-fold improvement. This is the engineering rationale behind virtually all industrial material handling equipment.

---

### 3.2 Derivation: Wheelbarrow Effort with Load + Handle Weight

**Given:** A wheelbarrow carries a load of $L = 400$ N (≈ 40.8 kg) in the tray. The handles (and operator's effective arm weight) exert a downward force of $H = 80$ N on the rear. Geometry: load center of gravity is $d_l = 0.5$ m forward of the wheel axle; handle center of gravity is $d_e = 1.0$ m behind the wheel axle. Find the upward lift force $E$ at the handles to just balance the wheelbarrow.

**Free-Body Diagram Logic:**
- The wheel is the **pivot/fulcrum** (assume wheel is locked; we are analyzing the static "lift-off" condition).
- Sum of moments about the wheel axle = 0 (equilibrium).

**Taking clockwise moments as positive (load side) and counter-clockwise as positive (handle side):**

$$E \times d_e - L \times d_l - H \times d_{h} = 0$$

Where $d_h$ is the horizontal distance of handle CoG behind the wheel — but since the handle weight acts vertically downward directly on the handle, it creates a counter-moment with the handle's vertical force. For pure vertical loading, the handle's own weight and the effort share the same line of action at the handle.

**Refined equilibrium (moments about the wheel):**

$$E \times d_e = L \times d_l$$

$$E = \frac{L \times d_l}{d_e}$$

Substituting values:

$$E = \frac{400 \times 0.5}{1.0} = 200 \text{ N}$$

**Including handle self-weight** (creates additional moment — to be lifted):

$$E_{total} = \frac{L \times d_l + H \times d_{eh}}{d_e}$$

$$E_{total} = \frac{400 \times 0.5 + 80 \times 0.5}{1.0} = \frac{200 + 40}{1.0} = 240 \text{ N}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Note on the simplification:** The above treats the operator's hands as a single point load at the handle. In practice, two-handed lifting distributes force asymmetrically and is a frequent cause of musculoskeletal disorders (MSDs) — a key **KTU safety concern**.

---

### 3.3 Derivation: Pallet Rack Storage – Stacking Safety

**Given:** A standard pallet of mass $m_p = 25$ kg carries a load of $m_L = 500$ kg. The pallet base area is $A = 1.2$ m × 1.0 m = 1.2 m². Pallets are stacked $n = 4$ high.

**Load on the bottom pallet:**

$$W_{bottom} = (m_p + m_L) \times n \times g$$

$$W_{bottom} = (25 + 500) \times 4 \times 9.81$$

$$W_{bottom} = 525 \times 4 \times 9.81 = 20{,}601 \text{ N}$$

**Pressure on the bottom pallet:**

$$P_{bottom} = \frac{W_{bottom}}{A} = \frac{20{,}601}{1.2} = 17{,}167.5 \text{ Pa} \approx 17.17 \text{ kPa}$$

A standard wooden pallet has a crush strength of approximately **50 to 100 kPa** — so 4-high stacking is safe. However, **6-high stacking** would yield:

$$P_{6-high} = \frac{525 \times 6 \times 9.81}{1.2} = 25{,}751 \text{ Pa} \approx 25.75 \text{ kPa}$$

Still within the safe zone, but dynamic loads (forklift impacts) can transiently double the load, making **6-high stacking risky** without reinforced pallets.

---

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Hazardous Material Storage Compatibility

| Hazard Class | Compatible With | INCOMPATIBLE With (DO NOT STORE TOGETHER) | Required Separation |
|--------------|-----------------|-------------------------------------------|---------------------|
| **Flammable Liquids** (UN Class 3) | Other flammables (in same cabinet) | Oxidizers, Acids, Bases | ≥ 6 m or fire-rated wall |
| **Oxidizers** (UN Class 5) | Other oxidizers (with caution) | Flammables, Organic peroxides, Reducing agents | ≥ 6 m |
| **Acids** (UN Class 8 – acidic) | Other acids | Bases, Cyanides, Sulfides, Water-reactives | Separate acid cabinet |
| **Bases** (UN Class 8 – basic) | Other bases | Acids, Water-reactives | Separate base cabinet |
| **Water-Reactive** (UN Class 4.3) | Other water-reactives (in dry area) | Aqueous solutions, Hydrated salts | Dry storage, sealed containers |
| **Toxics** (UN Class 6) | Other toxics (in ventilated cabinet) | Food, Living quarters, Acids (vapour reaction) | ≥ 15 m, exhausted enclosure |
| **Compressed Gases** (UN Class 2) | Same gas type | Incompatible gases (e.g., O₂ with H₂, Cl₂ with NH₃) | Chain restraints, ≥ 6 m apart |
| **Explosives** (UN Class 1) | Only same division | All other classes | Magazine, licensed storage |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner Trap:** Students often confuse "incompatible" with "different." Two different flammables ARE compatible (e.g., gasoline and diesel can share a cabinet). Two different acids may NOT be compatible (e.g., nitric acid + acetic acid = explosive mixture).

---

### 3.5 Step-by-Step: Toppling Stability of a Stored Cylinder

**Given:** A compressed gas cylinder of height $h = 1.5$ m and base diameter $d = 0.23$ m is stored upright. Determine if the cylinder topples when struck by a lateral force equal to **10% of its weight**.

**Solution:**

**Step 1:** Calculate the base half-width:

$$\frac{T_{width}}{2} = \frac{0.23}{2} = 0.115 \text{ m}$$

**Step 2:** The centre of gravity of a uniform cylinder is at half its height:

$$h_{cg} = \frac{h}{2} = \frac{1.5}{2} = 0.75 \text{ m}$$

**Step 3:** Compute the stability ratio:

$$SR = \frac{T_{width}/2}{h_{cg}} = \frac{0.115}{0.75} = 0.153$$

**Step 4:** Since $SR \ll 1.0$, the cylinder is **highly unstable** in the lateral direction. Any lateral force will topple it.

**Step 5:** Practical safety measures (all KTU-relevant):
- **Chain the cylinder** to a wall or rack at $1/3$ and $2/3$ of its height.
- Use a **cylinder cart** with a stabilizer base.
- Install **anti-tip cages** in storage racks.
- Always store with the **valve-protection cap** in place.

## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A – Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question Paper]
**Q: Define a dolly. List any four types of dollies used in industrial material handling with one specific application of each.**

**Model Answer:**

A **dolly** is a low, wheeled platform used to transport heavy or bulky loads over short horizontal distances by manual push/pull. The four types are:

| Type | Application |
|------|-------------|
| **Flat Dolly** | Moving crates in warehouses |
| **Platform Trolley** | Bulk material transport in factories |
| **Pallet Dolly** | Carrying loaded standard pallets |
| **Hand Truck (Two-Wheel)** | Moving LPG cylinders, sack goods |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Marks Distribution (3 M):** Definition (1 M) + Four types with applications (2 M — 0.5 M each).

---

#### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]
**Q: Differentiate between a single-wheel and a two-wheel wheelbarrow in terms of stability, maneuverability, and operator effort.**

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | Single-Wheel Wheelbarrow | Two-Wheel Wheelbarrow |
|-----------|--------------------------|------------------------|
| **Stability** | Lower (narrow base) | Higher (wider base) |
| **Maneuverability** | Excellent (tight turns) | Moderate (wider turn radius) |
| **Operator Effort** | Higher (full balance required) | Lower (weight shared with second wheel) |
| **Typical Use** | Construction, uneven terrain | Smooth factory floors, bulk loading |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Marks Distribution (3 M):** Table format (2 M) + One example distinguishing (1 M).
