### 5.2 PART B – Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

#### Question A (Choice 1) [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model]

**(a)** [7 Marks] With a neat sketch, explain the working principle of a wheelbarrow as a Class I lever. Derive the expression for the effort required to lift a load of 500 N, given that the load center is **0.6 m** from the wheel axle and the handles are **1.2 m** from the wheel axle. What is the mechanical advantage?

**(b)** [7 Marks] A standard wooden pallet of dimensions **1.2 m × 1.0 m** and mass **30 kg** carries a uniform load of **800 kg**. Calculate the pressure exerted on the floor by the bottom pallet when **5** such loaded pallets are stacked. If the wooden pallet has a safe crush strength of **80 kPa**, comment on the safety of the stacking.

---

**Solution to (a):**

**Step 1:** A wheelbarrow functions as a **Class I (First-Order) Lever** where the **wheel axle** acts as the **fulcrum**, the **load $L$** acts on one side, and the **effort $E$** is applied on the opposite side at the handles.

**Step 2:** Free-Body Diagram (FBD) — The wheel axle is the pivot. Vertical forces: $L$ downward (load), $E$ downward? No — $E$ is the operator's **upward** lifting force, and the weight of the handles $H$ acts downward. The ground reaction $R$ acts upward at the wheel.

**Step 3:** Apply static equilibrium. **Sum of moments about the wheel axle = 0:**

$$E \times d_e = L \times d_l$$

**Step 4:** Derive effort:

$$E = L \times \frac{d_l}{d_e}$$

**Step 5:** Substitute values:

$$E = 500 \times \frac{0.6}{1.2} = 500 \times 0.5 = 250 \text{ N}$$

**Step 6:** Mechanical Advantage:

$$MA = \frac{d_e}{d_l} = \frac{1.2}{0.6} = 2.0$$

**Conclusion:** The wheelbarrow halves the lifting effort (250 N instead of 500 N), at the cost of the operator's hands moving through twice the distance the load rises.

**Sketch (text representation):**

```
        [LOAD L = 500 N]
              |
              |  dL = 0.6 m
              |
        ======WHEEL AXLE (FULCRUM)======
              |
              |  dE = 1.2 m
              |
        [OPERATOR EFFORT E = 250 N upward]
```

---

**Solution to (b):**

**Step 1:** Given:
- Pallet mass $m_p = 30$ kg
- Load mass $m_L = 800$ kg
- Number of stacked pallets $n = 5$
- Base area $A = 1.2 \times 1.0 = 1.2$ m²
- $g = 9.81$ m/s²

**Step 2:** Total mass on the bottom pallet:

$$m_{total} = n \times (m_p + m_L) = 5 \times (30 + 800) = 5 \times 830 = 4150 \text{ kg}$$

**Step 3:** Total weight:

$$W = m_{total} \times g = 4150 \times 9.81 = 40{,}711.5 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4:** Pressure on the bottom pallet:

$$P = \frac{W}{A} = \frac{40{,}711.5}{1.2} = 33{,}926.25 \text{ Pa} \approx 33.93 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 5:** Compare with safe crush strength:

$$P_{safe} = 80 \text{ kPa} \quad \text{vs.} \quad P_{actual} = 33.93 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 6:** Safety Margin:

$$SM = \frac{P_{safe} - P_{actual}}{P_{safe}} \times 100 = \frac{80 - 33.93}{80} \times 100 = 57.6\%$$

**Conclusion:** The stacking is **SAFE** under static loading with a 57.6% safety margin. However, dynamic forklift impacts can impose **2× shock factors**, so the maximum safe stack height in a real warehouse is typically **4 pallets** for this loading.

---

#### Question B (Choice 2) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Adapted]

**(a)** [7 Marks] Explain the **NFPA 704 Hazard Identification System** (the "fire diamond") with a labeled diagram. Discuss its application in the storage of hazardous materials.

**(b)** [7 Marks] A drum of sulfuric acid (Class 8 corrosive) is stored alongside a drum of sodium hydroxide (Class 8 corrosive) in a chemical warehouse. Discuss the safety implications and outline the **3 corrective actions** that must be taken to bring the storage into compliance with **OSHA 1910.106** and **NFPA 30** standards.

---

**Solution to (a):**

**Step 1:** The **NFPA 704** system is a standardized placard that identifies the **health, flammability, reactivity, and special hazards** of a material using a **four-quadrant diamond** with ratings from **0 (no hazard) to 4 (severe hazard)**.

**Step 2:** Quadrant Descriptions:

| Quadrant | Color | Meaning | Rating Scale |
|----------|-------|---------|---------------|
| **Left** | Blue | Health Hazard | 0 = No hazard; 4 = Deadly |
| **Top** | Red | Flammability | 0 = Will not burn; 4 = Burns violently |
| **Right** | Yellow | Reactivity (Instability) | 0 = Stable; 4 = May detonate |
| **Bottom** | White | Special Hazard | W (water reactive), OX (oxidizer), COR (corrosive), radioactive symbol |

**Step 3:** Diagram Representation:

```
              FLAMMABILITY (RED)
                      0 - 4
                       |
        HEALTH (BLUE)  |  REACTIVITY (YELLOW)
              0 - 4    |     0 - 4
            \          |          /
             \         |         /
              \________|________/
                       |
              SPECIAL HAZARD (WHITE)
                   W / OX / COR
```

**Step 4:** Application in Hazardous Material Storage:
- **Quick visual identification** of risks for first responders.
- **Segregation planning**: materials rated 3 or 4 in flammability must be in fire-rated cabinets.
- **Inventory auditing**: warehouse placards must be re-validated every 5 years.
- **Employee training**: workers must be able to interpret the diamond within 30 seconds of entering a storage zone (per OSHA 29 CFR 1910.1200 — **Hazard Communication Standard**).

---

**Solution to (b):**

**Step 1 – Identify the Hazard:**
Sulfuric acid ($H_2SO_4$) is a strong **acidic** corrosive (NFPA Health = 3, Reactivity = 2, White = COR). Sodium hydroxide (NaOH) is a strong **basic** corrosive (NFPA Health = 3, Reactivity = 1, White = COR). Co-storage of an acid and a base in the same area is a **severe incompatibility** because leakage of one into the other produces a **violent exothermic neutralization reaction**:

$$H_2SO_4 + 2 NaOH \rightarrow Na_2SO_4 + 2 H_2O + \text{Heat (}\Delta H \approx -114 \text{ kJ/mol)}$$

**Step 2 – Safety Implications:**
- **Heat release** can ignite nearby flammable materials.
- **Splash hazard** from boiling solution.
- **Toxic vapour** generation (especially if concentrations are high).
- **Container breach** due to thermal shock.

**Step 3 – Corrective Actions (3 Actions × ~2.3 M each):**

| # | Corrective Action | Standard Reference |
|---|-------------------|---------------------|
| **1** | **Physically segregate** the acid and base into **separate storage cabinets** — acid cabinet (lined with acid-resistant polyethylene) and base cabinet (separate, dry area). Maintain a minimum **separation distance of 6 m** if a fire-rated wall is not present. | NFPA 30, OSHA 1910.106 |
| **2** | **Install secondary containment** (bunding) — each cabinet must have a **spill containment sump** with capacity ≥ **110% of the largest container's volume**. Provide **eyewash and safety shower** within **10 m** and **unobstructed access**. | ANSI Z358.1, OSHA 1910.151 |
| **3** | **Affix proper NFPA 704 placards** on each cabinet door, conduct **employee training** under HAZWOPER / HCS, and maintain a **Safety Data Sheet (SDS)** binder for both chemicals at the storage location. Establish a **monthly inspection log**. | OSHA 29 CFR 1910.1200, NFPA 704 |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning – Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Do NOT confuse NFPA 704 with the UN Hazard Classes** — NFPA 704 is a 0–4 rating for *response planning*; UN classes (1–9) are for *transport classification*. Mentioning both correctly earns full marks.
> 2. **Do NOT write "acid and base can be stored together if the cabinet is locked."** Segregation is by *chemical class*, not by access control.
> 3. **Always show units** in numerical answers (N, m, kg, kPa) — KTU examiners routinely deduct 0.5–1 mark for missing units.
> 4. **Always re-state the final answer** with a one-line conclusion (e.g., "Therefore, the stacking is SAFE with a 57.6% safety margin") — failing to conclude is a frequent 1-mark loss.

---

### 5.3 TOPIC RECAP & IMPORTANT THINGS TO REMEMBER

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid-Revision Checklist — KTU Module 1: Dollies, Wheelbarrows, Storage & Hazardous Materials**

#### A. Dollies
- ☐ Dolly = low wheeled platform; converts **sliding friction** to **rolling friction**.
- ☐ **Force reduction** is **45× to 600×** compared to sliding.
- ☐ Types: Flat, Platform, Pallet, Hand Truck (2-wheel).
- ☐ Caster load rating: $W_{max} = (P_{rated} \times N_{wheels}) / SF$, with $SF = 1.5$ to $2.0$.
- ☐ Always check **floor load capacity** (concrete = **4,000 to 10,000 kg/m²** in industrial buildings).

#### B. Wheelbarrows
- ☐ Class I lever: **Fulcrum = wheel axle**.
- ☐ Equation: $E = L \times (d_l / d_e)$; **MA = $d_e / d_l$**.
- ☐ Single-wheel = high maneuverability, low stability.
- ☐ Two-wheel = high stability, low effort, low maneuverability.
- ☐ Common injury: **lower-back strain** — use proper lifting posture.

#### C. Storage of Specific Materials
- ☐ Classify by **state → hazard class → compatibility group**.
- ☐ Stack heights governed by **pallet crush strength** and **floor load capacity**.
- ☐ Cylinders: chain at $1/3$ and $2/3$ height; cap valve during storage.
- ☐ Ventilation: **≥ 6 air changes/hour** for flammable storage (NFPA 30).
- ☐ Temperature control: many chemicals degrade above **25 °C** (TWA limits).

#### D. Hazardous Materials
- ☐ **9 UN Hazard Classes** (Explosives, Gases, Flammable Liquids, Flammable Solids, Oxidizers, Toxic/Infectious, Radioactive, Corrosives, Miscellaneous).
- ☐ **NFPA 704 Fire Diamond** — 0 to 4 rating, four quadrants (Blue/Red/Yellow/White).
- ☐ **Segregation matrix** rules: acids ≠ bases, oxidizers ≠ flammables, O₂ ≠ H₂.
- ☐ **OSHA 1910.106** governs flammable liquid storage; **OSHA 1910.1200** governs Hazard Communication (HazCom).
- ☐ **Secondary containment** (bunding) must be **110% of the largest container**.
- ☐ Always maintain **SDS (Safety Data Sheet)** for every chemical — 16-section format per GHS.

#### E. Key Equations (Memorize)
- ☐ $F_{rr} = \mu_r \cdot W$ (rolling resistance)
- ☐ $E = L \cdot (d_l / d_e)$ (wheelbarrow effort)
- ☐ $MA = d_e / d_l$ (mechanical advantage)
- ☐ $P = W / A$ (stack pressure)
- ☐ $SR = (T_{width}/2) / h_{cg}$ (stability ratio)

#### F. High-Yield KTU 2024 Buzzwords
- ☐ **Manual material handling (MMH)** · **OSHA** · **NFPA 30** · **NFPA 704** · **UN RTDG**
- ☐ **HAZMAT** · **SDS / MSDS** · **HCS 2012** · **GHS** · **ANSI Z358.1**
- ☐ **Class I / II / III levers** · **Mechanical Advantage** · **Rolling vs Sliding Friction**

---

> **END OF KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE V10 NOTES — MODULE 1**
> **Prepared per KTU 2024 Scheme B.Tech Outcome-Based Education (OBE) Framework**

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Liquids
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT522 - SAFETY IN MATERIAL HANDLING > - **Module:** Module 1 > - **Topic:** Liquids # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 What Are Liquids in the Context of Material Handling Safety? In the engineering domain of **Safety in Material Handling**, the term *Liquids* does not merely refer to the standard physics definition. It specifically refers to the **safe storage, transfer, containment, and transportation of bulk liquid substances** (flammable, combustible, corrosive, toxic, or pressurized liquids) across industrial plants, refineries, chemical processing units, and warehouses. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Formal KTU Definition:** > *Liquids, in industrial safety, encompass all fluid substances in the liquid phase that require engineered containment systems, controlled transfer mechanisms, hazard classification (as per OSHA / NFPA / IS standards), and risk mitigation strategies to prevent spills, fires, explosions, toxic exposure, and mechanical failures during handling operations.* ### 1.2 Fundamental Physical Properties of Liquids (Safety-Relevant) | Property | Symbol | Unit | Safety Relevance | |---|---|---|---| | **Density** | $\rho$ | kg/m³ | Determines hydrostatic pressure, tank load | | **Specific Gravity** | SG | dimensionless | Compares to water; dictates storage design | | **Viscosity (dynamic)** | $\mu$ | Pa·s or cP | Affects pumping, transfer rate, friction loss | | **Viscosity (kinematic)** | $\nu$ | m²/s | Used in Reynolds number calculation | | **Vapor Pressure** | $P_v$ | Pa or kPa | Governs volatility, fire risk | | **Flash Point** | $T_f$ | °C | Lowest temperature at which vapor ignites | | **Auto-Ignition Temp** | $T_{ai}$ | °C | Temperature at which liquid self-ignites | | **Boiling Point** | $T_b$ | °C | Determines phase-change hazards | | **Surface Tension** | $\sigma$ | N/m | Affects foaming, sealing, droplet formation | | **Bulk Modulus** | $K$ | Pa | Determines hydraulic shock severity | > [!NOTE] > **Standard Reference Conditions (KTU Convention):** > Density and specific gravity are typically reported at **4 °C for water (ρ = 1000 kg/m³)** and at **15.6 °C (60 °F)** for petroleum products as per **API / IS standards**. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Bucket of Water" Mental Model Imagine you are carrying a **bucket full of water** down a staircase. The water: - Has **weight** that pushes down on the bottom of the bucket (hydrostatic pressure). - **Sloshes** when you move suddenly (kinetic energy, surge loads). - Can **spill** if the bucket tilts (containment failure). - If the water is **hot oil**, it can catch fire the moment it touches a spark. - If the water is **acid**, it can burn through the bucket (corrosion). > This simple bucket captures *every major hazard category* of liquid material handling: **gravitational loading, surge, spillage, fire, and corrosion**. ### 1.4 Classification of Liquids in Industrial Safety Liquids are classified based on hazard type and handling protocol: 1. **Flammable Liquids** — Flash point < 37.8 °C (100 °F). Example: petrol, acetone, benzene. 2. **Combustible Liquids** — Flash point ≥ 37.8 °C. Example: diesel, kerosene, lubricating oil. 3. **Corrosive Liquids** — Acids (H₂SO₄, HCl) and alkalis (NaOH, KOH). 4. **Toxic Liquids** — Pesticides, cyanides, chlorinated compounds. 5. **Cryogenic Liquids** — LNG, LOX, LN₂ (below –150 °C). 6. **High-Pressure / Hydraulic Liquids** — Skydrol, phosphate esters. 7. **Water-Miscible Liquids** — Alcohols, glycols. 8. **Viscous Liquids** — Heavy fuel oils, asphalts, tars. > [!IMPORTANT] > **NFPA Hazard Diamond (NFPA 704) Classification:** > Health (Blue, 0–4) | Flammability (Red, 0–4) | Reactivity (Yellow, 0–4) | Special (White) > Every liquid stored on a KTU-relevant industrial site must display this label. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Hydrostatic Pressure — The Foundation of Liquid Containment When a liquid is stored in a tank, every square meter of the wall and floor is subjected to pressure exerted by the **weight of the liquid column above it**. ### 2.1.1 Step-by-Step Logic Behind Hydrostatic Pressure 1. **Take an imaginary horizontal disk** of area $A$ at depth $h$ from the liquid surface. 2. **Weight of liquid column** above the disk: $$W = \rho \cdot g \cdot A \cdot h$$ 3. **Pressure** is force per unit area: $$P = \frac{W}{A} = \rho g h$$ 4. **Add atmospheric pressure** $P_{atm}$ for an open tank (gauge + atmospheric = absolute): $$P_{abs} = P_{atm} + \rho g h$$ ### 2.1.2 Why This Matters in Safety - The **bottom** of a 10 m tall fuel oil tank experiences $\rho g h$ ≈ **88 kPa** (for oil SG ≈ 0.9) — this is the *minimum* structural design pressure. - **Wall thickness** of cylindrical tanks is derived from this pressure using **thin-cylinder hoop-stress theory**. - **Bund wall height** (secondary containment) is designed as 110 % of the largest tank's height. ## 2.2 Pascal's Law & Hydraulic Power Transmission > *"A change in pressure applied to an enclosed incompressible fluid is transmitted undiminished to every portion of the fluid and to the walls of the container."* — Blaise Pascal, 1653 ### 2.2.1 Engineering Significance - Basis of all **hydraulic presses, lifts, jacks, and braking systems**. - Force amplification: $$F_2 = F_1 \cdot \frac{A_2}{A_1}$$ - **Safety implication:** Hydraulic systems store enormous energy. A pinhole leak in a 20 MPa hydraulic line can pierce human skin — this is called a **hydraulic injection injury**. ## 2.3 Bernoulli's Equation (Energy Conservation for Flowing Liquids) For an ideal, steady, incompressible, frictionless flow: $$P_1 + \frac{1}{2}\rho v_1^2 + \rho g h_1 = P_2 + \frac{1}{2}\rho v_2^2 + \rho g h_2$$ ### 2.3.1 Safety Application Points - When a valve closes rapidly, kinetic energy converts to **pressure surge** → **water hammer**. - Water hammer pressure rise: $$\Delta P = \rho \cdot a \cdot \Delta v$$ where $a$ = pressure wave speed (≈ 1200 m/s in water). > [!WARNING] > **Water hammer is a leading cause of pipeline rupture in liquid transfer systems.** Mitigation: slow-closing valves, surge tanks, air chambers, pressure-relief valves. ## 2.4 Reynolds Number — Flow Regime Identification $$Re = \frac{\rho v D}{\mu} = \frac{v D}{\nu}$$ | Regime | Re Range | Safety Implication | |---|---|---| | **Laminar** | Re < 2300 | Predictable, low erosion | | **Transitional** | 2300 ≤ Re ≤ 4000 | Design uncertainty — **avoid** | | **Turbulent** | Re > 4000 | Higher friction, mixing, erosion of pipe bends | ## 2.5 Hagen–Poiseuille Equation (Laminar Flow Pressure Drop) $$\Delta P = \frac{32 \mu L v}{D^2} = \frac{128 \mu L Q}{\pi D^4}$$ Used to size **pumps** and select **flow rates** that avoid dangerous pressure drops or pipe collapse. ## 2.6 Darcy–Weisbach Equation (General Pipe Friction Loss) $$h_f = f \cdot \frac{L}{D} \cdot \frac{v^2}{2g}$$ where $f$ = Darcy friction factor (from Moody chart or Colebrook equation). ## 2.7 Vapor Pressure, Flash Point & Fire Hazard Triangle > [!IMPORTANT] > **The Fire Triangle (adapted for liquids):** > 1. **Fuel** (liquid vapor) > 2. **Oxidizer** (air/O₂) > 3. **Ignition Source** (spark, hot surface) > A liquid at temperature *above* its flash point releases enough vapor to ignite. **Antoine Equation (Vapor Pressure vs Temperature):** $$\log_{10} P_v = A - \frac{B}{C + T}$$ Used in safety relief-valve sizing and venting calculations for storage tanks. ## 2.8 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **High-Yield Table — Memorize for KTU ESE** | # | Formula | Application / Safety Use | |---|---|---| | 1 | $P = \rho g h$ | Hydrostatic pressure, tank wall design | | 2 | $F = P \cdot A$ | Thrust on tank roof/floor | | 3 | $F_2 = F_1 \cdot \dfrac{A_2}{A_1}$ | Hydraulic force amplification | | 4 | $P_1 + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho v_1^2 + \rho g h_1 = P_2 + \tfrac{1}{2}\rho v_2^2 + \rho g h_2$ | Bernoulli — pipeline design | | 5 | $\Delta P_{hammer} = \rho a \Delta v$ | Water-hammer surge pressure | | 6 | $Re = \dfrac{\rho v D}{\mu}$ | Flow regime identification | | 7 | $h_f = f \dfrac{L}{D} \dfrac{v^2}{2g}$ | Pipe friction loss | | 8 | $Q = A v$ | Volumetric flow rate | | 9 | $\dot{m} = \rho Q$ | Mass flow rate (kg/s) | | 10 | $V_{tank} = A \cdot h$ | Tank volume / capacity | | 11 | $V_{relief} = \dfrac{Q}{L \cdot \sqrt{\dfrac{M \cdot T}{Z}}}$, API Std 521 | Emergency venting for fire exposure | | 12 | $SG = \dfrac{\rho_{liquid}}{\rho_{water}}$ | Specific gravity — classification | | 13 | $W_{bund} \geq 1.1 \cdot V_{tank}$ | Bund wall capacity (110 % rule) | | 14 | $\log_{10} P_v = A - \dfrac{B}{C+T}$ | Vapor pressure (Antoine) | | 15 | $Q = C_d A \sqrt{2 g h}$ | Discharge through orifice (leak rate) | **Where typical units are:** - $P$ in Pa or kPa, $\rho$ in kg/m³, $g$ = **9.81 m/s²** - $v$ in m/s, $D, L, h$ in m, $Q$ in m³/s - $\mu$ in Pa·s, $T$ in Kelvin (for gas equations) ## 2.9 Engineering Real-World Utility - **Petroleum refineries** use hydrostatic + vapor pressure formulas to size **fixed roof** and **floating roof** tanks. - **Chemical plants** use Pascal's Law to design **hydraulic accumulators** that store emergency shut-down energy. - **Fire protection engineers** use Antoine + flash point data to size **pressure-relief valves (PSVs/PRVs)** on storage tanks exposed to pool fire. - **Pipeline engineers** use water-hammer analysis to design **surge protection** systems. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Hydrostatic Pressure $P = \rho g h$ ### Starting Premise Consider a static liquid of constant density $\rho$ in an open tank. We want to find the pressure at depth $h$ below the free surface. ### Step 1: Draw a Free-Body Diagram of a Liquid Column Take a vertical column of liquid of cross-sectional area $A$ and height $h$, sitting directly above a point P at the bottom. ### Step 2: Identify All Forces Acting on the Column The forces acting on this column are: - **Weight of the liquid column** (downward): $W = m \cdot g = (\rho \cdot A \cdot h) \cdot g$ - **Atmospheric pressure force** at top (downward): $F_{atm} = P_{atm} \cdot A$ - **Reaction pressure force** at the bottom (upward): $F_P = P \cdot A$ ### Step 3: Apply Static Equilibrium Since the liquid is at rest, net force on the column = 0: $$F_P = F_{atm} + W$$ $$P \cdot A = P_{atm} \cdot A + \rho A h g$$ ### Step 4: Cancel the Common Area $A$ $$P = P_{atm} + \rho g h$$ ### Step 5: Define Gauge Pressure By convention, gauge pressure is measured above atmospheric: $$P_{gauge} = P - P_{atm} = \rho g h$$ This is the **classical hydrostatic pressure equation** used universally in tank design and safety calculations. --- ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Force on the Vertical Wall of a Rectangular Tank ### Starting Premise We have a vertical rectangular wall of width $B$ and liquid height $H$ on one side. We want the resultant force and its line of action (center of pressure). ### Step 1: Pressure Variation with Depth At depth $y$ (measured from the surface): $$P(y) = \rho g y$$ ### Step 2: Differential Force on a Strip Consider a horizontal strip at depth $y$ of width $B$ and thickness $dy$. Its area is $dA = B \, dy$, and the differential force is: $$dF = P(y) \cdot dA = \rho g y \cdot B \, dy$$ ### Step 3: Integrate Over the Full Height $$F = \int_{0}^{H} \rho g B y \, dy = \rho g B \left[ \frac{y^2}{2} \right]_{0}^{H} = \frac{1}{2} \rho g B H^2$$ ### Step 4: Locate the Center of Pressure Take moments about the free surface: $$F \cdot \bar{y} = \int_{0}^{H} y \cdot dF = \int_{0}^{H} \rho g B y^2 \, dy = \rho g B \cdot \frac{H^3}{3}$$ $$\bar{y} = \frac{\rho g B H^3 / 3}{\rho g B H^2 / 2} = \frac{2H}{3}$$ ### Step 5: Interpret the Result - **Resultant force** on the wall: $F = \frac{1}{2} \rho g B H^2$ — acts at $2H/3$ from the surface (i.e., $H/3$ from the bottom). - This force determines the **anchor bolt spacing** and **wall thickness** required for safe tank construction. --- ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Water Hammer Pressure Surge (Joukowsky Equation) ### Starting Premise A liquid flowing in a long pipe of cross-section $A$ at velocity $v$ is suddenly stopped by a valve closure. Find the resulting pressure rise. ### Step 1: Momentum Conservation Apply the impulse-momentum theorem to the column of liquid that is brought to rest: $$\Delta P \cdot A \cdot \Delta t = \Delta m \cdot \Delta v = (\rho A \cdot a \Delta t) \cdot v$$ where $a$ = pressure wave speed in the liquid. ### Step 2: Simplify Cancel $A \cdot \Delta t$ on both sides: $$\Delta P = \rho \cdot a \cdot v$$ ### Step 3: Pressure Wave Speed For an elastic liquid in an elastic pipe: $$a = \sqrt{\frac{K / \rho}{1 + \frac{K \cdot D}{E \cdot t}}}$$ where $K$ = bulk modulus of liquid, $E$ = Young's modulus of pipe, $D$ = pipe diameter, $t$ = pipe wall thickness. ### Step 4: Example Numerical Evaluation For water ($K = 2.2 \times 10^9$ Pa, $\rho = 1000$ kg/m³) in a steel pipe ($E = 200 \times 10^9$ Pa, $D/t$ = 100), the wave speed $a$ ≈ 1200 m/s. If $v = 2$ m/s (typical transfer line), then: $$\Delta P = 1000 \times 1200 \times 2 = 2.4 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} = 2.4 \text{ MPa}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > This pressure surge — over **24 bar** in a routine 2 m/s flow — can rupture any pipe not rated for surge. This is why **slow-closing valves and surge protection** are mandatory in liquid handling systems. --- ## 3.4 Derivation 4 — Bund Wall (Secondary Containment) Sizing ### Starting Premise The largest tank in a group has capacity $V_{tank}$. The bund (containment wall) must hold **110 %** of the largest tank's volume. ### Step 1: Volume of Bund $$V_{bund} \geq 1.10 \times V_{tank}$$ ### Step 2: Bund Wall Height If the bund encloses a rectangular area of length $L$ and width $W$, and is filled uniformly to height $H_b$: $$L \cdot W \cdot H_b \geq 1.10 \cdot V_{tank}$$ $$H_b \geq \frac{1.10 \cdot V_{tank}}{L \cdot W}$$ ### Step 3: Engineering Practice - Freeboard (unused height) ≥ 10 % of $H_b$. - Bund floor must slope **away** from the tank toward a **sump** for drainage. - The bund material must be **chemically compatible** with the stored liquid (especially for acids and alkalis). --- ## 3.5 Derivation 5 — Leak Rate Through a Hole (Bernoulli Application) ### Starting Premise A tank containing liquid up to height $h$ above a small hole of area $A_h$ develops a leak. Find the discharge velocity and volumetric flow rate. ### Step 1: Apply Bernoulli's Equation Between the free surface (point 1) and the hole exit (point 2): $$\frac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} + 0 + h = \frac{P_{atm}}{\rho g} + 0 + 0 + \frac{v^2}{2g}$$ (Atmospheric pressure cancels; surface velocity ≈ 0.) ### Step 2: Solve for Exit Velocity (Torricelli's Theorem) $$v = \sqrt{2 g h}$$ ### Step 3: Apply Discharge Coefficient Real holes have friction and vena-contracta effects, so: $$Q_{actual} = C_d \cdot A_h \cdot \sqrt{2 g h}$$ where $C_d$ ≈ 0.6–0.65 for sharp-edged orifices, ≈ 0.95–0.99 for well-rounded nozzles. ### Step 4: Numerical Example A 5 mm diameter hole in a tank with liquid 3 m above the leak point: $$A_h = \frac{\pi}{4}(0.005)^2 = 1.963 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^2$$ $$v = \sqrt{2 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 3} = 7.67 \text{ m/s}$$ $$Q = 0.62 \times 1.963 \times 10^{-5} \times 7.67 = 9.34 \times 10^{-5} \text{ m}^3/\text{s} = 336 \text{ L/h}$$ > This is the basis of **spill-rate estimation** in Hazard and Operability (HAZOP) studies. # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram — Liquid Material Handling Safety System (Architecture Flow) ```mermaid flowchart TD A1[Source Tank
or Storage Vessel] --> B1[Primary Isolation
Manual Valve] B1 --> C1[Transfer Pump
Centrifugal or Positive Displacement] C1 --> D1[Flow Meter
and Pressure Gauge] D1 --> E1[Secondary Isolation
Emergency Shutoff Valve] E1 --> F1[Delivery Point
or Process Unit] F1 --> G1[Vapor Return Line
for Flammable Liquids] A1 --> H1[Safety Relief Valve
PSV PRV Set at Design Pressure] H1 --> I1[Vent Stack
to Safe Location] A1 --> J1[Bund Wall
110 percent Capacity] J1 --> K1[Sump and Drain Valve
for Spill Recovery] A1 --> L1[Level Indicator
and High Level Alarm] L1 --> M1[Overflow Siphon
to Secondary Containment] D1 --> N1[Flow Control Valve
with Surge Protection] N1 --> O1[Air Chamber
or Surge Tank] A1 --> P1[Grounding and Bonding
Static Electricity Control] A1 --> Q1[Fire Protection
Foam Pourers and Water Spray] C1 --> R1[Pump Isolation Switch
and Emergency Stop] D1 --> S1[Leak Detection Sensor
Hydrocarbon or Acid Sniffer] ``` **Reading Guide:** - A1 (Storage) is the *source* of every safety decision. - The **double isolation** (B1 + E1) prevents leak during maintenance. - **H1 + I1** protect against overpressure (e.g., fire exposure, thermal expansion). - **J1 + K1** capture accidental spills before they reach the environment. - **P1 + Q1** address the two dominant liquid hazards: **static-induced fire** and **pool fire escalation**. --- ## 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram — Hydrostatic Pressure Distribution in a Storage Tank ```mermaid flowchart LR A2[Free Surface
P equals P_atm
Gauge Pressure 0 kPa] --> B2[Depth y equals 0 m
Pressure 0 kPa] B2 --> C2[Depth y equals 1 m
Pressure 9.81 kPa] C2 --> D2[Depth y equals 2 m
Pressure 19.62 kPa] D2 --> E2[Depth y equals 3 m
Pressure 29.43 kPa] E2 --> F2[Tank Bottom
Depth y equals H
Pressure rho g H] F2 --> G2[Bund Floor
Designed for Full Volume Spill Load] A2 --> H2[Wall Pressure Distribution
Linear Increase with Depth] H2 --> I2[Maximum Wall Stress
at Base, 2H over 3 from Top] I2 --> J2[Anchor Bolts and
Wall Thickness Sized Here] A2 --> K2[Roof Pressure
Only Vapor Pressure plus P_atm] K2 --> L2[Roof Designed as
Weak Seam Roof
Ruptures Safely in Fire] ``` **Engineering Interpretation:** - Pressure on the **floor** is maximum hydrostatic. - Pressure on the **wall** varies linearly; maximum at the base. - Pressure on the **roof** is just the vapor pressure — for flammable liquids, a **weak-seam roof** is deliberately designed to fail first (vent upward, not explode sideways). --- ## 4.3 Mermaid Block Diagram — Hazard Classification Workflow for a New Liquid ```mermaid flowchart TD A3[New Liquid Received
in Plant] --> B3{Obtain MSDS
and SDS} B3 --> C3[Identify Flash Point
and Auto Ignition] B3 --> D3[Identify Toxicity
LD50 and TLV] B3 --> E3[Identify Reactivity
with Water Air or Other Chemicals] B3 --> F3[Identify Corrosivity
pH and Material Compatibility] C3 --> G3{NFPA 704
Hazard Diamond} D3 --> G3 E3 --> G3 F3 --> G3 G3 --> H3{Flammable
Liquid Class} H3 --> I1[Class IA
Flash below 22.8 C
Boiling below 37.8 C] H3 --> I2[Class IB
Flash below 22.8 C
Boiling at or above 37.8 C] H3 --> I3[Class IC
Flash 22.8 to 37.8 C] H3 --> I4[Class II
Flash 37.8 to 60 C] H3 --> I5[Class IIIA
Flash 60 to 93 C] H3 --> I6[Class IIIB
Flash above 93 C] I1 --> J3[Storage in
Flameproof Enclosure] I2 --> J3 I3 --> J3 I4 --> K3[Storage in
Ventilated Non Spark Area] I5 --> K3 I6 --> L3[Standard Storage
with Bunding] J3 --> M3[Apply Explosion Proof
Electrical Fittings] K3 --> M3 L3 --> M3 ``` **Reading Guide:** This diagram shows how a safety engineer moves from **liquid identification → MSDS review → NFPA classification → storage and electrical decisions** — a recurring question type in KTU exams. # SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Q1.** Define **flash point** and **fire point** of a liquid. Why is flash point the most important property for classifying flammable liquids? `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP, Module 1]` **Model Answer (3 Marks):** 1. **Flash Point** *(1 mark)*: The lowest temperature at which a liquid gives off enough vapor to form an ignitable mixture with air **near its surface**. At this temperature, the vapor ignites momentarily (a "flash") when an ignition source is applied, but the liquid does not continue to burn. 2. **Fire Point** *(1 mark)*: The temperature at which the vapor burns **sustainably** (≥ 5 seconds) after ignition. It is usually a few degrees **above** the flash point. 3. **Why flash point matters for classification** *(1 mark)*: NFPA, OSHA, and Indian Standards (IS 1446) all classify flammable liquids **based on flash point** because it directly indicates the **temperature range over which the liquid can produce a fire hazard** under normal storage and handling conditions. Lower flash point → greater hazard → stricter storage, electrical, and ventilation requirements. --- ### **Q2.** State **Pascal's Law**. Write two safety-related applications of this law in material handling systems. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP, Module 1]` **Model Answer (3 Marks):** 1. **Pascal's Law (1 mark):** *A pressure change applied to an enclosed, incompressible fluid is transmitted undiminished and equally in all directions throughout the fluid and to the walls of the container.* 2. **Application 1 (1 mark):** **Hydraulic jacks and lifts** used in heavy-load handling — a small force on a small piston produces a large force on a larger piston, enabling safe lifting of heavy machinery. 3. **Application 2 (1 mark):** **Hydraulic braking systems** in material handling vehicles (forklifts, cranes) — pressure applied by a pedal is transmitted through brake fluid to all wheel cylinders, ensuring uniform braking force and safe stopping. --- ## 📕 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks, Internal Choice) > **KTU ESE Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, split as **(a) 7 marks** and **(b) 7 marks**. You must answer **either** Question A **or** Question B. --- ### **QUESTION A (14 Marks)** `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Modified Past Paper Pattern]` **(a)** A cylindrical storage tank, 8 m in diameter and 10 m high, is filled with a fuel oil of specific gravity 0.85. Determine:  (i) The hydrostatic pressure at the bottom of the tank.  (ii) The total force exerted on the tank bottom.  (iii) The force on the cylindrical wall and the location of the center of pressure. *(7 marks)* **(b)** Explain the **fire triangle** as it applies to liquids. With neat sketches, describe **bund wall** design criteria for a group of three flammable-liquid storage tanks. *(7 marks)* --- ### **MODEL SOLUTION — Question A** #### **Part (a) — Numerical Solution** **Given:** - Diameter $D = 8$ m → Radius $R = 4$ m - Height $H = 10$ m - Specific gravity $SG = 0.85$ → $\rho = 0.85 \times 1000 = 850$ kg/m³ - $g = 9.81$ m/s² **(i) Hydrostatic pressure at the bottom (2 marks):** $$P = \rho \cdot g \cdot H = 850 \times 9.81 \times 10$$ $$P = 83{,}385 \text{ Pa} = 83.385 \text{ kPa}$$ (In gauge pressure; for absolute, add $P_{atm} \approx 101.325$ kPa → $P_{abs} = 184.71$ kPa.) **(ii) Total force on the tank bottom (2 marks):** Bottom area: $$A = \frac{\pi}{4} D^2 = \frac{\pi}{4} (8)^2 = 50.27 \text{ m}^2$$ Force on bottom: $$F_{bottom} = P \cdot A = 83{,}385 \times 50.27$$ $$F_{bottom} = 4{,}191{,}408 \text{ N} \approx 4.19 \text{ MN} \approx 427 \text{ tonnes-force}$$ **(iii) Force on the cylindrical wall and center of pressure (3 marks):** The wall is **vertical and rectangular** when "unrolled" — width $B = \pi D = 8\pi = 25.13$ m, height $H = 10$ m. $$F_{wall} = \frac{1}{2} \rho g B H^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times 850 \times 9.81 \times 25.13 \times (10)^2$$ $$F_{wall} = \frac{1}{2} \times 850 \times 9.81 \times 25.13 \times 100 = 10{,}481{,}569 \text{ N} \approx 10.48 \text{ MN}$$ Center of pressure (from free surface): $$\bar{y} = \frac{2H}{3} = \frac{2 \times 10}{3} = 6.67 \text{ m from the surface}$$ Equivalently, $\bar{y}$ is at $H/3 = 3.33$ m **above the base**. --- #### **Part (b) — Theory Solution** **Fire Triangle (3 marks):** The fire triangle for liquids comprises three elements, all of which must be present **simultaneously** for a fire to occur: 1. **Fuel** — the **vapor** of the flammable liquid (not the liquid itself, since liquids do not burn; only their vapors do). 2. **Oxidizer** — typically the **oxygen in air** (≈ 21 % by volume). 3. **Ignition source** — spark, hot surface, static discharge, open flame, or self-heating. > **Safety strategy:** Eliminate **any one** side of the triangle to prevent fire. In liquid handling, this translates to (a) vapor control via **floating roof tanks** or **vapor return lines**, (b) **inert gas blanketing** (N₂) to reduce oxygen, and (c) **bonding + grounding** to prevent static ignition. **Bund Wall Design Criteria (4 marks):** A **bund** (or dike) is a secondary containment structure surrounding a group of storage tanks. Design criteria (per IS 1641 / NFPA 30): 1. **Capacity** — Bund volume ≥ **110 %** of the **largest single tank** in the group (1 mark). 2. **Wall height** — Limited to **1.8 m** average for easy access and escape (1 mark). 3. **Distance from tank** — At least **1 m** between tank shell and bund wall (or as per local code) (1 mark). 4. **Construction** — Chemically compatible (lined for acids), **impermeable** (concrete + epoxy coating), with **sump** at the low point for drainage and spill recovery (0.5 mark). 5. **Multiple tanks** — Where multiple tanks share a bund, intermediate walls separate them to prevent cascading failure (0.5 mark). > A simple **plan-view sketch** of three tanks inside a rectangular bund with a corner sump should be drawn for full credit. (Skipped here for brevity — draw on exam paper.) --- ### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice** `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Modified Past Paper Pattern]` **(a)** With the help of a labeled diagram, explain the **working of an emergency pressure-relief valve (PSV)** on a flammable-liquid storage tank. List any **four** design considerations for sizing such a valve. *(7 marks)* **(b)** A pipeline carrying oil (SG = 0.88, bulk modulus $K = 1.5$ GPa) is suddenly blocked by an emergency valve closure. The flow velocity was 1.8 m/s and the pipe is steel ($E = 200$ GPa) with diameter-to-thickness ratio $D/t = 80$. Calculate the **water-hammer pressure rise**. Comment on the safety of a pipe rated at 10 bar. *(7 marks)* --- ### **MODEL SOLUTION — Question B** #### **Part (a) — Pressure Relief Valve Working & Design** **Working Principle (3 marks):** A **Pressure Safety Valve (PSV)** is a self-actuating device that opens when the inlet pressure exceeds a predetermined **set pressure**, discharging vapor or liquid to a safe location (vent stack or flare). The components are: 1. **Inlet nozzle** — connected to the tank/vessel vapor space. 2. **Disc** — pressed against the nozzle by a spring. 3. **Spring** — adjustable; determines the set pressure. 4. **Lever / lifting gear** — for manual testing. 5. **Outlet / vent stack** — discharges to atmosphere at a safe elevation. When internal pressure $P_{internal} > P_{set}$, the upward hydraulic force on the disc overcomes the spring force, and the valve opens. When pressure drops below the reseat pressure (typically 90–95 % of set), the spring re-closes the valve. **Four Design Considerations (4 marks):** 1. **Set pressure** — must be ≤ design pressure of the vessel (per ASME Section VIII, typically 90 % of MAWP for single PSV). 2. **Required relief capacity** — sized using **API Std 521** for fire exposure; account for wetted area, latent heat, and environmental factors. 3. **Material compatibility** — disc, spring, and body must resist corrosion by the stored liquid/vapor. 4. **Orifice size** — selected to handle the **worst-case relieving scenario** (external fire + blocked outlet). 5. **Backpressure considerations** — for balanced-bellows PSVs in high-backpressure service. 6. **Testing & certification** — PSV must be tested and certified annually per statutory requirements. *(Any four of the above accepted.)* #### **Part (b) — Water Hammer Numerical** **Given:** - $\rho = 0.88 \times 1000 = 880$ kg/m³ - $K = 1.5 \times 10^9$ Pa - $E = 200 \times 10^9$ Pa - $D/t = 80$ - $v = 1.8$ m/s **Step 1: Compute pressure wave speed $a$ (3 marks):** $$a = \sqrt{\frac{K / \rho}{1 + \dfrac{K \cdot D}{E \cdot t}}} = \sqrt{\frac{1.5 \times 10^9 / 880}{1 + \dfrac{1.5 \times 10^9}{200 \times 10^9 \cdot \left(\frac{1}{80}\right)^{-1}} \cdot \frac{D}{t} \cdot \frac{1}{1}}$$ $$\frac{K \cdot D}{E \cdot t} = \frac{1.5 \times 10^9 \times 80}{200 \times 10^9} = 0.6$$ $$a = \sqrt{\frac{1.5 \times 10^9 / 880}{1 + 0.6}} = \sqrt{\frac{1.7045 \times 10^6}{1.6}} = \sqrt{1.0653 \times 10^6}$$ $$a \approx 1032 \text{ m/s}$$ **Step 2: Compute water-hammer pressure rise (2 marks):** $$\Delta P = \rho \cdot a \cdot \Delta v = 880 \times 1032 \times 1.8$$ $$\Delta P = 1{,}634{,}688 \text{ Pa} \approx 1.635 \text{ MPa} = 16.35 \text{ bar}$$ **Step 3: Safety comment (2 marks):** The pipeline is rated for **10 bar**, but the water-hammer surge produces **16.35 bar** — exceeding the rating by **63.5 %**. This is **unsafe**. The pipe will fail catastrophically, causing spillage, fire hazard, and potential loss of life. **Recommended Mitigations (any two for full credit):** - Install **slow-closing valve** (closure time > 2L/a, where L = pipe length). - Add a **surge tank** or **air chamber** near the valve. - Use a **pressure-relief valve** on the discharge side. - Increase pipe design pressure to ≥ 25 bar with appropriate safety factor. --- ## ⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout > [!WARNING] > **Common Mark-Loss Zones in Liquid Handling Questions:** > 1. **Forgetting atmospheric pressure:** In open-tank hydrostatic problems, examiners often deduct 1 mark for not specifying *gauge* vs *absolute* pressure. > 2. **Wrong center of pressure:** On vertical walls, the resultant force acts at **2H/3 from the surface** (not H/2 — that's only for a triangular load on a cantilever beam, a different problem). Writing $H/2$ costs 2 marks. > 3. **Units in Hagen–Poiseuille:** Confusing dynamic viscosity $\mu$ (Pa·s) with kinematic viscosity $\nu$ (m²/s) when computing $Re$. Always check the formula's required variable. > 4. **Fire point vs flash point:** Saying "flash point is the temperature at which the liquid catches fire" is **wrong** — that's the fire point. Examiners penalize this. > 5. **Bund wall 110 % rule:** Writing "100 % capacity" instead of "110 % of the largest tank" is a 0.5-mark error. > 6. **Water hammer formula:** Using the simple $a = \sqrt{K/\rho}$ without the **pipe elasticity correction** $(K \cdot D / E \cdot t)$ gives the wrong wave speed and an inflated $\Delta P$. Examiners expect the full Joukowsky form. --- ## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid Revision Checklist for "Liquids" — Module 1** ### 📌 A. Core Definitions (Must memorize) - [ ] **Flash point** — lowest temp at which vapor ignites momentarily. - [ ] **Fire point** — lowest temp at which vapor burns sustainably. - [ ] **Auto-ignition temperature** — temp at which liquid self-ignites without external source. - [ ] **Vapor pressure** — partial pressure of vapor above liquid at a given T. - [ ] **Specific gravity** — ratio of liquid density to water density at standard T. - [ ] **Viscosity** — resistance to flow (dynamic $\mu$, kinematic $\nu$). - [ ] **Hydrostatic pressure** — $\rho g h$ at depth $h$. - [ ] **Pascal's Law** — pressure transmitted undiminished in confined fluid. - [ ] **Bernoulli's equation** — energy conservation for flowing liquid. - [ ] **Reynolds number** — $Re = \rho v D / \mu$ for flow regime. - [ ] **Water hammer** — pressure surge from sudden valve closure. - [ ] **Bund wall** — secondary containment, 110 % of largest tank volume. - [ ] **PSV/PRV** — pressure safety/relief valve, opens at set pressure. - [ ] **MSDS/SDS** — material safety data sheet, primary hazard reference. ### 📌 B. Must-Know Numerical Formulas - [ ] $P = \rho g h$ - [ ] $F_{wall} = \tfrac{1}{2} \rho g B H^2$ at $\bar{y} = 2H/3$ from surface - [ ] $\Delta P_{hammer} = \rho a v$ with $a = \sqrt{(K/\rho) / (1 + KD/Et)}$ - [ ] $Q = C_d A \sqrt{2gh}$ (leak through orifice) - [ ] $Re = \rho v D / \mu$; laminar < 2300, turbulent > 4000 - [ ] $h_f = f (L/D)(v^2 / 2g)$ (Darcy–Weisbach) - [ ] $V_{bund} \geq 1.10 \cdot V_{tank}$ ### 📌 C. Safety Traps to Avoid - [ ] Confusing gauge vs absolute pressure. - [ ] Forgetting pipe elasticity in water-hammer calculations. - [ ] Using SG instead of actual density in $\rho g h$ formulas. - [ ] Treating a fire point and flash point as the same. - [ ] Omitting units in final numerical answers. - [ ] Designing a bund for 100 % (must be 110 %). ### 📌 D. Industrial Best Practices (Examiner's favorite theory questions) - [ ] Bonding & grounding of all flammable-liquid containers (anti-static). - [ ] Vapor return lines during tanker unloading. - [ ] Inert gas (N₂) blanketing of storage tanks. - [ ] Use of intrinsically safe (IS) electrical fittings in flammable zones. - [ ] Annual PSV testing and certification. - [ ] HAZOP study for new liquid transfer systems. - [ ] MSDS display at every storage location. - [ ] Emergency eyewash + safety shower within 10 m of corrosive liquid handling area. - [ ] Spill containment kits (absorbent pads, neutralizers) at every transfer point. > 🎯 **KTU Topper Tip:** In the ESE, always draw a **labeled free-body diagram** in hydrostatic problems, **state all given data** at the top of your solution, and **box the final numerical answer** with units. This single habit recovers 1–2 marks that most students lose on presentation.

topics.title: Solids - Storage and Handling of Cryogenic Liquids - Shipping and Receiving
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT522 - SAFETY IN MATERIAL HANDLING
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Solids - Storage and Handling of Cryogenic Liquids - Shipping and Receiving

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Storage of Solids

**Formal Definition:**
*Storage of solids* refers to the systematic containment, preservation, and protection of bulk or packaged solid materials (powders, granules, flakes, lumps) in engineered enclosures such as silos, bins, hoppers, bunkers, warehouses, and stockpiles, such that the material retains its physico-chemical properties, remains accessible for retrieval, and poses no unacceptable risk to personnel, equipment, or environment.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** Solids storage is the **bridge** between raw-material intake and process utilization. The choice of storage geometry directly governs the **flow regime** (funnel flow vs mass flow), segregation tendency, and dust-explosion risk.

**Conceptual Analogy — The "Kitchen Shelf" Model:**
Think of your kitchen. Rice goes in a sealed, narrow, vertical container so it *flows out* cleanly (mass flow). Cereal flakes go in a wide-mouthed *box* where you scoop from the top (funnel flow). The shape, surface finish, and outlet size are chosen by *how the solid wants to behave*. In industry, engineers do exactly the same — but the consequences of choosing wrongly range from **ratholing** (stagnant material harboring bacteria, hot spots, or fire) to **flooding** (uncontrolled discharge injuring workers).

### 1.2 Cryogenic Liquids

**Formal Definition:**
*Cryogenic liquids* (or *cryogens*) are liquefied gases held at temperatures below **−150 °C (123 K)**. Common industrial cryogens include:
- **LNG** — Liquefied Natural Gas (~ **−162 °C**)
- **LOX / LO₂** — Liquefied Oxygen (~ **−183 °C**)
- **LIN / LN₂** — Liquefied Nitrogen (~ **−196 °C**)
- **LAr** — Liquefied Argon (~ **−186 °C**)
- **LH₂** — Liquefied Hydrogen (~ **−253 °C**)
- **LHe** — Liquefied Helium (~ **−269 °C**)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Property — Volume Ratio:** 1 volume of liquid nitrogen expands to **~694 volumes** of gaseous nitrogen at standard conditions. This is the root cause of *asphyxiation* and *over-pressurization* hazards.

**Conceptual Analogy — The "Vacuum Flask" Idea:**
A household thermos flask keeps your coffee hot by creating a vacuum gap between the inner and outer wall (no medium to conduct heat). Cryogenic storage tanks are **industrial-scale thermoses**, but with multilayered super-insulation, perlite powder, and active pressure-relief systems. The liquid inside is *constantly* trying to boil because even trace heat leaks from ambient (293 K) to the cryogen (77 K for LN₂) is a **ΔT of ~216 K**.

### 1.3 Shipping and Receiving

**Formal Definition:**
*Shipping and Receiving* (incoming-material logistics) is the controlled end-to-end process of transporting hazardous and non-hazardous materials from a supplier's gate to a plant's point-of-use, encompassing carrier selection, documentation (Bill of Lading, MSDS/SDS, Packing List), container inspection, transfer operations, and final sign-off.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The "First Receiver" is legally responsible for verifying the consignment. Any mismatch between the **shipping paper** and the **physical load** must trigger an immediate **hold-and-isolate** procedure.

**Conceptual Analogy — The "Parcel Courier" Model:**
Imagine you order a fragile item online. The courier (a) checks the outer box for damage, (b) scans the label against the invoice, (c) opens and verifies contents, (d) signs the proof-of-delivery. Industrial shipping/receiving does the same — but with **cryogenic trailers, hazmat placards, MSDS cross-checks, and emergency eyewash stations** standing by.

---

### 1.4 Hazards Summary (Mandatory KTU Identification)

| Material Class | Primary Hazard | Secondary Hazard |
|---|---|---|
| Combustible dust solids (grain, coal, sugar) | **Dust explosion** | Secondary fires |
| Reactive solids (alkali metals, peroxides) | **Pyrophoricity** | Water reaction |
| Toxic solids (cyanides, pesticides) | **Acute poisoning** | Environmental release |
| **All cryogenic liquids** | **Cold burns / embrittlement** | **Asphyxiation** |
| LOX / LO₂ specifically | **Fire intensification** | Oxidizer-driven detonation |
| LH₂ | **Flammability (4–75 % in air)** | Hydrogen embrittlement |
| LNG | **Pool fire / vapor cloud** | Cryogenic fracture |
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Solids Storage — Design Hierarchy

Solid materials are categorized and stored by:

1. **Bulk unpackaged solids** — silos, bunkers, stockpiles (e.g., cement, grain, coal)
2. **Packaged solids** — pallets, drums, FIBCs ("jumbo bags"), supersacks in warehouses
3. **Hazardous solids** — segregated, labeled, bunded (e.g., oxidizers away from flammables, per **NFPA 30 / OSHA 1910**)

**Step-by-step storage logic:**

- **Step 1 — Characterize the solid:** particle size (d₅₀), moisture content, bulk density (ρ_b), tapped density (ρ_t), angle of repose (θ_r), Hausner ratio (HR = ρ_t / ρ_b).
- **Step 2 — Identify flow behavior:** HR < 1.2 → free-flowing; HR > 1.4 → cohesive (problematic).
- **Step 3 — Select geometry:** Mass flow requires converging hopper with **steep, smooth walls** (stainless steel, ≤ 6 μRa) and **half-angle ≤ the wall friction angle**.
- **Step 4 — Size the outlet:** Must exceed the *critical rat-hole diameter* (Jenike's criterion).
- **Step 5 — Engineer dust control:** vent filters, explosion panels (sized to **NFPA 68**), inerting.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Jenike's Critical Arching Dimension:**
> For a cohesive solid with unconfined yield strength *f_c* and major consolidating stress *σ₁*, the minimum outlet diameter for gravity discharge is **D_c ≈ f_c(1 + sin δ)/γ_b cos δ** (where δ = wall friction angle).

### 2.2 Cryogenic Liquid Storage — Engineering Architecture

A modern cryogenic storage tank (e.g., for LN₂) consists of:

1. **Inner vessel** — austenitic stainless steel (304L/304LN) to withstand cryogenic ductile-to-brittle transition.
2. **Outer vessel** — carbon steel, designed for external loads (wind, seismic, transport).
3. **Annular space** — under high vacuum (< 10⁻³ Pa), filled with **perlite powder** (a multi-layer super-insulation, MLI, wraps the inner vessel).
4. **Pressure control** — economizer circuit, vaporizer, pressure relief valves (PRVs) and rupture discs.
5. **Instrumentation** — level (differential-pressure, capacitance), temperature (PT-100), pressure, gas detectors.

**Step-by-step cryogenic storage logic:**

- **Step 1 — Quantify heat ingress:** Q_leak = (k_eff · A · ΔT) / L_insul. For vacuum + MLI, **k_eff ≈ 1–3 mW/(m·K)**.
- **Step 2 — Predict boil-off:** ṁ_boil = Q_leak / L_v. For LN₂, L_v ≈ **199.3 kJ/kg** at 1 atm.
- **Step 3 — Size pressure relief:** Vent area A_v is computed per **API 520 / ASME BPVC §VIII** for fire case (Q = 43,200 · F · A_w^0.82).
- **Step 4 — Plan for vapor displacement:** Filling a warm tank generates massive flash gas; use **phase separator / fill-with-vapor-return lines**.
- **Step 5 — Plan emergency response:** Oxygen depletion sensors, PPE (cryogenic gloves, face shield, closed shoes), water deluge for skin contact.

### 2.3 Shipping & Receiving — Process Flow

1. **Pre-shipment** — Supplier tests, fills, labels per **UN/ADR/IMDG/IATA** depending on mode.
2. **In-transit** — Placarding, segregation, emergency response numbers.
3. **At gate** — Driver declares; security checks seals; weighbridge verification.
4. **Unloading bay** — Wheel chocks, earthing for flammable cryogens, MSDS in local language, supervisor sign-off.
5. **Storage transfer** — Pump (for liquids) or conveyor (for solids) under permit-to-work.
6. **Documentation close-out** — Goods Receipt Note (GRN), quarantine if needed, ERP update.

> [!NOTE]
> **UN Hazard Classes most relevant to this module:**
> - **Class 2.1** — Flammable gas (H₂, CH₄)
> - **Class 2.2** — Non-flammable, non-toxic gas (N₂, Ar, CO₂)
> - **Class 2.3** — Toxic gas (Cl₂, NH₃)
> - **Class 5.1** — Oxidizer (LOX, H₂O₂)
> - **Class 4.3** — Water-reactive (Na, CaC₂)
> - **Class 9** — Miscellaneous (dry ice, asbestos)

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **CRITICAL FORMATTING NOTE:** All absolute-value or "divides" symbols below use **\vert** or **\mid** to avoid breaking the markdown table.

| # | Formula | Description | Units / Standard |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $Q \;=\; \dfrac{k \cdot A \cdot \Delta T}{L}$ | Fourier conduction through insulation | W (k in W/mK, A in m²) |
| 2 | $\dot{m}_{\text{boil}} \;=\; \dfrac{Q_{\text{leak}}}{L_v}$ | Mass boil-off rate | kg/s |
| 3 | $\dot{V}_{\text{gas}} \;=\; \dot{m}_{\text{boil}} \cdot \dfrac{\rho_{\text{liq}}}{\rho_{\text{gas, STP}}}$ | Equivalent gas volume rate | m³ STP/s |
| 4 | $A_w \;=\; \dfrac{Q}{43{,}200 \cdot F}$ | API 520 wetted relief area (fire case, scfm equivalent) | in² |
| 5 | $\dfrac{dP}{dt} \;=\; \dfrac{Q}{V} \cdot \dfrac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma} \cdot \dfrac{R}{M}$ | Pressure rise in sealed adiabatic cryo-tank | Pa/s |
| 6 | $\theta_r$ | Angle of repose (flowability index) | degrees |
| 7 | $\sigma_{cr} \;=\; \dfrac{\rho_b \cdot g \cdot D_c}{4 \cdot (1 + \sin\delta) / \cos\delta}$ | Jenike's rat-hole stress (simplified) | Pa |
| 8 | $V_{\text{ratio}} \;=\; \dfrac{\rho_{\text{liq}}}{\rho_{\text{gas, STP}}}$ | Liquid-to-gas expansion ratio | dimensionless |
| 9 | $D_c \;=\; \dfrac{f_c \cdot (1 + \sin\delta)}{\rho_b \cdot g \cdot \cos\delta}$ | Critical outlet diameter for rat-hole prevention | m |
| 10 | $K_{\text{st}} \;\vert\; P_{\max}$ | Dust explosion severity indices (Bartknecht) | bar·m/s, bar |

**Key Standard Properties (use in numerical problems):**

| Cryogen | Boiling Point (°C) | Density (kg/m³) | Latent Heat L_v (kJ/kg) | Expansion Ratio |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| LN₂ | −196 | 808 | 199.3 | 1 : 694 |
| LOX | −183 | 1141 | 213.1 | 1 : 861 |
| LAr | −186 | 1395 | 161.0 | 1 : 840 |
| LNG (CH₄) | −162 | 422 | 510.0 | 1 : 625 |
| LH₂ | −253 | 71 | 445.6 | 1 : 850 |
| LHe | −269 | 125 | 20.9 | 1 : 757 |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why these formulas matter in real engineering:**
> - **(1)–(3)** are the bedrock of every cryogenic tank vendor's heat-leak guarantee and product-loss insurance policy.
> - **(4)** determines whether your pressure relief valve will *survive* a fire engulfment — failure here leads to BLEVE.
> - **(5)** tells you how long a *failing* tank can hold before rupture — used in HAZOP time-to-escape studies.
> - **(9)** determines whether your silo will discharge at all or rat-hole indefinitely.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Heat Leak Through a Cryogenic Tank (Vacuum + MLI)

A double-wall cryogenic tank has an inner radius r_i, outer radius r_o, and an annular vacuum gap of effective thermal conductivity k_eff. Steady-state 1-D radial conduction gives:

$$
Q \;=\; \frac{2\pi k_{\text{eff}} L \, (T_{\text{out}} - T_{\text{in}})}{\ln(r_o / r_i)}
$$

**Step 1 —** Write Fourier's law in cylindrical coordinates: $Q = -k A \frac{dT}{dr}$, where $A = 2\pi r L$.

**Step 2 —** Separate variables and integrate from r_i to r_o:

$$
\int_{T_{\text{in}}}^{T_{\text{out}}} dT \;=\; -\frac{Q}{2\pi k L} \int_{r_i}^{r_o} \frac{dr}{r}
$$

**Step 3 —** Evaluate:

$$
T_{\text{out}} - T_{\text{in}} \;=\; -\frac{Q}{2\pi k L} \ln\!\left(\frac{r_o}{r_i}\right)
$$

**Step 4 —** Rearrange for Q:

$$
Q \;=\; \frac{2\pi k_{\text{eff}} L \, (T_{\text{out}} - T_{\text{in}})}{\ln(r_o / r_i)}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** A spherical LNG tank with r_i = 18 m, r_o = 19 m, k_eff = 0.001 W/mK, T_out − T_in = (293 − 113) = 180 K gives **Q ≈ 1.6 kW** — a typical boil-off loss that must be removed by an economizer / recondenser. If insulation degrades (k_eff ↑ 10×), Q ↑ 10×, the **boil-off rate is 10× faster**, and the tank vents gas continuously — direct economic loss and GHG emission.

### 3.2 Derivation: Boil-Off Rate and Equivalent Gas Volume

Starting from the heat-leak result above, the mass of liquid vaporized per second equals the heat input divided by the latent heat of vaporization:

$$
\dot{m}_{\text{boil}} \;=\; \frac{Q}{L_v}
$$

The equivalent **standard volumetric** flow of vapor at STP (T_STP = 273.15 K, P_STP = 101.325 kPa) is:

$$
\dot{V}_{\text{STP}} \;=\; \frac{\dot{m}_{\text{boil}} \, R_{\text{sp}} \, T_{\text{STP}}}{P_{\text{STP}}} \;=\; \frac{Q \, R_{\text{sp}} \, T_{\text{STP}}}{L_v \, P_{\text{STP}}}
$$

**Worked Numerical Example (KTU-typical):**

A 50 m³ LN₂ storage tank has an average heat leak of **Q = 80 W** through its vacuum+MLI insulation. Calculate the daily boil-off mass and the equivalent N₂ gas volume at STP.

Given: L_v = 199.3 kJ/kg, T_STP = 273.15 K, P_STP = 101.325 kPa, R_sp(N₂) = 296.8 J/(kg·K).

**Step A — Daily heat input:**

$$
Q_{\text{day}} \;=\; 80 \text{ W} \times 86{,}400 \text{ s/day} \;=\; 6.912 \times 10^{6} \text{ J/day}
$$

**Step B — Boil-off mass:**

$$
m_{\text{boil}} \;=\; \frac{Q_{\text{day}}}{L_v} \;=\; \frac{6.912 \times 10^{6}}{199.3 \times 10^{3}} \;=\; 34.68 \text{ kg/day}
$$

**Step C — Equivalent gas volume at STP:**

$$
V_{\text{STP}} \;=\; m_{\text{boil}} \times 694 \;=\; 34.68 \times 694 \;=\; 24{,}068 \text{ L/day} \approx 24.07 \text{ m}^3/\text{day}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Result interpretation:** 34.68 kg of LN₂ lost per day corresponds to **24 m³ of gaseous N₂** released into the surroundings — enough to displace the air in a small room multiple times over. This is why **storage rooms for cryogens must be ventilated at ≥ 6 air changes per hour** (per NFPA 55 / IS 14495).

### 3.3 Derivation: Pressure Rise in a Hermetically Sealed Cryogenic Vessel

For an insulated tank with the relief valve *removed* (worst case — relief failure), assume vapor behaves as an ideal gas in an adiabatic, constant-volume boundary receiving heat leak Q (W):

$$
\frac{dP}{dt} \;=\; \frac{Q}{V} \cdot \frac{\gamma - 1}{\gamma} \cdot \frac{R}{M}
$$

**Step 1 —** Internal energy of an ideal gas: $U = m c_v T$, with $c_v = R/(\gamma-1)M$ per unit mass.

**Step 2 —** Energy balance over a small time dt: $Q \, dt = dU = m c_v dT = \frac{m R}{(\gamma-1) M} dT$.

**Step 3 —** Differentiate ideal gas law $PV = (m/M) R T$ at constant V:

$$
V \, dP \;=\; \frac{m R}{M} \, dT \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; dT \;=\; \frac{V M}{m R} \, dP
$$

**Step 4 —** Substitute into the energy equation:

$$
Q \, dt \;=\; \frac{m R}{(\gamma-1)M} \cdot \frac{V M}{m R} \, dP \;=\; \frac{V}{\gamma-1} \, dP
$$

**Step 5 —** Rearrange:

$$
\frac{dP}{dt} \;=\; \frac{(\gamma-1) Q}{V} \;\; \text{(adiabatic, constant V)}
$$

For a *real cryogenic tank* with some vapor mass m_vapor in the headspace, integrating gives the time to reach the set pressure of the relief valve:

$$
t_{\text{relief}} \;=\; \frac{V_{\text{vapor}} (P_{\text{set}} - P_0)}{(\gamma - 1) \, Q / V}
$$

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Storage Selection

| Material Behaviour | Silo Geometry | Outlet Design | Vent / Relief | Critical Failure Mode |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Free-flowing, non-dusting (sand, salt) | Mass-flow conical | Circular, 1.5× D_c | Filtered vent | Rat-hole |
| Cohesive, fine (flour, cement) | Mass-flow with vibrator | Circular, 2× D_c, aerated | Filter + explosion panel | Arching |
| Segregating (wide PSD) | Multiple outlets / blended | Mass-flow | Filtered | Size segregation |
| Hygroscopic (fertilizer) | Hermetically sealed | Sealed rotary valve | Pressure-vacuum vent | Caking |
| Cryogenic liquid | Vacuum-jacketed tank | Top-fill / bottom-drain | PRV + rupture disc | Loss of vacuum |
| Flammable cryogen (LNG/LH₂) | Double-wall + N₂ purge | Vapor return | Flame arrestor + PRV | Pool fire / vapor cloud |

### 3.5 Derivation: API 520 Fire-Case Relief Sizing

For a cryogenic tank exposed to external fire, the heat input is given by API 520:

$$
Q \;=\; 43{,}200 \cdot F \cdot A_w^{0.82}
$$

where **F** is an environment factor (1.0 for unobstructed, 0.5 for water spray, 0.3 for earth-covered). The required **wetted area** for the relief is:

$$
A_w \;=\; \left(\frac{Q_{\text{required}}}{43{,}200 \cdot F}\right)^{1/0.82}
$$

**Worked Numerical Example:**

A 100 m³ vertical LOX tank has a wetted area of A_w = 25 m². Compute the required relief area assuming **F = 1.0**.

**Step A —** Required fire heat input:

$$
Q_{\text{required}} \;=\; 43{,}200 \times 1.0 \times (25)^{0.82}
$$

**Step B —** Evaluate $25^{0.82}$:

$$
\ln(25) \;=\; 3.2189 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; 0.82 \times 3.2189 \;=\; 2.6395 \;\;\Rightarrow\;\; e^{2.6395} \;=\; 14.01
$$

So $25^{0.82} \approx 14.01$.

**Step C —** Compute Q:

$$
Q_{\text{required}} \;=\; 43{,}200 \times 14.01 \;=\; 605{,}232 \text{ kW} \;(\text{API 520 units, effectively BTU/hr-equivalent})
$$

**Step D —** Required relief wetted area for a re-evaluated scenario where Q = 600,000 units:

$$
A_w^{\text{req}} \;=\; \left(\frac{600{,}000}{43{,}200 \times 1.0}\right)^{1/0.82} \;=\; (13.89)^{1.2195} \;\approx\; 25.5 \text{ m}^2
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters:** The relief device must pass *more* vapor than the worst-case heat input. Undersizing is the #1 root cause of catastrophic cryogenic tank failure during engulfment fires (e.g., the 1947 Texas City disaster lessons).
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Cryogenic Storage Tank — Block-Level Functional Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph OuterVessel [OUTER VESSEL - Carbon Steel]
        direction TB
        Support1[Skirt Support]
        Manhole1[Top Manway]
        Grounding1[Earthing Boss]
    end
    subgraph Annulus [ANNULAR SPACE - Vacuum + MLI]
        direction TB
        Vacuum1[Vacuum < 1e-3 Pa]
        Perlite1[Perlite Powder 5 cm]
        MLI1[MLI 30 layers Al foil]
    end
    subgraph InnerVessel [INNER VESSEL - SS304L]
        direction TB
        LIQLiq1[Cryogenic Liquid]
        HeadSpace1[Headspace Vapor]
        Inlet1[Top Fill Nozzle]
        Outlet1[Bottom Drain]
        PRV1[Pressure Relief Valve]
        RD1[Rupture Disc]
        Lvl1[Level Transmitter]
        Temp1[PT100 Sensor]
        Pres1[Pressure Gauge]
    end
    Support1 --> Annulus
    Manhole1 --> Annulus
    Annulus --> InnerVessel
    Inlet1 --> LIQLiq1
    Outlet1 --> LIQLiq1
    PRV1 --> HeadSpace1
    RD1 --> HeadSpace1
    Lvl1 --> LIQLiq1
    Temp1 --> LIQLiq1
    Pres1 --> HeadSpace1
    Grounding1 -.earthing.-> InnerVessel
```

### 4.2 Shipping & Receiving — Process Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Supplier Dispatch] --> B[In-Transit Placarding]
    B --> C[Gate Security - Seal Check]
    C --> D{Documentation Match?}
    D -- No --> D1[Hold and Isolate - Raise NCR]
    D -- Yes --> E[Unloading Bay - Wheel Chock + Earth]
    E --> F{Hazmat Class?}
    F -- Class 2.1 2.3 --> F1[Gas Detector Active - Area Permit]
    F -- Class 5.1 Oxidizer --> F2[Segregate from Flammables]
    F -- Class 4.3 Water Reactive --> F3[Dry Area - No Water Sources]
    F -- Non-hazardous --> F4[Standard SOP]
    F1 --> G[Transfer to Storage]
    F2 --> G
    F3 --> G
    F4 --> G
    G --> H[MSDS / SDS File Update]
    H --> I[GRN Sign-off in ERP]
    I --> J[Material Released to Stock]
```

### 4.3 Solids Storage — Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    A[Incoming Solid] --> B{Hazardous?}
    B -- Yes --> C[Segregated Bunded Warehouse]
    B -- No --> D{Bulk or Packaged?}
    D -- Packaged --> E[Pallet Racking - FIFO]
    D -- Bulk --> F{Free-Flowing HR<1.2?}
    F -- Yes --> G[Mass-Flow Silo - Steep Cone]

    G --> I[Dust Filter + Explosion Panel]
    H --> I
    I --> J[Silo Discharge to Process]
    C --> K[Special Permit Access]
    E --> L[Pick and Place to Process]
    K --> L
    J --> L
```

### 4.4 Receiving Process — Acceptance Matrix

| Stage | Tool / Document | Responsible | Hold-Point? |
|---|---|---|---|
| Pre-arrival | Advance shipping notice (ASN) | Logistics | No |
| Gate | Bill of lading, seal, weighbridge | Security | **Yes** — if seal broken |
| Bay | MSDS/SDS cross-check, container inspection | Shift engineer | **Yes** — if damage |
| Transfer | Permit-to-work, gas test, earthing | Operations | **Yes** — cryogenic transfer |
| Stock | Goods Receipt Note (GRN), ERP | Storekeeper | **Yes** — if quarantine needed |
