# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024]**
Classify material handling equipment. List any **four** types of industrial trucks with one application each.

**Model Answer:**

Material handling equipment is classified into **five categories**:
1. **Transport Equipment** — moves materials between locations.
2. **Positioning Equipment** — places materials at a precise point.
3. **Unit Load Formation Equipment** — combines items into one load.
4. **Storage Equipment** — holds materials between operations.
5. **Identification & Control Equipment** — tracks material flow.

**Four types of industrial trucks with applications:**

| # | Type | Application |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Forklift Truck (Counterbalanced)** | Lifting and transporting palletized loads in warehouses |
| 2 | **Reach Truck** | Order picking in narrow-aisle racking systems |
| 3 | **Pallet Jack (Electric)** | Horizontal movement of pallets in distribution centers |
| 4 | **Order Picker** | Multi-level picking in high-bay warehouses |

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Model Paper 2024]**
Differentiate between an **EOT crane** and a **gantry crane**. State **two** safety devices mandatory on both.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | EOT (Electric Overhead Travelling) Crane | Gantry Crane |
|---|---|---|
| **Support Structure** | Bridge runs on **overhead runway rails** mounted on building columns | Bridge is supported by **legs running on ground-level rails** |
| **Installation** | Requires strong building structure; high capital cost | Can be used **outdoors**; lower building modification cost |
| **Coverage** | Full bay width × length of building | Limited to gantry span; can extend to open yards |
| **Application** | Machine shops, assembly halls, steel plants | Shipyards, container terminals, stockyards |

**Common mandatory safety devices (both):**
1. **Overload Limit Switch / Load Moment Indicator (LMI)** — cuts off hoisting if load exceeds rated capacity.
2. **Upper and Lower Travel Limit Switches** — prevent over-hoisting and over-lowering the hook.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **classification of cranes** with neat sketches. Discuss the working principle and applications of **bridge cranes** and **tower cranes**. State the **safety devices** used in each. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A steel drum weighing **$W = 1500$ kg** is to be lifted using a **3-leg wire rope sling**. Each leg makes an angle of **$45°$ with the vertical**. Calculate:
(i) Tension in each sling leg.
(ii) Horizontal compressive force on the drum.
(iii) Required factor of safety if each leg has a WLL of **$700$ kg**.
(iv) Comment on the safety of the operation. **[7 marks]**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Classification of Cranes and Bridge/Tower Crane Detail

**Classification of Cranes:**

1. **Overhead Travelling Cranes**
   - Bridge Cranes (EOT) — single girder and double girder
   - Gantry Cranes
2. **Rotating Cranes**
   - Jib Cranes (wall, pillar, articulating)
   - Mobile Cranes (truck-mounted, crawler, rough terrain, all-terrain)
   - Tower Cranes
3. **Special Purpose Cranes**
   - Container handling cranes (STS, RTG, RMG)
   - Loader cranes
   - Marine cranes (deck, offshore)

**Bridge Crane (EOT) — Working Principle and Application:**

A bridge crane consists of:
- **Bridge girders** spanning the bay, supported on end-carriages running on overhead runway rails.
- **Trolley** that travels along the bridge carrying the hoist.
- **Hoist** with hook for vertical lifting.
- **Three motors** control: bridge travel, trolley travel, and hoisting.

The crane operator controls all three motions via a **pendant push-button station** or **radio remote**.

**Applications:** Heavy machinery workshops, steel mills, power plants, assembly lines for medium-to-heavy components (5–500 tonnes).

**Safety Devices in Bridge Crane:**
- Overload limit switch
- Upper and lower hoist limit switches
- Bridge and trolley travel limit switches
- Anti-collision devices (when multiple cranes on same runway)
- Emergency stop
- Warning horn and lights
- Buffer springs at end of travel

**Tower Crane — Working Principle and Application:**

A tower crane has:
- A **vertical mast (tower)** of lattice or tubular construction.
- A **horizontal jib** at the top with a counter-jib carrying counterweights.
- A **slewing unit** that rotates the jib 360°.
- A **hoist winch** in the slewing platform.
- A **climbing frame** for self-erection.

The operator sits in a **cab at the top of the mast** or controls from the ground.

**Applications:** High-rise building construction, large infrastructure projects (bridges, dams), industrial chimney erection.

**Safety Devices in Tower Crane:**
- **Load Moment Indicator (LMI)** — prevents overturning by limiting load × radius.
- **Slewing limiter** — restricts rotation to safe range.
- **Hoist travel limiter**
- **Anemometer** — stops crane in winds above 50 km/h.
- **Anti-two-block device** — prevents hook from contacting the jib.
- **Out-of-plumb indicator** — warns if mast is not vertical.

---

#### Part (b) — Sling Tension Numerical

**Given:**
- $W = 1500$ kg
- $n = 3$ legs
- $\theta = 45°$ from vertical
- WLL per leg = $700$ kg

**(i) Tension in each sling leg:**

$$
T = \dfrac{W}{n \cos\theta} = \dfrac{1500}{3 \cdot \cos 45°} = \dfrac{1500}{3 \cdot 0.7071} = \dfrac{1500}{2.1213}
$$

$$
T = 707.1 \text{ kg per leg}
$$

**(ii) Horizontal compressive force on the drum:**

$$
F_H = n \cdot T \cdot \sin\theta = 3 \cdot 707.1 \cdot \sin 45° = 3 \cdot 707.1 \cdot 0.7071
$$

$$
F_H = 1500 \text{ kg (compressive)}
$$

**Insight:** The total inward squeeze is numerically equal to the load weight — this is because at 45°, the load splits equally between vertical lift and horizontal compression.

**(iii) Factor of Safety:**

$$
FoS = \dfrac{WLL}{T} = \dfrac{700}{707.1} = 0.99
$$

**(iv) Safety Comment:**

> [!WARNING]
> The lift is **UNSAFE** — the Factor of Safety is **0.99**, meaning the **tension exceeds the working load limit** of the sling. The sling is on the verge of failure. **Recommended action:** Use a sling with WLL of at least **$2100$ kg per leg** (to achieve FoS of 3:1) OR reduce the sling angle to make the lift more vertical, thereby reducing $T$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the construction and working of a **belt conveyor**. Derive the equation for the **power required to drive the belt conveyor**. State **five safety devices** used. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** An **electric hoist** lifts a load of **$W = 3000$ kg** at a speed of **$6$ m/min**. The overall efficiency of the hoist is **$80\%$**. Calculate:
(i) The output power (at the hook).
(ii) The input power (motor rating).
(iii) If the wire rope diameter is **$16$ mm**, calculate the **minimum drum diameter** as per IS code.
(iv) State the factor of safety used in your calculation. **[7 marks]**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Belt Conveyor Detail

**Construction of a Belt Conveyor:**

A belt conveyor consists of:
- **Belt** — endless loop of rubber/PVC, the carrying medium.
- **Head Pulley** — driving pulley at the discharge end.
- **Tail Pulley** — idler pulley at the loading end.
- **Idlers** — rollers supporting the belt and load (carrying and return).
- **Take-up Device** — maintains belt tension (gravity, screw, or hydraulic).
- **Drive Unit** — motor + gearbox + coupling.
- **Loading Chute** — directs material onto the belt.
- **Belt Cleaners / Scrapers** — remove carry-back material.
- **Structure** — stringers, galleries, walkways.

**Working Principle:**
The motor drives the **head pulley** via a gearbox. Friction between the pulley and the belt makes the belt move. The load is loaded at the **tail end** via the chute, carried on the top belt, and discharged at the **head end** over the pulley.

**Power Required — Derivation:**

Consider a belt conveyor with:
- Tight side tension = $T_1$
- Slack side tension = $T_2$
- Belt speed = $v$ (m/s)

The net driving force on the belt is:

$$
F = T_1 - T_2
$$

The power transmitted to the belt is the work done per second:

$$
P = F \cdot v = (T_1 - T_2) \cdot v \quad \text{(in Watts)}
$$

Converting to **kW**:

$$
P = \dfrac{(T_1 - T_2) \cdot v}{1000} \quad \text{kW}
$$

Including the **efficiency** of the drive system $\eta$:

$$
P_{motor} = \dfrac{(T_1 - T_2) \cdot v}{1000 \cdot \eta} \quad \text{kW}
$$

**Five Safety Devices in Belt Conveyors:**
1. **Belt Sway Switches** — trip the belt if it drifts off-center.
2. **Pull-cord Emergency Stop** — accessible along entire belt length.
3. **Belt Rip Detection Sensor** — detects longitudinal tears.
4. **Zero-Speed Switch** — trips if belt stops under load (chute jam).
5. **Belt Slip Detection** — alarms if belt slips on the drive pulley.

---

#### Part (b) — Hoist Numerical

**Given:**
- $W = 3000$ kg
- $v = 6$ m/min $= 0.1$ m/s
- $\eta = 0.80$
- $d_{rope} = 16$ mm

**(i) Output Power (at the hook):**

$$
P_{out} = W \cdot g \cdot v = 3000 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.1 = 2943 \text{ W} = 2.943 \text{ kW}
$$

**(ii) Input Power (Motor Rating):**

$$
P_{in} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{\eta} = \dfrac{2.943}{0.80} = 3.679 \text{ kW}
$$

**Practical choice:** A standard **3.7 kW or 5 HP** motor would be selected.

**(iii) Minimum Drum Diameter (IS 3177):**

For a **wire rope** in a hoisting application, IS 3177 specifies:

$$
D_{drum} \geq 30 \cdot d \quad \text{to} \quad 45 \cdot d
$$

Using the conservative lower bound for **frequent duty**:

$$
D_{drum} = 30 \cdot 16 = 480 \text{ mm}
$$

Using the **upper bound** for **severe duty** (frequent starting, heavy loads):

$$
D_{drum} = 45 \cdot 16 = 720 \text{ mm}
$$

**Adopt:** $D_{drum} = 480$ mm for standard industrial use; **720 mm** for continuous heavy duty.

**(iv) Factor of Safety:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per **IS 3177 / FEM 1.001**, the recommended Factor of Safety for wire rope on hoists is:
> - **3.5:1** for general material handling
> - **5:1 to 6:1** for personnel lifts and crane applications
>
> In our calculation, a 30× drum-to-rope ratio implicitly assumes a **standard 5:1 Factor of Safety** on rope breaking strength.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Confusing the angle reference** — In sling problems, $\theta$ may be from vertical *or* horizontal. KTU consistently uses **angle from vertical**. A wrong angle in the problem can change the answer by 40%.
> 2. **Forgetting compressive force** — Examiners frequently test whether you understand that low sling angles create *crushing* hazards, not just overload.
> 3. **Unit conversion errors** — kg-force vs Newtons is a top reason for marks lost. Always convert to SI unless the problem explicitly allows gravities.
> 4. **Neglecting efficiency** — In hoist power calculations, students often forget $\eta$ and write only $P = Wv$, losing 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Naming equipment generically** — Don't write "crane" alone. Be specific: "EOT bridge crane," "gantry crane," "tower crane." KTU rewards **terminology precision**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### High-Density Revision Checklist

- [ ] **5 categories** of MH equipment: Transport, Positioning, Unit Load, Storage, Control.
- [ ] **Crane types:** Bridge (EOT), Gantry, Jib, Mobile, Tower — know capacity and application of each.
- [ ] **Hoist types:** Manual chain, electric wire rope, electric chain, pneumatic, hydraulic — pneumatic is for **hazardous zones**.
- [ ] **Conveyor types:** Belt, Roller, Chain, Screw, Pneumatic, Bucket Elevator — know the medium they carry.
- [ ] **Industrial trucks:** Forklift, Reach truck, Pallet jack, Order picker, AGV.
- [ ] **Master formula:** $T = W / (n \cos\theta)$ for multi-leg slings.
- [ ] **Horizontal squeeze force:** $F_H = n T \sin\theta$ — increases as sling angle becomes more horizontal.
- [ ] **Wire rope Factor of Safety:** 3.5:1 (material) to 5:1 (personnel).
- [ ] **Drum-to-rope ratio:** $D/d \geq 30$ to $45$ (IS 3177).
- [ ] **Hoist power:** $P = Wv / \eta$ (Watts if W in N, v in m/s).
- [ ] **Belt conveyor power:** $P = (T_1 - T_2) v / 1000$ in kW.
- [ ] **Screw conveyor capacity:** depends on diameter, pitch, rpm, filling factor, density.
- [ ] **Forklift stability triangle:** CG must remain inside the 3-wheel contact triangle.
- [ ] **Crane LMI:** Load × Radius = Constant (max moment).
- [ ] **Tower crane safety:** Anemometer (wind), LMI, anti-two-block, slewing limiter.
- [ ] **Belt conveyor safety:** Pull-cord, sway switches, zero-speed, slip detection, rip sensor.
- [ ] **Hook dimensions:** Tensile stress uses $d$ (root diameter); Shear stress uses $d \times t$.
- [ ] **OSHA / IS codes to remember:** IS 3177 (wire rope), IS 3938 (electric hoists), IS 2065 (hoists), OSHA 1910.179 (overhead cranes), OSHA 1910.178 (forklifts).
- [ ] **Pneumatic hoists** are intrinsically safe — preferred in **explosive atmospheres** (Zone 1 / Zone 2).
- [ ] **AGVs** use LIDAR + SLAM in modern installations — guide path is no longer physical.
- [ ] **Bucket elevators** are vertical conveyors; **spillage and dust** are major safety concerns.
- [ ] **Always inspect MHE before use** — pre-use checklist is a legal requirement under Factories Act 1948 (India) and OSHA (US).
- [ ] **Color code (KTU expects):** Yellow = caution, Red = danger, Green = safety, Blue = mandatory PPE.
- [ ] **Trolley + Bridge + Hoist = EOT** — remember this 3-component structure.
- [ ] **Jib crane reach = radius of semicircular coverage** — used for **workstation-specific** lifting.
- [ ] **Sling efficiency** decreases with sling angle — keep $\theta \geq 60°$ for safe lifts.
- [ ] **Critical sling angle** at $\theta = 60°$ for 2-leg sling — $T = W$ (tension equals full weight).
- [ ] **Conversion factors to remember:** 1 HP = 746 W, 1 kgf = 9.81 N, 1 m/min = 1/60 m/s.

> **Final Exam Tip:** When answering KTU Part B questions on Types, **always start with a labeled diagram** (even a hand-drawn one), then proceed with the **construction → working → safety devices** structure. Examiners allocate **1.5 to 2 marks** for diagrams and 1 mark for the safety section explicitly.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Strength and Working Load Inspection
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT522 - SAFETY IN MATERIAL HANDLING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Strength and Working Load Inspection

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Strength and Working Load Inspection** is a systematic, documented engineering procedure mandated under KTU's Industrial Safety framework (PESFT522) for evaluating the residual load-bearing capacity, structural integrity, and serviceability of lifting equipment, rigging hardware, and material handling accessories before, during, and after use. The procedure verifies that every load-bearing component possesses adequate **Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS)** to sustain the imposed **Working Load Limit (WLL)** — also termed the **Safe Working Load (SWL)** or **Rated Capacity (RC)** — with an acceptable margin of safety.

According to the operational tenets of safety engineering, the inspection regime encompasses two synergistic actions:

- **Strength Inspection:** A technical assessment of the material's *capacity to resist* applied mechanical stress, typically verified through **Non-Destructive Testing (NDT)** methods such as visual, magnetic particle, dye penetrant, ultrasonic, and electromagnetic induction techniques.
- **Working Load Inspection:** A functional verification of the equipment's *capacity to perform* its rated lifting duty under statutory conditions, validated through **Proof Load Testing (PLT)**, dimensional checks, and **Factor of Safety (FoS)** reconciliation.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight — PESFT522 / Module 2**
> Strength and Working Load Inspection is a **CO2-mapped, high-weightage topic** in the KTU 2024 scheme. The expected cognitive levels span **Apply (L3)**, **Analyze (L4)**, and **Evaluate (L5)** of the Revised Bloom's Taxonomy. Questions frequently appear in **Part B (14 marks)** sections of End Semester Examinations (ESE).

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine a **wooden bridge** in a Kerala village that must support a fully loaded goods auto (say, 2 tonnes). The bridge was originally built to handle 5 tonnes (its design strength). Over years, termites silently chew the timber, rain rots the planks, and bolts loosen. Would you trust it blindly? Certainly not.

**Strength Inspection** is like a structural engineer walking over the bridge with a magnifying glass, tapping beams with a hammer to listen for hollow sounds (ultrasonic testing analogy), looking for cracks (visual inspection analogy), and measuring the thickness of the timber (dimensional check). They are asking: *"Is the material still strong enough to hold?"*

**Working Load Inspection** is like posting a 2-tonne truck loaded with sandbags on the bridge for 24 hours and observing if there is any deflection, cracking, or settlement (proof load testing analogy). They are asking: *"Can it actually perform its job safely under the rated load?"*

Together, these two inspections ensure that the **actual strength of the equipment** always remains comfortably **above the load you intend to place on it** — a margin we call the **Factor of Safety**.

### 1.3 Key Terminology & Physical Constants

The following standardized terms are uniformly adopted across KTU question papers, IS (Indian Standard) codes, ASME B30 series, and OSHA 1910.184:

- **Working Load Limit (WLL):** The maximum mass or force that a lifting device is *authorized* to sustain in service, expressed in **kilograms (kg)**, **tonnes (t)**, **pounds (lb)**, or **Newtons (N)**. The value is **permanently stamped** on every certified item.
- **Ultimate Tensile Strength (UTS):** The maximum tensile stress a material can withstand before failure, measured in **N/mm²** or **MPa**.
- **Breaking Load (BL) / Minimum Breaking Force (MBF):** The load at which the component physically fractures, established by destructive testing on representative samples.
- **Factor of Safety (FoS) / Design Factor (DF):** A dimensionless numerical ratio (typically **4:1, 5:1, or 10:1** depending on application) expressing the margin between breaking strength and working load.
- **Proof Load (PL):** A static test load, generally **1.25 × to 2.0 × the WLL**, applied to a component to verify manufacturing integrity and structural soundness.
- **Standard Acceleration due to Gravity:** $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ (used when converting mass to force in Newtons).

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard WLL Definition per IS 2266 / IS 3177:**
> The Working Load Limit is defined as the maximum load that may be applied to a new, unused item of equipment under specified service conditions. Once a component shows wear, deformation, or corrosion, its *effective* WLL must be re-evaluated by a competent person.

### 1.4 Classification of Inspection Regimes

Inspections are stratified into a **four-tier hierarchy** per KTU's competency-based learning model and aligned with international standards:

| Tier | Inspection Type | Frequency | Performed By | Statutory Basis |
|------|----------------|-----------|---------------|-----------------|
| 1 | **Initial / Commissioning Inspection** | Before first use | Third-party certifying agency | IS 2266, ASME B30.9 |
| 2 | **Frequent Inspection** | Daily to monthly | Operator / Rigger | OSHA 1910.184(e) |
| 3 | **Periodic Inspection** | Quarterly to annual | Competent Person (Engineer) | IS 3177, Factory Rules |
| 4 | **Thorough / Annual Examination** | Yearly (or as mandated) | Authorized Inspector | Factories Act 1948, Sec. 29 |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Theoretical Foundations of Strength Inspection

The strength of any lifting accessory is governed by **three primary physical mechanisms** that must be inspected continuously:

**A. Material Degradation Mechanisms**
- **Metallurgical fatigue:** Micro-crack initiation and propagation under cyclic loading.
- **Corrosion (oxidation, pitting, galvanic attack):** Loss of effective cross-sectional area.
- **Wear and abrasion:** Surface material loss due to friction with sheaves, drums, or adjacent components.
- **Plastic deformation:** Permanent bending, necking, or twisting beyond elastic limits.
- **Heat damage:** Loss of temper in alloy steel, evidenced by discoloration (straw, blue, or black oxide tints).

**B. Geometric Dimensional Depletion**
- Reduction in wire rope diameter (typically rejected at **10% loss** from nominal).
- Hook throat opening widening (rejection threshold: **5% to 15%** stretch beyond original).
- Chain link elongation (rejection at **3% to 5%** pitch extension).
- Shackle pin wear and bending.

**C. Service-Induced Anomalies**
- Kinks, birdcaging, or popped cores in wire ropes.
- Gouges, nicks, or weld spatter on slings.
- Missing or illegible identification tags.

### 2.2 The Master Equation: Working Load Limit Derivation

The cornerstone relationship in lifting safety engineering is:

$$WLL = \frac{\text{Minimum Breaking Load (MBL)}}{\text{Factor of Safety (FoS)}}$$

**Interpretation logic:**
- The numerator (MBL) is an *intrinsic material property* determined through controlled destructive tensile tests on virgin samples of the same batch.
- The denominator (FoS) is a *design conservatism multiplier* that absorbs uncertainty in load estimation, dynamic effects, wear accumulation, manufacturing variability, and human error.
- For example, a wire rope with MBL = **400 kN** designed with FoS = **5:1** will have:
  - WLL = 400 / 5 = 80 kN ≈ 8,160 kgf.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Insight:**
> The Factor of Safety for **fibre rope slings** is typically **5:1**, for **wire rope slings** is **5:1**, for **chain slings** is **4:1**, for **metal mesh slings** is **4:1**, and for **synthetic webbing slings** is **5:1**. Always cross-verify FoS against the relevant IS code for the question attempted.

### 2.3 Proof Load Testing Theory

**Proof Load Testing (PLT)** is a *non-destructive* verification of structural integrity wherein the component is loaded to a value **above its WLL but below its yield point**, held for a defined duration, and then examined for permanent deformation.

$$P_{proof} = K_{proof} \times WLL$$

where $K_{proof}$ is the **proof load factor**, typically ranging from **1.25 to 2.0** depending on the standard:
- For wire ropes and slings: $K_{proof} = 2.0$
- For overhead crane hooks: $K_{proof} = 1.25$ (per IS 3181)
- For chain slings (grade T or V): $K_{proof} = 2.0$ (per IS 2740)

**The Three Conditions for PLT Validation:**
1. The component must sustain the proof load for a minimum of **1 to 3 minutes** (sometimes 5 minutes for crane hooks) without any permanent set.
2. No visible cracks, deformation, or surface distress should appear.
3. The component must return to its original dimensions within **0.1% to 0.5%** tolerance upon load release.

### 2.4 Sling Tension Analysis (Multi-Leg Sling Geometry)

When a load is lifted using a multi-leg sling, the **tension in each leg** depends on the **sling angle (θ)** measured from the horizontal plane. This is a critical working load inspection parameter because operators frequently underestimate the dramatic tension increase at shallow angles.

For a **2-leg (bridle) sling** lifting a vertical load $W$:

$$T_{leg} = \frac{W}{2 \sin\theta}$$

For a **3-leg sling:**

$$T_{leg} = \frac{W}{3 \sin\theta}$$

For an **n-leg sling:**

$$T_{leg} = \frac{W}{n \sin\theta}$$

**Engineering insight:** As the sling angle θ approaches **0°** (i.e., sling becomes horizontal), $\sin\theta \to 0$, and the leg tension **mathematically approaches infinity**. In practice, sling angles below **30° from horizontal** (or above **60° from vertical**) are **strictly prohibited** under most safety standards.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Valuation Tip:**
> A common student error in KTU exams is confusing the sling angle with its complement. Always clarify whether θ is measured *from the horizontal* or *from the vertical*. The KTU answer key consistently measures θ **from the horizontal**.

### 2.5 Wire Rope Efficiency Formula

Wire rope strength cannot be calculated by simple summation of individual wire strengths. An **efficiency factor (η)** is applied to account for the geometry of helical lay, inter-wire friction, and core compression:

$$F_{rope} = \eta \times \sum_{i=1}^{n} F_{wire,i}$$

where:
- $F_{rope}$ = breaking force of the wire rope (N)
- $F_{wire,i}$ = breaking force of the i-th individual wire (N)
- $\eta$ = rope construction efficiency, typically **0.75 to 0.90** for 6×19 construction, **0.80 to 0.92** for 6×37 construction.

A more practical design formula used in KTU problems is:

$$WLL_{rope} = \frac{F_{rope} \times \eta_{design}}{\text{FoS}}$$

### 2.6 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Symbolic Form | Standard / Code Reference | Engineering Application |
|---|-------------------|---------------|---------------------------|--------------------------|
| 1 | Working Load Limit | $WLL = MBL / FoS$ | IS 2266, IS 3177 | All lifting gear |
| 2 | Proof Load | $P_{proof} = K_{proof} \times WLL$ | IS 3181, IS 2740 | Crane hooks, chains |
| 3 | Single Vertical Sling Tension | $T = W$ | IS 3177 | Straight vertical hitch |
| 4 | 2-Leg Sling Tension | $T = W / (2 \sin\theta)$ | IS 3177 | Bridle hitch |
| 4 | n-Leg Sling Tension | $T = W / (n \sin\theta)$ | IS 3177 | Multi-leg hitch |
| 5 | Choker Hitch Reduction | $T = W / (2 \mu)$ where $\mu$ = friction | ASME B30.9 | Choker configuration |
| 6 | Basket Hitch Load per Leg | $T = W / n$ | ASME B30.9 | Symmetric basket |
| 7 | Mass to Force Conversion | $F = m \times g$ where $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ | Universal | SI system |
| 8 | Wire Rope Efficiency | $F_{rope} = \eta \sum F_{wire}$ | IS 2266 | Wire rope design |
| 9 | Drum / Sheave D/d Ratio | $D/d \geq 18$ (running) or $\geq 30$ (critical) | IS 2266 | Drum diameter |
| 10 | Hook Throat Rejection Limit | Open dimension $\geq 1.15 \times$ original | IS 3181, OSHA | Hook inspection |
| 11 | Wire Rope Diameter Rejection | Diameter $\leq 0.90 \times$ nominal | IS 2266 | Rope wear |
| 12 | Chain Pitch Elongation Limit | Pitch $\geq 1.05 \times$ original | IS 2740 | Chain wear |
| 13 | Safety Factor Multipliers | FoS: Wire rope = 5, Chain = 4, Fibre = 5, Webbing = 5 | IS / ASME | All slings |
| 14 | D/d ratio for sheaves | $D \geq 18 d$ (standard) | IS 2266 | Sheave sizing |
| 15 | Working Load Limit for Sling at Angle | $WLL_{\theta} = WLL_{90} \times \sin\theta$ | ASME B30.9 | Angled slings |

> [!NOTE]
> **Critical Convention Used in KTU Marking:**
> When the question states "lift a 5-tonne load using a 2-leg sling at 60° from horizontal," the model answer must:
> 1. Convert mass to weight: $W = 5000 \times 9.81 = 49{,}050 \text{ N}$.
> 2. Apply geometry: $T = 49{,}050 / (2 \sin 60°) = 49{,}050 / 1.732 = 28{,}320 \text{ N}$.
> 3. Compare to WLL with appropriate FoS.

### 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

The principles above govern safety in:
- **Construction industry:** Crane lifts, tower crane derricking, scaffolding tie-backs.
- **Port and shipping:** Container handling, ship-to-shore gantry operations.
- **Manufacturing:** Heavy machinery installation, die handling, furnace charging.
- **Power sector:** Turbine rotor lifting, transformer installation.
- **Oil & gas:** Module lifts on offshore platforms, drilling rig assembly.
- **Disaster response:** Crane operations during building collapse rescue (Kerala State Disaster Management scenarios).

A single miscalculation in sling tension or failure to retire a corroded hook can result in catastrophic fatalities, making this topic one of the **most exam-relevant modules** in the entire PESFT522 syllabus.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Exhaustive Derivation: Effective Working Load of an Angled Sling

**Problem Setup (Typical KTU Pattern):**
A 2-leg wire rope sling is to be used to lift a structural steel component of mass **3,000 kg**. The sling legs are inclined at **45° from the horizontal**. Each leg has a minimum breaking load of **150 kN**. Determine:
- (a) The tension developed in each sling leg during the lift.
- (b) The actual Factor of Safety available.
- (c) Whether the lift is safe, with justification.

**Step 1: Compute the Vertical Load (Weight)**

We must convert the mass of the steel component into weight (force). Using the standard acceleration due to gravity $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$:

$$W = m \times g = 3000 \times 9.81 = 29{,}430 \text{ N} \approx 29.43 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2: Resolve the Geometry of the Sling System**

In a 2-leg (bridle) sling, the two legs share the vertical load equally when the angle is symmetric. Each leg carries a tension $T$ that has both a vertical component and a horizontal component. The vertical component of one leg is $T \sin\theta$, where $\theta = 45°$ is measured from the horizontal.

The vertical equilibrium equation is:

$$2 \times T \sin\theta = W$$

**Step 3: Solve for Leg Tension T**

Rearranging for T:

$$T = \frac{W}{2 \sin\theta}$$

Substituting the numerical values:

$$T = \frac{29{,}430}{2 \times \sin 45°}$$

Now, $\sin 45° = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2}} = 0.7071$. Therefore:

$$T = \frac{29{,}430}{2 \times 0.7071} = \frac{29{,}430}{1.4142}$$

$$T = 20{,}811.85 \text{ N} \approx 20.81 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4: Compute the Effective Factor of Safety**

The Minimum Breaking Load (MBL) of each leg is given as **150 kN**. The effective FoS is:

$$\text{FoS}_{eff} = \frac{MBL_{leg}}{T_{leg}} = \frac{150}{20.81} = 7.21$$

**Step 5: Decision on Safety**

The statutory minimum Factor of Safety for wire rope slings is **5:1** (per IS 3177 / ASME B30.9). Since the effective FoS = 7.21 > 5.0, the sling configuration is **safe for the proposed lift**. However, the *margin* is only 1.21× above the minimum, suggesting that for any increase in load, sling wear, or dynamic amplification (swinging load), the configuration would become unsafe.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 14-Mark Question Pattern:**
> For full marks, the student must (i) state the governing formula, (ii) show substitution with units, (iii) compute the answer with correct significant figures, and (iv) conclude with a safety verdict referencing the relevant IS code. Skipping any of these four steps typically costs 2 to 3 marks.

### 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Proof Load Calculation and Validation

**Problem Setup:**
A 6×37 wire rope sling of nominal diameter **24 mm** has a manufacturer-specified MBL of **385 kN** and a recommended WLL of **77 kN** (FoS = 5:1). During a periodic inspection, the inspector applies a proof load of **154 kN** for 2 minutes. After load release, the rope shows **0.4% permanent elongation** and **2 broken outer wires in one strand**. Determine whether the sling passes the proof load test.

**Step 1: Verify the Proof Load Factor**

$$K_{proof} = \frac{P_{proof}}{WLL} = \frac{154}{77} = 2.0$$

This matches the IS 2266 requirement ($K_{proof} = 2.0$ for wire ropes). ✓

**Step 2: Verify the Proof Load is Below Yield**

For a typical improved plow steel (IPS) wire rope, the yield load is approximately **65% of MBL**:

$$F_{yield} \approx 0.65 \times 385 = 250.25 \text{ kN}$$

Since $P_{proof} = 154 \text{ kN} < F_{yield} = 250.25 \text{ kN}$, the proof load is **below yield** and is a *true* non-destructive test. ✓

**Step 3: Check Permanent Elongation Tolerance**

The IS 2266 limit for permanent set after proof loading is **1.0%** of the original gauge length. Measured set = **0.4%**, which is **within tolerance**. ✓

**Step 4: Check Broken Wire Count**

Per IS 2266, the rejection criteria for **6×37 wire rope** is:
- **More than 12 broken wires in one rope lay length**, OR
- **More than 4 broken wires in one strand in one rope lay length**, OR
- **Visible reduction in rope diameter exceeding 10%**.

The sling shows only **2 broken outer wires in one strand**, which is **well within the rejection limit** of 4 per strand. ✓

**Step 5: Final Verdict**

The sling **passes the proof load test** and may be returned to service, with a recommendation to:
- Re-inspect in **3 months** instead of the standard 6 months, due to detected wire breakage.
- Log the test certificate in the equipment register.

### 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Sling Angle vs Leg Tension (3,000 kg load)

The following table is a **frequently asked 3-mark question** in KTU exams. It demonstrates the dramatic impact of sling angle on leg tension:

| Sling Angle (from Horizontal) | $\sin\theta$ | Leg Tension (kN) for 2-Leg Sling | % Increase Over Vertical Lift |
|-------------------------------|--------------|----------------------------------|-------------------------------|
| **90° (vertical)** | 1.000 | 14.72 | 0% (baseline) |
| **75°** | 0.966 | 15.23 | 3.5% |
| **60°** | 0.866 | 16.99 | 15.4% |
| **45°** | 0.707 | 20.81 | 41.4% |
| **30°** | 0.500 | 29.43 | 100.0% |
| **20°** | 0.342 | 43.03 | 192.3% |
| **10°** | 0.174 | 84.60 | 474.7% |
| **5°** | 0.087 | 169.20 | 1,049.4% |

> [!NOTE]
> **Observation:** As the sling angle decreases from 90° to 30°, the leg tension **doubles**. Below 30°, the tension becomes *catastrophically* high. This is why **sling angles below 30° from horizontal are strictly prohibited** in lifting operations.

### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Acceptance vs Rejection Thresholds

A **high-yield 7-mark question** in KTU ESE often asks students to tabulate rejection criteria. The standard table is:

| Component | Inspection Parameter | Acceptance Limit | Rejection Threshold | Reference |
|-----------|----------------------|------------------|----------------------|-----------|
| **Wire Rope** | Diameter reduction | $\leq 7\%$ | $\geq 10\%$ | IS 2266 |
| **Wire Rope** | Broken wires per lay length (6×19) | $\leq 6$ | $\geq 12$ | IS 2266 |
| **Wire Rope** | Broken wires per lay length (6×37) | $\leq 10$ | $\geq 22$ | IS 2266 |
| **Wire Rope** | Corrosion (pitting) | Surface only | Section loss visible | IS 2266 |
| **Chain Sling** | Elongation at any 5-pitch length | $\leq 3\%$ | $\geq 5\%$ | IS 2740 |
| **Chain Sling** | Gouge / wear depth | $\leq 5\%$ of stock | $\geq 10\%$ of stock | IS 2740 |
| **Shackle** | Pin wear | $\leq 2\%$ | $\geq 5\%$ | IS 2740 |
| **Hook** | Throat opening | $\leq 1.10 \times$ original | $\geq 1.15 \times$ original | IS 3181, OSHA |
| **Hook** | Twist / bend | $\leq 10°$ | $\geq 15°$ | IS 3181 |
| **Synthetic Sling** | Cuts, abrasions, melting | Minor surface | Through-the-thickness | ASME B30.9 |
| **D/d ratio** | Drum/sheave diameter | $\geq 18 d$ | $\leq 16 d$ | IS 2266 |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Master Flowchart: Strength & Working Load Inspection Cycle

The following **Mermaid block-level functional architecture** illustrates the complete inspection decision tree, mapping the iterative workflow from initial commissioning to final retirement of a lifting component.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[New Lifting Component] --> B{Initial Type Test by Manufacturer}
    B -- Pass --> C[Stamp WLL + Certificate of Test]
    B -- Fail --> D[Reject at Source]
    C --> E[Commissioning Inspection On-Site]
    E --> F{Visual + Dimensional Check}
    F -- Fail --> G[Quarantine + Investigate]
    F -- Pass --> H[Register in Lifting Gear Log]
    H --> I{Frequent Inspection: Daily/Monthly}
    I -- Pass --> J[Continue in Service]
    I -- Damage Detected --> G
    J --> K{Periodic Inspection: Quarterly}
    K -- Pass --> L{Proof Load Test if Required}
    K -- Defects --> G
    L --> M{Pass Proof Test}
    M -- Yes --> N[Recompute WLL with Wear Factor]
    M -- No --> G
    N --> O{Annual Thorough Examination}
    O -- Pass --> P[Certify for Next Annual Cycle]
    O -- Critical Defects --> Q[Permanent Retirement + Destruction]
    G --> R{Repair Feasible}
    R -- Yes --> S[Repair by Competent Agency]
    R -- No --> Q
    S --> L
    P --> I
```

### 4.2 Sling Geometry Schematic (Mermaid)

The following schematic depicts the geometric force resolution in a 2-leg (bridle) sling, with explicit angle and force vector annotations:

```mermaid
graph LR
    Load[(Load W = 3000 kg)]:::load
    Hook[Common Hook Point]:::hook
    Leg1[Left Sling Leg<br/>Tension T1<br/>Angle theta from horizontal]:::leg
    Leg2[Right Sling Leg<br/>Tension T2<br/>Angle theta from horizontal]:::leg
    Anchor1[Left Anchor Point]:::anchor
    Anchor2[Right Anchor Point]:::anchor
    Load --> Hook
    Hook --> Leg1
    Hook --> Leg2
    Leg1 --> Anchor1
    Leg2 --> Anchor2
    classDef load fill:#FFD700,stroke:#B8860B,stroke-width:2px
    classDef hook fill:#C0C0C0,stroke:#808080,stroke-width:2px
    classDef leg fill:#87CEEB,stroke:#4682B4,stroke-width:2px
    classDef anchor fill:#8B4513,stroke:#A0522D,stroke-width:2px
```

### 4.3 Decision Logic for Hook Inspection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    Start([Hook Under Inspection]) --> A1[Measure Throat Opening]
    A1 --> A2{Opening greater than 1.15 times original}
    A2 -- Yes --> Out1[REJECT Hook]
    A2 -- No --> B1[Check for Twist or Bend]
    B1 --> B2{Twist greater than 15 degrees}
    B2 -- Yes --> Out1
    B2 -- No --> C1[Inspect for Cracks via Dye Penetrant]
    C1 --> C2{Any Surface or Subsurface Crack Detected}
    C2 -- Yes --> Out1
    C2 -- No --> D1[Check Safety Latch Operation]
    D1 --> D2{Latch Functional and Spring-Loaded}
    D2 -- No --> Out2[Replace Latch or REJECT]
    D2 -- Yes --> D3[Measure Section Loss from Wear]
    D3 --> D4{Wear greater than 10 percent of original section}
    D4 -- Yes --> Out1
    D4 -- No --> Pass[APPROVE: Mark Inspection Date + Inspector ID]
    Out1 --> Action[Remove from Service<br/>Tag as Condemned<br/>Destroy or Return to Vendor]
    Out2 --> Action
    Pass --> Done[Return to Service]
    Action --> Done
```

### 4.4 Block-Level Functional Architecture: NDT Method Selection

When visual inspection is insufficient, the inspector must select an appropriate **Non-Destructive Testing (NDT)** method. The decision depends on the material, defect type, and accessibility:

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Defect Suspected] --> B{Material Type}
    B -- Ferromagnetic --> C[Magnetic Particle Testing MPT]
    B -- Any --> D[Dye Penetrant Testing DPT]
    B -- Any --> E[Ultrasonic Testing UT]
    B -- Wire Rope --> F[Electromagnetic Induction Rope Tester]
    C --> G[Detect Surface and Near-Surface Cracks]
    D --> G
    E --> H[Detect Internal Flaws and Thickness]
    F --> I[Detect Broken Wires and Loss of Metallic Area]
    G --> J[Mark Defect Location + Depth]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K{Criticality Assessment}
    K -- Minor --> L[Schedule Re-Inspection]
    K -- Major --> M[Immediate Removal from Service]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Mermaid block diagrams instead of physical drawings?**
> The KTU-PREMIER-ENGINE protocol mandates Mermaid-compliant diagrams to ensure all students can reproduce the figure in their answer scripts. Physical hand-drawn slings, hooks, or NDT schematics risk evaluator subjectivity, whereas block diagrams provide a clear, evaluable, and unambiguous representation of the inspection logic.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model]**

**Define the term "Working Load Limit (WLL)" of a lifting device. How is it related to the Minimum Breaking Load (MBL) of the device?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Working Load Limit (WLL)**, also known as the Safe Working Load (SWL), is defined as the **maximum load (mass or force) that a lifting device is authorized to support in service** under normal operating conditions, as established by the manufacturer and certified by a competent person.

**Relationship to MBL:**

$$WLL = \frac{MBL}{\text{Factor of Safety (FoS)}}$$

The MBL is the load at which the component fails under static tensile testing. By dividing the MBL by a numerical Factor of Safety (e.g., 4:1 for chain, 5:1 for wire rope), the WLL provides a **conservative operational ceiling** that absorbs uncertainties such as dynamic loading, wear, manufacturing variability, and human error.

*(Award 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for the formula, 1 mark for the qualitative explanation of FoS purpose.)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024 Model]**

**List any four rejection criteria for wire rope slings as per IS 2266.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The following criteria mandate immediate withdrawal of a wire rope sling from service:

1. **Diameter reduction exceeding 10%** of the nominal diameter measured between crowns.
2. **More than 12 broken wires in one rope lay length** (for 6×19 construction), or **more than 22 broken wires** (for 6×37 construction).
3. **Evidence of severe corrosion**, including pitting, section loss, or red-rust staining between strands.
4. **Birdcaging, kinking, popped core, or heat damage** (evidenced by discoloration).
5. *(any 4 of the above)*

*(Award 0.75 mark per correctly stated criterion, up to 3 marks.)*

---

### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — Option Set 1**

**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the procedure for conducting a **Proof Load Test** on a wire rope sling of MBL 400 kN and recommended WLL 80 kN. State the standards and acceptance criteria.

**(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **3-leg wire rope sling** is used to lift a steel plate weighing **4,500 kg**. Each leg is inclined at **30° from the horizontal**. Each leg has an MBL of **120 kN**.
- (i) Calculate the tension in each sling leg.
- (ii) Determine the actual Factor of Safety available.
- (iii) Comment on the safety of the lift.

**(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer (a) — Proof Load Test Procedure (7 marks):**

1. **Pre-Test Verification (1 mark):**
   - Confirm the sling has a valid manufacturer's certificate stating MBL, construction (e.g., 6×36), and WLL.
   - Visually inspect the sling for any pre-existing damage; reject if any defects are observed.
   - Record ambient temperature (proof load tests are typically conducted at 20 ± 5°C).
   - Identify the test load value using the formula $P_{proof} = 2.0 \times WLL = 2.0 \times 80 = 160 \text{ kN}$.

2. **Test Setup (1 mark):**
   - Rig the sling in a calibrated test bed (universal testing machine or dead-weight rig).
   - Attach calibrated load cells, dial gauges, or extensometers for force and elongation measurement.
   - Apply a **pre-load of 5–10% of the proof load** to remove slack and bed the system.

3. **Loading Procedure (2 marks):**
   - Gradually increase the load in increments of 10–20% of the proof load, with a pause at each stage to record elongation.
   - Reach the full proof load of **160 kN** within 3 to 5 minutes (per IS 2266).
   - **Hold the proof load for 2 to 5 minutes** (per IS 2266 / IS 3177), observing for any visible distress.

4. **Unloading and Measurement (2 marks):**
   - Gradually release the load, recording residual elongation at zero load.
   - Measure the **permanent set** as a percentage of original gauge length.
   - **Acceptance criteria:** Permanent set must be **less than 1.0%** of original length (per IS 2266). The sling must show **no visible cracks, necking, or strand separation**.

5. **Documentation and Certification (1 mark):**
   - Issue a **Proof Load Test Certificate** stating: date, test load, observed permanent set, inspector name, and unique sling ID.
   - Mark the sling with the inspection date and the next due date.
   - Reject and destroy any sling that fails the test (cut into pieces to prevent re-use).

---

**Model Answer (b) — 3-Leg Sling Calculation (7 marks):**

**(i) Tension in each sling leg (3 marks):**

Convert mass to weight using $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$:

$$W = 4500 \times 9.81 = 44{,}145 \text{ N} = 44.145 \text{ kN}$$

For a 3-leg sling inclined at $\theta = 30°$ from horizontal:

$$T_{leg} = \frac{W}{3 \sin\theta} = \frac{44.145}{3 \times \sin 30°} = \frac{44.145}{3 \times 0.5}$$

$$T_{leg} = \frac{44.145}{1.5} = 29.43 \text{ kN}$$

**(ii) Actual Factor of Safety (2 marks):**

$$\text{FoS}_{actual} = \frac{MBL_{leg}}{T_{leg}} = \frac{120}{29.43} = 4.08$$

**(iii) Safety Comment (2 marks):**

The statutory minimum Factor of Safety for wire rope slings is **5:1** (per IS 3177). Since the actual FoS of **4.08** is **less than 5.0**, the lift is **NOT SAFE** in its present configuration. Recommended corrective actions:
- Increase the sling angle to **at least 38.8°**, at which $\sin\theta$ rises sufficiently to bring FoS up to 5.0.
- OR use a sling with a higher MBL (minimum required MBL per leg = $5 \times 29.43 = 147.15$ kN).
- OR add a fourth sling leg to reduce per-leg tension to 22.07 kN, raising FoS to 5.44.

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — Option Set 2**

**[KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** Describe in detail the **different types of Non-Destructive Testing (NDT) methods** used in strength inspection of lifting equipment, with a clear note on which defects each method can detect.

**(7 marks)**

**(b)** A crane hook has a stamped WLL of **10 tonnes**. During annual inspection, the throat opening is found to have **stretched from 80 mm to 94 mm**, and a **crack of length 8 mm** is detected on the hook's curved section. Apply the **rejection criteria** and decide whether the hook should be condemned. Justify your answer with IS 3181 and OSHA references.

**(7 marks)**

---

**Model Answer (a) — NDT Methods (7 marks):**

**1. Visual Testing (VT) (1 mark):**
The most basic and widely used method. Inspectors examine the component with the naked eye, often aided by magnifiers, borescopes, or fiber-optic cameras. Detects: surface cracks, corrosion, deformation, missing markings, weld defects, and gross geometric errors. **Limitation:** Cannot detect subsurface flaws.

**2. Magnetic Particle Testing (MPT) (1.5 marks):**
The component is magnetized, and fine ferromagnetic particles are applied to the surface. Flux leakage at defect sites causes particle accumulation, forming a visible indication. Detects: **surface and near-surface cracks** in ferromagnetic materials (carbon steel, alloy steel hooks, chains). **Limitation:** Restricted to ferromagnetic materials; surface must be clean.

**3. Dye Penetrant Testing (DPT) (1.5 marks):**
A colored or fluorescent liquid penetrant is applied to the surface, allowed to dwell, and then wiped off. A developer is then applied, drawing out penetrant trapped in defects, forming a visible indication. Detects: **surface-breaking cracks, porosity, laps, and laps in welds**, applicable to **all non-porous materials** (ferrous and non-ferrous). **Limitation:** Surface defects only; cannot detect subsurface flaws.

**4. Ultrasonic Testing (UT) (1.5 marks):**
High-frequency sound waves (1 to 25 MHz) are transmitted into the material via a piezoelectric transducer. Reflections from internal flaws or back-wall are analyzed. Detects: **internal cracks, laminations, voids, thickness measurement, and corrosion mapping**. **Limitation:** Requires skilled operators; surface must be reasonably smooth; couplant required.

**5. Electromagnetic Induction (EMI) Rope Tester (1.5 marks):**
A specialized instrument for wire ropes. A magnetic field is established through the rope, and sensors detect leakage flux caused by **broken wires, corrosion, and section loss**. Detects: **localized faults (LF) and large-scale loss of metallic cross-sectional area (LMA)** in wire ropes. **Limitation:** Specific to wire ropes.

---

**Model Answer (b) — Hook Rejection Decision (7 marks):**

**Step 1: Compute Throat Opening Stretch (2 marks):**

$$\text{Stretch} = \frac{94 - 80}{80} \times 100\% = \frac{14}{80} \times 100\% = 17.5\%$$

**Step 2: Compare to Rejection Criteria (2 marks):**

Per **IS 3181** and **OSHA 1910.180 (Overhead and Gantry Cranes)**:
- Hook throat opening must be **rejected if stretched by more than 15%** of the original dimension.
- Actual stretch = 17.5% > 15% rejection threshold.
- **Verdict on criterion 1: REJECT (FAIL).**

**Step 3: Evaluate Crack Presence (2 marks):**

Per **IS 3181** and **ASME B30.10**:
- **Any visible crack on a load-bearing hook, regardless of length, is grounds for immediate rejection.**
- The 8 mm crack on the curved (high-stress) section of the hook is in a critical location.
- **Verdict on criterion 2: REJECT (FAIL).**

**Step 4: Final Decision and Documentation (1 mark):**

The hook **must be condemned and removed from service immediately**. The condemning officer should:
- Apply a **red "CONDEMNED" tag** to the hook.
- **Destroy the hook** by cutting through the shank (or return to manufacturer for scrap).
- Update the lifting gear register.
- Issue a replacement hook of equivalent or higher WLL, complete with manufacturer certificate and proof load test record.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Hook Inspection:**
> Students often make the following errors in KTU exams:
> 1. **Confusing the throat rejection threshold:** Some students write 5% or 10%; the correct values are **15% per IS 3181 / OSHA** and **10% per ASME B30.10**. Always read the question carefully for the reference standard.
> 2. **Calculating the stretch as a difference rather than a percentage:** $94 - 80 = 14$ mm is *not* the answer; the percentage elongation $(14/80 \times 100)$ is required.
> 3. **Overlooking the crack criterion:** Even if the throat opening were within tolerance, **a single visible crack is sufficient for rejection**. Never average multiple criteria.
> 4. **Forgetting destruction of condemned gear:** The examiner awards a mark for the proper disposal step. Condemned hooks must be physically destroyed to prevent re-use by unauthorized parties.

### 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

The following **rapid-revision checklist** consolidates the high-yield facts for this topic. Memorize and internalize these before any KTU examination on PESFT522.

- **WLL Definition:** WLL = MBL / FoS; WLL is the *maximum authorized* load, **not** the breaking load.
- **FoS by Equipment Type:** Chain = **4:1**, Wire Rope = **5:1**, Fibre Rope = **5:1**, Synthetic Webbing = **5:1**, Polyester Round-Sling = **5:1 to 7:1**.
- **Proof Load Factor:** Wire rope slings = **2.0× WLL**, Crane hooks = **1.25× WLL** (per IS 3181), Chain slings Grade T = **2.0× WLL**.
- **Sling Tension Formula:** $T = W / (n \sin\theta)$, where θ is **from horizontal** and n is the number of legs.
- **Minimum Sling Angle:** **30° from horizontal** (or 60° from vertical) is the industry-wide prohibition threshold.
- **Wire Rope Rejection:** $\geq 10\%$ diameter loss OR $\geq 12$ broken wires per lay (6×19) OR $\geq 22$ broken wires per lay (6×37).
- **Chain Rejection:** $\geq 5\%$ pitch elongation OR $\geq 10\%$ stock wear OR visible crack.
- **Hook Rejection (IS 3181 / OSHA):** Throat stretch $\geq 15\%$ OR twist $\geq 15°$ OR any visible crack OR worn section $\geq 10\%$.
- **D/d Ratio for Sheaves:** $\geq 18$ for general use, $\geq 30$ for high-cycle fatigue applications.
- **Inspection Frequency:** Initial → Frequent (Daily/Monthly) → Periodic (Quarterly) → Thorough (Annual).
- **g for Calculations:** Always use $g = 9.81 \text{ m/s}^2$ unless otherwise specified.
- **Three NDT Methods to Remember for KTU:** VT (surface), MPT (surface + near-surface, ferromagnetic only), DPT (surface, all metals), UT (subsurface + thickness), EMI (wire ropes).
- **Important IS Standards:** IS 2266 (Wire Rope), IS 3177 (Wire Rope Slings), IS 3181 (Hooks), IS 2740 (Chain Slings), IS 875 (Loading Standards), IS 807 (Cranes).
- **International References:** ASME B30.9 (Slings), ASME B30.10 (Hooks), OSHA 1910.180 (Cranes), OSHA 1910.184 (Slings).
- **Common Examiner Trap:** Forgetting unit conversions (mass → force using $g$).
- **Critical Safety Rule:** When in doubt, **always reject** the component — no KTU question ever rewards a reckless "continue in service" verdict.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Rope in Use
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT522 - SAFETY IN MATERIAL HANDLING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Rope in Use

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

A **rope in use** refers to a flexible tensile member (wire rope, fiber rope, or chain) that is actively employed in material-handling equipment such as cranes, hoists, elevators, winches, and slings for the purpose of lifting, lowering, suspending, or pulling loads. In the context of the KTU 2024 scheme and modern industrial safety standards, a rope is considered "in use" from the moment it is installed on the equipment until it is **condemned and discarded** based on documented wear, breakage, deformation, or corrosion criteria.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):**
> Rope in use is a critical safety component. Approximately **60% of all crane-related accidents** in material handling are attributable to rope failures caused by inadequate inspection, poor lubrication, overloading, or the use of condemned ropes beyond their discard criteria.

## 1.2 Classification of Ropes Used in Material Handling

Ropes in industrial material-handling applications are broadly classified into the following categories:

| S. No. | Type of Rope | Material of Construction | Typical Applications |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Wire Rope** | High-carbon steel wires | Cranes, hoists, elevators, draglines, suspension bridges |
| 2 | **Fiber Rope – Natural** | Manila (abaca), Sisal, Hemp | Light-duty slings, lashings, cargo handling |
| 3 | **Fiber Rope – Synthetic** | Nylon, Polyester, Polypropylene | Marine mooring, chemical plants, rescue operations |
| 4 | **Chain** | Alloy steel links | Heavy-duty slings, anchorage, towing |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy – The Human Tendon:**
> Think of a rope in a crane the same way a doctor thinks of a tendon in the human body. A tendon is the silent, invisible connector that bears the full weight of a muscle's pull — when it is healthy, you barely notice it; but when it frays, tears, or becomes inflamed, the entire limb fails. Likewise, a wire rope silently bears the entire load of a lifted object. A single broken wire is the equivalent of a "micro-tear" in a tendon — invisible at first, but a warning sign of catastrophic failure if ignored.

In a crane lifting a **5-tonne** steel beam, the wire rope (often just **20–40 mm** in diameter) holds the entire load. If you were to look at a single cross-section of that rope, you would see hundreds of tiny steel wires working in parallel — much like the muscle fibers in a tendon. Each wire carries a tiny share of the total load, but the rope as a whole can only be as strong as its **weakest, most corroded, or most abraded wire**.

## 1.4 Wire Rope Construction — Geometric Hierarchy

A wire rope is built in a strictly nested, hierarchical structure. From the smallest unit to the largest:

1. **Wire** — A single drawn steel filament (diameter typically **0.4 mm to 5.0 mm**).
2. **Strand** — A bundle of wires twisted together in a helical pattern. Common strand constructions:
   * **6×19** (6 strands × 19 wires) — General purpose, **flexible**.
   * **6×37** (6 strands × 37 wires) — More flexible, used on sheaves and drums.
   * **6×7** (6 strands × 7 wires) — Stiffer, used for guy ropes and standing ropes.
3. **Core** — The central element around which strands are laid. Two main types:
   * **Fiber Core (FC)** — Made of manila, sisal, jute, or polypropylene. Provides **cushioning, flexibility, and lubrication reservoir**.
   * **Steel Core (IWRC — Independent Wire Rope Core or WSC — Wire Strand Core)** — Provides **higher strength, resistance to crushing, and heat tolerance**.
4. **Rope** — The final assembled product: strands laid helically around the core.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Lay Classification:**
> * **Right Lay (Z-lay):** Strands run to the right when viewed from the end — most common.
> * **Left Lay (S-lay):** Strands run to the left.
> * **Ordinary Lay:** Direction of strand lay is **opposite** to the direction of wire lay within the strand. Resists rotation and abrasion.
> * **Lang's Lay:** Direction of strand lay is **same** as wire lay. Offers greater wear resistance on the crown of strands but tends to untwist under load.

## 1.5 Safety Factor — Definition

The **Factor of Safety (FoS)** is the ratio of the **Minimum Breaking Load (MBL)** of a rope to its **Safe Working Load (SWL)**.

$$\text{FoS} = \frac{\text{Minimum Breaking Load (MBL)}}{\text{Safe Working Load (SWL)}}$$

Typical KTU-recommended and IS-standard values:

| Application | Minimum Factor of Safety |
| :--- | :--- |
| Cranes and hoists (general) | **3.5** |
| Passenger elevators | **7.5 to 12.0** |
| Freight elevators | **5.0 to 7.0** |
| Mine hoists | **8.0 to 10.0** |
| Slings (wire rope) | **5.0** |
| Standing ropes (guy lines) | **3.5** |

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mechanics of a Wire Rope Under Load

A wire rope does **not** behave as a simple tension member. Because of its **helical geometry**, it is subjected to a **combination of axial tension, torsional moment, and bending stress** simultaneously. The key analytical relationships are as follows:

### 2.1.1 Effective Tensile Stress in a Helically Laid Rope

When a wire rope carries an axial load $F$, the load is shared among the wires. The tensile stress in each wire is amplified by the **helix angle** $\alpha$ of the wire:

$$\sigma_{\text{wire}} = \frac{F}{A_{\text{metallic}}} \cdot \frac{1}{\cos^{2}\alpha}$$

where:
* $F$ = applied axial load (N)
* $A_{\text{metallic}}$ = total metallic cross-sectional area of the rope (mm²)
* $\alpha$ = helix angle of the outer wires (typically $12^{\circ}$ to $18^{\circ}$ for standard lay)

### 2.1.2 Bending Stress Induced by Sheaves and Drums

When a rope passes over a sheave or is wound on a drum, each wire is subjected to **reversed bending**. The bending stress is given by:

$$\sigma_{b} = \frac{E \cdot d}{D}$$

where:
* $E$ = modulus of elasticity of the wire material ($\approx 2.0 \times 10^{5} \text{ N/mm}^2$ for steel)
* $d$ = diameter of an individual wire (mm)
* $D$ = diameter of the sheave or drum (mm)

> [!NOTE]
> **Why D/d Ratio Matters:**
> The KTU 2024 syllabus emphasizes the **D/d ratio** (sheave diameter ÷ rope diameter) as a critical design parameter. A ratio below **20:1** is considered unsafe for crane duty because it causes **excessive bending fatigue** and accelerates wire breakage on the crown of the strands.

### 2.1.3 Contact (Crossover) Stress

At the point where a wire crosses over another wire (either within a strand or against a sheave), extremely high **Hertzian contact stresses** develop. These stresses initiate micro-pitting and **fatigue cracks**, which propagate and eventually lead to wire breakage. This is why **inter-strand nicking** is a primary discard criterion.

## 2.2 The KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table consolidates every formula a KTU student must memorize for examination questions on "Rope in Use".

| S. No. | Concept | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | Factor of Safety | $\text{FoS} = \dfrac{\text{MBL}}{\text{SWL}}$ | MBL = Minimum Breaking Load, SWL = Safe Working Load | dimensionless |
| 2 | Safe Working Load | $\text{SWL} = \dfrac{\text{MBL}}{\text{FoS}}$ | — | N or kgf |
| 3 | Metallic Area | $A_{m} = \dfrac{\pi d^{2}}{4} \cdot n$ | $d$ = wire dia, $n$ = total number of wires | mm² |
| 4 | Rope Approx. Mass | $W = 0.0034 \, D^{2}$ | $D$ = rope diameter | kg/m |
| 5 | Rope Breaking Load (approx.) | $F_{u} = 0.36 \, D^{2}$ (for 6×19) | $D$ = rope diameter | kN (if D in mm) |
| 6 | Bending Stress | $\sigma_{b} = \dfrac{E \, d}{D}$ | $E$ = Young's modulus, $d$ = wire dia, $D$ = sheave dia | N/mm² |
| 7 | Combined Stress Check | $\sigma_{\text{total}} = \sigma_{t} + \sigma_{b} \leq \sigma_{\text{permissible}}$ | — | N/mm² |
| 8 | D/d Ratio (minimum) | $\geq 20:1$ (crane duty) | — | dimensionless |
| 9 | Wear (Area Loss) | $\%\text{Wear} = \dfrac{D_{\text{original}}^{2} - D_{\text{actual}}^{2}}{D_{\text{original}}^{2}} \times 100$ | — | % |
| 10 | Twist in Strands (Lang's vs Ordinary) | $\Delta T = 4 \pi^{2} R^{2} \cdot n \cdot \cos\alpha$ | $R$ = strand helix radius, $n$ = number of turns, $\alpha$ = helix angle | rad |

## 2.3 Real-World Utility in Engineering

| Industry | Application of Rope-in-Use Principles |
| :--- | :--- |
| **Construction** | Tower cranes; concrete bucket lifting; façade scaffolding tie-ropes |
| **Mining** | Shaft hoisting; dragline excavation; man-riding cages |
| **Ports & Shipping** | Container-handling cranes; ship-to-shore gantry cranes; mooring lines |
| **Power Generation** | Cooling tower lifts; turbine hall overhead cranes |
| **Oil & Gas** | Offshore platform cranes; drilling line inspection |
| **Manufacturing** | Overhead travelling cranes; ladle cranes in steel plants |

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Insight:**
> The strength of a wire rope is fundamentally determined by the **sum of the breaking loads of all its individual wires**, modified by a **loss factor** (typically **0.85 to 0.90**) to account for helical geometry, manufacturing defects, and end-termination efficiency.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivations

### 3.1.1 Derivation: Wire Rope Strength from First Principles

**Statement:** Derive an expression for the breaking load of a wire rope in terms of wire diameter, number of wires, and ultimate tensile strength of the wire material.

**Given Data (assume standard 6×19 rope):**
* Wire diameter, $d$ = **0.8 mm**
* Number of wires per strand = 19
* Number of strands = 6
* Ultimate Tensile Strength of wire, $\sigma_u$ = **1800 N/mm²**

**Step 1 — Cross-sectional area of a single wire**

$$A_{\text{wire}} = \frac{\pi d^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi (0.8)^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 0.64}{4} = 0.5027 \text{ mm}^{2}$$

**Step 2 — Total metallic cross-sectional area**

$$A_{\text{metallic}} = A_{\text{wire}} \times n = 0.5027 \times (19 \times 6) = 0.5027 \times 114 = 57.31 \text{ mm}^{2}$$

**Step 3 — Theoretical breaking load of all wires combined**

$$F_{\text{wires}} = A_{\text{metallic}} \times \sigma_u = 57.31 \times 1800 = 103{,}158 \text{ N} = 103.16 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the rope efficiency factor (k = 0.85)**

The helical geometry, twisting losses, and uneven load distribution reduce the actual breaking load to about 85% of the sum of individual wire strengths.

$$F_{\text{rope}} = F_{\text{wires}} \times k = 103.16 \times 0.85 = 87.69 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5 — Apply Factor of Safety (FoS = 5 for crane duty)**

$$\text{SWL} = \frac{F_{\text{rope}}}{\text{FoS}} = \frac{87.69}{5} = 17.54 \text{ kN} \approx 1.79 \text{ tonnes}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Interpretation:**
> Even though the individual wires have a combined capacity of over **10 tonnes**, the actual safe lifting capacity is only **~1.79 tonnes** — a clear illustration of why KTU mandates high Factors of Safety for rope-driven lifting equipment.

---

### 3.1.2 Derivation: Bending Fatigue Life of a Wire Rope

**Statement:** Estimate the bending stress developed in the outer wires of a 6×19 wire rope wound on a sheave, and determine the minimum sheave diameter to keep the stress below the fatigue limit.

**Given Data:**
* Rope diameter, $D_r$ = **24 mm**
* Outer wire diameter, $d$ = **1.6 mm** (for 6×19: $d \approx D_r/15$)
* Modulus of elasticity, $E$ = **2.0 × 10⁵ N/mm²**
* Allowable bending stress, $\sigma_{b,\text{allow}}$ = **600 N/mm²**

**Step 1 — Minimum D/d ratio from KTU standard**

$$\frac{D}{d} \geq 20 \quad \Rightarrow \quad D \geq 20 \times 1.6 = 32 \text{ mm}$$

But this is the **absolute minimum**. For **frequent lifting duty**, IS 3177 recommends:

$$\frac{D}{d} \geq 30 \quad \Rightarrow \quad D \geq 30 \times 1.6 = 48 \text{ mm}$$

**Step 2 — Bending stress at D = 50 mm sheave**

$$\sigma_{b} = \frac{E \cdot d}{D} = \frac{2.0 \times 10^{5} \times 1.6}{50} = \frac{320{,}000}{50} = 6{,}400 \text{ N/mm}^{2}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Critical Observation — Check Realism:**
> The calculated bending stress ($6400 \text{ N/mm}^2$) far exceeds the **ultimate tensile strength of the wire** ($\approx 1800 \text{ N/mm}^2$). This indicates that the simple $\sigma_b = E \cdot d / D$ equation is valid only when the wire remains in the **elastic regime AND the wire is free to slide** (no inter-wire friction). The corrected Hertzian analysis yields realistic values in the range **500–800 N/mm²**.

**Step 3 — Corrected bending stress using Hertzian contact theory (simplified)**

For a wire on a sheave, the corrected bending stress is:

$$\sigma_{b,\text{corrected}} \approx \frac{E \cdot d}{D} \cdot \frac{1}{1 + \frac{d}{D}} \approx \frac{E \cdot d}{D + d}$$

$$\sigma_{b,\text{corrected}} = \frac{2.0 \times 10^{5} \times 1.6}{50 + 1.6} = \frac{320{,}000}{51.6} = 6{,}202 \text{ N/mm}^{2}$$

> Even with the correction, the value is high. In practice, the **bending stress is much lower** because the rope **redistributes load across all wires**, and the **strands rotate slightly** to equalize strain. Real measured values in industrial ropes are in the range of **300–700 N/mm²** for D/d ≈ 30.

**Step 4 — Bending cycles to failure (S-N approach)**

Using the simplified Basquin's law for high-cycle fatigue of wire:

$$\sigma_{a} = \sigma'_{f} \cdot (2N_{f})^{b}$$

where:
* $\sigma_a$ = alternating bending stress
* $\sigma'_f$ = fatigue strength coefficient $\approx 0.6 \times \sigma_u = 1080 \text{ N/mm}^2$
* $b$ = fatigue strength exponent $\approx -0.10$ for steel wire
* $N_f$ = cycles to failure

Solving for $N_f$ when $\sigma_a = 600 \text{ N/mm}^2$:

$$600 = 1080 \cdot (2N_{f})^{-0.10}$$

$$(2N_{f})^{-0.10} = \frac{600}{1080} = 0.5556$$

$$(2N_{f})^{0.10} = \frac{1}{0.5556} = 1.800$$

$$\ln(2N_{f}) = \frac{\ln(1.800)}{0.10} = \frac{0.5878}{0.10} = 5.878$$

$$2N_{f} = e^{5.878} = 357.7$$

$$N_{f} = \frac{357.7}{2} \approx 179 \text{ cycles}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Engineering Takeaway:** A wire rope operating at the boundary of its fatigue limit will fail in approximately **179 bending cycles** (i.e., 179 passes over the sheave). This is why **frequent inspection** is mandated — most wire breaks accumulate invisibly inside the rope before any visible external damage appears.

---

### 3.1.3 Derivative: Percentage Reduction in Strength due to Diameter Loss (Wear)

**Statement:** A 24 mm wire rope shows a reduced diameter of 22 mm due to abrasion. Calculate the percentage loss in cross-sectional area and the corresponding reduction in breaking strength.

**Step 1 — Original area (proportional to D²)**

$$A_{\text{original}} \propto D_{\text{original}}^{2} = (24)^{2} = 576 \text{ units}$$

**Step 2 — Reduced area**

$$A_{\text{reduced}} \propto D_{\text{actual}}^{2} = (22)^{2} = 484 \text{ units}$$

**Step 3 — Percentage area loss**

$$\%\text{Loss} = \frac{576 - 484}{576} \times 100 = \frac{92}{576} \times 100 = 15.97\% \approx 16\%$$

**Step 4 — Strength reduction**

A **16% area loss** results in approximately a **16% reduction in breaking strength**. If the original MBL was **250 kN**, the new MBL is:

$$\text{MBL}_{\text{new}} = 250 \times \left(1 - \frac{16}{100}\right) = 250 \times 0.84 = 210 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 5 — Discard check against IS 9969 criteria**

| Reduction in Rope Diameter | Recommended Action |
| :--- | :--- |
| Up to **5%** | Continue in service; increase inspection frequency |
| **5% to 10%** | Critical zone; consider re-roping |
| **> 10%** | **IMMEDIATE CONDEMNATION** — rope must be discarded |

> [!WARNING]
> In our example, a **2 mm reduction** in a 24 mm rope represents **(8.3%)** diameter loss — this is in the **critical zone** and the rope should be re-evaluated for continued service. Note: the *diameter reduction percentage* is half the *area reduction percentage* because of the $D^2$ relationship.

---

## 3.2 Analytical Matrix — Discard Criteria Summary

The KTU 2024 scheme frequently asks students to identify **when a rope must be discarded**. The following matrix consolidates the international standard criteria (IS 9969, IS 3177, BS 6570, ISO 4309):

| S. No. | Defect Type | Description | Threshold for Condemnation |
| :---: | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| 1 | **Broken Wires (random)** | Random distribution of wire breaks | **10% of total wires in a rope lay length** |
| 2 | **Broken Wires (clustered)** | Multiple breaks in one strand within a small zone | **5 broken wires in one strand in one rope lay** |
| 3 | **Diameter Reduction** | Loss of rope diameter due to wear | **> 5% from nominal — critical; > 10% — condemn** |
| 4 | **Corrosion** | Visible rust, pitting, or etching | **Any visible deep pitting with broken wires — condemn** |
| 5 | **Bird-caging** | Strand displacement outward due to sudden unloading | **Any occurrence — condemn** |
| 6 | **Kinking** | Permanent deformation from improper handling | **Any visible kink — condemn** |
| 7 | **Crown Wear** | Abrasion of outer wire surface | **> 30% of wire diameter worn — condemn that strand zone** |
| 8 | **Heat Damage** | Discoloration from exposure to high temperature | **Any bronze/blue discoloration — condemn** |
| 9 | **Rope Stretch (Elongation)** | Permanent elongation beyond elastic limit | **> 8% for crane duty — condemn** |
| 10 | **Core Failure** | Fiber core rotted or steel core broken | **Any failure — condemn** |
| 11 | **D/d Ratio in Operation** | Sheave or drum diameter too small | **< 20:1 for crane duty — condemn the sheave, not rope** |

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
**"State and briefly explain any six discard criteria for a wire rope used in material-handling equipment."**

**Model Answer:**

The following are six key discard criteria as per IS 9969 and IS 3177:

1. **Broken Wires — Random Distribution:** When the number of broken wires exceeds **10%** of the total wires within one rope lay length, the rope must be condemned. Random breaks indicate progressive fatigue failure throughout the rope.

2. **Broken Wires — Clustered Distribution:** When **5 or more wires are broken in a single strand within a small zone** (one rope lay), the rope is condemned because clustered breaks indicate a localized stress concentration (typically over a sheave or drum).

3. **Reduction in Rope Diameter:** When the rope diameter is reduced by more than **10%** from the original nominal diameter due to wear, abrasion, or core failure, the rope is condemned. A 5–10% reduction puts the rope in the critical zone requiring re-evaluation.

4. **Severe Corrosion:** When deep pitting corrosion is visible on the outer wires, accompanied by any broken wires, the rope is condemned. Surface rust is acceptable but must be cleaned and lubricated.

5. **Bird-caging:** When the strands are displaced outward radially from the rope body, typically due to sudden release of a heavy load, the rope is permanently damaged and must be condemned.

6. **Kinking or Sharp Bends:** Any visible kink or sharp bend in the rope (caused by improper handling, dragging over edges, or improper spooling) creates a permanent weak point. Such a rope must be condemned immediately.

7. *(Bonus)* **Heat Damage:** Any bronze, blue, or black discoloration of the wires indicates exposure to temperatures above 400°C, which reduces the tensile strength of the steel. The rope is condemned.

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
**"Define Factor of Safety for a wire rope. State the minimum FoS recommended by KTU/IS standards for (i) a passenger elevator, (ii) a mine hoist, and (iii) a general-purpose crane."**

**Model Answer:**

The **Factor of Safety (FoS)** of a wire rope is defined as the ratio of the **Minimum Breaking Load (MBL)** of the rope to its **Safe Working Load (SWL)**:

$$\text{FoS} = \frac{\text{MBL}}{\text{SWL}}$$

| Application | Minimum Factor of Safety |
| :--- | :--- |
| (i) Passenger Elevator | **7.5 to 12.0** (typically **10.0**) |
| (ii) Mine Hoist (man-riding or mineral) | **8.0 to 10.0** |
| (iii) General Purpose Crane | **3.5 to 5.0** |

> [!NOTE]
> The high FoS for passenger elevators and mine hoists reflects the **catastrophic consequences of failure** involving human life. Material-handling cranes used in industrial settings (where failure primarily causes property damage and possible injury) operate at lower FoS values.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Scheme Rule:** Students must answer **ONE** of two internal-choice questions. Each question carries **14 marks**, divided into sub-parts (a) and (b) of **7 marks each**.

### Question Choice A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** With a neat sketch, describe the **construction of a 6×19 wire rope**. Explain the function of the **core**, and differentiate between **fiber core (FC)** and **steel core (IWRC/WSC)**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A **6×19 wire rope** has a diameter of **24 mm** and is made of wires with an ultimate tensile strength of **1800 N/mm²**. The wire diameter is **1.6 mm**. Calculate:
* (i) The total metallic cross-sectional area of the rope.
* (ii) The approximate breaking load of the rope, assuming a rope efficiency of 0.85.
* (iii) The safe working load if the crane operates with a factor of safety of **5.0**.
**(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question A

**Part (a) — Construction of 6×19 Wire Rope:**

A 6×19 wire rope consists of **6 strands laid helically around a central core**, with each strand containing **19 individual wires**.

**Construction Hierarchy (from smallest to largest):**

| Level | Component | Count in 6×19 | Function |
| :--- | :--- | :---: | :--- |
| 1 | Wire | 6 × 19 = **114 wires** | Carries the tensile load |
| 2 | Strand | **6** | Bundle of 19 wires twisted together |
| 3 | Core | **1** | Supports strands; provides cushioning/lubrication |

**Function of the Core:**
The core is the central element around which the outer strands are helically laid. It serves three primary functions:
1. **Maintain Strand Geometry** — keeps the strands in proper position and prevents them from collapsing inward.
2. **Cushioning** — absorbs the radial compressive forces between the strands and the sheave/drum, reducing inter-strand nicking.
3. **Lubrication Reservoir** — fiber cores are impregnated with lubricant that seeps out during operation to lubricate the inner wires.

**Comparison: Fiber Core (FC) vs. Steel Core (IWRC/WSC)**

| Property | Fiber Core (FC) | Steel Core (IWRC/WSC) |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Material | Manila, sisal, jute, or polypropylene | Independent wire rope or wire strand |
| Flexibility | **High** — more flexible rope | **Lower** — stiffer rope |
| Strength | Reduces overall rope strength by ~15% | Adds to rope strength |
| Heat Resistance | Poor — chars at high temperature | **Excellent** — survives high drum temperatures |
| Crushing Resistance | Low | **High** — preferred for multi-layer drum winding |
| Lubrication | Provides internal lubrication | Requires external lubrication |
| Typical Use | Single-layer drum, mobile cranes | Multi-layer drum, mine hoists, elevators |

> **Block Diagram Sketch of 6×19 Rope Cross-Section:**
> *(Refer to the Mermaid diagram in Section 4.1 above for the visual schematic.)*

---

**Part (b) — Numerical Calculation:**

**Given:**
* Rope designation: **6×19** → Total wires = 6 × 19 = **114 wires**
* Rope diameter, $D$ = **24 mm**
* Wire diameter, $d$ = **1.6 mm**
* Ultimate tensile strength, $\sigma_u$ = **1800 N/mm²**
* Rope efficiency factor, $k$ = **0.85**
* Factor of safety, $\text{FoS}$ = **5.0**

**(i) Total Metallic Cross-Sectional Area:**

Cross-sectional area of one wire:

$$A_{\text{wire}} = \frac{\pi d^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi (1.6)^{2}}{4} = \frac{\pi \times 2.56}{4} = 2.0106 \text{ mm}^{2}$$

Total metallic area for 114 wires:

$$A_{\text{metallic}} = 2.0106 \times 114 = 229.21 \text{ mm}^{2}$$

**(ii) Approximate Breaking Load:**

$$F_{\text{wires}} = A_{\text{metallic}} \times \sigma_u = 229.21 \times 1800 = 412{,}578 \text{ N} = 412.58 \text{ kN}$$

Apply rope efficiency factor:

$$F_{\text{rope}} = 412.58 \times 0.85 = 350.69 \text{ kN}$$

**(iii) Safe Working Load:**

$$\text{SWL} = \frac{F_{\text{rope}}}{\text{FoS}} = \frac{350.69}{5.0} = 70.14 \text{ kN}$$

Converting to tonnes (1 tonne-force ≈ 9.81 kN):

$$\text{SWL} = \frac{70.14}{9.81} = 7.15 \text{ tonnes}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Answer Summary (Part b):**
> * (i) Total metallic area = **229.21 mm²**
> * (ii) Breaking load = **350.69 kN** (≈ 35.7 tonnes)
> * (iii) Safe Working Load = **70.14 kN** (≈ **7.15 tonnes**)

---

### Question Choice B (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2, Alternative Choice]**

**(a)** Discuss the **routine inspection procedure** for a wire rope in service. List the items to be checked during:
* (i) Daily pre-shift inspection
* (ii) Weekly detailed inspection
* (iii) Quarterly comprehensive inspection
**(7 marks)**

**(b)** Two crane ropes are operated under identical loading conditions:
* Rope P: 6×19, 24 mm diameter, with **6 broken wires** randomly distributed in one rope lay length.
* Rope Q: 6×19, 24 mm diameter, with **6 broken wires** all in a single strand in one rope lay length.

Compare the discard status of Rope P and Rope Q. Justify with reference to IS 9969 / KTU standards. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution to Question B

**Part (a) — Routine Inspection Procedure:**

**(i) Daily Pre-Shift Inspection (Operator's Responsibility):**

Performed at the start of every shift by the crane operator before commencing work. The following items are checked:

1. **Visual Surface Check** — Look for any obvious damage, broken wires sticking out, cuts, or abrasions on the visible length of the rope.
2. **Lubrication Status** — Confirm the rope is adequately lubricated. A dry rope shows faster wear and reduced service life.
3. **Rope Reeving** — Verify that the rope is correctly seated in the sheave grooves and that the dead end is properly clamped.
4. **Kinks or Sharp Bends** — Visually scan the entire visible length for any kinks, hockles, or sharp bends.
5. **End Terminations** — Check that wire rope clips, sockets, or other terminations are tight and undamaged.
6. **Abnormal Noises** — Listen for any grinding or unusual sounds during a test lift, which may indicate sheave wear or rope damage.
7. **Hoist Limit Switch Test** — Verify that the hoist upper limit switch operates correctly to prevent over-winding.

**(ii) Weekly Detailed Inspection (Maintenance Personnel):**

Conducted by a trained maintenance technician using a checklist:
