---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** Define a *thimble* in rope fitting. State two reasons why a thimble is always recommended in a wire rope eye termination.

**Model Answer:**
A *thimble* is a grooved metallic (galvanized steel or stainless steel) insert placed inside a rope eye to:
1. **Prevent kinking and crushing** of the rope where it loops back on itself, by providing a smooth curved bearing surface that maintains the natural lay of the rope.
2. **Distribute the load** over a wider contact area (≥ 180°), reducing contact stress and improving fitting efficiency by approximately 15–25%.

It is classified as a *passive* fitting since it carries no load by itself but protects the rope at the termination point.

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** State the *saddle rule* for the installation of wire rope clips. What is the consequence of reversing it?

**Model Answer:**
**Saddle Rule:** In a wire rope clip termination, the **U-bolt** of the clip must be placed on the **dead end (short, non-load-carrying tail)** of the rope, and the **saddle (base plate)** must bear on the **live end (load-carrying portion)** of the rope.

**Consequence of Reversal:** Reversing the saddle rule concentrates crushing stress on the live end, disturbs the rope lay, and **reduces the termination efficiency from 80% to approximately 40–50%**, dramatically increasing the risk of rope slippage and catastrophic failure at the clip zone.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks with Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2]**

**(a)** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a **wedge socket** rope termination. List two advantages and two limitations. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 6 × 19 wire rope of diameter **24 mm** has a minimum breaking load of **360 kN**. The rope is terminated using a wedge socket of efficiency **80%** with a safety factor of **5:1** for general lifting service. Determine: **(i)** the Working Load Limit, **(ii)** the minimum number of wire rope clips (drop-forged) required if an alternative clip termination is used, and **(iii)** the correct spacing between clips. **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution to Question A

#### Part (a) — Wedge Socket Construction & Working (7 Marks)

A **wedge socket** is a forged steel body with a tapered internal cavity, a matching steel wedge, and a pin/bail for attachment. The wire rope is looped through the socket body, passed around the wedge, and the wedge is driven into the tapered socket using a proof load.

**Working Principle (Self-tightening action):**
When the rope is loaded in tension, the rope tension pulls the wedge deeper into the socket taper, increasing the gripping (normal) force on the rope. This is a *passive self-energizing* mechanism, although it is not perfectly self-locking (depends on friction).

**Key Features:**
- The **dead end** of the rope emerges from a small hole on the side.
- The **live end** exits through the main opening.
- A **bail (pin attachment)** is integrated at the top for shackle connection.

**Advantages:**
1. Quick field installation — no specialised swaging press required.
2. Re-usable — the wedge can be re-driven after inspection.

**Limitations:**
1. Lower efficiency (80–85%) compared to poured sockets (100%).
2. Sensitive to repeated shock loading; requires periodic re-torquing and re-wedging.

#### Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks)

**Given:** $d = 24$ mm, $F_b = 360$ kN, $\eta_{\text{wedge}} = 0.80$, $SF = 5$

**(i) Working Load Limit:**
$$F_{b,\text{term}} = \eta \times F_b = 0.80 \times 360 = 288 \text{ kN}$$
$$\text{WLL} = \frac{F_{b,\text{term}}}{\text{SF}} = \frac{288}{5} = \boxed{57.6 \text{ kN}}$$

**(ii) Number of clips for alternative termination (drop-forged):**
For $d = 24$ mm, we interpolate between 22 mm (4 clips) and 25 mm (5 clips) from the standard table. A **24 mm rope requires 4 drop-forged clips** (rounded up conservatively to 5 in field practice, but the minimum is 4).
$$\boxed{N_c = 4 \text{ to } 5 \text{ clips (drop forged)}}$$

**(iii) Clip spacing:**
$$S_c = 6d = 6 \times 24 = 144 \text{ mm} \approx \boxed{145 \text{ mm (5-3/4")}}$$

**Final Termination Length:**
$$L_{\text{term}} = S_c \times (N_c - 1) + 2 \times d = 144 \times 3 + 48 = \boxed{480 \text{ mm}}$$

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice
**[KTU University Exam – Model Paper 2024, Module 2 — Set B]**

**(a)** Explain the difference between a **D-shackle** and an **anchor (bow) shackle**. State the standard design rule for shackle pin diameter in relation to rope diameter. **(7 Marks)**

**(b)** A 32 mm 6 × 37 wire rope of breaking strength **620 kN** is connected to a load through a **D-shackle (WLL = 470 kN)**. Determine: **(i)** the WLL of the complete assembly, **(ii)** the minimum D/d ratio recommended for a sheave guiding this rope, and **(iii)** the bending stress induced in the outer wire at the sheave. Take $E = 1.1 \times 10^5$ N/mm². **(7 Marks)**

### Model Solution to Question B

#### Part (a) — D-Shackle vs Anchor Shackle (7 Marks)

| Feature | D-Shackle | Anchor (Bow) Shackle |
|---|---|---|
| **Body Shape** | "D"-shaped (straight sides) | "Oval/curved" (bow shape) |
| **Load Direction** | In-line (axial) only | Multi-directional (lateral loads OK) |
| **WLL for same pin dia** | Higher | Lower (typically 50–65% of D-shackle) |
| **Typical Use** | Straight lift, tie-down, tow | Multi-leg slings, side-pull |
| **Pin Type** | Screw pin / round pin | Safety bolt / round pin |

**Design Rule:** The shackle pin diameter should be **at least equal to the rope diameter** ($d_{\text{pin}} \geq d_{\text{rope}}$), and the **body cross-section must provide a WLL ≥ rope WLL** for the assembly to be balanced.

#### Part (b) — Numerical (7 Marks)

**Given:** $d = 32$ mm, $F_b = 620$ kN, $\text{WLL}_{\text{shackle}} = 470$ kN, $E = 1.1 \times 10^5$ N/mm², $SF = 5$

**(i) WLL of the assembly:**
Rope WLL = $F_b / SF = 620 / 5 = 124$ kN.
Shackle WLL = 470 kN.
The **rope governs** (weaker element).
$$\boxed{\text{WLL}_{\text{assembly}} = 124 \text{ kN}}$$

**(ii) Minimum D/d ratio for wire rope:**
$$D_{\min} = 20 \times d = 20 \times 32 = \boxed{640 \text{ mm}}$$

**(iii) Bending stress in the outer wire:**
$$\sigma_b = \frac{E \cdot d}{D} = \frac{1.1 \times 10^5 \times 32}{640} = \boxed{5500 \text{ N/mm}^2} = 5.5 \text{ kN/mm}^2$$
This is a theoretical peak stress; in design, the *equivalent bending stress* (with a stress concentration factor) is used and compared against the wire's endurance limit (~1200 N/mm²). The high value indicates the rope must be lubricated and sheave replaced if D drops below 20d.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Saddle Rule Reversal** — Inverting the clip orientation costs **2–3 marks** instantly in clip-installation questions.
> 2. **Efficiency Omission** — Students forget to multiply by η when computing WLL; this is a **3-mark deduction** in numericals.
> 3. **Safety Factor Confusion** — General lifting (5:1) vs. personnel lifting (10:1) is often mixed up — read the question carefully.
> 4. **Units Inconsistency** — Mixing mm with inches, kN with kgf — always include units in every intermediate step.
> 5. **Skipping the Spacing Step** — In clip problems, the spacing $S_c = 6d$ must be stated even if not asked.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Definition:** A rope fitting is a *load-transfer termination hardware* that connects a rope to a load, hook, or anchor.
- **Efficiency (η)** is the ratio of the *terminated* rope's breaking strength to the *free* rope's breaking strength, expressed in %.
- **Poured socket (100%) > Swaged (90–95%) > Mechanical splice (80–90%) > Wedge (80–85%) > Clips (75–80%) > Hand splice (75%).**
- **WLL formula:** $\text{WLL} = (\eta \times F_b)/\text{SF}$; SF = 5 for general, 10 for personnel.
- **Number of clips (drop-forged):** 3 for ≤ 13 mm, 4 for 19–22 mm, 5 for 25–29 mm, 6 for 32 mm.
- **Spacing of clips:** $S_c = 6d$, with a minimum of 3 inches (75 mm).
- **Saddle Rule:** U-bolt on **dead end**; saddle on **live end**. Reversal cuts efficiency by ~40%.
- **D/d ratio:** ≥ 20d for wire rope, ≥ 10d for fibre rope — this governs fatigue life.
- **Shackle pin diameter:** $d_{\text{pin}} \geq d_{\text{rope}}$.
- **Thimble use** improves efficiency by 15–25% and is mandatory in all wire rope eye terminations.
- **Inspection before every shift:** Look for cracks, deformation, wear, corrosion, and proper pin engagement.
- **Discard a fitting** if: wear > 10% of original dimension, cracks visible, threads stripped, or pin won't seat fully.
- **Galvanic corrosion warning:** Never mix zinc-coated fittings with stainless steel wire rope without isolation.
- **Proof loading:** All clip terminations must be loaded to 25% of WLL and re-torqued before service.
- **Tagging rule:** Every rope fitting in service must carry a colour-coded tag showing WLL, date of last inspection, and inspector ID.
- **Reeving rule:** A wire rope should never be reeved over a sheave of D < 20d under any circumstance.
- **Wedge socket** is *not* self-locking for all angles; verify $\tan α < μ$ or use proof-loading.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Inspection and Replacement - Slings
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT522 - SAFETY IN MATERIAL HANDLING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Inspection and Replacement - Slings

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Aligned)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Sling (Material Handling):** A sling is a flexible lifting accessory—fabricated from wire rope, alloy chain, metal mesh, fiber rope, or synthetic webbing—used in conjunction with a hoist or crane to attach a load to the lifting medium, distributing the load through single or multiple legs while maintaining a defined **Working Load Limit (WLL)** under prescribed sling angles.

In the context of KTU Module 2, **Inspection** refers to the systematic visual and tactile examination of slings at prescribed intervals to identify defects, degradation, or damage that may compromise lifting safety. **Replacement** refers to the formal withdrawal of a sling from service when it fails to meet the minimum acceptance criteria laid down by **ASME B30.9 (Slings)** and **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.184**.

### Classification of Slings (Per ASME B30.9)

| Sling Type | Construction Material | Typical WLL Range | Common Use |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Wire Rope Sling** | Steel wire rope (6×19, 6×37 IWRC) | 0.5 – 150 Tonnes | Heavy industry, shipyards |
| **Alloy Chain Sling** | Grade 80/100/120 alloy steel | 1 – 100 Tonnes | Foundries, steel plants |
| **Synthetic Web Sling** | Nylon / Polyester / Polypropylene | 1 – 50 Tonnes | Finished goods, delicate loads |
| **Wire Mesh Sling** | Interlocked steel wire mesh | 1 – 20 Tonnes | Hot slabs, sharp-edged loads |
| **Fiber Rope Sling** | Manila, sisal, polypropylene fiber | 0.5 – 5 Tonnes | Light duty, ship rigging |

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-world analogy:** Think of a sling like the **seatbelt of a heavy-lifting operation**. Just as a seatbelt has woven fibers that, when frayed, lose strength silently, a sling can appear intact on the outside while being dangerously weakened internally. Inspection is the "health check-up" of the sling—skipping it is like driving a car with worn-out brakes. The phrase *"a chain is only as strong as its weakest link"* applies to every leg, eye, or splice in a sling assembly.

### Key Physical Constants & Standards

- **Safety Factor (SF) for slings:** Typically **5:1** for wire rope, **4:1** for synthetic, and **4:1** for alloy chain (ratio of minimum breaking strength to WLL).
- **Standard governing inspection:** **ASME B30.9 – 2021** and **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.184**.
- **Design temperature limits:**
  - Nylon: **-40 °C to 90 °C**
  - Polyester: **-40 °C to 100 °C**
  - Polypropylene: **-40 °C to 80 °C**
- **Mandatory discard temperature for wire rope:** > **400 °C** exposure.

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 The Three-Tier Inspection Regime (ASME B30.9 / OSHA)

1. **Initial Inspection (Pre-Use):** Performed before the sling is placed into service for the very first time. Verifies certification, tagging, and absence of transit damage.
2. **Frequent Inspection (Each Shift / Daily):** Visual + tactile check by a *competent person* before each lifting operation. Looks for surface damage, missing tags, kinks, or obvious deformation.
3. **Periodic Inspection (Documented):** Conducted at intervals not exceeding **12 months** (or more frequently under severe service). Performed by a *qualified person* with full documentation.

> [!NOTE]
> A **"Competent Person"** is one who is trained to identify hazards and has authority to take corrective action. A **"Qualified Person"** possesses a recognized degree/certificate AND demonstrated knowledge of sling design, inspection, and failure modes.

### 2.2 Categories of Defects to Look For

- **Mechanical Damage:** Broken wires, cuts, abrasions, gouges, nicks, kinks, birdcaging.
- **Heat Damage:** Discoloration (blue/black), melted fibers, loss of galvanizing.
- **Chemical Damage:** Brittle or spongy texture, surface pitting, loss of dye color.
- **End Fitting Damage:** Worn eyes, bent hooks, corroded master links, missing/locked rings.
- **Dimensional Loss:** Reduction in rope diameter, link stretch, web wear, eye elongation.

### 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet (High-Yield)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains the core equations for sling tension, effective capacity, and derating due to sling angle. **Memorize these for ESE.**

| # | Quantity | Formula | Symbol Meaning | Units |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Vertical Leg Tension (Static) | $T_v = \dfrac{W}{N \cdot \sin\theta}$ | $W$ = load weight, $N$ = number of legs, $\theta$ = sling angle from horizontal | N or kgf |
| 2 | Horizontal Squeeze Force | $T_h = \dfrac{W}{N} \cdot \cot\theta$ | Component squeezing the load | N or kgf |
| 3 | Tension in One Leg | $T = \dfrac{W}{N \cdot \sin\theta}$ | Same as vertical tension per leg | N or kgf |
| 4 | Effective WLL (Multileg) | $\text{EWL} = \text{WLL} \times N \times \sin\theta$ | Reduced capacity at angle $\theta$ | Same as WLL |
| 5 | Mode Factor (Vertical Hitch) | $M = 1.0$ | Straight vertical pull | Dimensionless |
| 6 | Mode Factor (Choker Hitch) | $M = 0.75$ | Choke around load | Dimensionless |
| 7 | Mode Factor (Basket Hitch) | $M = 2.0$ | U-shaped cradle | Dimensionless |
| 8 | Mode Factor (Basket 90°) | $M = 1.4$ | Basket at 90° included | Dimensionless |
| 9 | Working Load Limit (Design) | $\text{WLL} = \dfrac{\text{Breaking Strength (BS)}}{\text{Safety Factor (SF)}}$ | Manufacturer's rated capacity | tonnes |
| 10 | D/d Ratio (Sheave/Pulley) | $\text{D/d} = \dfrac{D_{\text{sheave}}}{d_{\text{rope}}}$ | Minimum 20:1 for 6×19 rope | Dimensionless |
| 11 | Frequency Adjustment (Wire Rope) | $N_{bd} = \dfrac{n \cdot d}{L_h}$ | Broken wires per rope lay count | wires/lay |
| 12 | Diameter Reduction Limit | $\Delta d_{\max} = 0.05 \cdot d_{\text{nominal}}$ | Max 5 % reduction from nominal | mm |

### 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

In shipbuilding, container terminals, and power-plant construction, sling failure is one of the top three causes of fatal crane incidents (per OSHA statistics). A **10° reduction in sling angle** (from 90° to 80°) increases leg tension by **~15%**, which is why KTU examiners frequently test the ability to **derate capacity** for actual field geometry. The formula $\sin\theta$ is the critical "angle factor"—a 60° angle yields only **86.6%** of vertical capacity, while a 30° angle yields only **50%**, often making the lift unsafe.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Tension in a Sling Leg as a Function of Sling Angle

**Statement:** A two-leg sling lifts a load of weight $W$. Each leg makes an angle $\theta$ with the horizontal. Show that the tension in each leg is $T = \dfrac{W}{2\sin\theta}$, and hence the Effective Working Load is $\text{EWL} = \text{WLL} \times 2\sin\theta$.

**Step 1 — Free Body Setup:**
Consider a load in static equilibrium suspended by two symmetric sling legs. Each leg makes angle $\theta$ with the horizontal ceiling (i.e., $\theta$ measured upward from horizontal).

**Step 2 — Resolve Each Leg Tension:**
Each leg carries tension $T$ along its own axis. Resolving $T$ into components:
- Vertical (lifting) component: $T \sin\theta$
- Horizontal (squeeze) component: $T \cos\theta$

**Step 3 — Apply Vertical Equilibrium:**
The two vertical components together must support the full weight $W$.

$$
\sum F_y = 0 \quad\Rightarrow\quad 2 \cdot T \sin\theta - W = 0
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for Tension in One Leg:**

$$
T = \frac{W}{2 \sin\theta}
$$

> **Conversion logic:** Dividing by $2\sin\theta$ is required because only the *vertical* projection of $T$ does useful lifting work; the *horizontal* projection is wasted as a crushing force on the load.

**Step 5 — Effective Working Load (EWL):**
The WLL of the sling is its rated vertical single-leg capacity. For two legs, the maximum permitted vertical load $W_{\max}$ is reached when the *leg tension* equals the WLL of one leg:

$$
T = \text{WLL} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \frac{W_{\max}}{2\sin\theta} = \text{WLL}
$$

$$
\boxed{\;W_{\max} = 2 \cdot \text{WLL} \cdot \sin\theta = \text{EWL}\;}
$$

### 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (ESE-Style)

**Problem:** A two-leg wire rope sling has a single-leg WLL of **5 tonnes**. The load weighs **6 tonnes**. Determine whether the lift is safe at:
- (a) Sling angle $\theta = 90°$
- (b) Sling angle $\theta = 60°$
- (c) Sling angle $\theta = 30°$

**Solution:**

**(a) At $\theta = 90°$:**
$$
\text{EWL} = 2 \times 5 \times \sin 90° = 2 \times 5 \times 1.0 = 10 \text{ tonnes}
$$
Since $W = 6$ tonnes $<$ EWL $= 10$ tonnes ⇒ **SAFE**.

**(b) At $\theta = 60°$:**
$$
\text{EWL} = 2 \times 5 \times \sin 60° = 2 \times 5 \times 0.866 = 8.66 \text{ tonnes}
$$
Since $W = 6$ tonnes $<$ EWL $= 8.66$ tonnes ⇒ **SAFE**.

**(c) At $\theta = 30°$:**
$$
\text{EWL} = 2 \times 5 \times \sin 30° = 2 \times 5 \times 0.5 = 5.0 \text{ tonnes}
$$
Since $W = 6$ tonnes $>$ EWL $= 5.0$ tonnes ⇒ **UNSAFE — DO NOT LIFT**.

### 3.3 Analytical Removal Matrix (Wire Rope Slings — ASME B30.9)

| Defect Observed | Threshold for Removal | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Randomly distributed broken wires | **10 wires in one rope lay** (for 6×19) | Cumulative fatigue |
| Localized broken wires in one strand | **5 wires in one rope lay** | Concentrated stress |
| Reduction in rope diameter | **> 5 % of nominal** | Loss of metallic cross-section |
| Severe kinking or birdcaging | **Any occurrence** | Permanent plastic deformation |
| Heat damage (discoloration) | **> 400 °C exposure** | Loss of temper and tensile strength |
| Corrosion (pitting) | **Visible pitting with diameter loss** | Reduced fatigue life |
| Missing / illegible sling tag | **Any sling without tag** | Cannot verify WLL/serial |
| Crushed / flattened strands | **Any occurrence** | Loss of rope lay geometry |

> [!WARNING]
> **Common pitfall:** Students often confuse *randomly distributed* broken wires with *localized* broken wires. The limits are different—use the **stricter** limit when both are present.

### 3.4 Synthetic Web Sling Removal Criteria

| Defect | Removal Threshold |
|---|---|
| Melting or charring | Any visible melting/char |
| Cuts, tears, or snags | Exposing core yarns |
| Broken or missing stitches | More than 2 adjacent broken |
| Acid/alkali burns | Any visible chemical attack |
| Wear on cover | > 10 % of sling thickness |
| Knots (other than designed) | Any unintended knot |

### 3.5 Alloy Chain Sling Removal Criteria

| Defect | Removal Threshold |
|---|---|
| Stretch (elongation) | > 5 % of original length |
| Reduction in link diameter | > 10 % from nominal |
| Gouges, nicks, cracks | Any visible defect |
| Weld defects | Any crack in weld zone |
| Heat damage | Discoloration > 400 °C |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Flowchart: Sling Inspection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Sling Ready for Use?] --> B{Tag Present and Legible?}
    B -- No --> C[REMOVE FROM SERVICE - Reject]
    B -- Yes --> D{Pre-Use Visual Check}
    D --> E{Surface Defects?}
    E -- Kinking / Birdcaging --> C
    E -- Broken Wires > Limit --> C
    E -- Heat / Chemical Damage --> C
    E -- Diameter Loss > 5%] --> C
    E -- None Detected --> F{Correct Sling Type and WLL for Load?}
    F -- No --> G[Select Correct Sling]
    F -- Yes --> H{Check Sling Angle theta}
    H --> I{theta Greater than or Equal 30 deg}
    I -- No --> J[LIFT UNSAFE - Increase Angle]
    I -- Yes --> K{Compute EWL and Compare with W}
    K -- EWL Less than W --> L[LIFT UNSAFE - Down-Rate or Add Leg]
    K -- EWL Greater or Equal W --> M[PROCEED WITH LIFT - Log in Register]
    M --> N[Schedule Periodic Inspection Every 12 Months]
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Block Diagram: Functional Architecture of Sling Selection and Inspection

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph INPUT[INPUT PARAMETERS]
        A1[Load Weight W]
        A2[Load Geometry and Lift Points]
        A3[Sling Type and WLL]
        A4[Sling Angle theta]
    end
    subgraph COMPUTE[CALCULATION ENGINE]
        B1[Compute Leg Tension T = W slash N sin theta]
        B2[Compute EWL = WLL times N sin theta]
        B3[Compute Safety Margin = EWL minus W]
    end
    subgraph INSPECTION[INSPECTION SUBSYSTEM]
        C1[Visual - Surface Defects]
        C2[Tactile - Deformation]
        C3[Dimensional - Diameter and Length]
        C4[Documentation - Tag and Register]
    end
    subgraph OUTPUT[DECISION OUTPUT]
        D1[SAFE TO LIFT]
        D2[REJECT - REPLACE SLING]
        D3[RE-DESIGN LIFT PLAN]
    end
    INPUT --> COMPUTE
    COMPUTE --> OUTPUT
    INSPECTION --> OUTPUT
    C1 --> C2
    C2 --> C3
    C3 --> C4
    C4 --> D1
    C4 --> D2
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Schematic: Sling Geometry and Force Components

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P[Hook Point - Top]
    L[Load Center - Bottom]
    LH[Left Sling Leg - angle theta]
    RH[Right Sling Leg - angle theta]
    FV[Vertical Reaction 2T sin theta = W]
    FH[Horizontal Squeeze 2T cos theta]
    P --- LH
    P --- RH
    LH --- L
    RH --- L
    L --- FV
    L --- FH
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual cue for students:** When the sling angle $\theta$ approaches $0°$ (legs become nearly horizontal), $\sin\theta \to 0$ and tension $T \to \infty$. This is the *catastrophic* failure mode if a rigger slings a load at an extremely flat angle—the slings can snap before the load ever leaves the ground.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]**
*Define the term "Working Load Limit" of a sling. How is it related to the Safety Factor?*

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
The **Working Load Limit (WLL)** is the maximum load that a sling is authorized to lift in a *specific* hitch configuration (vertical, choker, basket) under ideal service conditions. It is related to the **Safety Factor (SF)** by the formula:
$$
\text{WLL} = \dfrac{\text{Minimum Breaking Strength (BS)}}{\text{Safety Factor (SF)}}
$$
For wire rope slings, SF is typically **5:1**, meaning a sling with breaking strength 25 tonnes has a WLL of 5 tonnes. WLL assumes a **vertical hitch** unless otherwise specified, and must be derated for other configurations.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*List any six (6) criteria for immediate removal of a wire rope sling from service as per ASME B30.9.*

**Model Answer (3 marks — ½ mark per point):**
1. More than **10 randomly distributed broken wires** in one rope lay (for 6×19 construction).
2. More than **5 broken wires in one strand** in one rope lay.
3. **Reduction in rope diameter** exceeding 5 % of nominal.
4. Evidence of **kinking, crushing, or birdcaging**.
5. **Heat damage** (discoloration indicating exposure > 400 °C).
6. **Severe pitting or corrosion** with measurable loss of section.
7. Missing, illegible, or altered **manufacturer's identification tag**.

---

### PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each)

> [!NOTE]
> **Internal Choice Structure (Per KTU 2024 ESE Pattern):** Both Question A and Question B are full-length 14-mark questions with sub-parts (a) = 7 marks and (b) = 7 marks. A student must answer EITHER A or B.

---

### ❑ QUESTION A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – July 2024]*

**Q. A (a) [7 marks]:** With the help of a neat sketch, explain the various types of slings used in material handling. State the typical applications and WLL ranges for each.

**Model Solution:**

**Types of Slings:**

1. **Wire Rope Slings** (Single-leg, Multi-leg, Bridle, Grommet): Made from 6×19 or 6×37 wire rope with IWRC. WLL range 0.5 – 150 tonnes. *Application:* Heavy industry, shipbuilding, structural steel erection.

2. **Alloy Chain Slings** (Grade 80/100/120): Welded link chain. WLL 1 – 100 tonnes. *Application:* Foundries, steel mills, abrasive environments.

3. **Synthetic Web Slings** (Nylon, Polyester, Polypropylene): Flat woven or round-sling. WLL 1 – 50 tonnes. *Application:* Finished goods, painted surfaces, delicate machinery.

4. **Wire Mesh Slings:** Interlocked steel wire mesh forming a flexible tube. WLL 1 – 20 tonnes. *Application:* Hot steel slabs, sharp-edged plates.

5. **Fiber Rope Slings** (Manila, Sisal, PP): Natural/synthetic fibers. WLL 0.5 – 5 tonnes. *Application:* Light duty, marine rigging.

**Sketch description (to be drawn by student):** Show 4 sling configurations — vertical hitch, choker hitch, basket hitch, and 2-leg bridle sling — labeled with angle θ and load W.

**(Marks distribution: 5 types × 1 mark each = 5 marks; applications = 1 mark; sketch = 1 mark.)**

---

**Q. A (b) [7 marks]:** A four-leg wire rope sling has a single-leg WLL of 8 tonnes. A 25-tonne load is to be lifted. Determine the minimum sling angle θ for a safe lift. What happens if the angle is reduced to 30°?

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Effective Working Load formula for 4 legs:**

$$
\text{EWL} = 4 \cdot \text{WLL} \cdot \sin\theta
$$

**Step 2 — Set EWL ≥ W for safe lift:**

$$
4 \times 8 \times \sin\theta \geq 25
$$

**Step 3 — Solve for sin θ:**

$$
\sin\theta \geq \frac{25}{32} = 0.78125
$$

**Step 4 — Determine angle:**

$$
\theta \geq \sin^{-1}(0.78125) = 51.32°
$$

**∴ Minimum safe sling angle ≈ 51.3°** (1 mark for setup, 2 marks for solving)

**Step 5 — At θ = 30°:**

$$
\text{EWL} = 4 \times 8 \times \sin 30° = 32 \times 0.5 = 16 \text{ tonnes}
$$

Since 16 tonnes < 25 tonnes, the lift is **UNSAFE**. The riggers must either increase θ above 51.3°, use a higher-rated sling, or distribute the load across additional lifting points. (3 marks for verification + recommendation)

---

### ❑ QUESTION B (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]*

**Q. B (a) [7 marks]:** Describe in detail the inspection regime for slings as recommended by ASME B30.9. Differentiate between a "competent person" and a "qualified person."

**Model Solution:**

**Three-Tier Inspection Regime:**

| Inspection Type | Frequency | Conducted By | Scope |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Initial** | Before first use | Qualified Person | Verify certification, tag, transit damage |
| **Frequent** | Each shift / daily | Competent Person | Surface defects, tags, hitch correctness |
| **Periodic** | ≤ 12 months | Qualified Person | Full documented check, measurements, NDT if required |

**Competent vs. Qualified Person:**

- **Competent Person:** Trained to identify *existing and predictable* hazards; has *authority* to take immediate corrective action (e.g., remove sling from service). Does not design slings.
- **Qualified Person:** Holds a *recognized degree or certificate* in engineering/inspection AND has *demonstrated knowledge* of sling design, materials, failure modes, and applicable codes. Can design, specify, and certify.

**(Marks: 4 marks for inspection table; 2 marks for definitions; 1 mark for severity-based frequency.)**

---

**Q. B (b) [7 marks]:** A wire rope sling shows 8 randomly distributed broken wires in one rope lay. The nominal rope diameter is 24 mm. The current measured diameter at the worst point is 22.7 mm. The sling tag is missing. As a Safety Officer, what is your decision? Justify with reference to ASME B30.9.

**Model Solution:**

**Evaluation Against ASME B30.9 Criteria:**

| Criterion | Observation | Threshold | Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| Randomly distributed broken wires | 8 wires in one lay | > 10 (for 6×19) | **PASS** (within limit) |
| Localized broken wires | Not specified | > 5 in one strand | Cannot judge |
| Diameter reduction | 22.7 / 24 = 0.9458 → 5.42% loss | > 5% | **FAIL** |
| Sling tag | Missing | Required | **FAIL** |

**Step 1 — Diameter check:**

$$
\Delta d = \frac{24 - 22.7}{24} \times 100\% = 5.42\%
$$

Since **5.42% > 5.0%**, the rope has exceeded the allowable diameter reduction.

**Step 2 — Tag check:**
ASME B30.9 mandates that each sling carry a permanent identification tag showing rated loads for vertical, choker, and basket hitches. A missing tag is grounds for immediate **removal from service**.

**Step 3 — Final Decision:**

> [!WARNING]
> **Decision: REMOVE FROM SERVICE IMMEDIATELY (RED-TAG & QUARANTINE).**

**Justification:** The sling fails on **two independent criteria** (diameter loss and missing tag). It is impossible to verify its WLL or history, and the diameter loss indicates cumulative fatigue. Per OSHA 29 CFR 1910.184(e)(3), damaged slings shall not be used. The sling must be destroyed (cut into pieces) and replaced.

**(Marks: 2 marks diameter calc; 2 marks tag rationale; 2 marks decision; 1 mark destruction/disposal.)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **🔴 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Common Pitfall:**
> 1. **Sling angle misinterpretation:** θ is measured from **horizontal** (not vertical). At 90° from horizontal, the sling is *vertical*. Many students invert this and lose 2–3 marks.
> 2. **Confusing WLL with Breaking Strength:** WLL is *not* the breaking load. Forgetting to divide by Safety Factor (5 for wire rope) is a frequent mistake.
> 3. **Mode factors in EWL:** A choker hitch has mode factor **0.75**, basket is **2.0**, basket at 90° is **1.4**. Forgetting to apply the mode factor is a 2-mark loss.
> 4. **Forgetting to express angle in radians** for some calculator functions — use the **sin⁻¹** form for clarity.
> 5. **Answering without a sketch** in Q.A(a) costs 1 mark; always include a labeled diagram.

---

### 📌 TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

- ✅ **Sling** = flexible lifting accessory classified by **construction (wire, chain, web, mesh, fiber)**.
- ✅ **Three inspections** = Initial, Frequent (each shift), Periodic (≤ 12 months).
- ✅ **Competent Person** = authority to act; **Qualified Person** = technical expertise.
- ✅ **Core formula:** $T = \dfrac{W}{N \sin\theta}$ and $\text{EWL} = \text{WLL} \times N \times \sin\theta$.
- ✅ **Mode factors:** Vertical = 1.0; Choker = 0.75; Basket = 2.0; Basket 90° = 1.4.
- ✅ **Wire rope removal limits:** 10 random broken wires/lay OR 5 localized in one strand; diameter loss > 5%; any kink.
- ✅ **Synthetic web removal:** any melting, char, exposure of core, > 10% wear.
- ✅ **Chain sling removal:** stretch > 5%; link diameter loss > 10%.
- ✅ **Critical safety rule:** Sling angle θ must always be **≥ 30°** from horizontal; below this the lift is unsafe.
- ✅ **Tagging is mandatory** — slings without legible ID tags are immediately rejected.
- ✅ **Destruction of rejected slings** (cut/disabled) is required to prevent accidental reuse.
- ✅ **Reference standards:** ASME B30.9 (latest revision), OSHA 29 CFR 1910.184, IS 2422 (Indian Standard for wire rope slings).
- ✅ **Real-world tip:** Always use a sling angle indicator (inclinometer) on field rigs to verify θ in practice.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT522 - SAFETY IN MATERIAL HANDLING
> - **Module:** Module 2
> - **Topic:** Types

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Definition of Material Handling Equipment Types

**Material Handling (MH)** is the movement, protection, storage, and control of materials and products throughout the process of manufacturing, distribution, consumption, and disposal. **Material Handling Equipment (MHE)** refers to the diverse range of mechanical, hydraulic, pneumatic, and automated systems designed to perform these functions safely and efficiently.

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, MHE is broadly classified into **five major categories** based on the function and movement pattern:

1. **Transport Equipment** — moves materials between locations (trucks, conveyors, AGVs)
2. **Positioning Equipment** — places materials precisely at a point (cranes, hoists, jibs)
3. **Unit Load Formation Equipment** — groups items into single loads (pallets, skids)
4. **Storage Equipment** — holds materials between operations (racks, bins, silos)
5. **Identification & Control Equipment** — tracks and manages material flow (barcodes, sensors)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Module 2 specifically emphasizes the **classification, working principles, and suitability** of cranes, hoists, conveyors, and industrial trucks. Students must be able to identify, compare, and select the correct equipment for a given industrial scenario.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy: The Human Body of a Factory

Imagine a factory is a **living human body**:
- **Bones** = Fixed storage racks and silos (give the body its shape).
- **Muscles** = Cranes and hoists (generate vertical movement/lifting force).
- **Arteries & Veins** = Conveyors (continuously flow material like blood).
- **Hands** = Industrial trucks, forklifts, and pallet jacks (precisely pick, carry, and place).
- **Nervous System** = Sensors, scanners, and control systems (coordinate everything).

Just as a body cannot function if one organ is missing, a factory's productivity is crippled without the proper combination of MHE types. **Safety in selection** is critical — using the wrong "muscle" for a heavy load (e.g., a manual hoist when an electric crane is needed) leads to catastrophic failure.

## 1.3 Primary Classification Principle

The most widely used engineering classification is based on the **Type of Motion and Path of Travel**:

| Motion Pattern | Path Type | Equipment Examples |
|---|---|---|
| Linear, Horizontal | Fixed path | Belt conveyors, roller conveyors, chain conveyors |
| Linear, Horizontal | Variable path | Forklifts, pallet trucks, AGVs |
| Linear, Vertical | Fixed path | Hoists, elevators, lift tables |
| Combined (3D) | Fixed path | Bridge cranes, gantry cranes, stacker cranes |
| Combined (3D) | Variable path | Mobile cranes, truck-mounted cranes |
| Continuous Flow | Fixed loop | Pneumatic conveyors, screw conveyors |

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Engineering Metric:** A material handling system's effectiveness is quantified by the **Unit Load Concept** — combining multiple items into a single load for one movement, reducing handling cost by up to **80%** compared to piece-by-piece handling.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Detailed Classification of Material Handling Equipment

### A. Cranes (Positioning + Heavy Lift Equipment)

**Cranes** are machines equipped with a **hoisting mechanism**, mounted on a structural frame, used to lift, lower, and move loads horizontally. They are the workhorses of heavy industry.

#### 1. Bridge Cranes (EOT – Electric Overhead Travelling)
- Consist of a **bridge** spanning the bay, a **trolley** travelling along the bridge, and a **hoist** suspended from the trolley.
- Move in **3 axes**: bridge travel (X), trolley travel (Y), hoist lift (Z).
- **Capacity range:** 1 tonne to 500+ tonnes.
- **Safety devices:** Overload limit switches, anti-collision buffers, emergency stop, travel limit switches.

#### 2. Gantry Cranes
- Similar to bridge cranes, but the bridge is supported by **legs running on rails at floor level** instead of an overhead runway.
- Used in **outdoor yards, shipyards, container terminals** where overhead runway is impractical.
- Lower installation cost, but limited by ground rail alignment.

#### 3. Jib Cranes
- Consist of a **horizontal jib (arm)** mounted on a vertical mast or wall.
- Provide **semicircular coverage** in a workstation.
- **Sub-types:** Wall-mounted jib, Free-standing pillar jib, Articulating jib, Mast-type jib.
- **Typical capacity:** 0.5 to 10 tonnes.
- **Safety feature:** Most have **360° slewing** with mechanical stops.

#### 4. Mobile Cranes
- **Truck-mounted (hydraulic)** — most common; telescopic boom.
- **Rough terrain** — for uneven construction sites.
- **Crawler-mounted** — heavy lift, low ground pressure.
- **All-terrain** — combines road mobility with off-road capability.
- **Pick-and-carry (industrial)** — short-distance load movement inside factories.

#### 5. Tower Cranes
- **Fixed-base** or **rail-mounted** vertical towers with horizontal jib.
- Used in **high-rise construction**.
- Heights: 40 m to 100+ m; jib length up to 80 m.
- **Safety systems:** Slewing limiter, load moment indicator (LMI), wind speed anemometer.

### B. Hoists (Vertical Lift Devices)

Hoists are **mechanical devices** that lift loads vertically, usually suspended from a fixed point or a trolley. They can be **manual, electrical, pneumatic, or hydraulic**.

#### 1. Manual Chain Hoists (Chain Blocks)
- Operated by pulling a hand chain.
- **Capacity:** 0.5 to 50 tonnes.
- Use **spur gear** or **spiral bevel gear** mechanism.
- **Factor of Safety:** minimum **4:1** for chain and **5:1** for hook.

#### 2. Electric Wire Rope Hoists
- **Most common in industry.**
- Capacity: 0.5 to 100+ tonnes.
- Mounted on a fixed point, monorail, or crane trolley.
- **Dual speed** available (slow for precision, fast for production).
- Safety: **Upper and lower limit switches**, overload cutout, thermal protection.

#### 3. Electric Chain Hoists
- Use calibrated load chain instead of wire rope.
- Suitable for **clean environments** (pharma, food).
- Capacity: 0.125 to 5 tonnes.
- Lighter, smaller headroom than wire rope hoists.

#### 4. Pneumatic / Air Hoists
- **Explosion-proof** — ideal for **hazardous atmospheres** (petroleum, paint, solvent plants).
- Operate on compressed air.
- **Continuous duty rating** — can run for long periods without overheating.

#### 5. Hydraulic Hoists
- Use fluid pressure to lift.
- Used where **slow, controlled, very high forces** are required (lifting heavy machinery for maintenance).
- Capacity: 2 to 100+ tonnes.

### C. Conveyors (Continuous Transport Equipment)

Conveyors move materials continuously between two points along a fixed path. They are the **highest-volume, lowest-cost-per-tonne** handling systems.

#### 1. Belt Conveyors
- A continuous **rubber or PVC belt** supported by idlers, driven by a head pulley.
- **Capacity range:** 50 to 10,000+ tonnes/hour.
- **Belt width:** 300 mm to 2400 mm.
- **Components:** Head pulley, tail pulley, idlers, take-up device, drive unit, belt scrapers.
- **Safety devices:** Belt sway switches, pull-cord emergency stop, belt rip detection, zero-speed switches.

#### 2. Roller Conveyors
- Use **gravity** (gravity roller) or **motorized drive** (powered roller) to move loads.
- **Suitable for:** Cartons, boxes, pallets with flat bottoms.
- **Limitations:** Cannot handle loose materials, soft bags.

#### 3. Chain Conveyors
- **Slat conveyors** — slats attached to chain; for heavy, hot, or sharp items.
- **Apron conveyors** — overlapping pans; for bulk materials.
- **Drag conveyors** — flights pull material in an enclosed trough.
- **Trolley conveyors** — overhead chain with hooks; for garment, paint, assembly lines.

#### 4. Screw Conveyors
- A **rotating helical screw** (auger) inside a trough or tube.
- Used for **bulk solids** (grain, cement, flour, plastic pellets).
- **Capacity:** 10 to 500 m³/hour.
- **Limitation:** Not for sticky, fibrous, or large-lump materials.

#### 5. Pneumatic Conveyors
- Move materials through pipes using **air or gas flow**.
- Two types: **Dilute phase** (high velocity) and **Dense phase** (low velocity, high pressure).
- Used for fine powders, granules, food grains, plastic pellets.
- **Closed system** — dust-free, hygienic.

#### 6. Bucket Elevators
- **Vertical** lifting of bulk materials using buckets attached to a belt or chain.
- **Capacity:** 10 to 800 m³/hour.
- **Spillage and dust control** are key safety issues.

### D. Industrial Trucks (Variable Path Transport)

#### 1. Forklift Trucks
- **Counterbalanced** — most common; load balanced by rear weight.
- **Reach trucks** — extendable mast for narrow aisles.
- **Order pickers** — operator rises with load for picking.
- **Pallet jacks** — manual or electric, low-lift.
- **Safety:** Overhead guard, load backrest, horn, reversing alarm, seat belt, capacity plate.

#### 2. Automated Guided Vehicles (AGVs)
- **Driverless** vehicles following a guide path (wire, magnetic tape, laser).
- Used in **warehouses, automotive assembly**.
- Modern variants use **LIDAR + SLAM** (no physical guide path).

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following formulas are **must-know** for KTU ESE and internal assessments:

| # | Parameter | Formula | Description |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Vertical Sling Tension** | $T_v = W$ | $W$ = load weight, straight vertical lift |
| 2 | **Sling Tension at Angle θ** | $T = \dfrac{W}{n \cos\theta}$ | $n$ = number of sling legs, $\theta$ = angle from vertical |
| 3 | **Sling Length (L) for horizontal** | $L = \dfrac{S}{2 \sin\theta}$ | $S$ = sling spread (horizontal distance) |
| 4 | **Allowable Load on Sling** | $P_{allow} = \dfrac{P_{rated}}{n}$ | where $n$ = number of legs |
| 5 | **Hook Stress (Tensile)** | $\sigma_t = \dfrac{4W}{\pi d^2}$ | $d$ = root diameter of hook shank |
| 6 | **Hook Shear Stress** | $\tau = \dfrac{W}{\pi d \cdot t}$ | $t$ = hook thickness |
| 7 | **Wire Rope Factor of Safety** | $FoS = \dfrac{Breaking\ Load}{Working\ Load}$ | Typically **5:1** for personnel lifts, **3.5:1** for materials |
| 8 | **Diameter of Wire Rope (d)** | $d = \sqrt{\dfrac{W}{K \cdot FoS}}$ | $K$ = rope efficiency factor (≈ 0.45 for 6×37) |
| 9 | **Drum Diameter (D)** | $D \geq 30 \cdot d$ to $45 \cdot d$ | $d$ = rope diameter (IS 3177 / IS 3938) |
| 10 | **Power to Drive Hoist (P)** | $P = \dfrac{W \cdot v}{\eta}$ | $v$ = lift velocity, $\eta$ = overall efficiency |
| 11 | **Belt Conveyor Belt Tension** | $T_{max} = T_1 + T_2$ | $T_1$ = tight side, $T_2$ = slack side |
| 12 | **Belt Conveyor Power** | $P = \dfrac{(T_1 - T_2) \cdot v}{1000}$ kW | $v$ = belt speed in m/s |
| 13 | **Screw Conveyor Capacity (Q)** | $Q = 60 \cdot \dfrac{\pi D^2}{4} \cdot s \cdot n \cdot \psi \cdot \gamma$ | $D$ = screw dia, $s$ = pitch, $n$ = rpm, $\psi$ = filling factor, $\gamma$ = density |
| 14 | **Forklift Stability (Forward Tip)** | $W \cdot L = C \cdot a$ | $L$ = load center, $C$ = counterweight, $a$ = wheelbase |
| 15 | **Crane Lifting Capacity (P)** | $P = \dfrac{M_{max}}{R_{min}}$ | $M_{max}$ = max moment, $R_{min}$ = min radius |
| 16 | **Wind Pressure on Crane** | $F = 0.5 \cdot \rho \cdot C_d \cdot A \cdot v^2$ | $C_d$ = drag coefficient, $A$ = projected area |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorization Tip:** For KTU exams, focus on formulas **1, 2, 3, 5, 7, 9, 10, 12, 14, 15** — these appear in over 90% of past paper numericals. Always convert units to SI (N, m, kg, m/s) before calculation.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Automotive assembly:** Overhead bridge cranes lift engine blocks (5–20 tonnes) along the assembly line, paired with AGVs transporting dashboards.
- **Ports & Shipping:** Gantry cranes (STS – Ship-To-Shore) load/unload containers at 30 moves/hour.
- **Warehousing (Amazon, Flipkart):** Roller conveyors + belt conveyors + AGVs form a fully automated fulfillment grid.
- **Power plants:** Bucket elevators feed coal into boilers; pneumatic conveyors move fly ash.
- **Construction:** Tower cranes lift steel, concrete buckets, and formwork to heights of 80+ meters.
- **Food industry:** Stainless steel pneumatic conveyors and electric chain hoists meet hygiene (FDA, FSSAI) requirements.

> [!NOTE]
> **Safety Statistic:** According to OSHA, **~25% of all industrial fatalities** involve cranes, hoists, and forklifts. Understanding equipment types, their load limits, and inspection criteria is therefore a **life-skill**, not just an exam topic.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Sling Tension with Multi-Leg Configuration

This is the **most frequently asked numerical** in KTU Module 2. Let us derive the governing equation from first principles.

### Given:
- A load of weight $W$ to be lifted using a sling with $n$ legs.
- Each leg makes an angle $\theta$ with the **vertical** (sometimes defined from horizontal — be careful, KTU usually takes angle from **vertical**).

### Step 1: Force Equilibrium of the Load

For the load to be in static equilibrium, the **sum of vertical components** of all sling tensions must equal the load weight.

$$
\sum F_{vertical} = 0
$$

### Step 2: Express Vertical Component of Each Sling

Each sling leg has tension $T$. Its vertical component is:

$$
T_v = T \cos\theta
$$

### Step 3: Equate to Load Weight

For $n$ identical sling legs:

$$
n \cdot T \cos\theta = W
$$

### Step 4: Solve for Sling Tension

$$
T = \dfrac{W}{n \cos\theta}
$$

### Step 5: Special Case Validation

**Case A — Single vertical sling ($\theta = 0°$):**

$$
T = \dfrac{W}{1 \cdot \cos 0°} = \dfrac{W}{1} = W \quad \text{(correct)}
$$

**Case B — Two-leg sling at 60° from vertical:**

$$
T = \dfrac{W}{2 \cos 60°} = \dfrac{W}{2 \cdot 0.5} = W
$$

> Notice: Tension in each leg **equals** $W$ at this angle! This is the **critical angle** beyond which the sling is overloaded for the same load.

**Case C — Two-leg sling at 30° from vertical (slings spread wider):**

$$
T = \dfrac{W}{2 \cos 30°} = \dfrac{W}{2 \cdot 0.866} = \dfrac{W}{1.732} = 0.577\,W
$$

**Case D — Two-leg sling at 15° from vertical (slings nearly horizontal):**

$$
T = \dfrac{W}{2 \cos 15°} = \dfrac{W}{2 \cdot 0.966} = \dfrac{W}{1.932} = 0.518\,W
$$

> **Surprising result:** As slings become more horizontal, tension in each leg *decreases* — but this is a **trap**! The horizontal component causes **enormous compressive crushing force on the load**. The slings can also **snap inward**, swinging the load violently.

### Step 6: Final Compact Form (with load factor)

For KTU exams, the final form typically includes a **load factor / safety factor**:

$$
T_{design} = \dfrac{W \cdot FoS}{n \cos\theta}
$$

---

## 3.2 Worked Numerical Example (KTU Pattern)

### Problem:
A steel plate weighing **$W = 2000$ kg** is to be lifted by a **4-leg wire rope sling**. Each leg makes an angle of **30° with the vertical**. Calculate:
1. Tension in each leg.
2. Resultant compressive (squeezing) force on the plate.
3. If the wire rope has a working load limit (WLL) of **$650$ kg per leg**, find the factor of safety.
4. State whether the lift is safe.

### Given:
- $W = 2000$ kg
- $n = 4$ legs
- $\theta = 30°$ from vertical
- WLL per leg = $650$ kg

### Solution:

**Part (i): Tension in each leg**

$$
T = \dfrac{W}{n \cos\theta} = \dfrac{2000}{4 \cdot \cos 30°} = \dfrac{2000}{4 \cdot 0.866} = \dfrac{2000}{3.464}
$$

$$
T = 577.4 \text{ kg per leg}
$$

**Part (ii): Compressive (Squeezing) Force on Load**

The horizontal component of each sling pulls the load inward:

$$
T_{horizontal} = T \sin\theta = 577.4 \cdot \sin 30° = 577.4 \cdot 0.5 = 288.7 \text{ kg per leg}
$$

Total inward compressive force (all 4 legs acting inward):

$$
F_{squeeze} = n \cdot T \cdot \sin\theta = 4 \cdot 577.4 \cdot 0.5 = 1154.8 \text{ kg}
$$

**Part (iii): Factor of Safety**

$$
FoS = \dfrac{WLL}{T} = \dfrac{650}{577.4} = 1.126
$$

**Part (iv): Safety Verdict**

> [!WARNING]
> **The lift is UNSAFE.** The required Factor of Safety for material handling per **IS 3177 / OSHA** is **minimum 3.5:1** (for material lifts). A FoS of 1.126 indicates the sling will operate **near its breaking load**, risking catastrophic failure.
>
> **Remediation:** Either (a) increase the sling angle to make $\cos\theta$ larger, OR (b) use a higher-rated sling (WLL ≥ 2021 kg per leg).

---

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Selection of Equipment Type

| Selection Criteria | Crane (EOT) | Hoist (Electric) | Belt Conveyor | Forklift | AGV |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Load Capacity** | 1–500+ t | 0.1–100 t | 50–10,000 tph | 0.5–45 t | 0.5–10 t |
| **Path** | Fixed 3D | Fixed vertical | Fixed linear | Variable | Programmable |
| **Flexibility** | Low | Low | Very Low | High | Very High |
| **Capital Cost** | Very High | Low–Medium | Medium | Medium | Very High |
| **Operating Cost** | Medium | Low | Very Low | High (manpower) | Low |
| **Skill Required** | Trained operator | Trained operator | Low | Licensed driver | Programmer |
| **Best Suited For** | Heavy fixed-route industry | Workshop lifting | Bulk material flow | Mixed warehouse | Modern automated warehouse |
| **Key Safety Concern** | Overturning, collapse | Hook failure, limit switch | Belt tear, entrapment | Tipping, pedestrian strike | Path deviation, sensor failure |

---

## 3.4 Derivation: Power Required by a Hoisting Mechanism

The **power** required to lift a load at a given speed through a system of efficiency $\eta$ is:

### Step 1: Work Done per Second
Work done per second (Power input required at the load point) is:

$$
P_{out} = W \cdot v
$$

Where:
- $W$ = weight in Newtons
- $v$ = lifting velocity in m/s

### Step 2: Account for System Losses
Mechanical losses occur in gears, sheaves, and bearings. These are lumped into an **overall efficiency** $\eta$ (typically 0.6 to 0.85 for a hoist):

$$
P_{in} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{\eta} = \dfrac{W \cdot v}{\eta}
$$

### Step 3: Express in kW
Divide by 1000 for kW:

$$
P_{in} \text{ (kW)} = \dfrac{W \cdot v}{1000 \cdot \eta}
$$

**Verification with numbers:** Lift 5000 kg at 5 m/min, $\eta = 0.8$:

$$
P = \dfrac{5000 \cdot 9.81 \cdot (5/60)}{1000 \cdot 0.8} = \dfrac{4905 \cdot 0.0833}{800} \approx 0.51 \text{ kW}
$$

Equivalent to a small electric motor — checks out.

## 4.2 Crane Sub-System Architecture (Block Diagram)

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph POWER["POWER SUPPLY"]
        PS["3-Phase AC Supply"]:::pwr
    end

    subgraph DRIVE["DRIVE SYSTEM"]
        M["Electric Motor"]:::drv
        GB["Gearbox Reducer"]:::drv
        BR["Brake"]:::drv
    end

    subgraph MECH["MECHANICAL TRANSMISSION"]
        DR["Drum"]:::mech
        WR["Wire Rope"]:::mech
        SH["Sheaves and Pulleys"]:::mech
    end

    subgraph HOOK["LIFTING ASSEMBLY"]
        HK["Hook Block"]:::hk
        LD["Load"]:::ld
    end

    subgraph SAFETY["SAFETY SYSTEMS"]
        OLS["Overload Limit Switch"]:::sf
        LLS["Upper and Lower Limit Switch"]:::sf
        EMO["Emergency Stop"]:::sf
        LMI["Load Moment Indicator"]:::sf
    end

    PS --> M
    M --> GB
    GB --> DR
    DR --> WR
    WR --> SH
    SH --> HK
    HK --> LD
    BR -.-> M
    OLS -.-> HK
    LLS -.-> SH
    EMO -.-> M
    LMI -.-> GB

    classDef pwr fill:#fee2e2,stroke:#991b1b,color:#000
    classDef drv fill:#dbeafe,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#000
    classDef mech fill:#d1fae5,stroke:#065f46,color:#000
    classDef hk fill:#fde68a,stroke:#92400e,color:#000
    classDef ld fill:#fbcfe8,stroke:#9d174d,color:#000
    classDef sf fill:#fecaca,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#000
```

## 4.3 Conveyor Operational Flow

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Material Inlet"]:::in --> B["Loading Chute"]:::in
    B --> C["Belt or Chain Drive"]:::drv
    C --> D["Idler Support System"]:::mech
    D --> E["Discharge Point"]:::out
    E --> F["Next Process Stage"]:::out
    G["Take-Up Tensioner"]:::saf --> D
    H["Belt Scraper"]:::saf --> E
    I["Emergency Pull Cord"]:::saf --> C

    classDef in fill:#e0e7ff,stroke:#3730a3
    classDef drv fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e

    classDef out fill:#fce7f3,stroke:#9d174d

```

## 4.4 Forklift Stability Triangle (Schematic)

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph STAB["FORKLIFT STABILITY TRIANGLE"]
        FL["Front Left Wheel"]:::wheel
        FR["Front Right Wheel"]:::wheel
        RL["Rear Axle Pivot"]:::wheel
        CG["Combined CG of Load and Truck"]:::cg
    end

    FL --- FR
    FR --- RL
    RL --- FL
    FL --- CG
    FR --- CG
    RL --- CG

    H1["Rule: CG must always remain INSIDE triangle"]:::note

    classDef wheel fill:#bfdbfe,stroke:#1e3a8a,color:#000
    classDef cg fill:#fca5a5,stroke:#7f1d1d,color:#000
    classDef note fill:#fef3c7,stroke:#92400e,color:#000
```
