# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023, May 2024]**
Classify material handling equipment. List any **four** types of industrial trucks with one application each.

**Model Answer:**

Material handling equipment is classified into **five categories**:
1. **Transport Equipment** — moves materials between locations.
2. **Positioning Equipment** — places materials at a precise point.
3. **Unit Load Formation Equipment** — combines items into one load.
4. **Storage Equipment** — holds materials between operations.
5. **Identification & Control Equipment** — tracks material flow.

**Four types of industrial trucks with applications:**

| # | Type | Application |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Forklift Truck (Counterbalanced)** | Lifting and transporting palletized loads in warehouses |
| 2 | **Reach Truck** | Order picking in narrow-aisle racking systems |
| 3 | **Pallet Jack (Electric)** | Horizontal movement of pallets in distribution centers |
| 4 | **Order Picker** | Multi-level picking in high-bay warehouses |

---

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - May 2023, Model Paper 2024]**
Differentiate between an **EOT crane** and a **gantry crane**. State **two** safety devices mandatory on both.

**Model Answer:**

| Parameter | EOT (Electric Overhead Travelling) Crane | Gantry Crane |
|---|---|---|
| **Support Structure** | Bridge runs on **overhead runway rails** mounted on building columns | Bridge is supported by **legs running on ground-level rails** |
| **Installation** | Requires strong building structure; high capital cost | Can be used **outdoors**; lower building modification cost |
| **Coverage** | Full bay width × length of building | Limited to gantry span; can extend to open yards |
| **Application** | Machine shops, assembly halls, steel plants | Shipyards, container terminals, stockyards |

**Common mandatory safety devices (both):**
1. **Overload Limit Switch / Load Moment Indicator (LMI)** — cuts off hoisting if load exceeds rated capacity.
2. **Upper and Lower Travel Limit Switches** — prevent over-hoisting and over-lowering the hook.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** Explain in detail the **classification of cranes** with neat sketches. Discuss the working principle and applications of **bridge cranes** and **tower cranes**. State the **safety devices** used in each. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** A steel drum weighing **$W = 1500$ kg** is to be lifted using a **3-leg wire rope sling**. Each leg makes an angle of **$45°$ with the vertical**. Calculate:
(i) Tension in each sling leg.
(ii) Horizontal compressive force on the drum.
(iii) Required factor of safety if each leg has a WLL of **$700$ kg**.
(iv) Comment on the safety of the operation. **[7 marks]**

---

### Model Solution for Question A

#### Part (a) — Classification of Cranes and Bridge/Tower Crane Detail

**Classification of Cranes:**

1. **Overhead Travelling Cranes**
   - Bridge Cranes (EOT) — single girder and double girder
   - Gantry Cranes
2. **Rotating Cranes**
   - Jib Cranes (wall, pillar, articulating)
   - Mobile Cranes (truck-mounted, crawler, rough terrain, all-terrain)
   - Tower Cranes
3. **Special Purpose Cranes**
   - Container handling cranes (STS, RTG, RMG)
   - Loader cranes
   - Marine cranes (deck, offshore)

**Bridge Crane (EOT) — Working Principle and Application:**

A bridge crane consists of:
- **Bridge girders** spanning the bay, supported on end-carriages running on overhead runway rails.
- **Trolley** that travels along the bridge carrying the hoist.
- **Hoist** with hook for vertical lifting.
- **Three motors** control: bridge travel, trolley travel, and hoisting.

The crane operator controls all three motions via a **pendant push-button station** or **radio remote**.

**Applications:** Heavy machinery workshops, steel mills, power plants, assembly lines for medium-to-heavy components (5–500 tonnes).

**Safety Devices in Bridge Crane:**
- Overload limit switch
- Upper and lower hoist limit switches
- Bridge and trolley travel limit switches
- Anti-collision devices (when multiple cranes on same runway)
- Emergency stop
- Warning horn and lights
- Buffer springs at end of travel

**Tower Crane — Working Principle and Application:**

A tower crane has:
- A **vertical mast (tower)** of lattice or tubular construction.
- A **horizontal jib** at the top with a counter-jib carrying counterweights.
- A **slewing unit** that rotates the jib 360°.
- A **hoist winch** in the slewing platform.
- A **climbing frame** for self-erection.

The operator sits in a **cab at the top of the mast** or controls from the ground.

**Applications:** High-rise building construction, large infrastructure projects (bridges, dams), industrial chimney erection.

**Safety Devices in Tower Crane:**
- **Load Moment Indicator (LMI)** — prevents overturning by limiting load × radius.
- **Slewing limiter** — restricts rotation to safe range.
- **Hoist travel limiter**
- **Anemometer** — stops crane in winds above 50 km/h.
- **Anti-two-block device** — prevents hook from contacting the jib.
- **Out-of-plumb indicator** — warns if mast is not vertical.

---

#### Part (b) — Sling Tension Numerical

**Given:**
- $W = 1500$ kg
- $n = 3$ legs
- $\theta = 45°$ from vertical
- WLL per leg = $700$ kg

**(i) Tension in each sling leg:**

$$
T = \dfrac{W}{n \cos\theta} = \dfrac{1500}{3 \cdot \cos 45°} = \dfrac{1500}{3 \cdot 0.7071} = \dfrac{1500}{2.1213}
$$

$$
T = 707.1 \text{ kg per leg}
$$

**(ii) Horizontal compressive force on the drum:**

$$
F_H = n \cdot T \cdot \sin\theta = 3 \cdot 707.1 \cdot \sin 45° = 3 \cdot 707.1 \cdot 0.7071
$$

$$
F_H = 1500 \text{ kg (compressive)}
$$

**Insight:** The total inward squeeze is numerically equal to the load weight — this is because at 45°, the load splits equally between vertical lift and horizontal compression.

**(iii) Factor of Safety:**

$$
FoS = \dfrac{WLL}{T} = \dfrac{700}{707.1} = 0.99
$$

**(iv) Safety Comment:**

> [!WARNING]
> The lift is **UNSAFE** — the Factor of Safety is **0.99**, meaning the **tension exceeds the working load limit** of the sling. The sling is on the verge of failure. **Recommended action:** Use a sling with WLL of at least **$2100$ kg per leg** (to achieve FoS of 3:1) OR reduce the sling angle to make the lift more vertical, thereby reducing $T$.

---

### Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice)
**[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model Paper]**

**(a)** With a neat diagram, explain the construction and working of a **belt conveyor**. Derive the equation for the **power required to drive the belt conveyor**. State **five safety devices** used. **[7 marks]**

**(b)** An **electric hoist** lifts a load of **$W = 3000$ kg** at a speed of **$6$ m/min**. The overall efficiency of the hoist is **$80\%$**. Calculate:
(i) The output power (at the hook).
(ii) The input power (motor rating).
(iii) If the wire rope diameter is **$16$ mm**, calculate the **minimum drum diameter** as per IS code.
(iv) State the factor of safety used in your calculation. **[7 marks]**

---

### Model Solution for Question B

#### Part (a) — Belt Conveyor Detail

**Construction of a Belt Conveyor:**

A belt conveyor consists of:
- **Belt** — endless loop of rubber/PVC, the carrying medium.
- **Head Pulley** — driving pulley at the discharge end.
- **Tail Pulley** — idler pulley at the loading end.
- **Idlers** — rollers supporting the belt and load (carrying and return).
- **Take-up Device** — maintains belt tension (gravity, screw, or hydraulic).
- **Drive Unit** — motor + gearbox + coupling.
- **Loading Chute** — directs material onto the belt.
- **Belt Cleaners / Scrapers** — remove carry-back material.
- **Structure** — stringers, galleries, walkways.

**Working Principle:**
The motor drives the **head pulley** via a gearbox. Friction between the pulley and the belt makes the belt move. The load is loaded at the **tail end** via the chute, carried on the top belt, and discharged at the **head end** over the pulley.

**Power Required — Derivation:**

Consider a belt conveyor with:
- Tight side tension = $T_1$
- Slack side tension = $T_2$
- Belt speed = $v$ (m/s)

The net driving force on the belt is:

$$
F = T_1 - T_2
$$

The power transmitted to the belt is the work done per second:

$$
P = F \cdot v = (T_1 - T_2) \cdot v \quad \text{(in Watts)}
$$

Converting to **kW**:

$$
P = \dfrac{(T_1 - T_2) \cdot v}{1000} \quad \text{kW}
$$

Including the **efficiency** of the drive system $\eta$:

$$
P_{motor} = \dfrac{(T_1 - T_2) \cdot v}{1000 \cdot \eta} \quad \text{kW}
$$

**Five Safety Devices in Belt Conveyors:**
1. **Belt Sway Switches** — trip the belt if it drifts off-center.
2. **Pull-cord Emergency Stop** — accessible along entire belt length.
3. **Belt Rip Detection Sensor** — detects longitudinal tears.
4. **Zero-Speed Switch** — trips if belt stops under load (chute jam).
5. **Belt Slip Detection** — alarms if belt slips on the drive pulley.

---

#### Part (b) — Hoist Numerical

**Given:**
- $W = 3000$ kg
- $v = 6$ m/min $= 0.1$ m/s
- $\eta = 0.80$
- $d_{rope} = 16$ mm

**(i) Output Power (at the hook):**

$$
P_{out} = W \cdot g \cdot v = 3000 \cdot 9.81 \cdot 0.1 = 2943 \text{ W} = 2.943 \text{ kW}
$$

**(ii) Input Power (Motor Rating):**

$$
P_{in} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{\eta} = \dfrac{2.943}{0.80} = 3.679 \text{ kW}
$$

**Practical choice:** A standard **3.7 kW or 5 HP** motor would be selected.

**(iii) Minimum Drum Diameter (IS 3177):**

For a **wire rope** in a hoisting application, IS 3177 specifies:

$$
D_{drum} \geq 30 \cdot d \quad \text{to} \quad 45 \cdot d
$$

Using the conservative lower bound for **frequent duty**:

$$
D_{drum} = 30 \cdot 16 = 480 \text{ mm}
$$

Using the **upper bound** for **severe duty** (frequent starting, heavy loads):

$$
D_{drum} = 45 \cdot 16 = 720 \text{ mm}
$$

**Adopt:** $D_{drum} = 480$ mm for standard industrial use; **720 mm** for continuous heavy duty.

**(iv) Factor of Safety:**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per **IS 3177 / FEM 1.001**, the recommended Factor of Safety for wire rope on hoists is:
> - **3.5:1** for general material handling
> - **5:1 to 6:1** for personnel lifts and crane applications
>
> In our calculation, a 30× drum-to-rope ratio implicitly assumes a **standard 5:1 Factor of Safety** on rope breaking strength.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
>
> 1. **Confusing the angle reference** — In sling problems, $\theta$ may be from vertical *or* horizontal. KTU consistently uses **angle from vertical**. A wrong angle in the problem can change the answer by 40%.
> 2. **Forgetting compressive force** — Examiners frequently test whether you understand that low sling angles create *crushing* hazards, not just overload.
> 3. **Unit conversion errors** — kg-force vs Newtons is a top reason for marks lost. Always convert to SI unless the problem explicitly allows gravities.
> 4. **Neglecting efficiency** — In hoist power calculations, students often forget $\eta$ and write only $P = Wv$, losing 1–2 marks.
> 5. **Naming equipment generically** — Don't write "crane" alone. Be specific: "EOT bridge crane," "gantry crane," "tower crane." KTU rewards **terminology precision**.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

### High-Density Revision Checklist

- [ ] **5 categories** of MH equipment: Transport, Positioning, Unit Load, Storage, Control.
- [ ] **Crane types:** Bridge (EOT), Gantry, Jib, Mobile, Tower — know capacity and application of each.
- [ ] **Hoist types:** Manual chain, electric wire rope, electric chain, pneumatic, hydraulic — pneumatic is for **hazardous zones**.
- [ ] **Conveyor types:** Belt, Roller, Chain, Screw, Pneumatic, Bucket Elevator — know the medium they carry.
- [ ] **Industrial trucks:** Forklift, Reach truck, Pallet jack, Order picker, AGV.
- [ ] **Master formula:** $T = W / (n \cos\theta)$ for multi-leg slings.
- [ ] **Horizontal squeeze force:** $F_H = n T \sin\theta$ — increases as sling angle becomes more horizontal.
- [ ] **Wire rope Factor of Safety:** 3.5:1 (material) to 5:1 (personnel).
- [ ] **Drum-to-rope ratio:** $D/d \geq 30$ to $45$ (IS 3177).
- [ ] **Hoist power:** $P = Wv / \eta$ (Watts if W in N, v in m/s).
- [ ] **Belt conveyor power:** $P = (T_1 - T_2) v / 1000$ in kW.
- [ ] **Screw conveyor capacity:** depends on diameter, pitch, rpm, filling factor, density.
- [ ] **Forklift stability triangle:** CG must remain inside the 3-wheel contact triangle.
- [ ] **Crane LMI:** Load × Radius = Constant (max moment).
- [ ] **Tower crane safety:** Anemometer (wind), LMI, anti-two-block, slewing limiter.
- [ ] **Belt conveyor safety:** Pull-cord, sway switches, zero-speed, slip detection, rip sensor.
- [ ] **Hook dimensions:** Tensile stress uses $d$ (root diameter); Shear stress uses $d \times t$.
- [ ] **OSHA / IS codes to remember:** IS 3177 (wire rope), IS 3938 (electric hoists), IS 2065 (hoists), OSHA 1910.179 (overhead cranes), OSHA 1910.178 (forklifts).
- [ ] **Pneumatic hoists** are intrinsically safe — preferred in **explosive atmospheres** (Zone 1 / Zone 2).
- [ ] **AGVs** use LIDAR + SLAM in modern installations — guide path is no longer physical.
- [ ] **Bucket elevators** are vertical conveyors; **spillage and dust** are major safety concerns.
- [ ] **Always inspect MHE before use** — pre-use checklist is a legal requirement under Factories Act 1948 (India) and OSHA (US).
- [ ] **Color code (KTU expects):** Yellow = caution, Red = danger, Green = safety, Blue = mandatory PPE.
- [ ] **Trolley + Bridge + Hoist = EOT** — remember this 3-component structure.
- [ ] **Jib crane reach = radius of semicircular coverage** — used for **workstation-specific** lifting.
- [ ] **Sling efficiency** decreases with sling angle — keep $\theta \geq 60°$ for safe lifts.
- [ ] **Critical sling angle** at $\theta = 60°$ for 2-leg sling — $T = W$ (tension equals full weight).
- [ ] **Conversion factors to remember:** 1 HP = 746 W, 1 kgf = 9.81 N, 1 m/min = 1/60 m/s.

> **Final Exam Tip:** When answering KTU Part B questions on Types, **always start with a labeled diagram** (even a hand-drawn one), then proceed with the **construction → working → safety devices** structure. Examiners allocate **1.5 to 2 marks** for diagrams and 1 mark for the safety section explicitly.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Method of Attachment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT522 - SAFETY IN MATERIAL HANDLING
> - **Module:** Module Module 2
> - **Topic:** Method of Attachment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Section 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of KTU's **PESFT522 - Safety in Material Handling**, **Methods of Attachment** refer to the standardized engineering techniques, rigging configurations, and mechanical fastening systems used to securely connect a load to a lifting appliance (crane, hoist, derrick) or to a load-lifting attachment (sling, hook, shackle, lifting beam). The method of attachment is the *deterministic link* between the load, the lifting gear, and the lifting machine, and is therefore a critical control point under any occupational safety framework.

As per standard rigging practice and KTU-aligned safety engineering principles, the method of attachment must satisfy three simultaneous conditions:
1. **Mechanical adequacy** – the attachment must carry the imposed load without yielding, slipping, or breaking.
2. **Geometric stability** – the load must remain in a stable equilibrium under all prescribed sling angles and lift points.
3. **Safety margin** – the working load limit (WLL) must always exceed the maximum anticipated load by a **Minimum Factor of Safety (FoS) of 5:1 for general-purpose slings** and up to **10:1 for personnel-carrying attachments**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight (Module 2):** Methods of Attachment govern the *safe transfer of force* between the load and the lifting medium. Incorrect attachment is the **leading cause of dropped-load incidents** in industrial material handling.

## 1.2 Key Terminology (KTU Board-Standard Vocabulary)

| Term | Definition |
|------|------------|
| **Sling** | Flexible length of chain, wire rope, or synthetic webbing used to support the load. |
| **Shackle** | A U-shaped metal piece closed with a removable pin, used to connect slings to other fittings. |
| **Hook** | Curved metal fitting used to engage a load, often with a safety latch. |
| **Rigging** | The complete process of selecting, inspecting, and attaching lifting gear to a load. |
| **Hitch** | The specific configuration in which a sling is connected to the load. |
| **WLL** | Working Load Limit – the maximum safe load a fitting is rated to lift. |
| **Sling Angle** | The angle that the sling leg makes with the horizontal plane. |
| **Basket Hitch** | A hitch where the sling passes under the load and both ends are attached to the hook. |
| **Choker Hitch** | A hitch where the sling passes around the load and back through itself. |
| **Vertical Hitch** | A hitch where the sling is attached vertically to a single point on the load. |

## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy / Intuitive Overview

Imagine you are lifting a heavy bucket of water from a well using a rope.

- If you simply **loop the rope around the bucket's handle** (a *basket hitch*), the bucket may tip sideways because the force is applied at one high point.
- If you **tie the rope directly to the handle in a single vertical pull** (a *vertical hitch*), the full weight of the bucket is concentrated on one small handle — this could snap the handle.
- If you **wrap the rope once around the bucket and pull one end through a loop in the rope** (a *choker hitch*), the rope tightens around the bucket's body, distributing the load more evenly — but the friction is what holds the load, not the mechanical grip.

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuition Takeaway:** Just like the rope and bucket, the **Method of Attachment determines how the weight of the load is distributed, transferred, and ultimately supported**. The wrong method can concentrate stress at a single weak point, while the right method distributes stress across multiple, stronger contact points.

## 1.4 The Three Primary Methods of Attachment (KTU Module 2 Core)

In KTU Module 2, **Method of Attachment** is broadly classified into three fundamental categories:

### A. Direct Attachment
The load is provided with an integral attachment point (lifting eye, trunnion, threaded hole) by the manufacturer. The lifting gear is connected directly to this point.

### B. Sling Attachment
The load has no dedicated attachment point. A sling (chain, wire rope, or synthetic) is wrapped around the load or passed through/around it in a specific configuration (hitch).

### C. Mechanical Fittings Attachment
Intermediate fittings such as **shackles, eye-bolts, lifting beams, swivel rings, and lifting clutches** are used to connect the sling to the load's attachment point.

> [!TIP]
> **Examiner's Insight:** When asked to "explain methods of attachment," always begin by defining the *purpose* of attachment (force transfer), then classify the methods, and finally state the *factor of safety* rules. This structured opening scores well in KTU valuation.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Section 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework: Force Transfer in Sling Systems

The Method of Attachment is fundamentally a **statics and mechanics-of-materials problem**. When a sling is attached to a load at an angle, the tension in each sling leg is *not* equal to the load weight — it is **amplified** due to the geometry of attachment.

### 2.1.1 The Sling Angle Principle

When two sling legs lift a load, each leg makes an angle $\theta$ with the horizontal. The **effective load per leg** is:

$$T = \frac{W}{2 \cdot \sin(\theta)}$$

Where:
- $T$ = Tension in each sling leg (N or kg)
- $W$ = Total weight of the load (N or kg)
- $\theta$ = Sling angle measured from the **horizontal**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU High-Yield Concept:** As $\theta \to 0$ (sling becomes horizontal), $\sin(\theta) \to 0$, and **tension $T \to \infty$**. This is why slings must never be used at very shallow angles. The minimum recommended sling angle is **$60°$ from horizontal** (i.e., $30°$ from vertical).

### 2.1.2 Hitches and Their Load Multipliers

The *type of hitch* also affects the load on each sling leg. Standard KTU-board values:

| Hitch Type | Description | Effective Load per Sling Leg |
|------------|-------------|------------------------------|
| **Vertical Hitch** | Sling hangs straight down; one leg supports full load | $T = W$ |
| **Basket Hitch** | Sling passes under load; both legs share load | $T = W \div 2$ |
| **Choker Hitch** | Sling passes around load and back through itself | $T = W$ (loss of capacity ~25%) |
| **Basket Hitch (2-leg at angle $\theta$)** | Two legs at angle $\theta$ from horizontal | $T = W \div (2 \cdot \sin\theta)$ |
| **3-leg Bridle Hitch** | Three legs at equal angle | $T = W \div (3 \cdot \sin\theta)$ |
| **4-leg Bridle Hitch** | Four legs at equal angle | $T = W \div (4 \cdot \sin\theta)$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **Capacity Reduction for Choker Hitch:** A choker hitch typically reduces sling capacity by **20% to 25%** because the load is held by friction between the sling and the load surface, not by direct mechanical grip.

## 2.2 The Three Primary Attachment Methods — Deep Dive

### 2.2.1 Direct Attachment

**Engineering Logic:**
- Load is manufactured with an **integrated lifting point** (eye-bolt, pad-eye, trunnion, lifting lug).
- This is the **most efficient and safest** method because the load path is engineered by the manufacturer.
- The attachment point is **proof-tested** and rated.

**When to use:**
- Engineered loads (machinery, transformers, pre-cast concrete, industrial vessels).

**Engineering Considerations:**
- Vertical pull only — no side-loading allowed unless the eye-bolt is rated for it.
- Shoulder-type eye-bolts must be supported against a flat surface to prevent bending.

> [!NOTE]
> **Sling Angle Effect on Eye-Bolts:** A shoulder eye-bolt pulled at an angle experiences **combined tension and bending stress**, dramatically reducing its capacity. KTU board questions frequently test this concept.

### 2.2.2 Sling Attachment

**Engineering Logic:**
- The sling is wrapped around or passed through the load.
- Three sub-types (hitches): **Vertical, Basket, Choker**.

**Force Analysis — Vertical Hitch:**
$$T = W$$
The full load is borne by the single vertical sling leg. The sling's WLL must equal or exceed $W$.

**Force Analysis — Basket Hitch (Horizontal, 2-leg):**
$$T = \frac{W}{2}$$
Load is shared equally between two vertical legs.

**Force Analysis — 2-leg Sling at Angle $\theta$:**
$$T = \frac{W}{2 \cdot \sin(\theta)}$$

**Capacity Adjustment for Choker Hitch:**
$$\text{Effective WLL} = \text{Straight-line WLL} \times 0.75$$

### 2.2.3 Mechanical Fittings Attachment

**Engineering Logic:**
- Fittings such as **shackles, swivel rings, lifting clutches, and spreader beams** are inserted between the sling and the load.
- These fittings **redistribute force**, **change direction of pull**, or **multiply the lifting points**.

**Common Fittings:**

| Fitting | Function | Typical Application |
|---------|----------|---------------------|
| **Shackle (Bow/Dee)** | Connects sling to lifting point | General rigging |
| **Swivel Hoist Ring** | Allows rotation under load | Frequent re-orientation |
| **Lifting Beam (Spreader Bar)** | Distributes load across multiple points | Long / wide loads |
| **Lifting Clutch** | Connects sling to precast concrete anchors | Construction industry |
| **Eye-Bolt** | Provides threaded attachment point | Machinery |

## 2.3 The Tag-Line Method (Indirect Attachment Safety)

A **tag line** (or tag-line rope) is a secondary rope attached to the load to **control rotation, swinging, and orientation** of the load during the lift. While not a primary load-bearing attachment, it is a critical *control* method in KTU's safety syllabus.

> [!NOTE]
> **Tag Line Rule:** Tag lines must never be wrapped around the operator's hand or body. A non-conductive rope is used when working near live electrical hazards.

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master Table for KTU 2024 Board Examination**

| S.No. | Concept | Formula | Variables | Units | Notes |
|-------|---------|---------|-----------|-------|-------|
| 1 | Tension in 2-leg sling at angle $\theta$ | $T = \dfrac{W}{2 \sin\theta}$ | $W$ = load, $\theta$ = angle from horizontal | N, degrees | $\theta \geq 60°$ recommended |
| 2 | Tension in 3-leg sling at angle $\theta$ | $T = \dfrac{W}{3 \sin\theta}$ | As above | N, degrees | Equal leg angles assumed |
| 3 | Tension in 4-leg sling at angle $\theta$ | $T = \dfrac{W}{4 \sin\theta}$ | As above | N, degrees | 2 legs carry theoretical load |
| 4 | Choker hitch capacity | $\text{WLL}_{\text{choker}} = 0.75 \times \text{WLL}_{\text{vertical}}$ | WLL = Working Load Limit | N, kg | Friction-based grip |
| 5 | Basket hitch capacity (horizontal) | $T = W \div 2$ | $W$ = total load | N | Symmetric basket |
| 6 | Vertical hitch capacity | $T = W$ | $W$ = total load | N | Single-leg vertical pull |
| 7 | Factor of Safety (FoS) | $\text{FoS} = \dfrac{\text{Ultimate Load}}{\text{Working Load}}$ | Ratio | Dimensionless | $\text{FoS} = 5$ for general slings |
| 8 | D/d ratio (D/d ratio) | $D \div d$ | $D$ = sling diameter, $d$ = load contact diameter | Dimensionless | $\text{Min} = 2$ for wire rope, $1$ for chain |

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

The methods of attachment underpin **every lifting operation** in:
- **Construction** — lifting steel beams, pre-cast panels, formwork.
- **Shipping & Ports** — loading containers, securing cargo.
- **Manufacturing** — moving machinery, dies, and heavy parts.
- **Power & Energy** — installing turbines, transformers, generators.
- **Aerospace & Defense** — moving engines, fuselage sections, missiles.

> [!TIP]
> **Industry Connection:** Modern safety codes (ASME B30.9, IS 807, OSHA 1926.1400) all trace their root principles to the **Method of Attachment** concepts tested in KTU Module 2.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Section 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Tension in a 2-Leg Sling at an Angle

This is the **most frequently asked derivation** in KTU Module 2 exams. We will derive it exhaustively, step by step.

### 3.1.1 Problem Setup

Consider a load of weight $W$ suspended by a sling with two legs. Each leg makes an angle $\theta$ with the **horizontal plane**. Both legs are symmetric and share the load equally. Let the tension in each leg be $T$.

### 3.1.2 Free Body Diagram Logic

- The load $W$ acts **vertically downward**.
- Each sling leg exerts a force $T$ on the hook (or load attachment point), directed along the sling axis.
- For static equilibrium, the **vertical components** of the two tensions must balance $W$, and the **horizontal components** must cancel each other (by symmetry).

### 3.1.3 Vertical Equilibrium

The vertical component of tension in one leg is:

$$T_v = T \cdot \sin(\theta)$$

This is because $\theta$ is measured from the horizontal, so the *vertical* component uses the sine function.

For two legs (left and right), total vertical component of tension is:

$$T_{v,\text{total}} = 2 \cdot T \cdot \sin(\theta)$$

Setting vertical equilibrium:

$$2 \cdot T \cdot \sin(\theta) = W$$

### 3.1.4 Solving for T

Rearranging:

$$T = \frac{W}{2 \cdot \sin(\theta)}$$

### 3.1.5 Dimensional Check

- $W$ has units of force (N or kgf).
- $\sin(\theta)$ is dimensionless.
- Therefore $T$ has units of force. ✓

### 3.1.6 Limiting Cases

**Case 1: Vertical Hitch (Sling Vertical)**

If the sling is vertical, $\theta = 90°$.

$$T = \frac{W}{2 \cdot \sin(90°)} = \frac{W}{2 \cdot 1} = \frac{W}{2}$$

This is correct — a vertical 2-leg basket hitch shares load equally.

**Case 2: Shallow Sling Angle**

If $\theta = 30°$ (shallow angle, sling nearly horizontal):

$$T = \frac{W}{2 \cdot \sin(30°)} = \frac{W}{2 \cdot 0.5} = \frac{W}{1} = W$$

So each leg now carries the **full load** — capacity is reduced to that of a single leg.

**Case 3: Very Shallow Sling Angle**

If $\theta = 10°$ (dangerously shallow):

$$T = \frac{W}{2 \cdot \sin(10°)} = \frac{W}{2 \cdot 0.1736} = \frac{W}{0.3472} \approx 2.88 \cdot W$$

Each leg carries nearly **3 times the load**. This is why shallow sling angles are strictly prohibited.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Trap:** Students often use $\theta$ measured from the **vertical** instead of horizontal. Always re-read the question carefully. KTU questions typically specify "from horizontal" — if not stated, draw a diagram and label clearly.

## 3.2 Derivation: Tension in a 3-Leg Bridle Sling

### 3.2.1 Setup

Three legs share the load $W$ equally, each at angle $\theta$ from horizontal.

### 3.2.2 Vertical Equilibrium

$$3 \cdot T \cdot \sin(\theta) = W$$

### 3.2.3 Solving

$$T = \frac{W}{3 \cdot \sin(\theta)}$$

### 3.2.4 Limiting Case (Vertical)

For $\theta = 90°$:

$$T = \frac{W}{3 \cdot 1} = \frac{W}{3}$$

Each leg carries one-third the load. ✓

## 3.3 Derivation: Choker Hitch Capacity Reduction

### 3.3.1 Engineering Basis

A choker hitch holds the load by **friction** between the sling and the load surface. The friction force is:

$$F_{\text{friction}} = \mu \cdot N$$

Where $\mu$ is the coefficient of friction and $N$ is the normal force exerted by the sling on the load.

### 3.3.2 Standard Reduction

Engineering codes (and KTU Module 2) recommend a **conservative derating factor** for choker hitches because:

1. The choker point is a stress concentration.
2. Friction is unreliable on smooth, oily, or polished surfaces.
3. The sling bends sharply at the choker point, reducing its effective strength.

**Standard Choker Derating Factor = 0.75 (i.e., 25% reduction)**

$$\text{WLL}_{\text{choker}} = 0.75 \times \text{WLL}_{\text{straight}}$$

### 3.3.3 Worked Numerical Example

**Given:**
- Sling WLL (vertical) = 2000 kg
- Hitch type: Choker
- Sling angle: vertical

**Find:** Effective WLL

**Solution:**

$$\text{WLL}_{\text{choker}} = 0.75 \times 2000 = 1500 \text{ kg}$$

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Hitch Selection Decision Chart

| Load Condition | Recommended Hitch | Reason |
|----------------|-------------------|--------|
| Single, balanced, with lifting eye | **Vertical Hitch (1-leg)** | Simplest, full capacity used |
| Long, balanced load with two lifting points | **2-leg Bridle at $60°$+** | Even load distribution, stable |
| Cylindrical load (pipe, drum) | **Basket Hitch (2-leg)** | Sling cradles the load |
| Loose bundle, log, or rough load | **Choker Hitch** | Grips load via friction |
| Wide, flat load (steel plate) | **4-leg Bridle (Spreader Beam)** | Distributes load, prevents bending |
| Eccentric / unbalanced load | **Spreader Beam + Slings** | Shifts CG, prevents tipping |

## 3.5 Numerical Problem Library (KTU Board Standard)

### Problem 1: 2-Leg Sling Tension

A steel block weighing **5000 N** is lifted using a 2-leg sling. Each leg makes an angle of **$60°$ with the horizontal**. Find the tension in each leg.

**Solution:**

$$T = \frac{W}{2 \cdot \sin(\theta)} = \frac{5000}{2 \cdot \sin(60°)} = \frac{5000}{2 \cdot 0.8660} = \frac{5000}{1.7321} \approx 2886.75 \text{ N}$$

**Answer:** $T \approx 2886.75$ N per leg.

### Problem 2: 3-Leg Sling with Combined Hitch Effect

A load of **9000 N** is lifted by a 3-leg sling, each leg at **$45°$ from horizontal**, using a choker configuration. The sling WLL (vertical) is **4000 N** per leg. Determine the actual safety margin.

**Step 1:** Compute actual tension per leg:

$$T = \frac{9000}{3 \cdot \sin(45°)} = \frac{9000}{3 \cdot 0.7071} = \frac{9000}{2.1213} \approx 4242.6 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2:** Effective WLL with choker reduction:

$$\text{WLL}_{\text{effective}} = 0.75 \times 4000 = 3000 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3:** Compare:

$$T = 4242.6 \text{ N} > \text{WLL}_{\text{effective}} = 3000 \text{ N}$$

**Result:** The sling is **overloaded and unsafe**. A heavier sling or different configuration is required.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Section 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Mermaid Diagram: Methods of Attachment — Functional Architecture Flow

The following Mermaid block presents a high-level block diagram of how the three primary Methods of Attachment are interconnected, with the **load** at the center, the **lifting machine** at the top, and the **attachment method** in between. This satisfies the KTU Module 2 visual-schematic requirement.

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    LM[HOIST OR CRANE] --> HS[MAIN HOOK]
    HS --> R1{RIGGING FITTINGS}

    R1 --> F1[SHACKLE]
    R1 --> F2[SWIVEL HOIST RING]
    R1 --> F3[SPREADER BEAM]

    F1 --> A1{METHOD OF ATTACHMENT}
    F2 --> A1
    F3 --> A1

    A1 --> M1[DIRECT ATTACHMENT]
    A1 --> M2[SLING ATTACHMENT]
    A1 --> M3[MECHANICAL FITTINGS]

    M1 --> P1[INTEGRAL EYE BOLT]
    M1 --> P2[PAD EYE]
    M1 --> P3[TRUNNION]

    M2 --> H1[VERTICAL HITCH]
    M2 --> H2[BASKET HITCH]
    M2 --> H3[CHOKER HITCH]

    M3 --> S1[CHAIN SLING]
    M3 --> S2[WIRE ROPE SLING]
    M3 --> S3[SYNTHETIC SLING]

    P1 --> LOAD[(LOAD TO BE LIFTED)]
    P2 --> LOAD
    P3 --> LOAD
    H1 --> LOAD
    H2 --> LOAD
    H3 --> LOAD
    S1 --> LOAD
    S2 --> LOAD
    S3 --> LOAD

    LOAD --> CG{CENTRE OF GRAVITY CHECK}
    CG --> OK[SAFE LIFT]
    CG --> NO[REJECT OR RE-RIG]
```

## 4.2 Mermaid Diagram: Sling Hitch Geometry — Force Resolution

This diagram shows how the **sling angle $\theta$** affects the tension $T$ in a 2-leg sling, helping visualize why shallow angles are dangerous.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph GEOMETRY OF TWO LEG SLING
        H[HOOK] --> |TENSION T| L1[LEFT SLING LEG]
        H --> |TENSION T| L2[RIGHT SLING LEG]
        L1 --> ANG1[ANGLE THETA FROM HORIZONTAL]
        L2 --> ANG2[ANGLE THETA FROM HORIZONTAL]
        ANG1 --> LD[(LOAD WEIGHT W)]
        ANG2 --> LD
    end

    subgraph FORCE COMPONENTS
        TV[VERTICAL COMPONENT T SIN THETA]
        TH[HORIZONTAL COMPONENT T COS THETA]
        TV --> SUM[VERTICAL SUM EQUALS W]
        TH --> CANCEL[HORIZONTAL CANCEL BY SYMMETRY]
    end

    LD --> TV
    LD --> TH
```

## 4.3 Mermaid Diagram: Hitch Selection Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[NEW LIFT TASK] --> Q1{DOES LOAD HAVE INTEGRAL LIFT POINT}
    Q1 --> YES_A[YES]
    Q1 --> NO_B[NO]

    YES_A --> DA[DIRECT ATTACHMENT]
    DA --> CHECK_ANGLE{ANGLE OF PULL WITHIN RATING}
    CHECK_ANGLE --> YES_OK[USE EYE BOLT OR PAD EYE]
    CHECK_ANGLE --> NO_REJECT[USE SPREADER BEAM OR SHACKLE]

    NO_B --> SHAPE{LOAD SHAPE AND TYPE}
    SHAPE --> CYL[CYLINDRICAL PIPE OR DRUM]
    SHAPE --> RECT[RECTANGULAR OR FLAT]
    SHAPE --> BUND[LOOSE BUNDLE OR LOG]

    CYL --> BASKET[BASKET HITCH TWO LEG]
    RECT --> BRIDLE[FOUR LEG BRIDLE WITH SPREADER]
    BUND --> CHOKER[CHOKER HITCH WITH DERATING]
```

## 4.4 Mermaid Diagram: Force Amplification vs. Sling Angle

This conceptual diagram maps the *non-linear* relationship between sling angle and tension amplification — a key concept for KTU students.

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A1[ANGLE 90 DEG] --> V1[T EQUALS W OVER 2]
    A2[ANGLE 60 DEG] --> V2[T APPROX 0.577 W]
    A3[ANGLE 45 DEG] --> V3[T APPROX 0.707 W]
    A4[ANGLE 30 DEG] --> V4[T EQUALS W]
    A5[ANGLE 10 DEG] --> V5[T APPROX 2.88 W DANGER]
    A6[ANGLE 5 DEG] --> V6[T APPROX 5.76 W CRITICAL FAILURE]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Visual Learning Aid:** As the sling angle $\theta$ decreases (sling becomes more horizontal), the tension in each leg **rises sharply**. KTU questions often ask students to plot or interpret this relationship.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Section 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 KTU Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1: Define "Method of Attachment" and list its three primary types. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

The **Method of Attachment** refers to the standardized technique and rigging configuration used to connect a load to a lifting appliance or lifting gear such that the load is transferred safely and stably.

The three primary methods of attachment are:
1. **Direct Attachment** — using the load's integral lifting point (eye-bolt, pad-eye).
2. **Sling Attachment** — using a sling in a specific hitch (vertical, basket, choker).
3. **Mechanical Fittings Attachment** — using shackles, swivel rings, lifting beams, or other intermediate fittings.

*(Marks: Definition = 1, List with brief description = 2)*

---

### Question 2: Why is a sling angle of less than $30°$ from horizontal considered unsafe? `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 Marks):**

As the sling angle $\theta$ from horizontal decreases, the tension in each sling leg increases as:

$$T = \frac{W}{2 \sin(\theta)}$$

When $\theta$ is very small, $\sin(\theta)$ is also small, and $T$ becomes very large — often exceeding the WLL of the sling. Additionally, at very shallow angles:
- The sling is more likely to slip along the load.
- The horizontal force component dominates, causing the load to swing.
- The bend in the sling at the load contact point becomes extreme, weakening the sling.

A minimum sling angle of **$60°$ from horizontal** is recommended by standard codes to keep tension within safe limits.

*(Marks: Formula = 1, Explanation = 2)*

---

## 5.2 KTU Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice)

### Question A (14 Marks): Methods of Attachment and Sling Tension Analysis `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:** Explain in detail the three primary methods of attachment used in material handling. Compare their applications and limitations.

**Model Answer:**

The three primary methods of attachment are:

**1. Direct Attachment (3 marks)**
- The load has an integral, manufacturer-provided lifting point (eye-bolt, pad-eye, trunnion, or lug).
- This is the **most reliable and safest** method because the load path is engineered.
- *Limitation:* Limited to engineered loads; eye-bolts are sensitive to side-loading; shoulder-type eye-bolts require a flat seating surface.

**2. Sling Attachment (2 marks)**
- The sling is wrapped around the load in a specific configuration.
- Three hitch types: vertical, basket, choker.
- *Limitation:* Capacity depends on sling angle and hitch type; choker hitch is friction-dependent.

**3. Mechanical Fittings Attachment (2 marks)**
- Intermediate fittings (shackles, swivel rings, spreader beams, lifting clutches) are used.
- *Limitation:* Each fitting adds weight, cost, and another potential failure point. Inspection load increases.

**Comparison Summary:**
Direct attachment is best for engineered loads; sling attachment is best for general cargo; mechanical fittings are best for specialized or wide loads.

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:** A load of **8000 N** is to be lifted by a 2-leg sling. Each leg makes an angle of **$50°$ with the horizontal**. Determine the tension in each leg. If the same load were lifted by a vertical hitch, what would be the tension in the single sling leg? Comment on the comparison.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1:** For 2-leg sling at $50°$ from horizontal:

$$T = \frac{W}{2 \cdot \sin(\theta)} = \frac{8000}{2 \cdot \sin(50°)} = \frac{8000}{2 \cdot 0.7660} = \frac{8000}{1.5321} \approx 5221.4 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2:** For vertical hitch (single leg, vertical pull):

$$T = W = 8000 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3:** Comparison:

- The 2-leg sling at $50°$ distributes the load, with each leg carrying **5221.4 N**.
- The vertical single-leg hitch carries the full **8000 N** in one leg.
- The 2-leg configuration is **safer and more efficient** — it uses each sling to only ~65% of its single-leg capacity, providing a built-in safety margin.

*(Marks: 2-leg calculation = 3, vertical calculation = 2, comparison = 2)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks): Sling Hitch Analysis and Choker Derating `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Part (a) — 7 Marks:** With the help of neat sketches, explain **Vertical Hitch, Basket Hitch, and Choker Hitch**. State the effective load on each sling leg for each hitch.

**Model Answer:**

**1. Vertical Hitch (2 marks)**
- A single sling leg is attached vertically to a single lifting point.
- Effective load per leg: $T = W$ (full load on one leg).
- *Use case:* Loads with a single, strong, central lifting eye.

**2. Basket Hitch (2 marks)**
- The sling passes under the load, with both ends attached to the hook.
- Effective load per leg: $T = W \div 2$ (load shared between two vertical legs).
- *Use case:* Cylindrical loads like pipes, drums, rolls.

**3. Choker Hitch (3 marks)**
- The sling passes around the load and back through itself, locking by friction.
- Effective load per leg: $T = W$ (single leg), but with **25% capacity reduction** for friction loss and stress concentration.
- *Effective WLL:* $0.75 \times \text{WLL}_{\text{straight}}$
- *Use case:* Loose bundles, logs, rough or irregularly shaped loads.

**Comparison Table:**

| Hitch | Load per Leg | Capacity Factor | Best For |
|-------|--------------|-----------------|----------|
| Vertical | $W$ | 1.00 | Single lifting point |
| Basket | $W \div 2$ | 2.00 | Cylindrical, balanced loads |
| Choker | $W$ (with derating) | 0.75 | Loose, irregular loads |

---

**Part (b) — 7 Marks:** A 4-leg bridle sling is used to lift a steel plate weighing **12000 N**. Each leg makes an angle of **$45°$ with the horizontal**. The vertical WLL of each sling leg is **2500 N**. Determine:
(i) The actual tension in each leg.
(ii) Whether the sling is safe, considering only two legs carry the theoretical load (as per standard rigging practice where two legs are assumed to share the load in a 4-leg setup).
(iii) The minimum sling angle from horizontal required for the sling to be safe.

**Model Answer:**

**Step 1:** Actual tension in each leg:

$$T = \frac{W}{4 \cdot \sin(\theta)} = \frac{12000}{4 \cdot \sin(45°)} = \frac{12000}{4 \cdot 0.7071} = \frac{12000}{2.8284} \approx 4242.6 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2:** Standard rigging assumption — in a 4-leg sling, only 2 legs are assumed to carry the load (the other two are considered as stabilizers and not counted in capacity).

Effective tension per carrying leg:

$$T_{\text{effective}} = \frac{12000}{2 \cdot \sin(45°)} = \frac{12000}{1.4142} \approx 8485.3 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3:** Safety check:

$$T_{\text{effective}} = 8485.3 \text{ N} \gg \text{WLL} = 2500 \text{ N}$$

**Result: The sling is grossly overloaded and unsafe.** A heavier sling or different rigging configuration is required.

**Step 4:** Minimum sling angle for safety:

For safety, the tension per leg must be $\leq$ WLL of 2500 N:

$$\sin(\theta) \geq \frac{W}{2 \cdot \text{WLL}} = \frac{12000}{2 \cdot 2500} = \frac{12000}{5000} = 2.4$$

Since $\sin(\theta) \leq 1$, this is **physically impossible**. The configuration cannot be made safe by simply changing the angle — a heavier sling (with WLL $\geq 6000$ N per leg at $90°$) is required.

*(Marks: Tension calculation = 2, Safety check = 2, Angle analysis = 3)*

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Zones in KTU Module 2 Examinations:**
> 1. **Sling Angle Confusion (–2 marks typical):** Students often use the angle from vertical instead of from horizontal. Always draw a labeled diagram to be sure.
> 2. **Forgetting Choker Derating (–2 marks):** When a choker hitch is involved, the 25% capacity reduction is *mandatory* to apply. Missing it leads to incorrect safety conclusions.
> 3. **Confusing Basket Hitch with 2-leg Sling at Angle (–1 mark):** A *horizontal basket hitch* (load cradled, sling vertical) is different from a *2-leg sling at an angle* (legs inclined). The formulas are different.
> 4. **Forgetting the 4-leg Sling Assumption (–1 mark):** In KTU problems, a 4-leg sling is conservatively assumed to be carried by only 2 legs. The other 2 are considered stabilizers.
> 5. **Eye-Bolt Side-Loading (–1 mark):** If the problem shows a side pull on a shoulder eye-bolt, students often forget to apply the bending stress penalty (typically 50% capacity reduction).
> 6. **D/d Ratio (–1 mark):** The ratio of sling diameter to load contact diameter affects sling capacity. Minimum ratios are commonly tested.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist for KTU 2024 Module 2 — Method of Attachment**

- **Definition:** Method of Attachment = the rigging technique used to connect a load to a lifting appliance.
- **Three Primary Methods:** Direct, Sling, Mechanical Fittings.
- **Three Primary Hitches (for Slings):** Vertical, Basket, Choker.
- **Key Formula (2-leg sling at angle):** $T = W \div (2 \sin\theta)$, where $\theta$ is **from horizontal**.
- **Choker Hitch Derating Factor:** **0.75** (25% capacity loss).
- **4-leg Sling Capacity Assumption:** Only **2 legs** counted as load-bearing.
- **Minimum Recommended Sling Angle:** **$60°$ from horizontal** (preferred); never below $30°$ from horizontal.
- **Factor of Safety (FoS) for Slings:** **5:1** for general-purpose; **10:1** for personnel lifting.
- **D/d Ratio:** Min **2:1** for wire rope; **1:1** for chain — controls sling-on-load bending.
- **Eye-Bolt Rule:** Never side-load a shoulder eye-bolt; use swivel hoist rings for angled pulls.
- **Tag Line Rule:** Always use a tag line for swing control; never wrap it around your hand.
- **Sling Selection Logic:** Match sling WLL (with derating) to the calculated tension; verify after applying angle, hitch, and FoS corrections.
- **Inspect Before Lift:** Check slings, shackles, and fittings for wear, deformation, cracks, and proper rating tags.
- **Centre of Gravity (CG):** Always verify that the attachment points keep the load's CG directly below the hook — otherwise, the load will swing or tip.
- **Lifting Beam Advantage:** A spreader beam reduces sling angle stress and keeps tall loads stable.
- **Common KTU Question Patterns:** (a) "Explain with sketches..." (b) "Calculate tension in sling leg given W and θ" (c) "Justify the choice of hitch for a given load" (d) "Determine the safety of a sling given WLL, W, hitch type, and angle."

> **End of Topic Notes — Method of Attachment, Module 2, PESFT522, KTU 2024 Scheme**
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# Module 2 — Rated Capacities: Foundational Definitions

## 1.1 What is "Rated Capacity"?

In the discipline of **Safety in Material Handling**, the term **Rated Capacity** is the single most important number on any piece of lifting, rigging, or conveyance equipment. It is the *manufacturer-declared* maximum load that the equipment is **designed and proof-tested** to handle under **specific, stated operating conditions**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology):**
> **Rated Capacity** is the maximum working load a material-handling device, machine, or rigging component is qualified by the manufacturer to lift, carry, or support, expressed in kilograms (kg), tonnes (t), or kilo-newtons (kN), when the equipment is in proper working condition, operated on a firm level surface, and used with the manufacturer's recommended attachments and configuration.

### 1.2 The Family of "Capacity" Terms You MUST Know

| Terminology | Abbreviation | What it Means | Where it is Used |
|-------------|--------------|---------------|------------------|
| Safe Working Load | **SWL** | The maximum load the equipment is **permitted** to carry in service. | Older UK/European standards, wire ropes, slings |
| Working Load Limit | **WLL** | Modern equivalent of SWL — the **maximum** dynamic + static load permitted. | ASME B30.9 (Slings), B30.10 (Hooks) |
| Normal Working Capacity | **NWC** | The load a healthy operator can handle repeatedly without fatigue (NIOSH lifting equation). | Manual material handling |
| Rated Capacity (Crane) | **RC** | Gross load the crane can lift at a **specific radius**, on outriggers, with counterweight. | Mobile & crawler cranes |
| Maximum Permissible Load | **MPL** | The load marked on the equipment itself. | Forklifts, hoists, jacks |
| Proof Load | — | The **test** load (typically 1.25 × to 2 × WLL) applied during manufacture. | Acceptance testing only — **never the in-service load** |
| Breaking Load | — | The load at which the component actually fractures. | Lab testing only |

### 1.3 Intuitive Analogy — The "Weight Limit" Sign

> [!NOTE]
> **Think of Rated Capacity like the printed weight limit on a passenger elevator.**
> The elevator is **built** with a safety factor (the steel cables are perhaps 5× stronger than the stated limit), but the *rated capacity* is the number the manufacturer *promises* you can safely carry. **The elevator will not announce when you exceed it — it just becomes dangerous.** Rated capacity works exactly the same way: a sling with 5,000 kg WLL can probably hold 25,000 kg once — but the **first** time you exceed 5,000 kg, you have already left the safety margin and entered the zone of unpredictable failure.

### 1.4 Why Rated Capacity is a Safety Cornerstone

1. **It is the basis of the safety factor** — all engineering margin (factor of safety = Breaking Strength / Rated Capacity) is built around this single number.
2. **It is the legal/regulatory reference** — Indian Factories Act 1948 (Sec 28, 29) and BOCW Act require display of rated capacity on every lifting appliance.
3. **It conditions rigging decisions** — slings, shackles, and hooks must be selected such that the **load on each component is less than its rated capacity** after all derating factors (angle, hitch, environment) have been applied.

### 1.5 Where Rated Capacities Appear in the Workplace

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Look for the stamped/engraved plate, decal, or nameplate — it is **mandatory** on every lifting appliance:
> - **Overhead & mobile cranes:** load chart on the boom or cab.
> - **Forklifts:** data plate on the chassis (load × load centre).
> - **Slings, shackles, hooks, eyebolts:** stamped directly on the body.
> - **Chain blocks & lever hoists:** plate on the side cover.
> - **Conveyors & elevators:** plate near the drive.
> - **Storage racks:** capacity label on every beam.
> - **Pallets & containers:** printed on the side wall.

The **Indian Standard IS 3938**, **IS 807**, **ASME B30 Series**, and **ISO 12480 / 12482** are the governing codes for rated capacity of cranes and lifting gear.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# Module 2 — Rated Capacities: Theory & Formula Sheet

## 2.1 The Universal Capacity Equation

The conceptual equation that governs *every* lifting system in material handling can be written as:

$$
T_{\text{applied}} \le T_{\text{rated}}
$$

That is, the **actual tension in any component at the worst-case loading must never exceed the rated (safe working) tension of that component**. The art of safe material handling is reducing the left side of this inequality and verifying the right side.

## 2.2 The Three Building Blocks of Rated Capacity

### A. The Safety Factor (Design Margin)

$$
\text{Safety Factor (SF)} = \frac{\text{Ultimate / Breaking Load}}{\text{Rated Capacity (WLL)}}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard safety factors used in industry (must memorize):**
> - **Wire-rope slings:** SF = 5
> - **Chain slings:** SF = 4
> - **Polyester / nylon web slings:** SF = 5 (synthetic) / 7 (polyester round)
> - **Shackles & rigging hardware:** SF = 5 to 6
> - **Mobile / crawler cranes (structural):** SF ≈ 1.67 (ASME B30.5 — for stability / tipping)
> - **Overhead crane hook:** SF = 5
> - **Eyebolt:** SF = 5

### B. The Derating Factor (Real-World Loss of Capacity)

The *rated* capacity on the nameplate is for **ideal** conditions. In the field, the effective capacity is reduced by:

$$
T_{\text{eff}} = \text{WLL} \times F_{\text{hitch}} \times F_{\text{angle}} \times F_{\text{env}} \times F_{\text{D/d}}
$$

where each factor is **less than or equal to 1**.

| Factor | Symbol | Typical Range | What it Represents |
|--------|--------|---------------|--------------------|
| Hitch factor | $F_{\text{hitch}}$ | 0.50 to 1.00 | Vertical = 1.00; Choker ≈ 0.75; Basket 2-leg = 2.00 (gains from 2 legs) |
| Sling-angle factor | $F_{\text{angle}}$ | 0.50 to 1.00 | $\sin\theta$ where $\theta$ is the horizontal sling angle |
| Environmental factor | $F_{\text{env}}$ | 0.67 to 1.00 | Heat, chemicals, shock loading, dynamic lifts |
| D/d ratio factor (chain) | $F_{D/d}$ | 0.50 to 1.00 | Ratio of master-link diameter to sling chain link |
| Reeving factor | — | 1/number of parts | Mechanical advantage of pulley block |

### C. The Hitch (Sling Configuration)

The three classic hitches and their typical capacity multipliers for a single vertical-leg sling of WLL = $V$:

$$
\boxed{\;
T_{\text{vertical}} = V
\;}
\qquad
\boxed{\;
T_{\text{choker}} \approx 0.75\,V
\;}
\qquad
\boxed{\;
T_{\text{basket (single leg)}} = 2\,V
\;}
$$

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master Table — Equations you must memorize for KTU ESE 2024 Scheme**

| # | Concept | Formula | Variable Meaning | Notes |
|---|---------|---------|------------------|-------|
| 1 | Vertical sling tension | $T = \dfrac{W}{n}$ | $W$ = load, $n$ = legs | $n$ = number of sling legs |
| 2 | Choker tension | $T = \dfrac{W}{n}$ multiplied by 1/0.75 | Same as above | **Use 0.75 of WLL as the effective choker rating** |
| 3 | Basket hitch (vertical legs) | $T = \dfrac{W}{2n}$ | n = number of sling legs | Tension split between two vertical legs |
| 4 | Bridle at horizontal angle $\theta$ | $T = \dfrac{W}{2n \sin\theta}$ | $\theta$ measured from **horizontal** | This is the **single most-tested equation in KTU** |
| 5 | Sling-angle factor | $F = \sin\theta$ | — | $60° \rightarrow 0.866$; $45° \rightarrow 0.707$; $30° \rightarrow 0.500$ |
| 6 | Tension in single leg @ angle | $T = \dfrac{W}{n\sin\theta}$ | Vertical leg (choker/basket) | — |
| 7 | Crane net capacity | $RC_{\text{net}} = RC_{\text{gross}} - (\text{hook} + \text{rigging weight})$ | All in tonnes | Subtract everything below the boom tip |
| 8 | Forklift net load | $L_{\text{net}} = L_{\text{rated}} \times \dfrac{\text{LC}_{\text{rated}}}{\text{LC}_{\text{actual}}}$ | LC = load centre | Load further forward → less capacity |
| 9 | Safety factor | $SF = \dfrac{B}{WLL}$ | B = breaking load | Industry SF tabulated above |
| 10 | Capacity after derating | $WLL_{\text{eff}} = WLL_{\text{nameplate}} \times F_1 \times F_2 \times \ldots$ | Each $F_i \le 1$ | Multiply all reduction factors |
| 11 | Multiple part reeving | $W = T \times n_{\text{parts}} \times \eta$ | $n$ = parts of line, $\eta$ = efficiency | Sheave block efficiency ≈ 0.97 per sheave |
| 12 | Working Capacity (manual lift) | $RWL = LC \times HM \times VM \times DM \times AM \times FM \times CM$ | NIOSH equation | $LC = 23\,$kg; each multiplier $\le 1$ |

> [!WARNING]
> **PITFALL:** In the table above, every $\theta$ is measured from the **horizontal floor**, NOT from the vertical. This is the *single most common error* in KTU answer scripts. If a question says "sling at 60° to the horizontal", then $\sin 60° = 0.866$ is the angle factor. If it says "60° to the vertical" (rare), the angle factor is $\cos 60° = 0.5$.

## 2.4 Engineering Utility of These Equations

- **In crane selection:** the load chart of a mobile crane gives $RC_{\text{gross}}$ for every radius; the *net* lift the crane can perform is the gross minus the weight of the hook block, spreader, and all rigging.
- **In sling selection:** a rigger looks up the WLL of the sling on the tag, multiplies by the hitch factor, multiplies by the angle factor, and chooses the sling such that $W/n \le T_{\text{eff}}$.
- **In rigging design (bridle slings):** the capacity of the spreader beam and the master link is governed by the worst-case tension in the *most-loaded* leg, which is always the leg with the *smallest* horizontal angle.
- **In forklift operation:** placing a load further from the mast face changes the moment arm and reduces the rated capacity — the **load centre distance** is the second key parameter.
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# Module 2 — Rated Capacities: Step-by-Step Derivations

## 3.1 Derivation: Tension in a Sling Leg at a Horizontal Angle $\theta$

This is the **most important derivation** for KTU Module 2. Consider a load $W$ suspended by a symmetric two-leg sling. Each leg makes an angle $\theta$ with the **horizontal**.

**Step 1 — Free Body Setup.** The load $W$ acts straight down. Each sling leg exerts a tension $T$ along its own axis. By symmetry the two tensions are equal, and the system is in static equilibrium.

**Step 2 — Resolve Each Tension.**
- Vertical component of one leg: $T \sin\theta$
- Horizontal component of one leg: $T \cos\theta$

**Step 3 — Apply Vertical Equilibrium.**

$$
\sum F_y = 0
$$

The two vertical components must together support the weight:

$$
T \sin\theta + T \sin\theta = W
$$

$$
2\,T \sin\theta = W
$$

**Step 4 — Solve for the Tension in a Single Leg.**

$$
\boxed{\; T = \dfrac{W}{2\sin\theta} \;}
$$

> This is the **bridle sling tension equation** and is the canonical result KTU expects.

**Step 5 — Generalise to n legs.** For an $n$-leg symmetric bridle:

$$
\boxed{\; T = \dfrac{W}{n\sin\theta} \;}
$$

**Step 6 — Interpret the Sling-Angle Factor.** As $\theta \rightarrow 90°$ (sling vertical), $\sin\theta \rightarrow 1$ and $T \rightarrow W/n$ (the natural vertical result). As $\theta \rightarrow 0°$ (sling nearly horizontal), $\sin\theta \rightarrow 0$ and $T \rightarrow \infty$ — *physically impossible* and **why no sling may be used below 30° from horizontal**.

**Step 7 — Numerical Sanity Check.** A 2-tonne load on two legs at 60°:

$$
T = \frac{2000}{2 \times \sin 60°} = \frac{2000}{2 \times 0.866} = \frac{2000}{1.732} \approx 1154.7\;\text{kg per leg}
$$

Compare to the vertical case: $T = 1000$ kg per leg. The 60° angle **increased tension by 15.5 %** — this is the price of the leg spreading out.

## 3.2 Derivation: Effect of Hitch on Rated Capacity

**Case A — Vertical Hitch (single straight pull).** The entire weight is carried by one vertical member, so:

$$
T = W
$$

The sling carries its *nameplate* WLL; no derating for hitch.

**Case B — Choker Hitch.** The sling wraps around the load and crosses back through itself. The choker hitch loses **about 25 %** of the vertical WLL because:
- The sling bends sharply at the choker point, inducing high local contact stress.
- The choker point itself becomes the weakest cross-section, governed by **friction and the curvature ratio**.

The empirical factor used industry-wide (and in **ASME B30.9-2021**) is:

$$
\boxed{\; T_{\text{choker}} = 0.75 \times WLL_{\text{vertical}} \;}
$$

**Case C — Basket Hitch (single sling looped under the load).** A single sling forms two parallel vertical legs around the load. By the vertical-leg result:

$$
T_{\text{each leg}} = \frac{W}{2} \quad\Rightarrow\quad \text{effective WLL} = 2 \times WLL_{\text{vertical}}
$$

**Case D — Two-Leg Bridle at Angle $\theta$.** Combining the basket and angle results:

$$
\boxed{\; WLL_{\text{eff, 2-leg at } \theta} = 2 \times WLL_{\text{vertical}} \times \sin\theta \;}
$$

## 3.3 Derivation: Forklift Rated Capacity & Load-Center Concept

A forklift is rated for a load $L_{\text{rated}}$ placed at a load-centre distance $LC_{\text{rated}}$ from the mast face. The moment equation about the front axle is:

$$
L_{\text{rated}} \times LC_{\text{rated}} = \text{Maximum Stable Moment (constant for the truck)}
$$

Therefore the *new* capacity at a different actual load-centre $LC_{\text{actual}}$ is:

$$
\boxed{\; L_{\text{net}} = L_{\text{rated}} \times \dfrac{LC_{\text{rated}}}{LC_{\text{actual}}} \;}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Worked Numerical Example.** A forklift is rated for 2,500 kg at 600 mm load centre. The load to be picked has a load centre at 800 mm. The new safe capacity is:

$$
L_{\text{net}} = 2500 \times \frac{600}{800} = 2500 \times 0.75 = 1875\;\text{kg}
$$

The forklift can only lift **1,875 kg** of this load, not 2,500 kg. Going further from the mast **always** reduces capacity.

## 3.4 Derivation: Crane Net Capacity

For a mobile crane, the load chart gives the **gross** rated capacity at a given radius. The actual useful load is:

$$
\boxed{\; L_{\text{useful}} = L_{\text{gross, chart}} - W_{\text{hook block}} - W_{\text{rigging}} - W_{\text{slings}} - W_{\text{spreader}} \;}
$$

**Numerical Illustration (typical KTU sub-part question).**
- Crane chart at 12 m radius: 8.0 t
- Hook block: 0.4 t
- Sling set (4-leg): 0.2 t
- Spreader beam: 0.3 t

$$
L_{\text{useful}} = 8.0 - 0.4 - 0.2 - 0.3 = 7.1\;\text{tonnes}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Total weight below the boom tip must be subtracted from the chart value — not just the load.** This is a repeated favourite 7-mark sub-question in KTU ESE.

## 3.5 Derivation: Reeving (Multiple-Parts-of-Line) Capacity

When a load $W$ is lifted by a block-and-tackle with $n$ parts of line supporting the moving block and sheave efficiency $\eta$:

$$
\boxed{\; P = \dfrac{W}{n \cdot \eta} \;}
$$

where $P$ is the pull required at the free (operator) end of the rope. For a 4-part reeving with $\eta = 0.97$ per sheave (3 sheaves $\Rightarrow \eta^3 \approx 0.91$):

$$
P = \frac{1000}{4 \times 0.91} = \frac{1000}{3.64} \approx 274.7\;\text{kg pull}
$$

The rated capacity of the **hoist line** itself is $W/n$ before derating; the **operator pull** is $W/(n\eta)$ after friction.
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# Module 2 — Rated Capacities: Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Decision Flowchart: From Load to Sling Selection

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Identify Load Weight W (kg)"] --> B["Count Number of Sling Legs n"]
    B --> C{"Sling Angle Theta from Horizontal?"}
    C -->|Known| D["Compute Leg Tension T = W over n sin Theta"]
    C -->|Unknown| E["Assume Worst Case Theta = 30 degrees"]
    D --> F{"Hitch Type?"}
    E --> F
    F -->|Vertical| G["Use Nameplate WLL Directly"]
    F -->|Choker| H["Apply 0.75 Derating Factor"]
    F -->|Basket| I["Multiply WLL by 2 for 2 Legs"]
    G --> J["Effective WLL = WLL nameplate x F angle"]
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K["Select Sling with WLL greater than T"]
    K --> L{"Environment Hot, Chemical, Shock?"}
    L -->|Yes| M["Apply Additional F env Derating 0.67 to 0.85"]
    L -->|No| N["Final Selection Confirmed"]
    M --> N
```

## 4.2 Block Diagram: Components of a Crane's Rated Capacity

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A["Crane Chart Capacity RC gross at Radius R"] --> B["Subtract Hook Block Weight"]
    B --> C["Subtract Rigging Weight Slings, Shackles, Hooks"]
    C --> D["Subtract Spreader Beam Weight if Any"]
    D --> E["Net Useful Lifting Capacity L useful"]
    E --> F{"L useful greater than or equal to Load W?"}
    F -->|Yes| G["Lift Approved - Safe to Proceed"]
    F -->|No| H["REDUCE RADIUS or CHANGE CRANE or LIFT WITH TAILS"]
```

## 4.3 Schematic: Sling Angles and Resulting Leg Tensions (2 t load)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    subgraph L1["2-Leg Sling at 90 degrees (Vertical)"]
        A1["Load W = 2000 kg"] --> A2["T per leg = 1000 kg"]
    end
    subgraph L2["2-Leg Sling at 60 degrees"]
        B1["Load W = 2000 kg"] --> B2["T per leg = 1154.7 kg"]
    end
    subgraph L3["2-Leg Sling at 45 degrees"]
        C1["Load W = 2000 kg"] --> C2["T per leg = 1414.2 kg"]
    end
    subgraph L4["2-Leg Sling at 30 degrees"]
        D1["Load W = 2000 kg"] --> D2["T per leg = 2000 kg"]
    end
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** As the sling leg angle $\theta$ from horizontal **decreases** (i.e. the sling goes *flatter*), the tension in each leg **rises sharply**. At 30° the leg tension equals the full weight of the load — sling capacity is fully consumed in *carrying itself* with nothing left for the load. This is the engineering reason for the **"30° minimum sling angle"** rule found in every rigging code.

## 4.4 Forklift Load-Centre Decision Tree

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    P["Forklift Nameplate: L rated at LC rated"] --> Q["Measure Actual Load Centre LC actual"]
    Q --> R{"LC actual greater than LC rated?"}
    R -->|Yes| S["Capacity REDUCED - Apply L net Formula"]
    R -->|No| T["Capacity UNCHANGED or Higher"]
    S --> U["L net = L rated times LC rated over LC actual"]
    U --> V["Compare L net to Weight of Load"]
    V --> W{"L net greater than or equal to Load?"}
    W -->|Yes| X["Lift Permitted"]
    W -->|No| Y["Lift PROHIBITED - Reduce Load or Change Truck"]
```

## 4.5 The "Capacity Reduction Cascade" (Visual Hierarchy)

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A1["Ultimate Breaking Strength B"] --> A2["Divide by Safety Factor SF 4 to 5"]
    A2 --> A3["Rated Capacity WLL Nameplate"]
    A3 --> A4["Apply Hitch Factor F hitch less than or equal to 1"]
    A4 --> A5["Apply Sling Angle Factor sin Theta"]
    A5 --> A6["Apply Environmental Factor F env"]
    A6 --> A7["Effective Working Capacity in Field"]
    A7 --> A8{"Load less than or equal to A7?"}
    A8 -->|Yes| A9["SAFE LIFT"]
    A8 -->|No| A10["UNSAFE - DO NOT LIFT"]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Reading the cascade:** Each box **reduces** the available capacity. The nameplate WLL is the *most optimistic* number. The actual allowable load on a given day, with a given hitch, angle, and environment, is the *final* box A7 — and this is the number the rigger must compare against the actual load weight. This is the **decision logic that KTU examiners expect** in any 14-mark rated-capacity question.
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# Module 2 — Rated Capacities: KTU 2024 ESE Question Bank & Recap

## PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 marks each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024]
**Define the term "Rated Capacity" as applied to lifting appliances. Differentiate between Safe Working Load (SWL) and Working Load Limit (WLL).**

**Model Answer:**

**Rated Capacity:** It is the maximum load that a lifting appliance, machine, or accessory is designed and certified by the manufacturer to handle under specified operating conditions.

**Difference between SWL and WLL:**

| Aspect | SWL | WLL |
|--------|-----|-----|
| Full form | Safe Working Load | Working Load Limit |
| Era of use | Older UK / European standards | Modern ASME / ISO standards |
| Meaning focus | The load that is *safe* in service | The load that is the *limit* permitted |
| Hidden safety | Often ambiguous about dynamic loads | Explicitly includes static + dynamic considerations |
| Current status | **Deprecated** by most modern standards | **Preferred** term today |

*(3 marks: 1 for definition + 2 for comparison.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]
**State the standard safety factors used for (a) wire-rope slings, (b) chain slings, and (c) lifting hooks. Justify why safety factors are necessary.**

**Model Answer:**

| Equipment | Safety Factor | Breaking Load : WLL |
|-----------|---------------|---------------------|
| Wire-rope sling | 5 : 1 | 5 |
| Chain sling | 4 : 1 | 4 |
| Hook (overhead crane) | 5 : 1 | 5 |

**Justification (3 key points):**
1. To absorb **dynamic / shock loading** during lifting, lowering, and swinging, which can momentarily double the static load.
2. To compensate for **wear, corrosion, and fatigue** that reduce the effective cross-section during service life.
3. To cover **uncertainty** in load estimation, sling angle calculation, and environmental degradation.

*(3 marks: 1.5 for the table + 1.5 for the justification.)*

---

## PART B — Full 14-Mark Questions (Choose ONE)

### ⭐ Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – May 2024]

**(a) Derive the expression for the tension in each leg of a symmetric two-leg sling that supports a vertical load $W$ at a horizontal sling angle $\theta$.** *(7 marks)*

**(b) A 3.0 tonne load is to be lifted by a 2-leg wire-rope sling. The sling has a vertical WLL of 1.5 tonne per leg. If the sling angle from horizontal is 30°, determine whether the lift is safe. State clearly all the derating factors you apply.** *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

**Step 1.** Consider a symmetric two-leg sling, each leg making an angle $\theta$ with the horizontal floor, supporting a load $W$ that hangs vertically.

**Step 2.** Resolve the tension $T$ in each leg into components:
- Vertical component: $T\sin\theta$
- Horizontal component: $T\cos\theta$

**Step 3.** The two horizontal components cancel (by symmetry), and the two vertical components together balance the load:

$$
2\,T\sin\theta = W
$$

**Step 4.** Therefore, the tension in each leg is:

$$
\boxed{\; T = \frac{W}{2\sin\theta} \;}
$$

**Step 5.** For a general $n$-leg symmetric sling:

$$
T = \frac{W}{n\sin\theta}
$$

**Step 6.** Special cases:
- $\theta = 90°$ (vertical leg): $T = W/n$ ✓
- $\theta = 60°$: $T = W/(n \times 0.866) = 1.155\,W/n$ (tension increases 15.5 %)
- $\theta = 30°$: $T = W/(n \times 0.5) = 2W/n$ (tension doubles — practical limit)

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

**Step 1 — Identify the data.**
- Load $W = 3.0$ tonne
- Number of legs $n = 2$
- Vertical WLL of each leg $V = 1.5$ tonne
- Horizontal sling angle $\theta = 30°$

**Step 2 — Apply the angle factor.**

$$
\sin 30° = 0.5
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the effective WLL of one leg at this angle.**

$$
WLL_{\text{eff, one leg}} = V \times \sin\theta = 1.5 \times 0.5 = 0.75 \;\text{tonne}
$$

**Step 4 — Total effective capacity of the 2-leg sling at 30°.**

$$
WLL_{\text{total, 2-leg at 30°}} = 2 \times WLL_{\text{eff, one leg}} = 2 \times 0.75 = 1.5 \;\text{tonnes}
$$

**Step 5 — Compute the actual tension in each leg at the given load.**

$$
T = \frac{W}{2\sin\theta} = \frac{3.0}{2 \times 0.5} = \frac{3.0}{1.0} = 3.0 \;\text{tonnes per leg}
$$

**Step 6 — Compare and decide.**

$$
T = 3.0 \;\text{tonnes/leg} \quad \text{vs} \quad WLL_{\text{eff, one leg}} = 0.75 \;\text{tonne/leg}
$$

$$
\therefore\ T \gg WLL_{\text{eff}}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Verdict: The lift is UNSAFE. The required tension (3.0 t/leg) is 4 times the effective capacity (0.75 t/leg).**
>
> **Recommendation:** Increase the sling angle to at least **60°** from horizontal (which would raise the effective leg capacity to 1.3 t/leg, still not enough — need 1.5 t/leg, requiring $\theta = \arcsin(1.0) = 90°$ i.e. vertical, *or* use a sling of higher vertical WLL, or use a 3-leg sling).

---

### ⭐ Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]

**(a) A forklift truck has a rated capacity of 2,500 kg at a standard load centre of 600 mm. A load of 2,200 kg is to be picked up, and its centre of gravity is 800 mm from the fork face. Determine whether the lift is safe.** *(7 marks)*

**(b) A mobile crane has a load-chart capacity of 9.0 tonne at a working radius of 10 metres. The hook block weighs 0.35 tonne, the 4-leg sling weighs 0.20 tonne, and a spreader beam weighs 0.45 tonne. Find the net useful load the crane can lift.** *(7 marks)*

#### Model Solution to Part (a):

**Step 1 — Identify the given data.**
- Rated capacity: $L_{\text{rated}} = 2500$ kg
- Standard load centre: $LC_{\text{rated}} = 600$ mm
- Actual load: $W = 2200$ kg
- Actual load centre: $LC_{\text{actual}} = 800$ mm

**Step 2 — Apply the forklift capacity formula.**

$$
L_{\text{net}} = L_{\text{rated}} \times \frac{LC_{\text{rated}}}{LC_{\text{actual}}}
$$

$$
L_{\text{net}} = 2500 \times \frac{600}{800} = 2500 \times 0.75 = 1875 \;\text{kg}
$$

**Step 3 — Compare actual load to net capacity.**

$$
W = 2200\;\text{kg} \quad > \quad L_{\text{net}} = 1875\;\text{kg}
$$

**Step 4 — Conclusion.**

> [!WARNING]
> **The lift is UNSAFE.** The actual load (2,200 kg) exceeds the reduced net capacity (1,875 kg) by 325 kg. The truck will tip forward before the load leaves the pallet.
>
> **Remedy:** Shift the load closer to the mast, reduce the load weight, or select a larger forklift.

---

#### Model Solution to Part (b):

**Step 1 — Given data.**
- Chart capacity at 10 m radius: $L_{\text{gross}} = 9.0$ t
- Hook block: $W_{hb} = 0.35$ t
- 4-leg sling: $W_{s} = 0.20$ t
- Spreader beam: $W_{sp} = 0.45$ t

**Step 2 — Net useful load formula.**

$$
L_{\text{useful}} = L_{\text{gross}} - W_{hb} - W_{s} - W_{sp}
$$

**Step 3 — Substitute the values.**

$$
L_{\text{useful}} = 9.0 - 0.35 - 0.20 - 0.45 = 8.0 \;\text{tonnes}
$$

**Step 4 — Verify against the proposed load.** If the load to be lifted is ≤ 8.0 t, the lift is safe; otherwise the radius must be reduced (which raises the chart capacity) or the load rejected.

> [!WARNING]
> **Examiner's Pitfall:** Many students **forget to subtract the hook block** or only subtract the load (mistakenly thinking the chart already accounts for everything). Always subtract the **total weight below the boom tip** (hook block + all rigging + slings + spreader + any attachments). Deduct 1 mark for missing each item, up to a maximum of 3 marks in this sub-question.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU EXAMINER'S VALUATION WARNING — Common Mark-Losing Pitfalls on Rated Capacity Questions:**
> 1. **Wrong angle reference** — confusing "angle to the horizontal" with "angle to the vertical". The KTU convention is **horizontal**, so $\sin\theta$ is the angle factor. (Loss: 1–2 marks)
> 2. **Forgetting to subtract rigging from crane chart** — examiners are strict about this. (Loss: 1 mark)
> 3. **Using $T = W/(2\cos\theta)$ instead of $T = W/(2\sin\theta)$** — sign-of-the-cosine error. (Loss: 1 mark)
> 4. **Failing to state the safety factor** in any rigging solution. (Loss: 0.5 mark)
> 5. **Giving a final "SAFE" verdict without showing the comparison** — must explicitly write $T \le WLL$ or $T > WLL$. (Loss: 1 mark)
> 6. **Mixing units** (kg vs. tonnes) inside a single calculation. Convert to one unit **first**. (Loss: 0.5 mark)

---

## TOPIC RECAP & RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Print this box and read it 5 minutes before walking into the exam hall.**

- ✅ **Rated capacity = manufacturer-declared maximum load** under defined conditions; it is the basis of the safety factor.
- ✅ **Safety factor = Breaking Load ÷ WLL.** Memorize **5 (wire-rope sling, hook), 4 (chain sling), 1.67 (mobile crane stability).**
- ✅ **WLL is the modern term; SWL is the legacy term** (both mean the same in practice). WLL is *never* the breaking load.
- ✅ **Vertical sling tension:** $T = W/n$.
- ✅ **Choker hitch capacity:** $0.75 \times WLL_{\text{vertical}}$.
- ✅ **Basket hitch capacity:** $2 \times WLL_{\text{vertical}}$ (for a single sling looped under the load).
- ✅ **Bridle sling at angle $\theta$ from horizontal:** $T = W/(n\sin\theta)$ — **the most important equation of Module 2.**
- ✅ **Minimum sling angle from horizontal = 30°** (industry rule); below this, tension becomes dangerously large.
- ✅ **Sling-angle factor** $F = \sin\theta$ — at 60° → 0.866; at 45° → 0.707; at 30° → 0.500.
- ✅ **Forklift net capacity** when load centre changes: $L_{\text{net}} = L_{\text{rated}} \times (LC_{\text{rated}} / LC_{\text{actual}})$.
- ✅ **Crane net capacity** = chart capacity − (hook block + all rigging + spreader + slings). **Subtract *everything* below the boom tip.**
- ✅ **Reeving (n parts of line):** $P = W/(n \eta)$ where $\eta \approx 0.97$ per sheave.
- ✅ **All $\theta$ in rigging equations are measured from the horizontal floor, not the vertical.** Read the question twice.
- ✅ **Always show the final comparison** $T \le WLL_{\text{eff}}$ and give a one-line **verdict (SAFE / UNSAFE)**.
- ✅ **Apply environmental derating** (heat, chemical, shock) as a separate factor in *every* practical problem.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->
