## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

#### **Question A1: Define a hoisting apparatus. List its main sub-categories.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A *hoisting apparatus* is a mechanical assembly used for the vertical lifting, lowering, and controlled suspension of loads by means of a prime mover, transmission system, and load-handling attachments.

Main sub-categories:

1. **Hoists** (chain block, lever hoist, electric hoist)
2. **Cranes** (EOT, gantry, jib, mobile, tower)
3. **Jacks** (screw, hydraulic, pneumatic)
4. **Winches** (hand, electric, air)
5. **Rigging gear** (slings, hooks, shackles, eye-bolts)
6. **Supporting elements** (wire ropes, chains, fibre ropes)

> [!TIP]
> **3-mark allocation:** 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for listing 4 sub-categories, 1 mark for examples.

---

#### **Question A2: What is the function of a safety latch on a lifting hook? State two statutory requirements for periodic inspection of a hoist.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

**Function of safety latch (1 mark):** A safety latch (also called a *mousing* or *safety catch*) is a spring-loaded or gravity-actuated device fitted to a lifting hook. Its function is to **prevent the sling or load from accidentally slipping off the hook** during lifting, lowering, or transit. It is a *passive* safety device that requires no operator action.

**Two statutory requirements for periodic inspection (2 marks):**

1. **Daily pre-shift visual inspection** by the operator (per IS 13367 / OSHA 1910.179). Items checked: hook for cracks, deformation, throat opening increase, safety latch operation, wire rope for broken wires (>10 % in one rope lay = condemn), brake function.

2. **Annual third-party load test** at **1.25 × SWL** (per IS 3177 / Factories Act 1948, Section 28). The test certificate is valid for 12 months only. A copy must be displayed on the crane/hoist.

> [!TIP]
> **3-mark allocation:** 1 mark for hook function, 1 mark per inspection requirement.

---

### 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 ESE Pattern:** Each module in PESFT522 has a 14-mark question with *internal choice*. You will get *either* Question A *or* Question B, and you must answer *one* of them fully. Both questions below are designed to be standalone (no shared sub-parts).

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — Block-and-Tackle, Law of Machines, and Sling Tension** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2024 Model]`

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

In a hoisting machine, the following test results were obtained:

| Load $W$ (N) | Effort $P$ (N) |
|---|---|
| 0 | 25 |
| 500 | 110 |
| 1000 | 195 |
| 1500 | 280 |
| 2000 | 365 |

(i) Find the **law of the machine** ($P = aW + b$) using any two data points.
(ii) Determine the **mechanical advantage, velocity ratio, and efficiency** at $W = 1500$ N. (Assume $V.R. = 12$ for this machine.)
(iii) What is the **maximum efficiency** that this machine can ever achieve, and at what load does it occur?

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

A 2-leg wire-rope sling, each leg of breaking load 60 kN and factor of safety 5, is used to lift a 4-tonne (40 kN) load. The angle between the two legs is 60°.

(i) Calculate the **SWL of each sling leg**.
(ii) Find the **tension in each leg** during the lift.
(iii) Determine whether the sling is **safe** for the operation, and state the *single most critical safety check* before lifting.

**Model Answer:**

#### Part (a) Solution:

**Step 1 — Find the Law of the Machine** (3 marks)

Using data points $(W_1 = 0, P_1 = 25)$ and $(W_2 = 2000, P_2 = 365)$:

$$P = aW + b$$

At $W = 0$: $b = P = 25$ N.

At $W = 2000$: $365 = 2000a + 25 \Rightarrow a = (365-25)/2000 = 340/2000 = 0.17$.

$$\boxed{P = 0.17W + 25}$$

**Step 2 — MA, VR, η at $W = 1500$ N** (3 marks)

Effort at $W = 1500$ N: $P = 0.17(1500) + 25 = 255 + 25 = 280$ N.

$$M.A. = \dfrac{W}{P} = \dfrac{1500}{280} = 5.357$$

$$V.R. = 12 \text{ (given)}$$

$$\eta = \dfrac{M.A.}{V.R.} = \dfrac{5.357}{12} = 0.4465 = 44.65\%$$

**Step 3 — Maximum efficiency** (1 mark)

As $W \to \infty$, $\eta \to 1/(a \cdot V.R.) = 1/(0.17 \times 12) = 1/2.04 = 0.4902 = 49.02\%$.

**Maximum efficiency is ~49 %, achieved asymptotically at very high loads (theoretically $W \to \infty$).**

#### Part (b) Solution:

**Step 1 — SWL of each leg** (2 marks)

$$SWL = \dfrac{B.L.}{F.S.} = \dfrac{60 \text{ kN}}{5} = 12 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 2 — Tension in each leg** (3 marks)

The angle between the two legs is 60°, so each leg is **30° from the vertical**.

$$T = \dfrac{W}{n \cos\theta} = \dfrac{40}{2 \times \cos 30°} = \dfrac{40}{2 \times 0.866} = \dfrac{40}{1.732} = 23.09 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3 — Safety check** (2 marks)

Per-leg tension = 23.09 kN > SWL = 12 kN.

**The sling is NOT safe.** The required tension per leg exceeds the SWL by ~92 %.

To make the lift safe:
- Use a 4-leg sling (each leg at 30° from vertical), $T = 40/(4 \times 0.866) = 11.55$ kN < 12 kN. ✓ Safe.
- *OR* increase the rope's breaking load: $B.L. = 23.09 \times 5 = 115.45$ kN (i.e., use a heavier-duty sling).
- *OR* increase the angle between legs to ≥ 90° (i.e., 45° each from vertical), $T = 40/(2 \times \cos 45°) = 40/1.414 = 28.28$ kN — *this is even worse*. So angle must be *decreased*.

**The most critical safety check before lifting:** Verify that the **horizontal sling angle is ≥ 30°** (which corresponds to leg angle from vertical ≤ 60°). At 60° between legs (30° from vertical), the horizontal angle is 60° — within the safe limit, but the load itself exceeds capacity. The check is therefore: *Do NOT exceed SWL of the sling system*.

**The most critical pre-lift check is the Sling Angle Indicator / Sling Tension calculation** — and confirming that the *load × angle factor* does not exceed the *rated capacity* of the sling assembly.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — Weston Differential Block, Wire Rope Design, and Crane Stability** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024 Model]`

**Part (a) — 7 marks:**

A **Weston differential pulley block** has a large sheave of diameter 240 mm and a small sheave of diameter 216 mm. The endless chain is on a sprocket of diameter 80 mm.

(i) Calculate the **velocity ratio** of the block.
(ii) If the block is used to lift a load of 30 kN with an overall efficiency of 35 %, find the **effort required at the chain**.
(iii) Find the **tightening-side and slack-side chain tensions**.

**Part (b) — 7 marks:**

A 6×37 wire rope (improved plough steel, $K = 5800$ N/mm²) is used to lift a 50 kN load. Factor of safety is 6.

(i) Calculate the **rope diameter**.
(ii) Find the **minimum drum diameter** and **minimum sheave diameter**.
(iii) State **two safety checks** that must be performed on the wire rope before each shift.

**Model Answer:**

#### Part (a) Solution:

**Step 1 — Velocity Ratio** (2 marks)

Given: $D = 240$ mm (large sheave), $d = 216$ mm (small sheave), $D_e = 80$ mm (effort sprocket).

For a Weston block, the chain wraps the load sheave directly. The net lift per revolution of the differential axle is $\pi(D-d)/2$ (half the difference, because the load hangs from both chains). The effort chain moves $\pi D_e$ per revolution.

$$V.R. = \dfrac{2D_e}{D - d} = \dfrac{2 \times 80}{240 - 216} = \dfrac{160}{24} = 6.667$$

> [!NOTE]
> Some textbooks use $V.R. = 2D/(D-d)$ assuming $D_e = D$. The formula above uses the *sprocket* diameter. Verify with your course textbook.

**Step 2 — Effort Required** (3 marks)

$$M.A. = V.R. \times \eta = 6.667 \times 0.35 = 2.333$$

$$P = \dfrac{W}{M.A.} = \dfrac{30 \text{ kN}}{2.333} = 12.86 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 3 — Chain Tensions** (2 marks)

Load supported by two chain parts: $T_1 + T_2 = W = 30$ kN.

Moment balance on the differential axle: $T_1 \times D = T_2 \times d$ (since the two sheaves rotate together, and assuming zero friction in the bearings — close approximation for chain block).

$$T_1 = \dfrac{W \times d}{D + d} = \dfrac{30 \times 216}{456} = 14.21 \text{ kN}$$

$$T_2 = \dfrac{W \times D}{D + d} = \dfrac{30 \times 240}{456} = 15.79 \text{ kN}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Note:** $T_1 + T_2 = 14.21 + 15.79 = 30$ kN ✓. The *tightening* side (where the load is being pulled up — the larger sheave) has the *higher* tension. This is opposite to intuition but follows from the moment balance.

#### Part (b) Solution:

**Step 1 — Rope Diameter** (3 marks)

$$d = \sqrt{\dfrac{W \times F.S.}{K}} = \sqrt{\dfrac{50000 \times 6}{5800}} = \sqrt{51.72} = 7.19 \text{ mm}$$

Adopt next standard size: $d = 8$ mm.

**Step 2 — Drum and Sheave Diameters** (2 marks)

$$D_{drum} = 25 \times 8 = 200 \text{ mm (minimum)}$$

$$D_{sheave} = 20 \times 8 = 160 \text{ mm (minimum)}$$

**Step 3 — Pre-shift Safety Checks** (2 marks)

1. **Broken wire count:** Count broken wires per rope lay. If broken wires exceed 10 % of the total wires in a rope lay (for 6×37 = 222 wires; hence 22 broken wires in one lay), the rope is *condemned* and must be replaced (per IS 2266).

2. **Diameter reduction:** Measure the rope diameter at three points. If the diameter is reduced by more than 10 % from the nominal (i.e., from 8 mm to 7.2 mm or less) due to internal wear or core failure, the rope is *condemned*.

3. *Alternative check:* Corrosion, kinking, bird-caging, or any heat damage.

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Top 5 Ways Students Lose Marks in Hoisting-Apparatus Questions (KTU 2024 ESE):**
>
> 1. **Confusing Weston block $V.R. = 2D/(D-d)$ with $V.R. = 2D_e/(D-d)$.** The $D$ in the *standard* formula is the *load sheave* diameter, not the effort sprocket. If the question specifies the sprocket diameter, *use it differently*. Always draw the block and label the diameters.
>
> 2. **Wrong angle in sling tension.** The angle in $T = W/(n\cos\theta)$ is measured from the **vertical**, not the horizontal. If the question says "60° from the horizontal," then $\theta = 30°$ from vertical. Many students get this reversed and lose 2 marks.
>
> 3. **Forgetting to round UP to the next standard rope size.** After calculating $d = 7.19$ mm, you MUST write "$d = 8$ mm (next standard size)". Examiner will *not* award full marks for a non-standard size even if the calculation is correct.
>
> 4. **Misapplying factor of safety values.** Hoisting F.S. = 5, *man-riding* F.S. = 10, fibre rope F.S. = 10. Using the wrong F.S. for the application = full marks lost.
>
> 5. **Not stating units in final answers.** KTU examiners *strictly* deduct 0.5 marks for missing units. Always write "$\sigma = 240$ **N/mm²**" or "$d = 8$ **mm**", never just the number.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Print this section and use it as a last-night revision checklist before the KTU ESE.**

- **Hoisting apparatus** = equipment for vertical (or near-vertical) lifting, lowering, and controlled suspension of loads.
- **Six sub-categories:** Hoists, Cranes, Jacks, Winches, Rigging gear, Supporting elements.
- **Law of Machines:** $P = aW + b$. The two constants are determined experimentally.
- **Efficiency:** $\eta = M.A./V.R. = W/(V.R. \times P)$. Always $\leq 1$, usually 30–90 % for hoists.
- **Power for hoisting:** $P = W \cdot v / \eta$ (Watts). Add $a/g$ factor only during *acceleration* (starting).
- **Weston Block:** $V.R. = 2D/(D-d)$ (without effort sprocket) or $V.R. = 2D_e/(D-d)$ (with sprocket). $D$ and $d$ are load sheaves; $D_e$ is the effort sprocket. Always self-locking when $D > d$ and chain has no slip.
- **Block-and-tackle with friction:** $P = (W/n) \times (1+\mu\pi)^{n-1}$. Efficiency *decreases* with more sheaves.
- **Wire Rope Breaking Load:** $B.L. = K \cdot d^2$ where $K$ depends on rope grade. Standard $F.S. = 5$ for hoisting.
- **Wire Rope Selection:** $d = \sqrt{W \times F.S. / K}$. Round up to next standard size.
- **Drum Diameter:** $D_{drum} \geq 25d$. **Sheave Diameter:** $D_{sheave} \geq 20d$. The drum needs the *larger* ratio (bends the rope repeatedly).
- **Hook Design:** Trapezoidal cross-section gives *equal-strength* design. F.S. = 3 to 5 depending on capacity and use.
- **Chain Link Stress:** $\sigma_{max} \approx 3WR/d^3$ (bending dominant). Stud-link chains have F.S. = 4.
- **Sling Tension:** $T = W/(n\cos\theta)$ where $\theta$ is from **vertical**. Horizontal sling angle must be $\geq 30°$.
- **Sling Capacity Multipliers** (per leg, for 60° horizontal angle): Single = 1.0, 2-leg = 1.73, 3-leg = 2.6, 4-leg = 3.0.
- **Screw Jack:** Self-locking if helix angle < friction angle (~6° for steel-on-steel). $M.A. = 2\pi l / p$ (approx).
- **Hydraulic Jack:** $M.A. = (D_{ram}/D_{pump})^2$. Very high M.A. (100–1000), high efficiency (~85–95 %).
- **Crane Stability:** $M_{stabilizing} \geq 1.5 \times M_{overturning}$ (per IS 4573, OSHA, and KTU convention).
- **Statutory F.S. values (IS codes):** Hoisting rope = 5, man-riding = 10, haulage = 6, fibre rope = 10, chain = 4, hook = 3–5.
- **Pre-shift inspection must include:** Hook (cracks, throat opening, latch), wire rope (broken wires, corrosion, diameter), brake (function test), limit switches, oil leaks, structural members.
- **Annual load test:** 1.25 × SWL, certificate valid 12 months, must be displayed.
- **Common rope grade constants $K$ (N/mm²):** 1370 MPa → 4900, 1570 MPa → 5800, 1770 MPa → 6700, 1960 MPa → 7600.
- **$g = 9.81$ m/s² always** in SI units for weight calculations.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types - Cranes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT522 - SAFETY IN MATERIAL HANDLING
> - **Module:** Module Module 3
> - **Topic:** Types - Cranes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Definition

A **crane** is a machine equipped with a **hoisting mechanism**, a **system of ropes or chains**, and one or more **sheaves (pulleys)** that are used to lift, lower, and move heavy loads horizontally. In KTU 2024 Scheme material handling terminology, a crane is classified as a **continuous-flow or intermittent-flow material handling equipment** designed primarily for vertical and horizontal transport of discrete unit loads over a fixed or semi-fixed work envelope.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Definition Standard:** According to the KTU Safety in Material Handling syllabus, a crane is defined as a "mechanical lifting device used to move loads in any direction by means of a hoisting mechanism, with safety interlocking features compliant with IS 3177 / IS 4573 standards."

### Key Terminology (KTU Board Standard Vocabulary)

| Term | Definition |
|---|---|
| **Hoist** | The mechanical assembly that performs the actual lifting (drum, motor, gear) |
| **Bridge** | The horizontal beam spanning the work area |
| **Trolley** | The carriage that moves horizontally along the bridge |
| **Sheave** | A pulley wheel over which the rope is reeved |
| **Hook Block** | The assembly at the rope end that carries the load hook |
| **Boom** | The projecting arm of a crane from which the load is suspended |
| **Jib** | An extension of the boom, often with a lattice or telescopic structure |
| **Outrigger** | Stabilising legs extended from a mobile crane base |
| **Counterweight** | Mass added opposite the load to balance moments |
| **SWL (Safe Working Load)** | The maximum load a crane is certified to lift under specific conditions |

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — Real-World Analogy

Think of a crane as the **"extended arm of an industrial giant."** Imagine a person reaching out to pick up a heavy box from the back of a truck — they would first plant their feet firmly (the **outriggers/base**), shift their body weight to balance (the **counterweight**), extend their arm outward (the **boom/jib**), and only then lift the load with their hand (the **hoist and hook**). Every action the person takes instinctively, a crane performs with engineered precision and load-rated components.

> [!NOTE]
> **Why Cranes Matter in Industry:** Cranes are indispensable in construction, shipyards, steel plants, ports, power stations, and warehouses. A single 100-tonne crawler crane can replace dozens of workers and weeks of manual effort, but improper use causes some of the deadliest accidents in the material handling industry — making **crane safety a mandatory KTU module focus.**

## 1.3 Classification Basis (KTU Board Pattern)

Cranes can be classified along **four standard axes** as per KTU 2024 Scheme:

1. **By Type of Motion** — Mobile, Stationary, or Hybrid
2. **By Type of Power** — Manual, Electric, Hydraulic, Diesel, Pneumatic
3. **By Structural Configuration** — Bridge, Boom, Jib, or Mast type
4. **By Service Classification** — Light-duty, Medium-duty, Heavy-duty, Extra-heavy-duty

## 1.4 The Safety Imperative

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crane-related accidents account for nearly 25% of all material handling fatalities globally.** The KTU 2024 syllabus places strong emphasis on understanding crane types because the **type of crane dictates the safety protocol, inspection checklist, and operator licensing requirements.**

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Major Types of Cranes (KTU Module 3 Core Syllabus)

The KTU 2024 Scheme Module 3 specifically focuses on classifying cranes into the following categories:

### 2.1.1 Overhead Travelling Crane (Bridge Crane)

**Operational Principle:**
- The crane consists of a **bridge girder** that spans the entire width of the bay.
- A **trolley** runs along the bridge and carries the hoist.
- The entire bridge runs on rails mounted on elevated runways along the building walls or columns.

**Key Features:**
- Maximum utilisation of floor space (no floor obstruction)
- Ideal for repetitive lifting in a fixed work area
- Common in machine shops, steel plants, warehouses

**KTU Safety Considerations:**
- Anti-collision devices between adjacent cranes
- Warning horns and travel limit switches
- **IS 807 / IS 3177** compliance for structural design

> [!NOTE]
> **Analogy:** Think of an overhead travelling crane as a "ceiling-mounted railway train" — the bridge is the track, the trolley is the engine car, and the hook is the freight container that moves both along the track and across it.

### 2.1.2 Gantry Crane

**Operational Principle:**
- Similar to an overhead travelling crane, but the bridge is supported by **legs running on ground-level rails** instead of elevated runways.
- Used outdoors or where building columns cannot support runway beams.

**Variants:**
- **Full Gantry** — Both legs run on rails
- **Semi-Gantry** — One leg runs on elevated runway, one on ground rail
- **Portable Gantry** — Small, manually operated, with rubber tyres

### 2.1.3 Jib Crane

**Operational Principle:**
- A **vertical mast (column)** supports a horizontal **jib arm**.
- The hoist trolley moves along the jib, and the entire jib can rotate around the mast.
- Provides a **circular work area**.

**Variants:**
- Wall-mounted jib crane
- Floor-mounted jib crane
- 360° slewing jib crane

**Use Case:** Workstations in machine shops, loading docks, and assembly lines.

### 2.1.4 Mobile Crane

**Operational Principle:**
- Mounted on a **truck, crawler, or wheeled carrier** for portability.
- Equipped with a **hydraulic telescopic boom** or **lattice boom**.

**Sub-Types:**

| Sub-Type | Description | Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Truck-mounted Crane** | Carrier is a rubber-tyred truck; travels on roads | Construction sites, road projects |
| **Crawler Crane** | Mounted on tracked undercarriage; heavy-duty | Heavy construction, mining |
| **Rough Terrain Crane** | All-wheel drive, short wheelbase | Off-road construction |
| **All-Terrain Crane** | Combines road and off-road capability | Versatile field operations |
| **Pick-and-Carry Crane** | Lifts and transports short distances | Factories, small yards |

### 2.1.5 Tower Crane

**Operational Principle:**
- A **vertical mast (tower)** of high structural steel.
- A **horizontal jib** (working jib) carries the load, balanced by a **counter-jib with counterweight**.
- **Slewing mechanism** rotates the jib 360°.

**Use Case:** High-rise building construction. Often the **tallest machine on a construction site**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Point:** Tower cranes are the most frequently asked crane type in KTU board exams due to their unique counter-jib balancing principle.

### 2.1.6 Floating Crane (Ship-mounted Crane)

**Operational Principle:**
- Mounted on a barge or ship.
- Used in **port operations, offshore construction, salvage**.

### 2.1.7 Telescopic Crane

**Operational Principle:**
- Uses a **multi-stage hydraulic boom** that can extend and retract.
- Versatile reach and compact transport dimensions.

### 2.1.8 Loader Crane (Knuckle-Boom Crane)

**Operational Principle:**
- Articulated boom with **multiple hinge points** (like a human arm).
- Mounted on trucks for **self-loading/unloading**.

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

The following equations are **mandatory** for KTU 2024 ESE problems on crane types:

| Formula | Symbol Meaning | Application Context |
|---|---|---|
| $W_{eff} = W \cdot \left(1 + k\right)$ | Effective load factor with shock factor $k$ | Calculating dynamic load on the crane |
| $M_{tip} = W \cdot R$ | Tip-over moment about the tipping edge | Mobile crane stability check |
| $M_{stab} = W_{cw} \cdot d_{cw} - W_{crane} \cdot d_{c}$ | Stabilising moment from counterweight | Tower crane, mobile crane |
| $SF = \dfrac{M_{stab}}{M_{tip}} \geq 1.5$ | Stability Factor (must be ≥ 1.5 per IS codes) | Crane safety against tipping |
| $T = \dfrac{W \cdot v}{g}$ | Hoist tension considering velocity $v$ | Dynamic tension in hoisting rope |
| $H = \dfrac{P \cdot \eta}{W \cdot v}$ | Hoist motor power calculation | Motor sizing for hoist |
| $D_{min} = d \cdot k_{bend}$ | Minimum sheave diameter ($k_{bend}$ = bend factor per IS) | Sheave/pulley design |
| $L_{slack} = \sqrt{L^2 + h^2} - L$ | Slack length calculation for sling | Sling selection |

> [!NOTE]
> **Bend Factor ($k_{bend}$):** As per **IS 3177**, the bend factor for wire rope over a sheave is typically **18 to 24** for ordinary hoist service. This is a **high-yield KTU value** to remember.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

The classification of cranes forms the foundation of:
- **Erection planning** in heavy industrial projects
- **Failure mode analysis** in accident investigations
- **Operator licensing regimes** (different licences for different crane types)
- **Cost estimation** in tender documents (BOQ)
- **Insurance and risk assessment** in industrial safety audits

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Industry Connect:** Engineers in the **FACT (Fertilisers and Chemicals Travancore), BPCL Kochi Refinery, Cochin Shipyard, and various Kerala construction projects** routinely work with the crane types listed above. Understanding crane types is essential for safety officers and plant engineers.

## 2.4 Step-by-Step Logic of Crane Selection

A KTU board favourite is the **systematic selection logic** for cranes. The decision tree is:

1. **Load Weight (W) Required** → Determines SWL class
2. **Lift Height (H) Required** → Determines hoist drum capacity
3. **Horizontal Reach (R) Required** → Determines boom/jib length
4. **Frequency of Use** → Determines duty cycle (FEM/CMAA classification)
5. **Work Environment** → Indoor/Outdoor, Hazardous, Marine
6. **Mobility Required** → Stationary vs Mobile
7. **Power Availability** → Electric, Diesel, Hydraulic

Each step narrows down the crane type, and **the final selection is validated by stability factor and structural calculations**.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Crane Stability Factor (Mobile Crane)

**Statement:** Derive the stability factor expression for a mobile crane with outriggers and verify the safety condition.

### Given:
- $W$ = Total lifted load (kg)
- $R$ = Horizontal radius from slewing axis to load centre of gravity (m)
- $W_{c}$ = Total weight of the crane (kg)
- $R_{c}$ = Horizontal distance from slewing axis to crane centre of gravity (m)
- $W_{cw}$ = Counterweight mass (kg)
- $R_{cw}$ = Horizontal distance from slewing axis to counterweight (m)
- $T$ = Reaction at tipping edge (kg)

### Derivation:

Consider a mobile crane pivoted about its tipping edge $O$ on the outrigger side. For static equilibrium under worst-case loading, the load is at maximum radius and the counterweight is on the opposite side.

**Step 1: Identify the moments about the tipping axis.**

Taking moments about the tipping edge, with tipping direction defined as the side opposite the load:

$$M_{tip} = W \cdot R$$

This is the **overturning moment** caused by the lifted load acting at radius $R$.

**Step 2: Identify the stabilising moments.**

The stabilising moments come from two sources:

$$M_{stab} = W_{c} \cdot R_{c} + W_{cw} \cdot R_{cw}$$

Where:
- $W_{c} \cdot R_{c}$ is the moment from the crane's own weight
- $W_{cw} \cdot R_{cw}$ is the moment from the counterweight

**Step 3: Apply the KTU safety condition.**

For the crane to be safe against tipping:

$$M_{stab} \geq M_{tip}$$

For practical safety with a **factor of safety of 1.5 (per IS 4573 and KTU syllabus)**:

$$SF = \frac{M_{stab}}{M_{tip}} = \frac{W_{c} \cdot R_{c} + W_{cw} \cdot R_{cw}}{W \cdot R} \geq 1.5$$

**Step 4: Working Example.**

A mobile crane has $W = 8000$ kg, $R = 6$ m, $W_{c} = 12{,}000$ kg, $R_{c} = 1.5$ m, $W_{cw} = 3000$ kg, $R_{cw} = 4$ m. Compute the stability factor.

**Step 4a: Compute $M_{tip}$.**

$$M_{tip} = 8000 \times 6 = 48{,}000 \text{ kg·m}$$

**Step 4b: Compute $M_{stab}$.**

$$M_{stab} = (12{,}000 \times 1.5) + (3000 \times 4) = 18{,}000 + 12{,}000 = 30{,}000 \text{ kg·m}$$

**Step 4c: Compute $SF$.**

$$SF = \frac{30{,}000}{48{,}000} = 0.625$$

**Step 4d: Interpretation.**

$$SF = 0.625 < 1.5 \implies \text{UNSAFE — Crane will tip!}$$

The crane is **unsafe** for this load configuration. To make it safe, either:
- Reduce the radius $R$ by moving closer to the load
- Add more counterweight
- Extend outriggers to change the tipping geometry

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Hoist Motor Power Calculation

**Statement:** Derive the power required to lift a load with a crane hoist, accounting for efficiency losses.

### Given:
- $W$ = Load to be lifted (kg)
- $v$ = Hoisting velocity (m/s)
- $g$ = Acceleration due to gravity = **9.81 m/s²**
- $\eta$ = Mechanical efficiency of hoist (typically 0.70 to 0.85)

### Derivation:

**Step 1: Compute the lifting force.**

The hoist must overcome the gravitational force on the load:

$$F = W \cdot g \quad \text{(in Newtons, when W is in kg)}$$

**Step 2: Compute the power output required at the hook.**

$$P_{out} = F \cdot v = W \cdot g \cdot v$$

**Step 3: Account for mechanical efficiency.**

The motor must deliver more power than the useful output, because of friction in gears, sheaves, and bearings:

$$P_{motor} = \frac{P_{out}}{\eta} = \frac{W \cdot g \cdot v}{\eta}$$

**Step 4: Substitute numerical values for KTU standard problem.**

For a $W = 2000$ kg load, $v = 0.5$ m/s, $\eta = 0.80$:

$$P_{motor} = \frac{2000 \times 9.81 \times 0.5}{0.80}$$

$$P_{motor} = \frac{9810}{0.80} = 12{,}262.5 \text{ W} \approx 12.26 \text{ kW}$$

Converting to horsepower:

$$P_{motor} = \frac{12.26}{0.746} \approx 16.44 \text{ HP}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Board Tip:** When solving power problems, **always state the efficiency value explicitly** and convert the final answer to both kW and HP for completeness — examiners reward unit awareness.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Sheave Diameter Selection

**Statement:** Determine the minimum sheave diameter for a crane hoist rope.

### Given:
- $d$ = Rope nominal diameter (mm)
- $k_{bend}$ = Bend factor (from **IS 3177 Table 4**)

### Derivation:

**Step 1: The relationship per IS 3177.**

The minimum sheave groove diameter $D_{min}$ is given by:

$$D_{min} = d \cdot k_{bend}$$

**Step 2: Select $k_{bend}$ for a typical crane.**

For a standard crane hoist with $6 \times 37$ construction wire rope, the bend factor is **$k_{bend} = 20$**.

**Step 3: Compute for $d = 16$ mm rope.**

$$D_{min} = 16 \times 20 = 320 \text{ mm}$$

So a sheave of **minimum 320 mm pitch diameter** is required. This is a KTU high-yield value.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Crane Type Comparison

| Crane Type | Max Lift Capacity Range | Reach / Span | Best Application | Critical Safety Feature |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Overhead Travelling** | 5–500 tonnes | Bay width (5–40 m) | Workshops, steel plants | Anti-collision switch |
| **Gantry** | 10–1000 tonnes | Up to 100+ m | Ports, shipyards, outdoor yards | Rail clamp / storm lock |
| **Jib** | 0.5–10 tonnes | 3–10 m radius | Workstation loading | Slew limit switch |
| **Mobile (Truck)** | 10–1200 tonnes | 15–100+ m radius | Construction, projects | Outrigger load sensor |
| **Crawler** | 50–3500 tonnes | 15–150 m radius | Heavy lift, mining | Track tension monitoring |
| **Tower** | 1.5–80 tonnes at jib tip | 30–85 m jib length | High-rise construction | Slew brake, wind speed sensor |
| **Telescopic** | 10–1200 tonnes | 20–120 m radius | Versatile field use | Boom extension interlock |
| **Floating** | 5–8000 tonnes | 15–100 m radius | Marine salvage, ports | List and trim indicator |

---

## 3.5 KTU Pattern Problem — Multi-Step Crane Selection

**Question:** A factory needs a crane to lift loads up to 5 tonnes in a 20 m wide bay, with 50 cycles per shift, indoor operation, electric power available. Recommend the crane type with full justification.

### Model Solution:

**Step 1: Capacity check** — 5 tonnes = 5,000 kg → Medium-duty class.

**Step 2: Span check** — Bay width 20 m → Overhead travelling crane or gantry feasible.

**Step 3: Frequency** — 50 cycles per shift → Moderate frequency, CMAA Class C or D.

**Step 4: Environment** — Indoor, electric power available → Electric overhead travelling crane is optimal.

**Step 5: Justification:**
- Indoor operation with no ground space → **Overhead travelling crane** saves floor space.
- Electric power allows clean, silent operation.
- Span 20 m is well within standard bridge girder range.
- Medium duty (Class C) matches the cycle count.

**Recommendation: Electric Overhead Travelling Crane (EOT), 5-tonne SWL, 20 m span, Class C duty.**

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Functional Architecture — Crane Classification Tree

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[CRANE] --> B[STATIONARY]
    A --> C[MOBILE]
    A --> D[TOWER]
    B --> B1[Overhead Travelling Bridge]
    B --> B2[Gantry Crane]
    B --> B3[Jib Crane]
    B1 --> B1A[Single Girder]
    B1 --> B1B[Double Girder]
    B2 --> B2A[Full Gantry]
    B2 --> B2B[Semi Gantry]
    B3 --> B3A[Wall Mounted]
    B3 --> B3B[Floor Mounted Slewing]
    C --> C1[Truck Mounted]
    C --> C2[Crawler Mounted]
    C --> C3[Rough Terrain]
    C --> C4[All Terrain]
    C --> C5[Pick and Carry]
    D --> D1[Flat Top Tower]
    D --> D2[Hammerhead Tower]
    D --> D3[Luffing Jib Tower]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Diagram Reading Guide:** Start at node A and traverse downward. Each branch represents a major crane category. For KTU ESE descriptive answers, this tree can be redrawn in the answer sheet to score full marks on classification questions.

## 4.2 Functional Block Diagram — Overhead Travelling Crane Architecture

```mermaid
graph LR
    CR[CRANE BRIDGE GIRDER] --> TR[TROLLEY]
    TR --> HS[HOIST ASSEMBLY]
    HS --> RB[WIRE ROPE]
    RB --> HB[HOOK BLOCK]
    HB --> LD[LOAD]
    CR --> R1[RAIL 1]
    CR --> R2[RAIL 2]
    TR --> M1[HOIST MOTOR]
    TR --> M2[TROLLEY MOTOR]
    M1 --> GB[GEARBOX]
    GB --> DR[DRUM]
    DR --> RB
    LD --> CWT[COUNTERWEIGHT OFFLOAD]
```

## 4.3 Tower Crane Functional Schematic

```mermaid
graph TD
    M[MAST / TOWER] --> SJ[SLEWING UNIT]
    SJ --> WJ[WORKING JIB]
    SJ --> CJ[COUNTER JIB]
    WJ --> TL[TROLLEY]
    TL --> HK[HOOK]
    CJ --> CW[COUNTERWEIGHT]
    M --> B[BASE]
    B --> F[FOUNDATION]
    M --> CS[CLIMBING SYSTEM]
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Insight from the Diagram:** The counter-jib and counterweight in a tower crane create the **stabilising moment** that allows the working jib to lift heavy loads at long radii. The slewing unit allows 360° rotation, and the climbing system enables the mast to be extended as the building rises.

## 4.4 Crane Safety System Architecture

```mermaid
graph TD
    OP[OPERATOR CABIN] --> CTL[CONTROL CONSOLE]
    CTL --> SLS[SWL LIMIT SWITCH]
    CTL --> OLS[OVERLOAD SENSOR]
    CTL --> TWS[TWO BLOCK WARNING]
    CTL --> ALS[ANTI TWO BLOCK]
    CTL --> SLS2[SLEW LIMIT]
    CTL --> BLS[BOOM LIMIT]
    CTL --> WSS[WIND SPEED SENSOR]
    OP --> IND[INDICATORS AND ALARMS]
    OLS --> EM[EMERGENCY STOP]
    WSS --> EM
    BLS --> EM
```

## 4.5 Mobile Crane Stability Geometry

```mermaid
graph LR
    A[CRANE CENTER] --> B[LIFTED LOAD]
    A --> C[COUNTERWEIGHT]
    B --> D[RADIUS R]
    C --> E[DISTANCE CW TO CENTER]
    A --> F[OUTRIGGER TIP EDGE]
    F --> G[TIPPING AXIS]
    B --> H[TIPPING MOMENT]
    C --> I[STABILIZING MOMENT]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the Stability Diagram:** The lifted load at radius $R$ creates the tipping moment $M_{tip} = W \cdot R$. The counterweight creates the stabilising moment $M_{stab} = W_{cw} \cdot d_{cw}$. If $M_{stab} < M_{tip}$, the crane tips about the outrigger edge. This visual logic directly supports Derivation 1 in Section 3.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1: [KTU University Exam - May 2024]
**"Define a crane and list any four types of cranes used in material handling."**

**Model Answer:**

A crane is a mechanical lifting device used to move heavy loads vertically and horizontally using a hoisting mechanism, ropes, sheaves, and a hook. It is widely used in construction, manufacturing, and logistics for safe material handling.

**Four types of cranes:**
1. Overhead travelling crane (bridge crane)
2. Gantry crane
3. Jib crane
4. Mobile crane (truck-mounted or crawler)

> [!NOTE]
> **Marks Distribution (KTU Pattern):** Definition = 1 mark, List of 4 types = 1 mark, Brief role of each = 1 mark.

---

### Question A2: [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]
**"What is the Safe Working Load (SWL) of a crane? State the standard factor of safety used for crane stability calculations."**

**Model Answer:**

**SWL Definition:** Safe Working Load (SWL) is the maximum load that a crane is certified by the manufacturer to lift under specified working conditions, including the self-weight of the lifting gear (hook, slings, blocks).

**Standard Factor of Safety:** As per **IS 4573** and KTU 2024 syllabus, the minimum factor of safety for crane stability against tipping is **1.5**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Memory Tip:** Always quote "1.5" for crane stability SF and "18–24" for sheave bend factor — these are the two most-asked numerical values in KTU exams.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024 Pattern]

**"With the help of a neat functional diagram, explain the construction, working, and safety features of a Tower Crane. Also derive the condition for stability of a tower crane under typical loading."**

#### Part (a) — Construction and Working (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**1. Major Components of a Tower Crane:**

| Component | Function |
|---|---|
| **Mast/Tower** | Vertical lattice structure providing height |
| **Slewing Unit** | Allows 360° rotation of the jib assembly |
| **Working Jib** | Horizontal arm carrying the trolley and hook |
| **Counter-Jib** | Opposite arm carrying counterweight for balance |
| **Counterweight** | Mass blocks to balance the load moment |
| **Trolley** | Moves along the jib to position the load |
| **Hook Block** | Suspends and lifts the load |
| **Climbing System** | Enables mast extension as building rises |
| **Operator Cabin** | Houses controls, located at top of mast |
| **Foundation** | Anchors the mast to the ground |

**2. Working Principle:**

- The trolley travels along the working jib to reach the required radial position.
- The hoist mechanism (in the cabin or at the jib) winds the rope, lifting the load.
- The slewing unit rotates the entire jib assembly to orient the load direction.
- The counter-jib with counterweight balances the moment created by the load.
- The climbing system hydraulically inserts new mast sections as the building rises.

**3. Safety Features:**

- **Slewing brake and lock** to prevent unintended rotation
- **Trolley travel limits** at both ends of the jib
- **Hoist over-wind and under-wind limits**
- **Wind speed anemometer** with automatic cut-off at high wind
- **Load Moment Indicator (LMI)** to prevent overloading
- **Anti-two-block device** to prevent hook collision with jib
- **Emergency descent system** in case of power failure

#### Part (b) — Stability Derivation (7 Marks)

**Setup:**
- $W$ = lifted load (kg) acting at radius $R$ from tower centre
- $W_{cw}$ = counterweight (kg) at distance $d_{cw}$ from tower centre (opposite side)
- $W_{t}$ = weight of tower structure acting at tower centre
- $T$ = reaction at the base

**Derivation:**

Taking moments about the base pivot (the foundation edge in the load direction):

**Step 1: Overturning moment.**

$$M_{tip} = W \cdot R$$

**Step 2: Stabilising moment from counterweight and tower self-weight.**

$$M_{stab} = W_{cw} \cdot d_{cw} + W_{t} \cdot 0 \text{ (assumed at axis)}$$

$$M_{stab} \approx W_{cw} \cdot d_{cw}$$

**Step 3: Safety condition.**

For stability:

$$M_{stab} \geq M_{tip} \cdot FS$$

With factor of safety 1.5:

$$\frac{W_{cw} \cdot d_{cw}}{W \cdot R} \geq 1.5$$

**Step 4: Numerical example.**

If $W = 5000$ kg, $R = 40$ m, $d_{cw} = 12$ m, find required $W_{cw}$.

$$W_{cw} \geq \frac{1.5 \times 5000 \times 40}{12} = \frac{300{,}000}{12} = 25{,}000 \text{ kg}$$

So a **25-tonne counterweight** is required — a typical value for medium-capacity tower cranes.

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 Pattern — Alternative Choice]

**"Classify cranes used in material handling. With neat functional diagrams, explain the working and applications of (i) Overhead Travelling Crane and (ii) Mobile Crane. Discuss the safety interlocks required for each."**

#### Part (a) — Overhead Travelling Crane (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**1. Construction:**
- **Bridge Girder(s)** — Span the full width of the bay.
- **End Carriages** — Mounted at both ends, run on elevated rails.
- **Trolley** — Carries the hoist, moves along the bridge.
- **Hoist Assembly** — Motor, gearbox, drum, wire rope, hook.
- **Runway Rails** — Fixed to building columns or supports.

**2. Working:**
- The hoist lifts the load vertically.
- The trolley moves the load across the bay (long travel).
- The bridge moves the load along the bay (cross travel).
- Combined motions allow 3-D placement of the load.

**3. Applications:**
- Machine shops, foundries, steel plants
- Warehouses with high-bay storage
- Power plants for turbine maintenance
- Automobile assembly lines

**4. Safety Interlocks:**
- **Anti-collision device** between adjacent cranes on the same runway
- **Travel limit switches** at both ends of bridge and trolley
- **Hoist upper and lower limits**
- **Overload protection** via load cell
- **Audible warning horn** before motion
- **Emergency stop** in cabin and on the floor
- **Phase failure relay** for electric supply
- **Earth fault relay** and main line isolator

#### Part (b) — Mobile Crane (7 Marks)

**Model Answer:**

**1. Construction:**
- **Carrier (Truck or Crawler)** — Provides mobility
- **Hydraulic Telescopic or Lattice Boom** — Provides reach
- **Outriggers** — Stabilising legs extended before lifting
- **Slewing Platform** — Rotates the boom
- **Operator Cabin** with LMI (Load Moment Indicator)
- **Counterweight** at the rear

**2. Working:**
- The truck drives to the site and outriggers are deployed.
- The boom is raised to the required angle.
- The hoist lifts the load and slewing positions it.
- For crawler cranes, tracks allow travel on soft ground.

**3. Applications:**
- Construction sites (buildings, bridges, towers)
- Equipment installation and maintenance
- Pipeline and infrastructure projects
- Emergency lifting and recovery

**4. Safety Interlocks:**
- **Outrigger load sensors** with warning if improperly set
- **LMI with auto shut-off** at rated capacity
- **Anti-two-block warning**
- **Boom angle and length indicators**
- **Slewing limit switches**
- **Wind speed sensor** for outdoor operations
- **Hook block safety latch**
- **Operator certification check** (interlock with operator ID)
- **Tyre pressure monitoring** for truck-mounted cranes
- **Track tension monitoring** for crawler cranes

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mistakes that Cost Marks in KTU Crane Questions:**
>
> 1. **Confusing SWL with breaking load** — SWL is the working load limit; breaking load = SWL × Factor of Safety. Many students use the wrong value.
> 2. **Forgetting the stability factor of 1.5** — Just stating $M_{stab} \geq M_{tip}$ without the factor is incomplete and costs 2 marks.
> 3. **Not naming the IS code** — Always cite **IS 3177** (cranes), **IS 4573** (stability), and **IS 807** (overhead cranes) to show code awareness.
> 4. **Omitting the operator's role in safety** — Even if the question is technical, examiners expect a line on operator licensing and pre-use checks.
> 5. **Unit inconsistency** — Mixing kgf and N, or mm and m in the same calculation. Always show unit conversion steps.
> 6. **Treating "crane" as a single device** — KTU 2024 specifically asks for **types** of cranes. Merely defining a crane without classification is a half-answer.
> 7. **No diagram** — A labelled sketch (even a simple block diagram) is **mandatory** for 7-mark sub-questions.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Quick-Revision Points for KTU Module 3 — Types of Cranes:**
>
> ✅ **Crane Definition:** Mechanical device for vertical and horizontal load movement using hoist + ropes + sheaves.
>
> ✅ **Major Types to Memorise (8 types):** Overhead Travelling, Gantry, Jib, Mobile (Truck/Crawler), Tower, Floating, Telescopic, Loader/Knuckle-Boom.
>
> ✅ **Tower Crane Anatomy:** Mast + Working Jib + Counter-Jib + Counterweight + Trolley + Slewing Unit.
>
> ✅ **Stability Formula (MUST memorise):**
>
> $$SF = \frac{W_{cw} \cdot d_{cw} + W_{crane} \cdot d_{c}}{W \cdot R} \geq 1.5$$
>
> ✅ **Hoist Power Formula:**
>
> $$P_{motor} = \frac{W \cdot g \cdot v}{\eta}$$
>
> ✅ **Sheave Diameter Formula:**
>
> $$D_{min} = d \cdot k_{bend} \quad \text{with } k_{bend} = 18 \text{ to } 24$$
>
> ✅ **SWL Definition:** Maximum rated lift load including lifting gear weight.
>
> ✅ **Standard Factor of Safety:** **1.5** for tipping stability; **5 to 6** for wire rope breaking.
>
> ✅ **Key IS Codes:** **IS 3177** (Cranes — Code of Practice), **IS 4573** (Stability), **IS 807** (Overhead Cranes), **IS 14467** (Mobile Crane Safety).
>
> ✅ **Crane Selection Logic:** Load → Height → Reach → Frequency → Environment → Mobility → Power.
>
> ✅ **Crane Type Selection Matrix:**
> - Indoor + Repetitive + Bay → **Overhead Travelling Crane**
> - Outdoor + Heavy + Long Span → **Gantry Crane**
> - Workstation + Circular area → **Jib Crane**
> - Mobile + Construction → **Mobile Crane (Truck or Crawler)**
> - High-rise construction → **Tower Crane**
> - Marine + Port → **Floating Crane**
>
> ✅ **Safety Interlocks (Top 5 Universal):** Overload limiter, Anti-two-block, Slew limit, Travel limits, Emergency stop.
>
> ✅ **Common Crane Accidents:** Tipping (most common), Two-blocking, Boom failure, Electrical contact, Dropped load.
>
> ✅ **Operator Requirement:** All crane operators in India must hold a **competency certificate** from a recognised institute and undergo **periodic medical fitness tests**.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT522 - SAFETY IN MATERIAL HANDLING
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Types of Material Handling Equipment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Material Handling Equipment (MHE)** refers to the mechanical systems, machinery, and auxiliary devices used for the **movement, protection, storage, and control** of materials and products throughout the processes of manufacturing, distribution, consumption, and disposal. The **types** of MHE are broadly classified based on **operational function**, **path of movement**, **power source**, and **nature of the load handled**.

As per the **KTU 2024 Scheme** for *Safety in Material Handling* (PESFT522), the study of *Types* in Module 3 is the foundational classification schema that helps engineers select the *right* equipment for a *given* application, ensuring both **operational efficiency** and **occupational safety** as mandated by the **Factories Act, 1948** and **Indian Standards (IS) codes**.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

Imagine you are moving furniture within a large house. You wouldn't use a wheelbarrow to carry a fragile glass cabinet (you'd use your hands with care), nor would you use a forklift to carry a single envelope (you'd just walk with it). The same logic applies in an industry — choosing the correct *type* of material handling equipment depends on:

- The **weight** of the load (Light: < 100 kg, Medium: 100–1000 kg, Heavy: > 1000 kg)
- The **distance** of movement (Short: < 50 m, Medium: 50–500 m, Long: > 500 m)
- The **path** (Fixed route vs. Variable route)
- The **frequency** (Continuous vs. Intermittent)
- The **shape & fragility** of the material (Solid, Liquid, Granular, Hazardous)

> [!NOTE]
> **Intuitive Bucket Analogy:** Think of the classification as a *bucket of tools*:
> - **Conveyors** = the *highway* (continuous flow on a fixed path)
> - **Cranes & Hoists** = the *elevator* (lifting heavy things vertically + horizontally)
> - **Industrial Trucks** = the *car/taxi* (flexible, point-to-point, short distances)
> - **Robotic Systems** = the *autopilot* (programmed, repetitive, precision)
> - **Storage Equipment** = the *wardrobe/shelf* (where things wait to be used)

## 1.3 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics (Bold Highlights)

- **Standard Pallet Dimensions:** 1200 mm × 1000 mm (ISO Standard), 48" × 40" (US Standard)
- **Standard Safe Working Load (SWL):** Expressed in **tonnes (t)** or **kilograms (kg)**
- **Standard Lift Height:** Forklifts typically 3 m to 6 m
- **Standard Conveyor Belt Speeds:** **0.5 m/s to 5 m/s** for general cargo
- **Standard Crane Hook Approach:** **600 mm** minimum approach distance (IS 807)
- **Standard Operating Temperature Range:** **−20 °C to +40 °C** for most MHE

## 1.4 Why Classification Matters (KTU 2024 Outcome Perspective)

The **Course Outcomes (COs)** of PESFT522 emphasize that a safety engineer must be able to *recommend the appropriate MHE type* based on workplace hazards. A wrong choice leads to:
- **Over 35% of all industrial accidents** in manufacturing sectors (per OSHA statistics)
- **Ergonomic disorders** (Manual Handling Operations Regulations)
- **Financial losses** from damaged goods

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Master Classification Tree of Material Handling Equipment

The types of MHE can be organized under **five master categories** that KTU examiners love to test:

### Category 1: Based on Path of Movement

| Sub-Type | Definition | Example |
|----------|------------|---------|
| **Fixed Path Equipment** | Move material along a predetermined, unchanging route | Belt conveyors, roller conveyors, overhead conveyors |
| **Variable Path Equipment** | Move material to any reachable point in a 2D or 3D zone | Forklifts, mobile cranes, AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles) |
| **Mixed Path Equipment** | Combine both fixed + variable path characteristics | Overhead bridge cranes (fixed rails + variable hook positioning) |

### Category 2: Based on Operational Function

| Sub-Type | Function |
|----------|----------|
| **Transporting** | Move material from one location to another |
| **Positioning / Orienting** | Reorient the material without major displacement |
| **Lifting / Hoisting** | Raise the material vertically |
| **Storing / Retrieval** | Hold material in a defined location |
| **Loading / Unloading** | Interface with transport vehicles |
| **Identification / Tracking** | Monitor the material in transit |

### Category 3: Based on Power Source

| Sub-Type | Power Source | Application |
|----------|--------------|-------------|
| **Manual** | Human effort | Light loads (< 25 kg) |
| **Electric (Battery)** | Battery + motor | Indoor, clean environments |
| **Electric (Cable)** | Mains electricity via festoon/cable reel | Overhead cranes, gantry cranes |
| **Internal Combustion (IC)** | Petrol/Diesel/LPG engine | Outdoor, heavy duty |
| **Hydraulic** | Hydraulic fluid pressure | Lifts, jacks, compact MHE |
| **Pneumatic** | Compressed air | Vacuum lifters, air hoists |

### Category 4: Based on the Nature of Material Handled

| Sub-Type | Material Examples |
|----------|-------------------|
| **Unit Load** | Pallets, drums, crates, bales |
| **Bulk Load** | Coal, grain, sand, ore, cement |
| **Liquid Load** | Oils, chemicals, water |
| **Gaseous Load** | LPG, oxygen, nitrogen (in cylinders) |
| **Hazardous Load** | Explosives, radioactive, corrosive |

### Category 5: The Most Common Equipment Types (KTU Favourite!)

> [!NOTE]
> **5.A — Conveyors**
> 1. **Belt Conveyor** — Continuous rubber/PVC belt on idlers; bulk and unit loads
> 2. **Roller Conveyor** — Gravity or powered rollers; cartons, totes
> 3. **Chain Conveyor** — En-masse and slat conveyors; heavy unit loads
> 4. **Screw (Auger) Conveyor** — Helical screw in trough; powders, granules
> 5. **Pneumatic Conveyor** — Air-suspended particles; chemicals, flour
> 6. **Bucket Elevator** — Buckets on belts; vertical lifting of bulk material
> 7. **Vibratory Conveyor** — Oscillating trough; hot castings, granular material
> 8. **Overhead / Trolley Conveyor** — Trolleys on overhead rail; assembly lines
> 9. **Cable Belt Conveyor** — Cable-supported belt; long distances, rough terrain

> [!NOTE]
> **5.B — Cranes**
> 1. **Overhead Travelling Crane (EOT)** — Bridge on rails, 3-axis movement
> 2. **Gantry Crane** — Ground-supported bridge (no building support)
> 3. **Jib Crane** — Pivoted arm; local circular coverage
> 4. **Tower Crane** — Vertical tower + horizontal jib; construction sites
> 5. **Mobile Crane** — Truck-mounted, telescopic/hydraulic
> 6. **Crawler Crane** — Tracked undercarriage; off-road sites
> 7. **Portal Crane** — Bridge on legs with cantilever; shipyards, ports
> 8. **Floating Crane** — Mounted on barge; port operations
> 9. **Stacker/Reclaimer Crane** — Bulk material storage yards

> [!NOTE]
> **5.C — Industrial Trucks & Forklifts**
> 1. **Counterbalance Forklift** — Most common, rear weight balances load
> 2. **Reach Truck** — Extending forks for narrow-aisle racking
> 3. **Order Picker** — Operator elevates with load for picking
> 4. **Pallet Jack (Manual/Electric)** — Horizontal pallet movement
> 5. **Side Loader** — Forks on side; long loads (lumber, pipes)
> 6. **Rough Terrain Forklift** — Construction/agricultural sites
> 7. **Articulated Forklift** — Hinged chassis; tight spaces
> 8. **Telehandler** — Telescopic boom + forks; variable reach

> [!NOTE]
> **5.D — Hoists & Lifts**
> 1. **Chain Hoist (Manual/Electric)** — Lifting by chain
> 2. **Wire Rope Hoist** — Lifting by wire rope; heavier loads
> 3. **Air Hoist** — Pneumatic; explosion-proof environments
> 4. **Scissor Lift Table** — Hydraulic vertical lift platform
> 5. **Winch** — Horizontal/vertical pull on a drum

> [!NOTE]
> **5.E — Storage & Retrieval Equipment**
> 1. **Pallet Racks** — Selective, drive-in, drive-through, push-back
> 2. **Cantilever Racks** — Long materials (pipes, lumber)
> 3. **Mezzanine Floors** — Elevated storage platforms
> 4. **AS/RS (Automated Storage & Retrieval Systems)** — Robotic stacker cranes
> 5. **Carousel Systems** — Horizontal/vertical rotating storage
> 6. **Bin Tippers & Dumpers** — Discharge contents of bins

> [!NOTE]
> **5.F — Auxiliary & Specialized MHE**
> 1. **AGV (Automated Guided Vehicle)** — Wire/magnetic/laser guided
> 2. **AMR (Autonomous Mobile Robot)** — SLAM navigation
> 3. **Drum Handler / Tilter** — Cylindrical load manipulation
> 4. **Vacuum Lifter** — Glass, sheet metal, panels
> 5. **Magnetic Lifter** — Ferrous plates, scrap
> 6. **Crane Attachments** — Slings, shackles, hooks, grabs, spreader beams

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### Table: Key Equations for Material Handling Equipment Selection & Safety

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Meaning | Unit | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $T = \dfrac{W \times S}{v}$ | T = Throughput, W = Load weight, S = Cycle factor, v = Velocity | kg/s, t/h | Conveyor capacity |
| 2 | $P = \dfrac{F \times v}{\eta}$ | P = Power, F = Tractive force, v = Velocity, η = Efficiency | W, kW | Belt conveyor motor |
| 3 | $F_t = \mu_r \cdot W \cdot g$ | F_t = Tractive force, μ_r = Rolling resistance coeff., W = Weight | N | Wheel/roller resistance |
| 4 | $F_i = W \cdot g \cdot \sin\theta$ | F_i = Incline force, θ = Angle of inclination | N | Inclined conveyor force |
| 5 | $F_a = W \cdot g \cdot \cos\theta \cdot \mu_f$ | F_a = Friction force, μ_f = Friction coeff. | N | Skid/load friction |
| 6 | $T_{total} = F_t + F_i + F_a + F_b$ | T_b = Belt bend resistance | N | Total tractive force |
| 7 | $\sigma = \dfrac{W}{A}$ | σ = Stress in sling/rope, A = Cross-section | N/mm² | Wire rope safety check |
| 8 | $SF = \dfrac{Ultimate\ Tensile\ Strength}{Working\ Load}$ | SF = Safety Factor | dimensionless | Sling/rope selection |
| 9 | $Q = 3600 \cdot v \cdot A \cdot \rho \cdot \phi$ | Q = Conveyor capacity, A = Cross-section, ρ = Density, φ = Fill factor | t/h | Belt conveyor capacity |
| 10 | $v = \dfrac{\pi \cdot D \cdot N}{60}$ | v = Belt speed, D = Pulley diameter, N = RPM | m/s | Drum/pulley kinematics |
| 11 | $SWL = \dfrac{Breaking\ Load}{SF}$ | Safe Working Load | kg, t | Crane/hoist rated capacity |
| 12 | $h = \dfrac{v^2}{2g}$ | h = Drop height from lift failure | m | Safety clearance calc. |
| 13 | $T_{lift} = (W_{load} + W_{hooking\ gear}) \cdot g$ | Total lift force on hoist | N | Hoist design load |
| 14 | $M_{overturning} = W_{load} \cdot R$ | M = Overturning moment, R = Reach radius | N·m | Crane stability check |
| 15 | $M_{stabilizing} = W_{crane} \cdot r$ | W_crane = Crane weight, r = Stabilizing lever arm | N·m | Crane stability check |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Boundary Conditions to Remember:**
> - **Belt conveyor inclination:** Maximum **18° to 20°** for crushed stone; **15° to 18°** for coal; **10° to 12°** for sand
> - **Standard Safety Factor for Slings:** **5:1** for normal lifts (IS 2760)
> - **Standard Safety Factor for Wire Ropes:** **3.5:1 to 5:1** depending on service (IS 2266)
> - **Minimum SF for Lifting Chains:** **4:1** (IS 2760)
> - **Maximum safe sling angle from vertical:** **45°** (sling angle, NOT rope angle)
> - **Crowd/Forklift safe aisle width:** 1.5 × truck width + 0.5 m clearance

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Conveyors:** Mining, cement plants, food processing, airports (baggage), e-commerce warehouses (Amazon fulfilment)
- **Cranes:** Shipyards, steel mills, power plants, construction (high-rise buildings)
- **Forklifts:** Warehouses, manufacturing, ports, retail distribution
- **AGVs/AMRs:** Modern smart factories (Industry 4.0), automotive assembly
- **Hoists:** Workshops, garages, small manufacturing units

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Analytical Matrix: Selection Logic for MHE Type

The **selection of the correct MHE type** is governed by a 6-step decision framework. This is a high-weightage KTU question (often asked for 7–14 marks).

### Step 1: Characterize the Load

- **Weight (W):** in kg or tonnes
- **Volume / Dimensions:** length, width, height
- **Shape:** uniform, irregular, cylindrical, granular
- **Physical State:** solid, liquid, gas, slurry
- **Hazard Class:** non-hazardous, flammable, toxic, corrosive, radioactive
- **Temperature:** ambient, hot (> 200 °C), cryogenic
- **Fragility:** rigid, semi-rigid, fragile

### Step 2: Characterize the Movement

- **Distance (D):** Short (< 50 m), Medium (50–500 m), Long (> 500 m)
- **Path Geometry:** Horizontal, vertical, inclined, mixed
- **Frequency:** Continuous, intermittent, occasional
- **Throughput Required:** kg/h or t/h

### Step 3: Characterize the Environment

- **Indoor / Outdoor**
- **Clean / Dusty / Hazardous (ATEX zone)**
- **Temperature extremes**
- **Space constraints** (narrow aisles, low ceiling, obstructions)

### Step 4: Apply the Selection Rule

| Condition | Recommended MHE Type |
|-----------|----------------------|
| Short horizontal, light unit load, fixed path | **Roller conveyor (gravity)** |
| Long horizontal, bulk solid | **Belt conveyor** |
| Vertical lifting of bulk material | **Bucket elevator** |
| Vertical lifting of heavy unit load | **Crane / Hoist** |
| Variable path, medium distance, palletized | **Forklift truck** |
| Long distance, repetitive, variable | **AGV** |
| Hazardous, explosion-proof zone | **Air hoist / pneumatic conveyor** |
| Fragile sheet (glass) | **Vacuum lifter with crane** |
| Long horizontal, very long distance | **Cable belt conveyor** |

### Step 5: Validate Safety Constraints

- Check the **SWL** vs. actual load
- Verify the **Safety Factor** (SF) of slings/ropes
- Confirm **operator certification** (e.g., forklift licence)
- Inspect **guards, alarms, limit switches**
- Plan for **fall arrest / dropped object protection**

### Step 6: Economic and Ergonomic Validation

- Calculate **Cost per Ton Handled** (CTH):

$$
CTH = \dfrac{Capital\ Cost + Operating\ Cost\ (annual)}{Annual\ Throughput\ (tonnes)}
$$

- Verify **ergonomic acceptability** (NIOSH Lifting Equation for manual handling)

## 3.2 Exhaustive Derivation: Belt Conveyor Capacity

The most-tested derivation in KTU Module 3.

### Given

A troughed belt conveyor must transport **coal** with the following parameters:
- Belt width, $B = 1000\ \text{mm} = 1.0\ \text{m}$
- Belt speed, $v = 2.5\ \text{m/s}$
- Bulk density of coal, $\rho = 800\ \text{kg/m}^3$
- Cross-sectional area of load on troughed belt, $A = 0.0925\ \text{m}^2$
- Surcharge angle, $\beta = 20^\circ$
- Idler troughing angle, $\alpha = 35^\circ$
- Idler spacing, $L_i = 1.2\ \text{m}$
- Belt + idler mass per unit length, $M = 35\ \text{kg/m}$
- Coefficient of rolling resistance, $\mu_r = 0.025$
- Conveyor length (horizontal), $L = 250\ \text{m}$
- Lift height (vertical), $H = 25\ \text{m}$
- Drive efficiency, $\eta = 0.92$

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Mass flow rate (Throughput) $T$**

$$
T = 3600 \cdot v \cdot A \cdot \rho
$$

**Substitute the values:**

$$
T = 3600 \times 2.5 \times 0.0925 \times 800
$$

**Compute the product step by step:**

$$
T = 3600 \times 2.5 = 9000
$$

$$
T = 9000 \times 0.0925 = 832.5
$$

$$
T = 832.5 \times 800 = 666{,}000\ \text{kg/h}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Convert to tonnes per hour:**

$$
T = \dfrac{666{,}000}{1000} = 666\ \text{t/h}
$$

**Step 2: Tractive force components**

**2a. Force to move empty belt (F_t):**

$$
F_t = \mu_r \cdot M \cdot g \cdot L
$$

$$
F_t = 0.025 \times 35 \times 9.81 \times 250
$$

$$
F_t = 0.025 \times 35 = 0.875
$$

$$
F_t = 0.875 \times 9.81 = 8.584
$$

$$
F_t = 8.584 \times 250 = 2146\ \text{N}
$$

**2b. Force to lift the material vertically (F_i):**

The mass of material per metre of belt:

$$
M_m = A \cdot \rho = 0.0925 \times 800 = 74\ \text{kg/m}
$$

$$
F_i = M_m \cdot g \cdot H
$$

$$
F_i = 74 \times 9.81 \times 25
$$

$$
F_i = 74 \times 9.81 = 725.94
$$

$$
F_i = 725.94 \times 25 = 18{,}148.5\ \text{N}
$$

**2c. Friction force on the loaded belt (F_a):**

This is the friction from carrying the material horizontally. We approximate:

$$
F_a = M_m \cdot g \cdot L \cdot f
$$

where $f = 0.012$ (typical secondary resistance factor for troughed belt).

$$
F_a = 74 \times 9.81 \times 250 \times 0.012
$$

$$
F_a = 74 \times 9.81 = 725.94
$$

$$
F_a = 725.94 \times 250 = 181{,}485
$$

$$
F_a = 181{,}485 \times 0.012 = 2177.8\ \text{N}
$$

**2d. Belt bending / flexing resistance (F_b):**

Empirical: $F_b = 0.03 \cdot (B/1000) \cdot L \cdot g$

$$
F_b = 0.03 \times 1.0 \times 250 \times 9.81 = 73.6\ \text{N}
$$

**Step 3: Total Tractive Force (F_total)**

$$
F_{total} = F_t + F_i + F_a + F_b
$$

$$
F_{total} = 2146 + 18{,}148.5 + 2177.8 + 73.6 = 22{,}545.9\ \text{N}
$$

**Step 4: Required Motor Power (P)**

$$
P = \dfrac{F_{total} \cdot v}{\eta}
$$

$$
P = \dfrac{22{,}545.9 \times 2.5}{0.92}
$$

$$
P = \dfrac{56{,}364.75}{0.92} = 61{,}266\ \text{W}
$$

$$
P \approx 61.3\ \text{kW}
$$

**Step 5: Select a Standard Motor**

Choose the next standard size: **75 kW** (allowing 22% safety margin).

## 3.3 Exhaustive Derivation: Crane Stability Check (Slewing Crane)

### Given

A mobile crane on outriggers must lift a load:
- Maximum load weight, $W = 15\ \text{t}$
- Hook block + rigging weight, $W_h = 0.5\ \text{t}$
- Maximum radius from slewing axis, $R = 18\ \text{m}$
- Crane weight (counterweight included), $W_c = 30\ \text{t}$
- Distance from slewing axis to centroid of crane, $r = 2.5\ \text{m}$ (towards the back)

### Step-by-Step Solution

**Step 1: Total Overturning Moment ($M_{OT}$)**

$$
M_{OT} = (W + W_h) \times R
$$

$$
M_{OT} = (15 + 0.5) \times 18
$$

$$
M_{OT} = 15.5 \times 18 = 279\ \text{t·m}
$$

**Step 2: Stabilizing Moment ($M_{ST}$)**

$$
M_{ST} = W_c \times r
$$

$$
M_{ST} = 30 \times 2.5 = 75\ \text{t·m}
$$

**Step 3: Stability Check (Factor of Safety against overturning)**

$$
FS_{stability} = \dfrac{M_{ST}}{M_{OT}}
$$

$$
FS_{stability} = \dfrac{75}{279} = 0.269
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Stability is INADEQUATE!** The factor of safety is far less than the minimum required **1.5** for static tipping and **1.25** for dynamic conditions.
>
> **Remedies:**
> - Extend outriggers to **increase effective $r$** (move load line further inward)
> - Use **heavier counterweight** to increase $W_c$
> - **Reduce the load** $W$ to a value that gives $M_{OT} \leq 50\ \text{t·m}$
> - **Reduce the radius** $R$ (move crane closer to load)

**Step 4: Maximum Safe Load (for $FS = 1.5$)**

$$
M_{OT, max} = \dfrac{M_{ST}}{FS} = \dfrac{75}{1.5} = 50\ \text{t·m}
$$

$$
(W_{load} + W_h) = \dfrac{50}{18} = 2.78\ \text{t}
$$

$$
W_{load, max} = 2.78 - 0.5 = 2.28\ \text{t}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The crane can only lift a maximum of **2.28 tonnes at 18 m radius** safely. For the full 15 t, the radius must be reduced to $\dfrac{50}{15.5} = 3.23\ \text{m}$.

---

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]

**Classify material handling equipment based on the path of movement. Give two examples for each type.**

**Model Answer:**

Material handling equipment can be classified into three types based on the path of movement:

1. **Fixed Path Equipment** — The material moves along a predetermined and unchanging route.
   - *Examples:* Belt conveyor, roller conveyor, chain conveyor, overhead conveyor.

2. **Variable Path Equipment** — The material can be moved to any reachable point within a 2D or 3D zone.
   - *Examples:* Forklift truck, mobile crane, Automated Guided Vehicle (AGV).

3. **Mixed Path Equipment** — A combination of fixed and variable path characteristics.
   - *Examples:* Overhead travelling (EOT) crane (fixed rails + variable hook position), gantry crane.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Mnemonic:** *F-V-M = Fixed, Variable, Mixed* — the three pillars of MHE classification by path.

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]

**Differentiate between an EOT crane and a Gantry crane.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | EOT (Electric Overhead Travelling) Crane | Gantry Crane |
|---------|------------------------------------------|--------------|
| **Support** | Bridge supported on rails mounted on building columns/structure | Bridge supported on legs running on ground rails |
| **Building** | Requires a strong building structure | Can be used outdoors, no building needed |
| **Floor Space** | Floor remains clear (overhead usage) | Floor partially obstructed by gantry legs |
| **Span** | Limited by building column spacing | Can be very large (shipyards, container yards) |
| **Mobility** | Limited to building runway | Can be very long; some are rail-mounted gantries (RMG) |
| **Application** | Workshops, machine shops, steel plants indoors | Ports, shipyards, stockyards, container terminals |

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU ESE Pattern:** Part B questions carry **14 marks**, typically split into **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**, with an **internal choice** between *Question A* and *Question B* on the same module.

---

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam — Model Paper 2024]

**(a)** Classify the different **types of conveyors** used in industry. Explain the working principle, construction, and typical applications of a **belt conveyor** and a **screw conveyor**. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A belt conveyor with the following specifications transports coal:
- Belt width: **800 mm**
- Belt speed: **1.5 m/s**
- Bulk density of coal: **850 kg/m³**
- Cross-sectional area of load: **0.075 m²**
- Conveyor length: **150 m (horizontal)**
- Lift height: **10 m**
- Coefficient of rolling resistance: **0.025**
- Mass per metre of belt and idlers: **28 kg/m**
- Drive efficiency: **0.90**

Calculate the **(i)** mass throughput in t/h, and **(ii)** the required motor power in kW. **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question A

#### Part (a): Types of Conveyors

Conveyors are classified into the following types based on the mechanism of material transport:

1. **Belt Conveyors** — A continuous belt (rubber/PVC) runs over idler pulleys; used for **bulk** and **unit** loads over long distances.
2. **Roller Conveyors** — Gravity-driven or motor-driven rollers; used for cartons, totes, and pallets.
3. **Chain Conveyors** — En-masse or slat type; used for heavy unit loads, pallets, and hot materials.
4. **Screw (Auger) Conveyors** — A helical screw rotates inside a trough; used for powders and granules.
5. **Pneumatic Conveyors** — Material suspended in air/gas inside pipes; used for fine particles.
6. **Bucket Elevators** — Buckets on a belt; vertical lifting of bulk material.
7. **Vibratory Conveyors** — Trough vibrates to move material; used for hot castings and fragile items.
8. **Overhead / Trolley Conveyors** — Trolleys on overhead rail; used in assembly lines.
9. **Cable Belt Conveyors** — Belt supported by cables; used for very long distances.

**Working Principle of Belt Conveyor:**

The belt is supported by a set of **idlers** (troughed at 20°–45°) and runs between two terminal pulleys — a **drive pulley** (head end) and a **tail pulley** (load end). The motor drives the head pulley via a gearbox. Material is loaded at the tail, carried on the upper (carrying) run, and discharged at the head. The return run is supported by **flat idlers**. A **take-up pulley** maintains belt tension.

**Working Principle of Screw Conveyor:**

A **helical screw blade** (auger) rotates inside a **U-shaped trough** (or a pipe for tubular conveyors). Material enters at one end and is pushed forward by the rotating screw. The screw is driven by a motor through a gearbox. Output depends on **diameter, pitch, RPM**, and **fill factor (15–45%)**. Used for **cement, fly ash, grain, food grains, sand, wood chips**.

**Construction of Belt Conveyor:**

- Head section (drive, motor, gearbox, head pulley)
- Tail section (tail pulley, take-up)
- Carrying idlers (troughed) at 1.0–1.5 m spacing
- Return idlers (flat) at 2.5–3.0 m spacing
- Belt (textile, steel-cord, PVC)
- Loading and discharge chutes
- Skirt boards (containment)
- Belt cleaners (scrapers)

**Construction of Screw Conveyor:**

- Trough (U-shape) or pipe
- Helical screw blade (right-hand or left-hand, with or without centre pipe)
- End bearings and intermediate hanger bearings
- Drive unit (motor + gearbox)
- Inlet and outlet spouts
- Covers (dust-tight)

**Typical Applications:**

| Conveyor | Application |
|----------|-------------|
| Belt Conveyor | Coal in thermal power stations, ore in mines, baggage in airports, sand in cement plants |
| Screw Conveyor | Cement, fly ash, lime, food grains, animal feed, wood chips, sugar |

#### Part (b): Belt Conveyor Numerical Solution

**Given:**
$B = 0.8\ \text{m}, v = 1.5\ \text{m/s}, \rho = 850\ \text{kg/m}^3, A = 0.075\ \text{m}^2, L = 150\ \text{m}, H = 10\ \text{m}, \mu_r = 0.025, M = 28\ \text{kg/m}, \eta = 0.90$

**Step 1: Mass Throughput (T)**

$$
T = 3600 \cdot v \cdot A \cdot \rho
$$

$$
T = 3600 \times 1.5 \times 0.075 \times 850
$$

$$
T = 3600 \times 1.5 = 5400
$$

$$
T = 5400 \times 0.075 = 405
$$

$$
T = 405 \times 850 = 344{,}250\ \text{kg/h}
$$

$$
T = 344.25\ \text{t/h}
$$

**Step 2: Mass of material per metre of belt**

$$
M_m = A \cdot \rho = 0.075 \times 850 = 63.75\ \text{kg/m}
$$

**Step 3: Tractive Force Components**

**Empty belt force $F_t$:**

$$
F_t = \mu_r \cdot M \cdot g \cdot L
$$

$$
F_t = 0.025 \times 28 \times 9.81 \times 150 = 1029.9\ \text{N}
$$

**Lift (incline) force $F_i$:**

$$
F_i = M_m \cdot g \cdot H = 63.75 \times 9.81 \times 10 = 6253.6\ \text{N}
$$

**Loaded horizontal friction $F_a$** (using $f = 0.012$):

$$
F_a = M_m \cdot g \cdot L \cdot f = 63.75 \times 9.81 \times 150 \times 0.012 = 1125.6\ \text{N}
$$

**Belt bend resistance $F_b$:**

$$
F_b = 0.03 \times (B/1) \times L \times g = 0.03 \times 0.8 \times 150 \times 9.81 = 35.3\ \text{N}
$$

**Step 4: Total Tractive Force**

$$
F_{total} = 1029.9 + 6253.6 + 1125.6 + 35.3 = 8444.4\ \text{N}
$$

**Step 5: Motor Power**

$$
P = \dfrac{F_{total} \cdot v}{\eta} = \dfrac{8444.4 \times 1.5}{0.90} = \dfrac{12{,}666.6}{0.90} = 14{,}074\ \text{W}
$$

$$
\boxed{P \approx 14.1\ \text{kW}}
$$

**Select a standard motor: 15 kW or 18.5 kW.**

---

### Question B (14 Marks) [Alternative Choice — KTU University Exam — Dec 2024]

**(a)** Classify the different **types of cranes** used in industry. With a neat diagram, describe the construction and working of an **EOT (Electric Overhead Travelling) crane**. Mention four **safety devices** used in EOT cranes. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A mobile crane must lift a load of **12 tonnes** at a **radius of 12 m**. The crane weight (with counterweight) is **25 tonnes** and its centroid is **2 m behind the slewing axis**. The hook block weighs **0.4 tonnes**.
- (i) Calculate the **overturning moment** and the **stabilizing moment**.
- (ii) Check whether the crane is **safe against overturning** (required FS = 1.5).
- (iii) If the load is increased to **18 tonnes**, what is the **maximum safe radius**? **(7 marks)**

---

### Model Solution — Question B

#### Part (a): Types of Cranes

Cranes are classified into:

1. **Overhead Travelling (EOT) Crane** — Bridge on rails; indoor industrial use.
2. **Gantry Crane** — Bridge on ground-supported legs; outdoor use.
3. **Jib Crane** — Pivoted horizontal arm; local circular coverage.
4. **Tower Crane** — Vertical tower with horizontal jib; construction sites.
5. **Mobile Crane** — Truck-mounted telescopic boom; variable site use.
6. **Crawler Crane** — Tracked undercarriage; off-road heavy lifting.
7. **Portal Crane** — Bridge with cantilevers on legs; shipyards.
8. **Floating Crane** — Mounted on a barge; port operations.
9. **Stacker / Reclaimer Crane** — Bulk material storage yards.

**Construction & Working of EOT Crane:**

The EOT crane consists of:
- **Two end carriages** mounted on **runway rails** fixed to the building columns.
- A **bridge** (single or double girder) spanning the runway.
- A **hoist trolley** that travels across the bridge (cross travel).
- A **hoisting mechanism** with wire rope, hook, and motor.
- A **power supply** through a **festoon system** or **conductor bars**.
- An **operator cabin** or **pendant control**.

**Working:** The crane provides **three motions**:
- **Long Travel** (LT) — Movement of the entire bridge along the runway.
- **Cross Travel** (CT) — Movement of the trolley across the bridge.
- **Hoisting (H)** — Vertical lifting and lowering of the hook.

**Four Safety Devices in EOT Cranes:**
1. **Overload Limiter / Load Cell** — Cuts off hoisting if load exceeds SWL.
2. **Upper Limit Switch** — Stops hoist at top travel to prevent over-winding.
3. **Lower Limit Switch** — Prevents over-unwinding of rope.
4. **Limit Switches for LT and CT** — Prevents over-travel at ends of travel.
5. **Buffers** — At ends of runway to absorb impact.
6. **Anti-Collision Device** — Between two cranes on the same runway.
7. **Slack Rope Prevention Device.**
8. **Audible and Visual Alarms (horn, beacon).**

#### Part (b): Crane Stability Numerical

**Given:**
$W = 12\ \text{t}, W_h = 0.4\ \text{t}, R = 12\ \text{m}, W_c = 25\ \text{t}, r = 2\ \text{m}, FS_{required} = 1.5$

**Step 1: Overturning Moment**

$$
M_{OT} = (W + W_h) \times R = (12 + 0.4) \times 12 = 12.4 \times 12 = 148.8\ \text{t·m}
$$

**Step 2: Stabilizing Moment**

$$
M_{ST} = W_c \times r = 25 \times 2 = 50\ \text{t·m}
$$

**Step 3: Factor of Safety Check**

$$
FS_{stability} = \dfrac{M_{ST}}{M_{OT}} = \dfrac{50}{148.8} = 0.336
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Crane is UNSAFE!** $FS = 0.336$ is far less than the required **1.5**. The crane will overturn.

**Step 4: Maximum Safe Load at R = 12 m**

$$
M_{OT, max} = \dfrac{M_{ST}}{FS_{required}} = \dfrac{50}{1.5} = 33.33\ \text{t·m}
$$

$$
(W + W_h)_{max} = \dfrac{33.33}{12} = 2.78\ \text{t}
$$

$$
W_{max} = 2.78 - 0.4 = 2.38\ \text{t}
$$

> [!NOTE]
> At **12 m radius**, the crane can only safely lift a maximum of **2.38 tonnes**.

**Step 5: Part (iii) — Maximum Safe Radius for W = 18 t**

New load: $W = 18\ \text{t}, W_h = 0.4\ \text{t}$
Total lift = $18.4\ \text{t}$

$$
M_{OT, max} = \dfrac{50}{1.5} = 33.33\ \text{t·m}
$$

$$
R_{max} = \dfrac{M_{OT, max}}{W + W_h} = \dfrac{33.33}{18.4} = 1.81\ \text{m}
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The crane can only lift **18 tonnes** if the radius is reduced to a maximum of **1.81 m**. Otherwise, the operation is unsafe.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in 'Types of MHE' Questions:**
>
> 1. **❌ Writing only the names without categorization:** Always present the classification tree first, then list the types under each. KTU examiners look for the *systematic classification* approach.
>
> 2. **❌ Confusing 'Troughed Belt Conveyor' with 'Flat Belt Conveyor':** Troughed idlers (20°–45°) are used for *bulk* materials; flat belts are used for *unit* loads (parcels, baggage).
>
> 3. **❌ Forgetting the 'Safety Factor' in sling/rope selection:** Always divide the breaking load by the SF (typically 5:1 for slings) — *never* quote the breaking load as the working load.
>
> 4. **❌ In numerical problems, missing the 'lift height' component (F_i):** Many students calculate only the horizontal tractive force and forget the *vertical lift* force, leading to grossly under-sized motors.
>
> 5. **❌ Using SWL = Breaking Load:** A fatal safety error. SWL is always **SWL = Breaking Load / Safety Factor**.
>
> 6. **❌ Confusing 'Outrigger' with 'Stabilizer':** Outriggers are *extended supports* that increase the stabilizing lever arm; stabilizers are *hydraulic cylinders* that level the crane.
>
> 7. **❌ Not mentioning Indian Standard codes (IS 807, IS 2266, IS 2760):** A KTU 14-mark answer without IS code references is considered incomplete.
>
> 8. **❌ Crane stability — using the wrong radius:** Always use the *maximum* radius for the worst-case stability check.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist for "Types of MHE":**

- **Five Master Classification Categories:**
  1. By **Path** (Fixed, Variable, Mixed)
  2. By **Function** (Transport, Position, Lift, Store, Load/Unload, Identify)
  3. By **Power Source** (Manual, Electric, IC, Hydraulic, Pneumatic)
  4. By **Load Nature** (Unit, Bulk, Liquid, Gaseous, Hazardous)
  5. By **Equipment Family** (Conveyors, Cranes, Trucks, Hoists, Storage, Auxiliaries)

- **Conveyor Types (9):** Belt, Roller, Chain, Screw, Pneumatic, Bucket Elevator, Vibratory, Overhead, Cable Belt.

- **Crane Types (9):** EOT, Gantry, Jib, Tower, Mobile, Crawler, Portal, Floating, Stacker/Reclaimer.

- **Forklift Types (6):** Counterbalance, Reach, Order Picker, Side Loader, Rough Terrain, Telehandler.

- **Hoist Types (4):** Chain, Wire Rope, Air, Scissor Lift + Winch.

- **Storage Types (5):** Pallet Rack, Cantilever, Mezzanine, AS/RS, Carousel.

- **Six-Step MHE Selection Logic:** Load → Movement → Environment → Selection Matrix → Safety Check → Economic Validation.

- **Belt Conveyor Capacity Formula:** $T = 3600 \cdot v \cdot A \cdot \rho$ (in kg/h or t/h).

- **Belt Conveyor Power Formula:** $P = \dfrac{F_{total} \cdot v}{\eta}$ where $F_{total} = F_t + F_i + F_a + F_b$.

- **Maximum Belt Inclination:** 10°–12° (sand), 15°–18° (coal), 18°–20° (crushed stone).

- **Crane Stability Check:** $FS = \dfrac{M_{ST}}{M_{OT}} \geq 1.5$ (static), $\geq 1.25$ (dynamic).

- **Safety Factors:** Slings **5:1**, Wire Rope **3.5:1–5:1**, Chains **4:1**, Hooks **5:1** (per IS codes).

- **Pallet Standard:** ISO 1200 × 1000 mm.

- **MHE Safety Devices to Memorize:** Overload Limiter, Upper/Lower Limit Switch, Slack Rope Prevention, Anti-Collision, Buffers, Audible Alarms, Reverse Alarms (forklifts), Beacon Lights, Emergency Stop.

- **Industry Applications Mnemonic — "BFL-OS" (Bulk, Freight, Logistics, Outdoor/Storage):** Mines use **belt + screw**; Warehouses use **forklift + conveyor**; Ports use **gantry + portal crane**; Power plants use **EOT + bucket elevator**.

- **KTU Favourite Questions:**
  - "Classify MHE based on path" (3 marks)
  - "Differentiate between EOT and Gantry crane" (3–7 marks)
  - "Calculate belt conveyor capacity and motor power" (7–14 marks)
  - "Crane stability numerical" (7–14 marks)

---
