- ✅ **UNCRPD (United Nations Convention on the Rights of Persons with Disabilities)** — India ratified in **2007**.

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Tip:** In KTU 2024 exams, always mention **the specific IS standard number** (e.g., "As per IS 4963:1987") alongside any dimension. Examiners award extra marks for citing standards explicitly.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Types - Escalator
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT522 - SAFETY IN MATERIAL HANDLING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Types - Escalator

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

An **escalator** is a continuous, power-driven, belt-type vertical transportation device consisting of a series of linked, motor-driven steps arranged on an endless chain that moves along a fixed track, used to transport people between floors of a building. It is classified as a **Class B passenger conveyor** under mechanical material handling equipment.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:** An escalator is a moving staircase — a power-driven, continuously operating conveyor used for transporting passengers between different floor levels. It combines the functions of a staircase and a conveyor, providing safe, high-volume pedestrian flow in commercial, transit, and industrial facilities.

## 1.2 Working Principle (Conceptual Foundation)

The escalator operates on the principle of a **step-chain conveyor** mechanism:

- A series of flat, hinged steps are mounted on two parallel endless roller chains
- The chains are driven by an electric motor (typically **7.5 kW to 60 kW**) through a gearbox
- Each step remains **flat (horizontal)** on the upper and lower runways, and forms a staircase pattern in the inclined section through a **truss and track cam system**
- The speed of the escalator is governed by the **escalator step speed formula**:

$$v_{step} = \frac{\pi \cdot d_{sprocket} \cdot N}{60}$$

where $v_{step}$ = step linear velocity (m/s), $d_{sprocket}$ = drive sprocket diameter (m), $N$ = sprocket RPM.

## 1.3 Intuitive Real-World Analogy

> [!TIP]
> **Think of an escalator as a "vertical conveyor belt made of stair steps."** Imagine a treadmill (continuous moving surface) that, instead of staying flat, gently tilts upward to form a moving staircase. Each individual step rises from the floor like a flat platform, remains flat while you stand on it, and then sinks back into the floor at the top — much like a magic trick where stairs keep appearing and disappearing in an endless loop. The steps have a hidden hinge mechanism that rotates them at exactly the right moments to form stairs on the slope and a flat surface at the top and bottom.

## 1.4 Standard Inclination Angles (IS 4591 / EN 115)

| Standard | Common Inclination | Speed |
|----------|-------------------|-------|
| IS 4591 (India) | **30°** and **35°** | 0.5 m/s (typical) |
| EN 115 (Europe) | **30°** and **35°** | 0.5 m/s – 0.65 m/s |
| Heavy-duty | 30° | up to 0.75 m/s |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The two most universally accepted inclinations are **30° (preferred for public use)** and **35° (saves floor space)**. The angle of inclination is a critical design parameter influencing safety, throughput, and structural load.

## 1.5 Basic Components (Foundation for Understanding Types)

1. **Truss / Supporting Structure** – Carries the entire load
2. **Step Assembly** – Tread + riser + rollers
3. **Step Chain / Band Chain** – Endless loop power transmission
4. **Drive System** – Motor + gearbox + brake
5. **Handrail System** – Continuous loop, moves synchronously with steps
6. **Track System** – Upper and lower tracks with comb plate transitions
7. **Balustrade** – Side panels and glass balustrades
8. **Control System** – Sensors, E-stops, fire safety interlocks
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Classification of Escalators — The Master Taxonomy

Escalators are classified based on **five major criteria** that KTU examiners frequently test:

### A) Based on Trajectory / Path Geometry
1. **Straight (Inclined) Escalator** — Most common; moves in a straight diagonal line
2. **Spiral / Helical Escalator** — Follows a curved helical path (rare, complex)
3. **Criss-Cross (Scissor / Scissor-type) Escalator** — Two escalators form an X pattern
4. **Parallel Escalators** — Multiple units run side-by-side in same direction

### B) Based on Application / Load
1. **Light-duty / Commercial** — Malls, offices (≈ 0.5 m/s)
2. **Heavy-duty / Public Transit** — Metro stations, airports (≈ 0.65 – 0.75 m/s)
3. **Industrial Escalator** — Manufacturing/warehousing for workers

### C) Based on Step Type
1. **Step-type (most common)** — Discrete flat steps linked by chain
2. **Moving Walkway (Travelator)** — Continuous flat belt (covered separately)
3. **Spiral step-type** — Curved steps on a helical track

### D) Based on Direction of Travel
1. **Up Escalator (Uplifter)**
2. **Down Escalator (Downlifter)**
3. **Reversible Escalator** — Direction can be switched via control

### E) Based on Environmental Use
1. **Indoor Escalator** — Standard protection
2. **Outdoor Escalator** — Weatherproof, with canopy
3. **Heavy-Oil / Hazardous Escalator** — Explosion-proof (industrial)

## 2.2 Detailed Type Analysis

### Type 1: Straight (Inclined) Escalator
- **Geometry:** A single straight inclined truss between two floors
- **Inclination:** 30° or 35°
- **Application:** Shopping malls, airports, railway stations
- **Capacity:** ~5,000–8,000 persons/hour
- **Advantages:** Simple design, low cost, easy maintenance
- **Disadvantages:** Occupies inclined floor space

### Type 2: Spiral / Helical Escalator
- **Geometry:** Curved steps moving along a helical path
- **Mechanism:** Each step is individually curved, with a custom-shaped truss
- **Application:** Premium retail spaces, museums, decorative buildings
- **Advantages:** Architecturally elegant, fits non-linear floor plans
- **Disadvantages:** Very expensive, lower speed (≤ 0.5 m/s), complex maintenance

### Type 3: Criss-Cross (Scissor) Escalator
- **Geometry:** Two escalators arranged in a crossed configuration — one ascends, the other descends, intersecting at the mid landing
- **Application:** Multi-storey shopping centres with limited central core
- **Advantages:** Compact central footprint, visually striking
- **Disadvantages:** Users must traverse over/under; safety concerns at intersection

### Type 4: Parallel Escalator
- **Geometry:** Two or more escalators run side-by-side in the same direction
- **Application:** Very high-traffic zones (e.g., Dubai Metro, Beijing Subway)
- **Advantages:** Higher throughput, redundancy during maintenance
- **Disadvantages:** Increased structural load, higher capital cost

### Type 5: Reversible Escalator
- **Geometry:** Standard straight escalator with reversible drive motor
- **Direction logic:** Controlled by sensors detecting passenger flow (especially in metro stations)
- **Safety interlock:** Must halt before reversing; anti-creep device required

### Type 6: Outdoor Escalator
- **Protection rating:** **IP54 / IP55 minimum**
- **Features:** Waterproof motor housing, drainage in pits, anti-corrosion treatment, weather canopy
- **Application:** Hilly cities (e.g., Medellín, Hong Kong hillside escalators)

### Type 7: Industrial / Heavy-Duty Escalator
- **Capacity:** Reinforced step, higher load (≥ 120 kg/step)
- **Application:** Factories, power plants, mine shafts (rare)
- **Safety:** Robust handrail, step missing detection, fire-resistant panels

## 2.3 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula / Parameter | Expression | Standard Unit | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Step linear speed | $v = \frac{\pi d N}{60}$ | m/s | Where $d$ = sprocket diameter, $N$ = RPM |
| 2 | Theoretical capacity | $C = \frac{3600 \cdot v}{0.4}$ | persons/hr | Assumes 1 person per 0.4 m step width |
| 3 | Vertical rise | $h = L \sin\theta$ | m | $L$ = inclined length, $\theta$ = inclination |
| 4 | Inclined length | $L = \frac{h}{\sin\theta}$ | m | |
| 5 | Drive motor power | $P = \frac{m \cdot g \cdot v \cdot \sin\theta}{\eta}$ | kW | $\eta$ = drive efficiency (0.75 – 0.85) |
| 6 | Step width (typical) | $W = 0.6 / 0.8 / 1.0$ | m | 600 mm, 800 mm, 1000 mm standard |
| 7 | Handrail speed | $v_h = v_{step}$ | m/s | Must match step speed synchronously |
| 8 | Brake stopping distance | $s = \frac{v^2}{2a}$ | m | $a$ = deceleration (0.5 – 1.0 m/s²) |
| 9 | Step cycle time | $T = \frac{p}{v}$ | s | $p$ = step pitch (typically 0.4 m) |
| 10 | Maximum rise (IS 4591) | $h_{max} \leq 6$ (per flight) | m | If higher, intermediate landing required |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Tip:** Always state the inclination (30° or 35°) and the standard (IS 4591 / EN 115) when answering escalator design questions. Examiners deduct marks if these are omitted.

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Urban transit:** Metro systems rely heavily on reversible heavy-duty escalators handling 20,000+ passengers/hour per direction
- **Smart cities:** Outdoor escalators integrated with IoT sensors for predictive maintenance
- **Disaster-prone zones:** Modern escalators in coastal cities use **sodium-resistant** stainless steel (AISI 316L) to prevent corrosion
- **Hillside cities:** Cities like Medellín (Colombia) use **outdoor public escalators** in low-income hillside neighbourhoods to replace 350+ step stairways, providing equitable urban mobility
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Theoretical Passenger Capacity of an Escalator

The theoretical capacity is the maximum number of passengers an escalator can transport per hour, assuming continuous loading.

**Step 1 — Define the parameters:**
- Let $v$ = step linear velocity (m/s)
- Let $w$ = width of a single step (m), so two people side-by-side occupy approximately $2w$
- Let $p$ = pitch (distance between two consecutive steps, typically 0.4 m)

**Step 2 — Calculate the time interval between successive steps:**

A new step arrives at the landing every time the chain advances by one pitch $p$.

$$\Delta t = \frac{p}{v} \text{ seconds}$$

**Step 3 — Number of steps per hour:**

A step passes a given point once every $\Delta t$ seconds. Therefore, in 3600 seconds (1 hour):

$$N_{steps} = \frac{3600}{\Delta t} = \frac{3600 \cdot v}{p}$$

**Step 4 — Apply loading factor:**

Each step can carry **2 passengers** at standard occupancy. The practical capacity uses a step spacing of 0.4 m (one passenger per step edge):

$$C = \frac{3600 \cdot v}{0.4} \text{ persons/hr}$$

**Step 5 — Substitute the standard step speed $v = 0.5$ m/s:**

$$C = \frac{3600 \times 0.5}{0.4} = \frac{1800}{0.4}$$

$$C = 4500 \text{ persons/hr}$$

> **Engineering Interpretation:** A standard 0.5 m/s indoor escalator moves **4,500 people per hour**, sufficient for medium-traffic commercial buildings. Doubling the speed to 1.0 m/s would double the capacity but requires additional safety features.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Drive Motor Power Requirement

To lift passengers against gravity, the motor must overcome the gravitational component of total load.

**Step 1 — Total lifted mass per unit time:**

Assume the escalator is fully loaded with $n$ persons, each of mass $m$ (typically 75 kg), moving at velocity $v$ along an inclination $\theta$.

The number of persons on the incline at any time is:

$$n = \frac{L}{p} \times (\text{passengers per step})$$

**Step 2 — Gravitational power required:**

The vertical component of velocity is $v \sin\theta$. The mechanical power to lift a mass $M$ at this vertical rate is:

$$P_{gross} = M \cdot g \cdot v \cdot \sin\theta$$

**Step 3 — Account for drive efficiency:**

$$\eta = \frac{P_{gross}}{P_{motor}} \implies P_{motor} = \frac{M \cdot g \cdot v \cdot \sin\theta}{\eta}$$

**Step 4 — Sample calculation:**

Let $M = 1500$ kg (loaded escalator), $v = 0.5$ m/s, $\theta = 30°$, $\eta = 0.8$:

$$P_{motor} = \frac{1500 \times 9.81 \times 0.5 \times \sin 30°}{0.8}$$

$$P_{motor} = \frac{1500 \times 9.81 \times 0.5 \times 0.5}{0.8}$$

$$P_{motor} = \frac{3678.75}{0.8} = 4598.4 \text{ W} \approx 4.6 \text{ kW}$$

Add a safety factor of 1.5 to 2.0:

$$P_{design} = 4.6 \times 1.8 = 8.28 \text{ kW}$$

Therefore, a standard **7.5 kW to 11 kW motor** is selected for a typical commercial escalator.

## 3.3 Analytical Comparison Matrix: Escalator Types

| Property | Straight | Spiral | Criss-Cross | Reversible | Outdoor | Industrial |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| Inclination (°) | 30 / 35 | Variable | 30 / 35 | 30 / 35 | 30 | 30 |
| Speed (m/s) | 0.5 – 0.75 | 0.3 – 0.5 | 0.5 | 0.5 – 0.65 | 0.5 | 0.5 – 0.65 |
| Step width (mm) | 600/800/1000 | 800/1000 | 800/1000 | 800/1000 | 1000 | 1000 |
| Capacity (pax/hr) | 4500 – 8000 | 2000 – 4000 | 4500 – 6000 | 4500 – 7000 | 4500 | 6000+ |
| Cost index (relative) | 1.0× | 6.0× | 1.3× | 1.1× | 1.5× | 1.8× |
| Maintenance cost | Low | Very High | Medium | Medium | Medium | High |
| IP Rating | IP21 | IP21 | IP21 | IP21 | IP55 | IP54 |
| Common use | Malls, offices | Premium retail | Atria, malls | Metro | Hill stations | Factories |

## 3.4 Safety Limits and Boundary Conditions

| Parameter | Limit | Source |
|---|---|---|
| Maximum rise per flight | 6 m (IS 4591) | Bureau of Indian Standards |
| Maximum inclination | 35° | EN 115 / IS 4591 |
| Minimum step width | 600 mm | Safety standard |
| Maximum step speed | 0.75 m/s (public), 0.5 m/s (heavy) | IS 4591 |
| Handrail speed tolerance | $\pm 2\%$ of step speed | EN 115-1:2017 |
| Static load on step | 120 kg minimum | EN 115 |
| Emergency stop response | ≤ 0.5 s | EN 115-1 |
| Comb plate gap | ≤ 4 mm | IS 4591 |
<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Classification Tree of Escalator Types

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[ESCALATOR MASTER CLASSIFICATION] --> B[Based on Trajectory]
    A --> C[Based on Application]
    A --> D[Based on Direction]
    A --> E[Based on Environment]

    B --> B1[Straight Inclined 30 / 35 deg]
    B --> B2[Spiral Helical Curved]
    B --> B3[Criss-Cross Scissor]
    B --> B4[Parallel Multi-Unit]

    C --> C1[Light Duty Malls]
    C --> C2[Heavy Duty Metro]
    C --> C3[Industrial Plants]

    D --> D1[Uplifter Upward]
    D --> D2[Downlifter Downward]
    D --> D3[Reversible Bi-Directional]

    E --> E1[Indoor IP21]
    E --> E2[Outdoor IP55]
    E --> E3[Hazardous Zone Ex-Proof]

    B1 --> B1a[Most Common 80 percent share]
    B2 --> B2a[Premium Use Curved Steps]
    B3 --> B3a[X-Configuration Atrium]
    C1 --> C1a[0.5 m/s 4500 pax per hour]
    C2 --> C2a[0.65 m/s 6000 to 8000 pax per hour]
    D3 --> D3a[Direction Auto-Switch via Sensors]
    E2 --> E2a[Weatherproof Canopy Required]
```

## 4.2 Functional Architecture of a Straight Escalator System

```mermaid
graph LR
    POWER[Three Phase Power Supply 415V 50Hz] --> CTRL[Motor Control Centre MCC]
    CTRL --> MOTOR[Drive Motor 7.5 to 11 kW Induction]
    MOTOR --> GEAR[Gear Reducer 30:1 Ratio]
    GEAR --> CHAIN[Step Chain Endless Loop]
    CHAIN --> STEPS[Step Assembly 600 to 1000 mm wide]
    CHAIN --> TRACK[Track System Upper and Lower Truss]
    STEPS --> COMB1[Upper Comb Plate]
    STEPS --> COMB2[Lower Comb Plate]
    MOTOR --> HAND[Handrail Drive System]
    HAND --> HANDRAIL[Continuous Handrail Loop]
    CTRL --> SENSOR1[Step Missing Sensor]
    CTRL --> SENSOR2[Handrail Speed Sensor]
    CTRL --> SENSOR3[Comb Plate Sensor]
    CTRL --> ESTOP[Emergency Stop Button]
    CTRL --> BRAKE[Electromagnetic Brake]
    BRAKE --> CHAIN
    SENSOR1 --> CTRL
    SENSOR2 --> CTRL
    SENSOR3 --> CTRL
    ESTOP --> CTRL
```

## 4.3 Safety Interlock Flow

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    START[Escalator Running] --> CHECK1{All Safety Sensors OK?}
    CHECK1 -->|Yes| NORMAL[Continue Normal Operation]
    CHECK1 -->|No| TRIP[Activate Emergency Stop]
    TRIP --> BRAKE[Apply Electromagnetic Brake]
    BRAKE --> STOP[Escalator Halts in less than 0.5 s]
    STOP --> ALARM[Audible and Visual Alarm]
    ALARM --> LOG[Incident Logged in PLC]
    LOG --> RESET[Manual Reset Required by Engineer]
    NORMAL --> MONITOR[Continuous Monitoring of Handrail Speed Step Integrity Comb Plate]
    MONITOR --> CHECK1
```

## 4.4 Cross-Section of a Step (Component Detail)

```mermaid
graph TB
    STEP[STEP ASSEMBLY CROSS SECTION] --> TREAD[Tread Surface Anti-Slip Grooves]
    STEP --> RISER[Riser Vertical Face]
    STEP --> ROLLER1[Front Roller Chain Engagement]
    STEP --> ROLLER2[Rear Roller Track Engagement]
    STEP --> CLEAT[Demountable Cleat Yellow Safety Strip]
    STEP --> HINGE[Internal Hinge Mechanism]
    HINGE --> ROT[Allows Flat-to-Step Rotation on Track Cam]
```
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 📘 SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. Define an escalator. Mention any two standard inclinations. `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

**Model Answer:**
An escalator is a power-driven, continuously moving staircase-type conveyor used to transport passengers between floor levels in buildings. It consists of linked steps driven by an endless chain on a fixed inclined truss.

The two standard inclinations are:
1. **30°** (preferred for public use under IS 4591)
2. **35°** (used where floor space is limited, especially in metro stations)

### Q2. List any four types of escalators based on trajectory. `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

**Model Answer:**
1. **Straight (Inclined) Escalator** — Most common; runs in a straight inclined line
2. **Spiral / Helical Escalator** — Curved steps on a helical track (premium retail)
3. **Criss-Cross / Scissor Escalator** — Two escalators forming an X-pattern
4. **Parallel Escalator** — Multiple units running side-by-side for high traffic

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice)

### 📝 Question A (Choice 1) `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]`

> **Q.A.** **(a)** Classify escalators in detail based on (i) trajectory, (ii) application, and (iii) environment. Mention one application of each. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **Q.A.** **(b)** A straight escalator has a vertical rise of 6 m, inclination of 30°, and step speed of 0.5 m/s. Calculate (i) the inclined length, (ii) the theoretical hourly capacity, and (iii) the brake stopping distance assuming deceleration of 0.75 m/s². **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

| Basis | Type | Application |
|---|---|---|
| Trajectory | Straight Inclined | Shopping malls, offices |
| Trajectory | Spiral Helical | Premium retail stores, museums |
| Trajectory | Criss-Cross | Multi-storey atrium of malls |
| Trajectory | Parallel | High-traffic metro stations |
| Application | Light-Duty Commercial | Cinema halls, malls (0.5 m/s) |
| Application | Heavy-Duty Transit | Metro, airports (0.65 m/s) |
| Application | Industrial | Power plant, factory worker movement |
| Environment | Indoor | Standard office building |
| Environment | Outdoor | Hillside cities (e.g., Medellín) |
| Environment | Hazardous Zone | Chemical plants, oil refineries |

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

**Given:** $h = 6$ m, $\theta = 30°$, $v = 0.5$ m/s, $a = 0.75$ m/s²

**(i) Inclined length:**

$$L = \frac{h}{\sin\theta} = \frac{6}{\sin 30°} = \frac{6}{0.5} = 12 \text{ m}$$

**(ii) Theoretical hourly capacity:**

$$C = \frac{3600 \cdot v}{0.4} = \frac{3600 \times 0.5}{0.4} = 4500 \text{ persons/hr}$$

**(iii) Brake stopping distance:**

$$s = \frac{v^2}{2a} = \frac{(0.5)^2}{2 \times 0.75} = \frac{0.25}{1.5} = 0.1667 \text{ m} \approx 0.17 \text{ m}$$

**Final Answers:**
- $L = 12$ m
- $C = 4500$ persons/hr
- $s \approx 0.17$ m (17 cm)

---

### 📝 Question B (Choice 2) `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]`

> **Q.B.** **(a)** Explain with neat sketches the working of a straight escalator. List its main components. **(7 Marks)**
>
> **Q.B.** **(b)** Compare straight, spiral, and criss-cross escalators on the basis of cost, capacity, maintenance, and typical application. **(7 Marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a):

A **straight escalator** operates on the **step-chain conveyor principle**:

1. **Truss:** A rigid steel frame inclined at 30° or 35° supports the entire mechanism
2. **Drive System:** A 3-phase induction motor (7.5–11 kW) drives a sprocket through a worm-gear reducer
3. **Step Chain:** Two endless roller chains carry the step assemblies in a continuous loop
4. **Steps:** Each step pivots on its axle. The tread remains flat (horizontal) on the upper and lower runs, but forms a staircase on the inclined run. This is achieved through a **track cam system** that rotates each step 90° as it rounds the upper sprocket
5. **Handrail:** A continuous rubber loop moves at exactly the same speed as the steps, supported on rollers
6. **Comb Plates:** Yellow-painted combs at both ends prevent objects from being drawn into the gap
7. **Brake:** A fail-safe electromagnetic brake stops the escalator within 0.5 s on power failure

**Main Components:** Truss, drive motor, gearbox, step chain, steps, track system, handrail, balustrade, comb plates, control panel, emergency brake, sensors.

#### Model Solution — Part (b):

| Parameter | Straight | Spiral | Criss-Cross |
|---|---|---|---|
| Cost (relative) | 1.0× (lowest) | 6.0× (highest) | 1.3× (medium) |
| Capacity (pax/hr) | 4,500 – 8,000 | 2,000 – 4,000 | 4,500 – 6,000 |
| Maintenance | Low and simple | Very high and specialised | Medium complexity |
| Typical application | Malls, offices, metro | Premium retail, museums | Multi-storey atriums |
| Speed | 0.5 – 0.75 m/s | 0.3 – 0.5 m/s | 0.5 m/s |
| Structural load | Moderate | High (curved) | High (intersection) |
| Footprint | Inclined length × width | Compact (curved) | Compact (X-cross) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall — Common Mark Loss Points:**
> 1. **Forgetting to specify inclination angle** (30° or 35°) — examiner deducts 1 mark
> 2. **Wrong unit in capacity formula** — write as "persons/hour," not "pax" alone
> 3. **Skipping efficiency ($\eta$) in motor power calculation** — state value between 0.75–0.85
> 4. **Confusing escalator with elevator** — escalator is a conveyor, not a lifting cage
> 5. **Not stating the relevant Indian Standard (IS 4591) or European Standard (EN 115)** — always cite the governing code
> 6. **Spiral escalator is NOT a moving walkway** — students often confuse the two; spiral has discrete curved *steps*, walkways have continuous *belts*
> 7. **Missing safety features** in classification — every type description must include a brief safety note (comb plate, E-stop, handrail)

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember 🔁

- ⚙️ **Escalator = Moving Staircase + Conveyor** (continuous passenger conveyor)
- 📐 **Two standard inclinations:** **30°** (preferred) and **35°** (space-saving)
- 🚶 **Standard step speeds:** 0.5 m/s (commercial), 0.65–0.75 m/s (heavy-duty transit)
- 🧮 **Capacity formula:** $C = \frac{3600 \cdot v}{0.4}$ persons/hr (for single-person-per-step edge loading)
- 🏗️ **Maximum rise per flight:** **6 m** under IS 4591 (intermediate landing required beyond)
- 🔄 **Reversible escalators** are mandatory in metro systems to handle peak-hour directional imbalance
- 🌧️ **Outdoor escalators** must meet **IP55** protection rating
- 🔗 **Step-chain mechanism** with track cam system rotates each step from flat (loading zones) to stepped (incline) configuration
- 🛑 **Emergency brake** must halt the escalator within **0.5 s** of activation
- 🪜 **Handrail speed** must match step speed within **±2%** tolerance
- 🚨 **Five-class safety classification:** Step-missing sensor, handrail-speed sensor, comb-plate sensor, emergency stop, fire-mode override
- 📜 **Governing standards:** **IS 4591 (India)**, **EN 115 (Europe)**, **ASME A17.1 (USA)**
- 🏙️ **Real-world icon:** Medellín Metrocable escalators (outdoor, 6 stages, ~384 m total rise) — symbol of inclusive urban mobility
- ❌ **Common confusion:** Escalators are NOT elevators; they are a subset of **passenger conveyors** under material handling
- ✅ **Always cite the standard and inclination in KTU exam answers** to secure full marks
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Safety Devices and Brakes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT522 - SAFETY IN MATERIAL HANDLING > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Safety Devices and Brakes # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Definition A **brake** is a mechanical device that employs **friction, electrical resistance, or hydraulic force** to absorb kinetic energy and bring a moving machine or load to a controlled halt. In the domain of material handling — which includes cranes, hoists, conveyors, forklifts, and elevators — brakes are not optional accessories but **mandatory safety-critical subsystems** governed by standards such as **IS 3938 (Electric Wire Rope Hoists)**, **IS 3177 (Code of Practice for Cranes)**, and **ISO 12100 (Safety of Machinery – General Principles for Design)**. A **safety device**, in the broader KTU context, is any component — mechanical, electrical, electro-mechanical, or hydraulic — whose primary function is to **prevent, detect, or mitigate hazardous conditions** arising during the storage, movement, lifting, or lowering of materials. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU Syllabus Highlight (PESFT522 – Module 4):** Students must be able to classify brakes (mechanical friction, electromagnetic, hydraulic, thruster-operated), identify safety devices (limit switches, overload relays, slack rope switches, anti-two-blocking valves, emergency stops, gates interlocks, buffers, governors), and solve elementary numerical problems on **braking torque, stopping time, stopping distance, and heat dissipation** in friction-based drum and disc brake systems. ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cricket Ball and the Glove" Imagine a fast bowler hurls a cricket ball at **150 km/h** toward a fielder. The ball possesses enormous **kinetic energy**. How does the fielder stop it safely? He does **not** apply a hard rigid wall — the ball would rebound and cause injury. Instead, he uses a **soft leather glove** which, through controlled friction over a brief contact period, **dissipates the kinetic energy as heat and sound** while gradually decelerating the ball to zero velocity. Now substitute the cricket ball with a **1-tonne load descending from a 10-metre hoist**, the bowler with **gravity**, and the fielder's glove with a **brake shoe pressing against a rotating drum**. The principle is identical: - The brake **does not instantly destroy** the energy (which would create destructive shock loads). - It **converts kinetic energy into heat at a controlled rate**. - The **friction force × velocity × time** = energy dissipated. The safety device, in turn, is the **umpire's signal** that says "stop the match" — it is the trigger, sensor, or interlock that *decides* when the brake must be applied. > [!NOTE] > **KTU 2024 Scheme Note (CO Mapping):** This topic primarily addresses **CO3** — *Identify and apply safety devices and braking systems in material handling equipment.* Bloom's cognitive levels covered: **Remember (L1), Understand (L2), Apply (L3), and Analyze (L4)**. ## 1.3 Classification Overview (At a Glance) | Category | Examples | Primary Function | |----------|----------|------------------| | **Friction Brakes** | Shoe brake, Band brake, Disc brake, Cone brake | Convert KE into heat via dry/wet friction | | **Electrical Brakes** | Plugging, Dynamic, Regenerative | Dissipate energy in resistor / return to grid | | **Hydraulic Brakes** | Thruster-operated, Oil-piston | Apply high torque via fluid pressure | | **Electro-mechanical** | Solenoid brake, Fail-safe spring-applied | Combined actuation and energy storage | | **Safety Devices** | Limit switch, Overload cut-out, Slack rope, Anti-2-block, E-stop, Governor, Buffer, Interlock | Detect/Prevent hazardous conditions | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Fail-Safe Principle (KTU 2024 Board Favourite):** In crane/hoist applications, the **default state of the brake must be APPLIED (closed)**. Loss of power → brake engages automatically. This is called a **"normally closed" or "spring-set, electrically-released"** brake. It is non-negotiable for vertical lifting. --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operating Principle of a Friction Brake A friction brake works on the principle of **kinetic friction between two contacting surfaces** — a stationary element (brake shoe/band/pad) and a rotating element (drum or disc). When contact is established, a **normal force N** is generated at the interface, producing a tangential friction force: $$F_f = \mu N$$ where $\mu$ is the coefficient of friction between the lining material and the drum/disc. ### 2.1.1 The Four-Stage Logic of Braking 1. **Detection Stage** — A safety device (limit switch, overload sensor, operator command) senses the need to arrest motion. 2. **Actuation Stage** — Energy (electric, hydraulic, pneumatic, mechanical) is delivered to the brake mechanism to engage the friction surfaces. 3. **Energy Conversion Stage** — The friction force acts over the deceleration distance, converting **kinetic energy of the moving mass into heat** in the lining and drum/disc. 4. **Heat Dissipation Stage** — Heat is radiated, convected, and conducted away to the surroundings. **Inadequate heat dissipation → brake fade → failure.** > [!NOTE] > **Brake Fade** is the temporary loss of braking effectiveness due to excessive temperature rise in the friction lining, causing $\mu$ to drop. This is a **commonly examined KTU concept** in long-descent or emergency-stop scenarios. ## 2.2 Types of Brakes — Engineering Analysis ### 2.2.1 Block / Shoe Brake (Single and Double) A lever pivots a friction block against a rotating drum. The torque produced is: $$T_b = F_c \cdot \mu \cdot R$$ where $F_c$ is the contact (clamping) force and $R$ is the drum radius. Double-shoe brakes produce **twice the torque** of single-shoe for the same actuation force because both shoes self-energize in the direction of rotation. ### 2.2.2 Band Brake A flexible friction-lined band wraps around a drum, tightened by a lever or hydraulic cylinder. Torque equation: $$T_b = (T_1 - T_2) \cdot R$$ where $T_1$ and $T_2$ are tensions in the tight and slack sides of the band. They are related by the **belt friction (Eytelwein) equation**: $$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta}$$ where $\theta$ is the angle of wrap in radians. ### 2.2.3 Disc Brake Multiple pistons clamp friction pads against a rotating disc (rotor). The torque for a single-sided caliper with `n` pistons: $$T_b = n \cdot F_p \cdot \mu \cdot r_{eff}$$ where $r_{eff}$ is the effective radius (mean of inner and outer pad radius). ### 2.2.4 Thruster-Operated Hydraulic Brake An **oil-hydraulic thruster** (e.g., YORKE / Electrohydraulic Thruster) pushes the brake shoe against the drum. Used in **EOT (Electric Overhead Travelling) cranes** because it provides: - Smooth, modulated braking - High torque at low actuating effort - Automatic engagement on power failure (via internal spring) ### 2.2.5 Electromagnetic Shoe Brake A **solenoid coil** pulls an armature against spring force to release the brake; when de-energized, the spring re-applies the brake. This is the **standard fail-safe design** for hoist motions. ## 2.3 Safety Devices — Operational Roles | Device | Location | Function | |--------|----------|----------| | **Limit Switch (Upper/Lower)** | Hoist drum / hook block | Cuts hoist motor when hook reaches safe travel limit | | **Overload Limit Switch** | Rope dead-end or load pin | Trips hoist if load > SWL × 1.25 (per IS 3938) | | **Slack Rope Switch** | Hoist drum | Detects rope going slack (e.g., load on ground) and prevents reverse winding | | **Anti-Two-Block (A2B) Valve** | Hook block | Prevents hook block colliding with hoist sheave (crane booms) | | **Emergency Stop Button** | Operator cabin & pendant | Mushroom-head, latching, red pushbutton — must be hardwired | | **Over-Speed Governor** | Elevator / mine hoist | Mechanically triggers safety gear if car exceeds rated speed by > 15% | | **Buffers (Oil/Spring)** | Track ends, elevator pit | Absorb residual KE at end of travel | | **Phase Failure Relay / Line Contactor** | Motor starter | Prevents motor starting on phase loss or wrong sequence | | **Interlock (Gate/Door)** | Elevator / lift enclosure | Prevents motion unless all gates closed | | **Slack Chain / Belt Switch** | Conveyor | Trips conveyor on chain/belt break or slip | ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!IMPORTANT] > **Master the table below — these are the equations that appear in 90% of KTU numerical questions on this module.** | # | Quantity | Formula | Variables & Units | Application | |---|----------|---------|-------------------|-------------| | 1 | Friction Force | $F_f = \mu N$ | $\mu$ = coeff. of friction (–), $N$ = normal force (N) | All friction brakes | | 2 | Braking Torque (Shoe) | $T_b = F_c \cdot \mu \cdot R$ | $F_c$ = clamping force (N), $R$ = drum radius (m) | Block / shoe brake | | 3 | Band Friction | $\dfrac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta}$ | $T_1$ tight side, $T_2$ slack side (N), $\theta$ = wrap (rad) | Band brake torque calc | | 4 | Band The brake torque must always exceed the torque due to worst-case load + dynamic factors. The heat dissipation check ensures the lining temperature stays within the manufacturer's continuous-duty limit (typically **< 250°C** for organic linings). --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Braking Torque of a Single-Block Shoe Brake (Pivoted Type) **Setup:** A cylindrical drum of radius $R$ rotates anticlockwise. A friction block of width $b$ is pressed against the drum by a horizontal force $P$ applied to a lever of length $L$ pivoted at the centre of the drum. The line of action of the contact force passes at a perpendicular distance $a$ from the pivot. **Step 1 — Free Body of the Lever:** The lever is in rotational equilibrium. Taking moments about the pivot O: $$P \cdot L = R_N \cdot a - \mu R_N \cdot c$$ where $R_N$ is the normal reaction at the shoe-drum contact, and $c$ is the vertical offset of the contact point from the line of the pivot (for self-energizing geometry, $c$ may be positive or negative). **Step 2 — Solve for $R_N$:** $$P \cdot L = R_N (a - \mu c)$$ $$R_N = \frac{P \cdot L}{a - \mu c}$$ **Step 3 — Friction Force at the Contact:** $$F_f = \mu \cdot R_N = \frac{\mu \cdot P \cdot L}{a - \mu c}$$ **Step 4 — Braking Torque about the Drum Axis:** $$\boxed{T_b = F_f \cdot R = \frac{\mu \cdot P \cdot L \cdot R}{a - \mu c}}$$ **Step 5 — Limiting Condition (Self-Locking):** If $\mu c = a$, then $T_b \to \infty$ at finite $P$ — this is **self-locking**, dangerous because the brake cannot be released. Design rule: $\mu c < a$, and a typical safety margin is $a \ge 2 \mu c$. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Examiner's Insight:** In exam numericals, students often forget the **friction moment $\mu R_N \cdot c$** which either assists or opposes the applied moment depending on the direction of rotation. Always draw the FBD and mark whether the friction force creates a **clockwise or anticlockwise** moment about the pivot. ## 3.2 Derivation: Stopping Time and Distance for a Hoist Descending Under Load **Problem Frame:** A hoist lowers a load of mass $m$ at constant descending speed $v$. The brake is applied. Find (i) the stopping time, (ii) the stopping distance, and (iii) the heat generated. **Step 1 — Free Body During Deceleration:** Forces acting on the descending load (downward positive): - Weight: $W = mg$ (downward) - Rope tension: $T$ (upward, through brake) - Brake friction force: $F_f = \mu N$ (upward, opposing motion) Newton's second law (deceleration, so acceleration is **upward**): $$T + F_f - mg = m a$$ But during steady descent, $T = mg$. When brake is applied, $T$ drops (rope goes slack on the drum side) and **only $F_f$ decelerates the load**: $$F_f - mg = m a$$ Wait — careful: if $F_f > mg$, the load decelerates (slows down then reverses). If $F_f < mg$, it would accelerate, which is unsafe. In **controlled lowering**: $$F_f - mg = m a \quad (\text{with } a \text{ taken upward as positive deceleration})$$ **Step 2 — Solve for Deceleration:** $$a = \frac{F_f - mg}{m} = \frac{\mu N - mg}{m}$$ For the brake to **decelerate and stop** the load, we need $F_f > mg$ momentarily, so $\mu N > mg$. After velocity becomes zero, static friction holds the load: $\mu N \ge mg$. **Step 3 — Stopping Time (using $v = u - a t$, with $v = 0$):** $$\boxed{t_s = \frac{v}{a} = \frac{m \cdot v}{F_f - mg}}$$ **Step 4 — Stopping Distance (using $v^2 = u^2 - 2 a s$):** $$\boxed{s = \frac{v^2}{2 a} = \frac{m v^2}{2(F_f - mg)}}$$ **Step 5 — Heat Generated in the Lining (Energy Balance):** Initial KE: $E_k = \frac{1}{2} m v^2$ Work done against gravity during stop: $W_g = m g s$ (negative if we take KE change) Energy dissipated as heat in lining = Initial KE + Work done by gravity (if load descends during braking) — Work done by rope tension (which goes slack and does no work) = $\frac{1}{2} m v^2 + m g s$ Substituting $s = \frac{v^2}{2 a}$ and $a = \frac{F_f - mg}{m}$: $$\boxed{Q = \frac{1}{2} m v^2 + m g \cdot \frac{m v^2}{2(F_f - mg)} = \frac{m v^2}{2} \left( 1 + \frac{mg}{F_f - mg} \right) = \frac{m v^2}{2} \cdot \frac{F_f}{F_f - mg}}$$ > [!NOTE] > **If the brake is applied such that $F_f = mg$ (barely holds the load without deceleration),** then $a \to 0$, and theoretically $s \to \infty$ and $t_s \to \infty$ — the load never stops. In practice, this is why hoists are designed with a **brake factor of safety** such that $F_f = 1.5$ to $2.0$ times $mg$ at rated load. ## 3.3 Worked-Out KTU-Style Numerical > [!IMPORTANT] > **Solved Example (Model Question — Module 4, 14 Marks):** **Question:** A crane hoist lowers a load of **$5$ tonnes** at a steady speed of **$1.5$ m/s**. A double-shoe brake is applied. Each shoe has a coefficient of friction $\mu = 0.35$ and presses on a drum of radius $R = 0.25$ m. The brake is designed to develop a braking torque such that deceleration is $1.2$ m/s². Calculate: 1. The total braking torque required. 2. The normal force on each shoe. 3. The stopping time. 4. The stopping distance. 5. The heat generated in the brake lining. **Given:** $m = 5000$ kg, $v = 1.5$ m/s, $\mu = 0.35$, $R = 0.25$ m, $a = 1.2$ m/s², $g = 9.81$ m/s². **Solution:** **Part (1) — Total Braking Torque:** For a descending load being decelerated: $$F_f = m(g + a) = 5000 \times (9.81 + 1.2) = 5000 \times 11.01 = 55050 \text{ N}$$ $$T_b = F_f \cdot R = 55050 \times 0.25 = 13762.5 \text{ N·m}$$ **Part (2) — Normal Force on Each Shoe (Double-Shoe):** Total friction force from both shoes: $F_f = 2 \mu N_{shoe}$ $$N_{shoe} = \frac{F_f}{2 \mu} = \frac{55050}{2 \times 0.35} = \frac{55050}{0.70} = 78642.86 \text{ N}$$ $$\boxed{N_{shoe} \approx 78.64 \text{ kN per shoe}}$$ **Part (3) — Stopping Time:** $$t_s = \frac{v}{a} = \frac{1.5}{1.2} = 1.25 \text{ s}$$ **Part (4) — Stopping Distance:** $$s = \frac{v^2}{2 a} = \frac{(1.5)^2}{2 \times 1.2} = \frac{2.25}{2.4} = 0.9375 \text{ m}$$ **Part (5) — Heat Generated:** Using the energy balance: $$Q = \frac{1}{2} m v^2 \cdot \frac{F_f}{F_f - mg}$$ Substituting: $$Q = \frac{1}{2} \times 5000 \times (1.5)^2 \times \frac{55050}{55050 - 5000 \times 9.81}$$ $$Q = \frac{1}{2} \times 5000 \times 2.25 \times \frac{55050}{55050 - 49050}$$ $$Q = 5625 \times \frac{55050}{6000} = 5625 \times 9.175$$ $$\boxed{Q \approx 51609 \text{ J} \approx 51.6 \text{ kJ}}$$ --- # 4. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model QP]` **Define a "fail-safe brake" and explain its significance in crane hoisting applications.** **Model Answer (Target: 3 marks, ~80 words):** A fail-safe brake is one in which the **default (de-energized) state is the engaged/braking state**, typically achieved by spring-applied, electrically-released construction. In crane hoisting, vertical loads are supported against gravity. If power fails mid-lift, a fail-safe brake **automatically engages** via spring force, holding the load stationary and preventing uncontrolled descent, which could cause fatalities and equipment damage. IS 3938 and IS 3177 **mandate** fail-safe braking for all hoist motions. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall (–½ mark):** Students often confuse "fail-safe" with "fail-secure." In a hoist, fail-safe means the **brake is applied** on failure — not the motor. Memorize: **Spring Set, Electric Release (SSER)**. ### Question 2 `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model QP]` **List any six safety devices used in a modern EOT crane and state the specific hazard each one mitigates.** **Model Answer (Target: 3 marks, ~80 words):** | # | Safety Device | Hazard Mitigated | |---|---------------|------------------| | 1 | Upper Limit Switch | Hook block over-winding and collision with hoist sheave | | 2 | Overload Limit Switch | Lifting beyond Safe Working Load (SWL) — structural failure | | 3 | Emergency Stop Button | Imminent operator or bystander danger | | 4 | Slack Rope Switch | Reverse winding / drum damage when load lands | | 5 | Anti-Two-Block Valve | Hook hitting boom tip sheave (mobile cranes) | | 6 | Long Travel Limit Switch | Crane overrunning the gantry rail ends | | 7 | Phase Failure Relay | Motor single-phasing and burn-out | | 8 | Brake Wear Indicator | Undetected lining wear → brake failure | > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall (–½ mark):** Naming devices without stating the **hazard** loses marks. KTU answers must always link **device → hazard → consequence**. --- ## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice Provided) ### Question A (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2022]` **A crane hoist lowers a load of 8 tonnes at a constant speed of 2 m/s. A double-shoe brake is used. Each shoe has coefficient of friction 0.4 and presses on a brake drum of radius 0.3 m. The brake is set to produce a deceleration of 1.5 m/s². Determine:** **(a)** The total braking torque and the normal force on each shoe. **(7 marks)** **(b)** The stopping time, stopping distance, and total heat generated in the brake lining. **(7 marks)** **Given:** $m = 8000$ kg, $v = 2$ m/s, $\mu = 0.4$, $R = 0.3$ m, $a = 1.5$ m/s², $g = 9.81$ m/s². --- #### Model Solution to Question A: **Part (a) — Braking Torque and Normal Force per Shoe [7 marks]:** **Step 1 — Total friction force required for the desired deceleration (lowering load):** For a load being lowered and decelerated, the net upward force must exceed the weight: $$F_f = m(g + a) = 8000 \times (9.81 + 1.5) = 8000 \times 11.31$$ $$F_f = 90480 \text{ N} \approx 90.48 \text{ kN}$$ **Step 2 — Total braking torque on the drum:** $$T_b = F_f \cdot R = 90480 \times 0.3 = 27144 \text{ N·m}$$ $$\boxed{T_b = 27.144 \text{ kN·m} \approx 27.14 \text{ kN·m}}$$ **Step 3 — Normal force per shoe (double-shoe brake, friction from both shoes):** $$F_f = 2 \mu N_{shoe}$$ $$N_{shoe} = \frac{F_f}{2 \mu} = \frac{90480}{2 \times 0.4} = \frac{90480}{0.8} = 113100 \text{ N}$$ $$\boxed{N_{shoe} = 113.1 \text{ kN per shoe}}$$ --- **Part (b) — Stopping Time, Stopping Distance, Heat Generated [7 marks]:** **Step 1 — Stopping time:** $$t_s = \frac{v}{a} = \frac{2}{1.5} = 1.333 \text{ s}$$ $$\boxed{t_s = 1.333 \text{ seconds}}$$ **Step 2 — Stopping distance:** $$s = \frac{v^2}{2 a} = \frac{(2)^2}{2 \times 1.5} = \frac{4}{3} = 1.333 \text{ m}$$ $$\boxed{s = 1.333 \text{ m}}$$ **Step 3 — Heat generated in the lining (energy balance for descending load):** $$Q = \frac{1}{2} m v^2 \cdot \frac{F_f}{F_f - mg}$$ Calculate the weight: $$mg = 8000 \times 9.81 = 78480 \text{ N}$$ $$F_f - mg = 90480 - 78480 = 12000 \text{ N}$$ $$Q = \frac{1}{2} \times 8000 \times (2)^2 \times \frac{90480}{12000}$$ $$Q = 16000 \times 7.54 = 120640 \text{ J}$$ $$\boxed{Q \approx 120.64 \text{ kJ}}$$ **Verification (alternative method using work-energy):** - Initial KE: $\frac{1}{2} m v^2 = 16000$ J - Work done by gravity during descent through $s = 1.333$ m: $W_g = mg \cdot s = 78480 \times 1.333 = 104605$ J - Work done by brake friction = Initial KE + Work by gravity = $16000 + 104605 = 120605$ J ✓ (matches within rounding) > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall (–2 to –3 marks):** Students frequently use the **wrong formula** $F_f = m(g - a)$ for a descending load being decelerated. The correct sign convention: deceleration is **upward**, so the friction force must overcome both gravity AND provide the upward deceleration → use $F_f = m(g + a)$. A common marking deduction is 1–2 marks for this sign error. --- ### Question B (14 Marks) — `[KTU University Exam – May 2023, Module 4]` **(a)** With a neat sketch, explain the construction and working of a **thruster-operated shoe brake** used in EOT cranes. Discuss its **fail-safe** feature. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A conveyor belt is driven by a 15 kW motor at 1450 rpm through a reduction gearbox. An electromagnetic disc brake is fitted on the motor shaft. The disc has an effective radius of 0.15 m and the lining coefficient of friction is 0.32. The brake is engaged during a normal stop. Calculate: 1. The angular velocity of the disc and the equivalent mass moment of inertia reflected to the motor shaft if the conveyor system has $I = 1.2$ kg·m². **(3 marks)** 2. The braking torque and friction force required to stop the system in 4 seconds from full speed. **(4 marks)** **Given:** $P = 15$ kW, $N = 1450$ rpm, $r_{eff} = 0.15$ m, $\mu = 0.32$, $t_s = 4$ s, $I = 1.2$ kg·m². --- #### Model Solution to Question B: **Part (a) — Thruster-Operated Shoe Brake (7 marks):** **Construction (describe and sketch):** A thruster-operated brake consists of: 1. **A brake drum** keyed to the motor (or gearbox) shaft. 2. **Two brake shoes** lined with friction material (asbestos-free, sintered bronze, or organic compound), pivoted on a rigid frame. 3. **A hydraulic thruster unit** — essentially an oil-filled cylinder with a centrifugal impeller driven by a small three-phase motor. 4. **A linkage mechanism** (toggle or lever) connecting the thruster piston rod to the brake shoes. 5. **A compression spring** that pre-loads the shoes against the drum when the thruster is de-energized. **Working:** - **Energized state (motor running):** The thruster motor rotates an impeller, pumping oil into the cylinder. The piston extends against the spring force, the linkage retracts the shoes, and the drum rotates freely (un-braked). - **De-energized state (braking):** The thruster motor stops, oil drains back, the spring pushes the piston, the linkage forces the shoes against the drum, and friction produces braking torque. **Fail-Safe Feature:** The spring is the **default energy-storage element**. Power failure → spring pushes shoes onto drum → brake applies. This is the **Spring-Set, Thruster-Released (SSTR)** configuration. It satisfies the **IS 3938 / IS 3177** mandate that all hoist brakes must be fail-safe (fail to "ON" position, holding the load). > **Sketch (textual representation):** > ``` > ┌─────── Lever Arm ────────┐ > │ │ > [Shoe A]──Pivoted Frame──[Shoe B] > \ / > \ Brake Drum / > \ (rotating) / > \ | / / > \═══╪═══/ ← Drum > | > [Thruster + Spring] > | > [3-Phase AC Motor] > ``` **Part (b) — Disc Brake on Conveyor Motor (7 marks):** **Step 1 — Angular velocity of the disc:** $$\omega = \frac{2 \pi N}{60} = \frac{2 \pi \times 1450}{60} = \frac{9102.65}{60} = 151.71 \text{ rad/s}$$ $$\boxed{\omega = 151.71 \text{ rad/s}}$$ **Step 2 — Equivalent mass moment of inertia:** The problem states $I = 1.2$ kg·m² is the equivalent moment of inertia reflected to the motor shaft. So: $$\boxed{I_{eq} = 1.2 \text{ kg·m}^2}$$ **Step 3 — Angular deceleration:** $$\alpha = \frac{\omega}{t_s} = \frac{151.71}{4} = 37.93 \text{ rad/s}^2$$ **Step 4 — Required braking torque:** $$T_b = I_{eq} \cdot \alpha = 1.2 \times 37.93 = 45.51 \text{ N·m}$$ $$\boxed{T_b = 45.51 \text{ N·m}}$$ **Step 5 — Friction force on the disc surface:** $$F_f = \frac{T_b}{r_{eff}} = \frac{45.51}{0.15} = 303.4 \text{ N}$$ **Step 6 — Verification using friction relation $F_f = \mu N$:** $$N = \frac{F_f}{\mu} = \frac{303.4}{0.32} = 948.13 \text{ N}$$ $$\boxed{N \approx 948 \text{ N (clamping force per disc face)}}$$ > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Pitfall (–1 mark):** Students often forget to **convert rpm to rad/s** and use $N$ directly in subsequent equations. This leads to a 100× error in torque. Also, **equivalent moment of inertia** at the motor shaft is different from the load-side inertia by the square of the gear ratio — students must check whether the problem gives the "reflected" or "load-side" value. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Before you enter the KTU exam hall, ensure you can tick every box below:** - [ ] **Fail-Safe Brakes are MANDATORY for vertical lifting** (hoists, elevators, mine winders). The brake must apply on power failure. - [ ] **Spring-Set, Electric-Release (SSER)** is the standard construction for solenoid brakes; **Spring-Set, Thruster-Released (SSTR)** is standard for EOT crane shoe brakes. - [ ] The **belt friction equation** for band brakes: $\dfrac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta}$ — use $\theta$ in **radians**, not degrees. - [ ] For a **descending load** being decelerated, $F_f = m(g + a)$, **NOT** $m(g - a)$. - [ ] For a **rising load** being decelerated, $F_f = m(g - a)$; if $a > g$, the load is decelerating but not reversing. - [ ] **Stopping time** $t_s = \dfrac{v}{a}$; **stopping distance** $s = \dfrac{v^2}{2a}$; **heat generated** equals the energy balance $Q = F_f \cdot s$ for a level track, or $Q = F_f \cdot s + mg \cdot s$ for a descending load. - [ ] **Brake fade** is caused by $\mu$ dropping at high temperature — relevant for long descents and emergency stops. - [ ] **Overload limit switch** trips at **1.25 × SWL** (per IS 3938). - [ ] **Anti-Two-Block (A2B)** device prevents the hook block from hitting the boom tip sheave on mobile cranes. - [ ] **Emergency stop** must be **latching type** (twist-to-release or key-reset), **red mushroom-head**, and **hardwired** (not software-controlled). - [ ] **Buffers** are sized for residual KE at end of travel: elevator buffers limit $a_{buffer} \le 1g$ for passenger comfort. - [ ] **Phase Failure Relay (PFR)** prevents motor single-phasing damage — a must for all three-phase material handling motors. - [ ] **Brake lining materials**: organic (cheap, fade-prone), sintered (high-temp), ceramic (premium), asbestos-free (regulatory mandate). - [ ] The **self-energizing shoe brake** uses drum rotation to increase normal force — but if $\mu c = a$ (where $c$ is the offset of contact from pivot), it becomes **self-locking** and **must be avoided**. - [ ] In KTU numericals, always state: **Given data → Formula → Substitution → Final boxed answer with units**. - [ ] **Draw neat free-body diagrams** in lever-type brake problems; it earns ½ to 1 mark even if the final number is wrong. - [ ] Remember the **standard $g = 9.81$ m/s²** unless the problem specifies otherwise; do not approximate to 10. > **End of Module 4 Notes — Safety Devices and Brakes.** > **Best of luck for your KTU 2024 Scheme examinations!**

topics.title: Moving Walks - Man Lifts
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT522 - SAFETY IN MATERIAL HANDLING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Moving Walks - Man Lifts

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->

# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Moving Walks (Travelators / Autowalks)

### Formal Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology)
A **Moving Walk** is a continuously operating, slow-speed conveyor mechanism designed exclusively for the **horizontal or inclined transport of pedestrians** over short to medium distances. It consists of an endless belt, pallet, or slat surface running between two terminals, propelled by a geared drive motor, and supported by an enclosed structural truss.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Key Distinction:** Unlike *escalators*, moving walks are predominantly **horizontal (or mildly inclined, ≤ 12°)** and are intended for passenger transport on a *flat plane*, not for floor-to-floor vertical transitions.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine a **conveyor belt in a supermarket checkout, but scaled up to human size, mounted on the floor, and installed in an airport concourse**. You step onto it as if stepping onto a moving sidewalk in an airport, and it carries you effortlessly while you stand still relative to the belt. The mechanical principle is identical to a belt conveyor used for boxes — only the cargo is a human being, which dramatically changes the safety requirements.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Standard Operating Speed:** Modern moving walks operate at **0.5 m/s to 0.8 m/s** (≈ 30–48 m/min). This is the **speed of a brisk walk**, deliberately chosen so that a passenger can step on and off safely without falling.

### Primary Application Sectors
- **Airports** (long concourses, terminal-to-gate transit)
- **Shopping Malls & Hypermarkets** (parking-to-store, food-court access)
- **Metro / Subway Stations** (platform-to-concourse)
- **Exhibition Centers & Convention Halls**
- **Hospitals** (inter-building patient transit ramps)

---

## 1.2 Man Lifts (Aerial Work Platforms – AWPs / Personnel Elevators)

### Formal Definition
A **Man Lift** is a mobile or stationary **mechanized platform specifically engineered to elevate one or more workers, together with their tools and minor materials, to a defined working height** for tasks such as maintenance, installation, inspection, or overhead construction work.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Terminology Note:** In the KTU Safety Engineering context, "Man Lifts" refers to **Mobile Elevating Work Platforms (MEWPs)** under OSHA 1910.67 / 1910.68, *not* industrial freight elevators used for cargo.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Think of a **"cherry picker"** mounted on a truck that you see utility workers using to fix overhead power lines, or the **orange scissor-lift platform** you see inside a large warehouse where a worker is changing a high-bay light. The defining feature is: **the lifting system is integral to a wheeled/track-mounted base** and is *only* meant for personnel access — *not* for moving heavy goods.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Critical Safety Tag:** Man lifts are classified as **"Personnel-Carrying Devices"** under KTU Safety Module 4. They must **never** be used as material lifts, cranes, or freight elevators. Substituting a man lift for a forklift is the single most common cause of catastrophic AWP tip-overs in industrial accident data.

### Standard Classification (Based on ANSI A92.20 / OSHA)
| Group | Type | Typical Use |
|---|---|---|
| **Group A** | Scissor Lifts (Vertical Mast) | Indoor warehouse, maintenance |
| **Group B** | Boom-Supported MEWPs (Articulating / Telescopic) | Outdoor construction, utilities |
| **Special** | Truck-Mounted Aerial Lifts | Utility line work |
| **Special** | Self-Propelled Vertical Mast (Push-Around) | Light indoor tasks |

---

## 1.3 Why These Two Systems Are Grouped Together in KTU Module 4

Both systems share a **common risk profile**:

- **Human body is the cargo** → consequence of failure is fatality, not just property loss.
- **Continuous/passenger-carrying duty** → zero-defect reliability expected.
- **Public-access exposure** (especially moving walks) → operator may be untrained passenger.
- **Governed by overlapping safety codes** (EN 115, ANSI A92, OSHA 1910).

> [!TIP]
> **Mnemonic for KTU Exam:** "**M&M = Moving Walks + Man Lifts = People Movers**". Always remember: in KTU valuation, the examiner will deduct marks if you discuss design without referencing **passenger safety** and **standards compliance**.

---

## 1.4 Governing Safety Standards (Mandatory for KTU Answers)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Always cite the governing standard in your exam answer.** Examiners award additional marks for standard references.

| Equipment | Primary Indian/International Standard |
|---|---|
| Moving Walks | **EN 115-1:2017** (European), **IS 14665** (Indian), **ASME A17.1/CSA B44** (North American) |
| Man Lifts / AWPs | **ANSI A92.20** (Design), **OSHA 1910.67** (Vehicle-Mounted), **OSHA 1910.68** (Manlifts), **IS 11460** |
| Hydraulic Systems (common) | **ISO 4413** (Hydraulic Fluid Power), **IS 10497** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->

# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Moving Walks — Operating Principle & Components

### 2.1.1 Operating Principle
A moving walk is essentially a **horizontal/lightly inclined endless belt conveyor**. The upper surface (the *passenger-carrying surface*) moves continuously from one end (the *lower landing*) to the other (the *upper landing*), turns around at each end via a sprocket/roller system, and returns underneath through a *return run* inside the supporting truss.

The drive motor (typically a **geared AC motor with a worm reduction gearbox**) drives a **step-chain or drive sprocket** at the upper landing. The step-chain pulls the pallets (or belt) around the system. The handrails are driven synchronously with the steps via a separate chain running along the balustrade.

### 2.1.2 Two Main Construction Types
1. **Belt-Type (Light Duty)** — Single wide rubber belt, suitable for **horizontal** and very shallow inclinations.
2. **Pallet-Type (Heavy Duty)** — Individual aluminum pallets riding on a step-chain, suitable for **inclined** applications up to 12°.

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** If a question specifies **inclination > 6°**, the expected answer is the **pallet type**, not the belt type, because pallet-type tracks can be designed with stepped/level surfaces that remain horizontal at any truss angle.

### 2.1.3 Key Subsystems
- **Truss (Structural Frame):** Steel/galvanized structural enclosure supporting the entire length.
- **Drive System:** Motor + worm gearbox + drive sprocket, located at the upper landing.
- **Step / Pallet Assembly:** Pallet + rollers + step-chain.
- **Handrail System:** Continuous rubber loop running on the balustrade.
- **Balustrade:** Vertical side panels, typically glass or stainless steel.
- **Comb Plate:** Stationary toothed plate at landings where pallets retract into the comb (the most safety-critical component).
- **Safety Devices:** Step-chain monitor, handrail-speed sensor, comb-impact switch, overspeed governor, emergency stop buttons at both landings.

---

## 2.2 Man Lifts — Operating Principles by Type

### 2.2.1 Scissor Lift
A **scissor mechanism** consists of crossed steel arms pinned at the center. A horizontal hydraulic cylinder, mounted at the base, extends and pushes the arms apart vertically, lifting the platform. Each arm-pair can be linked in series (multi-stage) to multiply the lift height.

**Key geometry:** If each arm has length $L$ and the angle with the horizontal is $\theta$:

$$h = L \sin\theta, \qquad b = L \cos\theta$$

As $\theta \to 90°$, height reaches its maximum; as $\theta \to 0°$, the platform is at ground level.

### 2.2.2 Boom-Supported AWPs
- **Articulating Boom:** Two or more hinged boom sections + a small jib; capable of reaching *over* obstacles.
- **Telescopic Boom:** Single straight boom that extends linearly; offers greater horizontal reach.

Both rotate on a turret mounted on a vehicle or self-propelled wheeled chassis.

### 2.2.3 Self-Propelled Vertical Mast (Push-Around)
A compact, manually-positioned platform with a single vertical lifting mast. Used for **indoor, low-height tasks** (e.g., changing light bulbs, sign maintenance).

---

## 2.3 The "Why" Behind Critical Safety Numbers

| Parameter | Typical Value | Why This Number? |
|---|---|---|
| Moving walk nominal speed | **0.5 – 0.8 m/s** | Matches human walking speed; allows safe step-on/step-off with ≤ **0.1 m/s** relative velocity. |
| Maximum inclination | **≤ 12°** | Beyond 12°, passengers instinctively grab handrails, overloading the design. |
| Handrail height | **900 – 1100 mm** above pallet | Ergonomic reach for the 5th–95th percentile adult. |
| Comb-tooth engagement depth | **≥ 4 mm overlap** | Prevents shoe/sandal entrapment in the comb gap. |
| AWP design wind load | **≤ 12.5 m/s (≈ 45 km/h)** | Operational limit; above this, lower the platform. |
| Scissor lift safety factor (structural) | **2.5 to 4.0** (on yield) | Compensates for dynamic loading and lateral sway. |

---

## 2.4 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Memorize this table for Part B derivations.** KTU examiners frequently test power, belt tension, and scissor-lift cylinder force.

| # | Quantity | Formula | Units | Key Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Belt linear speed | $v = \dfrac{\pi D N}{60}$ | m/s | $D$ = drive sheave dia (m), $N$ = rpm |
| 2 | Tractive force on incline (moving walk) | $F = W(\sin\alpha + \mu \cos\alpha)$ | N | $W$ = total moving mass, $\alpha$ = inclination |
| 3 | Drive power | $P = \dfrac{F \cdot v}{\eta}$ | W | $\eta$ = drive efficiency (typically **0.75 – 0.85**) |
| 4 | Belt tension ratio (capstan/Eytelwein) | $\dfrac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta}$ | — | $\theta$ in radians, $\mu$ = wrap friction |
| 5 | Scissor lift platform height | $h = L \sin\theta$ | m | Single stage |
| 6 | Scissor lift width (footprint) | $b = L \cos\theta$ | m | At the pivot base |
| 7 | Hydraulic cylinder force (scissor) | $F_{cyl} = \dfrac{W_{total}}{\tan\theta}$ | N | At angle $\theta$, total platform load $W_{total}$ |
| 8 | Mechanical advantage (scissor) | $MA = \dfrac{1}{\tan\theta}$ | — | Increases as lift rises |
| 9 | Tipping stability of AWP | $SF = \dfrac{W_b \cdot d_r}{W_p \cdot h_p + F_w \cdot h_w}$ | — | Must be **> 1.5**; $W_b$ = ballast weight |
| 10 | Hydraulic pressure | $P_h = \dfrac{F_{cyl}}{A_{piston}}$ | Pa | $A_{piston}$ = piston cross-section area |
| 11 | Comb-tooth clearance | $g \le 4 \text{ mm}$ | mm | Per EN 115; checked at every maintenance |
| 12 | Handrail speed matching tolerance | $\Delta v \le \pm 5\%$ | — | Between pallet and handrail |

> [!TIP]
> **Formula #4 (Eytelwein) and #7 (Scissor cylinder force) are the two most-frequently asked derivations in KTU Part B.** Practice both.

---

## 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Moving walks** are a *public-safety-critical* system; their failure analysis is a textbook subject in **reliability engineering** and **human-factors engineering**.
- **Man lifts** are the canonical case study for **stability & tip-over analysis** in **vehicle dynamics**, with direct extensions to **crane stability** and **off-shore platform design**.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->

# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Drive Power Required for an Inclined Moving Walk

### Given Data (Typical KTU-Style Problem)
- Total moving mass (pallets + passengers + handrail): $W = 1500 \text{ kg}$ (i.e., $14715 \text{ N}$)
- Inclination angle: $\alpha = 8°$
- Coefficient of rolling friction: $\mu = 0.05$
- Nominal speed: $v = 0.65 \text{ m/s}$
- Drive efficiency: $\eta = 0.80$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Resolve weight into components along the incline.**

The total weight acts vertically downward. Resolving it along the direction of motion (parallel to the incline):

$$W_{\parallel} = W \sin\alpha$$

$$W_{\parallel} = 14715 \times \sin(8°) = 14715 \times 0.1392 = 2047.7 \text{ N}$$

**Step 2: Determine the normal reaction on the moving surface.**

The normal reaction equals the component of weight perpendicular to the incline:

$$N = W \cos\alpha = 14715 \times \cos(8°) = 14715 \times 0.9903 = 14571 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3: Calculate the frictional resistance force.**

Using the rolling-friction coefficient $\mu$:

$$F_{friction} = \mu N = 0.05 \times 14571 = 728.6 \text{ N}$$

**Step 4: Compute the total tractive effort required at the drive sprocket.**

The drive must overcome *both* the gravitational pull and the friction, since the system is moving *up* the incline:

$$F_{tractive} = W_{\parallel} + F_{friction}$$

$$F_{tractive} = 2047.7 + 728.6 = 2776.3 \text{ N}$$

Equivalently, combining into a single closed-form expression:

$$\boxed{F_{tractive} = W(\sin\alpha + \mu \cos\alpha) = 14715 \times (0.1392 + 0.05 \times 0.9903) = 2776 \text{ N}}$$

**Step 5: Compute the power delivered at the drive sprocket (output power).**

$$P_{output} = F_{tractive} \times v = 2776.3 \times 0.65 = 1804.6 \text{ W} \approx 1.80 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 6: Compute the motor input power (accounting for drive efficiency).**

Since the gearbox, chain, and bearings all introduce losses, the motor must supply more power than the output:

$$P_{motor} = \dfrac{P_{output}}{\eta} = \dfrac{1804.6}{0.80} = 2255.8 \text{ W} \approx 2.26 \text{ kW}$$

**Step 7: Apply the design safety factor.**

KTU safety standards require a **1.25 to 1.5× safety factor** on motor rating to handle start-up transients and load surges:

$$P_{design} = 1.5 \times P_{motor} = 1.5 \times 2255.8 = 3383.7 \text{ W}$$

**Engineering recommendation:** Select a **3.75 kW (5 HP)** standard motor.

### Final Result
$$\boxed{P_{design} \approx 3.4 \text{ kW (round up to next standard size, typically 3.75 kW)}}$$

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Hydraulic Cylinder Force in a Scissor Man-Lift

### Given Data
- Total vertical load on platform (workers + tools): $W_{total} = 1200 \text{ kg}$ (i.e., $11772 \text{ N}$)
- Arm length: $L = 2.0 \text{ m}$
- Instantaneous arm angle with horizontal: $\theta = 30°$

### Step-by-Step Derivation

**Step 1: Establish the geometry.**

In a scissor mechanism, the two arms cross at the central pivot. Each arm forms angle $\theta$ with the horizontal. The hydraulic cylinder is mounted horizontally at the base, and pushes the *lower ends* of the arms apart.

**Step 2: Free Body Diagram of One Arm.**

Consider one arm of length $L$. The forces acting on it are:
- A vertical force at the *top end* (where it supports the platform): $F_v = W_{total}/2$ (load shared between two arms).
- A horizontal force at the *bottom end* (from the cylinder): $F_h$ (unknown).
- Pin reaction at the *central pivot*: $R_x$, $R_y$ (internal to the assembly, can be ignored by symmetry).

**Step 3: Apply moment equilibrium about the central pivot.**

Taking moments about the central pivot (to eliminate the unknown pin reaction):

$$F_v \cdot \left(\dfrac{L}{2}\cos\theta\right) = F_h \cdot \left(\dfrac{L}{2}\sin\theta\right)$$

Note: The moment arm for the vertical force about the central pivot is the horizontal distance from the pivot to the top end, which is $\frac{L}{2}\cos\theta$. The moment arm for the horizontal force is the vertical distance from the pivot to the bottom end, which is $\frac{L}{2}\sin\theta$.

**Step 4: Simplify the moment equation.**

$$\dfrac{F_v \cos\theta}{1} = F_h \sin\theta$$

$$F_h = F_v \cdot \dfrac{\cos\theta}{\sin\theta} = F_v \cot\theta$$

**Step 5: Substitute $F_v = W_{total}/2$.**

$$F_h = \dfrac{W_{total}}{2} \cdot \cot\theta$$

**Step 6: Compute the total horizontal cylinder force.**

The cylinder pushes the *two* bottom ends of the two arms apart, so its force equals the sum:

$$F_{cyl} = 2 F_h = 2 \cdot \dfrac{W_{total}}{2} \cdot \cot\theta = W_{total} \cot\theta$$

Equivalently:

$$\boxed{F_{cyl} = \dfrac{W_{total}}{\tan\theta}}$$

**Step 7: Plug in numerical values.**

$$F_{cyl} = \dfrac{11772}{\tan(30°)} = \dfrac{11772}{0.5774} = 20391 \text{ N} \approx 20.4 \text{ kN}$$

**Step 8: Compute the required hydraulic pressure.**

If the piston diameter is $d = 80 \text{ mm}$, then:

$$A_{piston} = \dfrac{\pi d^2}{4} = \dfrac{\pi (0.080)^2}{4} = 5.027 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

$$P_h = \dfrac{F_{cyl}}{A_{piston}} = \dfrac{20391}{5.027 \times 10^{-3}} = 4.056 \times 10^6 \text{ Pa} \approx 40.6 \text{ bar}$$

### Final Result
$$\boxed{F_{cyl} \approx 20.4 \text{ kN} \quad ; \quad P_h \approx 40.6 \text{ bar (for } d = 80 \text{ mm piston)}}$$

### Engineering Insight
Notice that as $\theta$ **decreases** (platform near ground), $\tan\theta$ is very small, so $F_{cyl}$ becomes **very large**. This is why scissor lifts have **difficulty lifting off the ground** — the mechanical advantage is poorest at the bottom of the stroke. As the lift rises, $\theta$ approaches 90°, the cylinder force drops, but the platform becomes *less stable laterally*. This trade-off is fundamental to scissor-lift design.

---

## 3.3 Analytical Matrix: Failure Mode Comparison

| Failure Mode | Moving Walk | Man Lift | Root Cause | KTU Prevention Strategy |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Comb-plate shoe entrapment** | ✓ Most common | ✗ | Worn comb teeth, gap > 4 mm | Daily comb inspection |
| **Handrail slip** | ✓ | ✗ | Low handrail speed → skin friction | Synchronization tolerance ± 5% |
| **Tip-over** | ✗ (fixed installation) | ✓ Most common | Off-center load, soft ground | Outrigger pads, load-cell weighing |
| **Step chain breakage** | ✓ | ✗ | Fatigue, lubrication failure | Step-chain monitor sensor |
| **Hydraulic hose burst** | ✗ | ✓ Most common | Abrasion, aging | 2-yr replacement, hose burst valve |
| **Electrical fault / e-stop failure** | ✓ | ✓ | Relay welding | Dual-channel safety relay |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->

# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

> [!NOTE]
> The diagrams below are **Block-Level Functional Architecture** schematics, mapped to KTU Module 4 evaluation outcomes. Mermaid syntax is used; all node IDs are alphanumeric and labels are plain uppercase text to ensure clean rendering.

## 4.1 Moving Walk — Functional Architecture Flow

```mermaid
graph TD
    A[AC POWER SUPPLY] --> B[MOTOR CONTROL PANEL]
    B --> C[GEARED WORM REDUCTION UNIT]
    C --> D[DRIVE SPROCKET UPPER LANDING]
    D --> E[STEP CHAIN ENDLESS LOOP]
    E --> F1[PALLET ASSEMBLY TOP RUN]
    E --> F2[PALLET ASSEMBLY BOTTOM RETURN]
    D --> G[HANDRAIL DRIVE SHAFT]
    G --> H1[HANDRAIL LEFT SIDE]
    G --> H2[HANDRAIL RIGHT SIDE]
    F1 --> I1[COMB PLATE LOWER LANDING]
    F1 --> I2[COMB PLATE UPPER LANDING]
    F1 --> J[TRUSS STRUCTURAL SUPPORT]
    J --> K1[SPEED SENSOR]
    J --> K2[STEP CHAIN MONITOR]
    J --> K3[COMB IMPACT SWITCH]
    J --> K4[EMERGENCY STOP RELAY]
    K1 --> L[SUPERVISORY CONTROL BOARD]
    K2 --> L
    K3 --> L
    K4 --> L
    L --> B
    L --> M[FAULT INDICATION PANEL]
```

## 4.2 Man Lift — Type Classification Matrix

```mermaid
graph TD
    P[MAN LIFT EQUIPMENT]
    P --> Q1[GROUP A VERTICAL MAST]
    P --> Q2[GROUP B BOOM SUPPORTED]
    P --> Q3[SPECIAL TRUCK MOUNTED]
    P --> Q4[SPECIAL PUSH AROUND]
    Q1 --> R1[SCISSOR LIFT SINGLE STAGE]
    Q1 --> R2[SCISSOR LIFT MULTI STAGE]
    Q1 --> R3[VERTICAL MAST PUSH AROUND]
    Q2 --> S1[ARTICULATING BOOM]
    Q2 --> S2[TELESCOPIC BOOM]
    Q2 --> S3[ARTICULATING TELESCOPIC COMBO]
    Q3 --> T1[UTILITY BUCKET TRUCK]
    Q3 --> T2[FIRE AND RESCUE PLATFORM]
    Q4 --> U1[LOW LEVEL INDOOR PLATFORM]
    Q4 --> U2[STOCK PICKER VEHICLE]
    R1 --> V1[INDOOR WAREHOUSE USE]
    R2 --> V1
    S1 --> V2[OUTDOOR CONSTRUCTION]
    S2 --> V2
    S3 --> V2
    T1 --> V3[ELECTRICAL LINE WORK]
    T2 --> V4[EMERGENCY RESPONSE]
```

## 4.3 Safety Interlock System — Block Diagram

```mermaid
graph LR
    W1[OVERSPEED SENSOR] --> X1
    W2[COMB TILT SENSOR] --> X1
    W3[CHAIN BREAK DETECTOR] --> X1
    W4[HANDRAIL SPEED MISMATCH] --> X1
    W5[PLATFORM TILT SENSOR AWP] --> X2
    W6[OUTRIGGER PRESSURE SWITCH] --> X2
    W7[PLATFORM OVERLOAD CELL] --> X2
    W8[EMERGENCY STOP BUTTON] --> X2

    X2[SUPERVISORY RELAY MAN LIFT] --> Y2[BLOCK HYDRAULIC VALVE]
    Y1 --> Z[FAULT LOG AND DISPLAY]
    Y2 --> Z
    Z --> AA[OPERATOR ALERT]
```

## 4.4 Scissor Lift — Force / Geometry Schematic (Block Representation)

```mermaid
graph TD
    A1[PLATFORM WEIGHT W] --> A2[TOTAL VERTICAL LOAD WT]
    A3[WORKER AND TOOLS WL] --> A2
    A2 --> B1[ARM VERTICAL COMPONENT FV]
    A2 --> B2[ARM HORIZONTAL COMPONENT FH]
    B1 --> C1[ANGLE THETA]
    B2 --> C1

    D1 --> E1[HYDRAULIC PRESSURE PH]

```
