<!-- SECTION_5_START -->

# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper]**
**"List the principal safety devices that must be fitted on a modern moving walk. Mention the standard reference."**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks — Approx. 80–100 words)
A modern moving walk must be fitted with the following mandatory safety devices as per **EN 115-1 / IS 14665**:

1. **Comb-impact switch** — detects foreign object or shoe entrapment at the comb plate; immediately stops the drive.
2. **Step-chain monitor** — detects chain stretch or breakage via a sensor flag.
3. **Handrail-speed sensor** — ensures handrail velocity matches pallet velocity within **± 5%**; mismatch triggers stop.
4. **Overspeed governor** — trips if speed exceeds **1.2 × nominal** speed.
5. **Emergency stop buttons** at both landings, dual-channel, with normally-closed contactors.
6. **Drive motor overload relay** and **phase-failure relay**.

**Marking distribution:** ½ mark per device × 6 = 3 marks. Examiner's discretion on partial credit.

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**
**"Differentiate between a Scissor Lift and a Boom-Supported Man Lift with respect to working envelope and typical application."**

#### Model Answer (3 Marks — Tabular)
| Feature | Scissor Lift | Boom-Supported AWP |
|---|---|---|
| **Working Envelope** | **Vertical only** — straight up/down, limited horizontal reach | **3-D envelope** — vertical, horizontal, and over-obstacle reach |
| **Mechanism** | Crossed-arm scissor, hydraulic cylinder at base | Articulating or telescopic boom on a rotating turret |
| **Typical Use** | Indoor warehouse, installation on flat floors | Outdoor construction, utility, tree-care |
| **Stability Risk** | Tip-over on uneven floor | Tip-over due to over-reach or wind |
| **Lateral Reach** | Essentially **zero** | **Up to 20+ metres** |

**Marking:** 1 mark per major comparison row, plus ½ mark for citing ANSI A92.20 standard. Examiner expects mention of the *working envelope* concept.

---

## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Internal Choice)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Pattern:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks, typically split as **(a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks**. The student must answer **EITHER** Question A **OR** Question B (full 14 marks, no mixing).

---

### Question A (14 Marks)

**[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]**

**(a)** A horizontal moving walk is **60 m long** and must transport passengers across an airport concourse. The total moving mass (pallets + passengers + handrails) is estimated at **2000 kg**. The coefficient of rolling friction is **0.04**, and the drive efficiency is **0.78**. The moving walk is **horizontal** ($\alpha = 0°$). Determine:
- (i) The tractive force required at the drive sprocket. **(2 marks)**
- (ii) The output power at the rated speed of **0.65 m/s**. **(2 marks)**
- (iii) The motor input power. **(2 marks)**
- (iv) The recommended motor rating with a **1.5× safety factor**. **(1 mark)**

**(b)** With the aid of a neat block diagram, describe the **functional architecture of a moving walk** and explain **three critical safety interlock devices** that prevent passenger injury. Why is the comb-plate design considered the most safety-critical component? **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

### Part (a) — Numerical Solution

**Given:**
- Mass $m = 2000 \text{ kg}$, so weight $W = 2000 \times 9.81 = 19620 \text{ N}$
- Length = 60 m (not used for force, but relevant for context)
- $\mu = 0.04$, $\eta = 0.78$, $v = 0.65 \text{ m/s}$, $\alpha = 0°$

**Step (i): Tractive Force**

For a horizontal moving walk, $\sin 0° = 0$ and $\cos 0° = 1$:

$$F = W(\sin\alpha + \mu \cos\alpha) = 19620 \times (0 + 0.04 \times 1)$$

$$\boxed{F = 784.8 \text{ N} \quad (≈ 785 \text{ N})}$$

**Step (ii): Output Power**

$$P_{out} = F \cdot v = 784.8 \times 0.65 = 510.1 \text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{P_{out} \approx 510 \text{ W} \quad (≈ 0.51 \text{ kW})}$$

**Step (iii): Motor Input Power**

$$P_{motor} = \dfrac{P_{out}}{\eta} = \dfrac{510.1}{0.78} = 654.0 \text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{P_{motor} \approx 654 \text{ W} \quad (≈ 0.65 \text{ kW})}$$

**Step (iv): Recommended Motor with Safety Factor**

$$P_{design} = 1.5 \times 654.0 = 981.0 \text{ W}$$

$$\boxed{P_{design} \approx 1.0 \text{ kW (round up to next standard: 1.1 or 1.5 kW)}}$$

**Mark split:** 1.5 marks per numerical answer with units; 0.5 mark reserved for the final sentence. Total = 7 marks.

---

### Part (b) — Descriptive Solution

**Block Diagram of a Moving Walk:** (Refer to **Section 4.1** diagram in the notes — 2 marks for diagram).

**Three Critical Safety Interlock Devices:**

1. **Comb-Impact Switch (Comb-Plate Sensor)** — *(2 marks)*
   - A spring-loaded sensor behind each comb tooth at both landings.
   - On any foreign-object impact (e.g., shoe sole, child's finger, luggage wheel), the sensor deflects, breaks a contact, and trips the safety relay, applying the brake within milliseconds.
   - Reset is manual-only after physical inspection.

2. **Step-Chain Monitor (Broken Chain Detector)** — *(1.5 marks)*
   - A flag or vane attached to the step chain passes through a proximity sensor at each end.
   - If the chain breaks or stretches beyond tolerance, the flag does not pass → relay opens → drive stops and brake applies. Prevents runaway pallets.

3. **Handrail-Speed-Synchronization Sensor** — *(1.5 marks)*
   - A tachogenerator measures the handrail speed; it is compared to the pallet speed in a PLC.
   - If mismatch exceeds ±5%, the system stops. This prevents skin-friction burns from a slow handrail or grab-injury from a fast one.

**Why is the comb plate the most safety-critical component?** *(1.5 marks)*

The comb plate is the **transition zone** where pallets (moving) mesh with stationary comb teeth (at landings). It is the only location where a passenger's foot is **half-supported by moving pallets and half by stationary floor**, and where a small gap (≤ 4 mm) is unavoidable. Misalignment, broken comb teeth, or wear can cause **shoe entrapment**, **finger injury**, or **fingertip amputation** — making it the highest-frequency injury site on a moving walk. Hence, **daily comb inspection** is mandated by EN 115-1.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — *Alternative Choice*

**(a)** A **scissor-lift man lift** is required to elevate **two workers (each 80 kg) plus 60 kg of tools** to a working height of **6 m**. Each scissor arm has length $L = 2.5 \text{ m}$, and the lift has **2 stages** (two scissor pairs in series). When fully raised, the arm angle with the horizontal is **θ = 70°**. Determine:
- (i) The total vertical load on the platform. **(1.5 marks)**
- (ii) The platform height at the given angle (per stage). **(1.5 marks)**
- (iii) The total raised height (both stages). **(1.5 marks)**
- (iv) The required hydraulic cylinder force per stage. **(2.5 marks)**

**(b)** List and briefly explain **five operational safety rules** that must be enforced when using a scissor-lift man lift on a construction site, citing the **OSHA 1910.67 / ANSI A92.20** requirements. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution

### Part (a) — Numerical

**Given:**
- 2 workers × 80 kg + 60 kg tools = 220 kg → $W = 220 \times 9.81 = 2158 \text{ N}$
- $L = 2.5 \text{ m}$, $\theta = 70°$, 2 stages

**Step (i): Total Vertical Load**

$$W_{total} = (2 \times 80 + 60) \times 9.81 = 220 \times 9.81$$

$$\boxed{W_{total} = 2158 \text{ N}}$$

**Step (ii): Platform Height per Stage**

$$h = L \sin\theta = 2.5 \times \sin(70°) = 2.5 \times 0.9397$$

$$\boxed{h = 2.349 \text{ m per stage}}$$

**Step (iii): Total Raised Height (2 Stages)**

$$H = 2 \times h = 2 \times 2.349$$

$$\boxed{H = 4.70 \text{ m}}$$

> **KTU Insight:** This 4.70 m is *less* than the required 6 m working height. The student should conclude that either (a) the lift geometry is insufficient, (b) $\theta$ must be increased (arms must become more vertical), or (c) a third stage is needed. Mentioning this engineering judgement earns a **bonus ½ mark**.

**Step (iv): Hydraulic Cylinder Force per Stage**

Per stage, the supported load is $W_{total}/2$ (two scissor pairs share the platform). The cylinder force per stage is:

$$F_{cyl,per stage} = \dfrac{W_{total}/2}{\tan\theta} = \dfrac{2158/2}{\tan(70°)} = \dfrac{1079}{2.747} = 392.7 \text{ N}$$

$$\boxed{F_{cyl,per stage} \approx 393 \text{ N (per stage)}}$$

> **Note:** Total cylinder force across both stages = 2 × 393 = 786 N if cylinders are independent. This shows the *mechanical advantage* of the scissor mechanism at high angles: a 2158 N load is lifted with only ~786 N of cylinder force.

**Mark split:** Sub-parts (i)–(iii) carry 1.5 marks each; (iv) carries 2.5 marks. Total = 7 marks.

---

### Part (b) — Five Operational Safety Rules (7 Marks)

1. **Pre-Use Inspection (Daily)** — *1.5 marks* — Per OSHA 1910.67(c)(2)(i), a *visual and functional inspection* must be performed before each shift, including tires, hydraulic hoses, guardrails, controls, and emergency lowering. The operator must complete a written checklist.

2. **Outrigger / Wheel Chock Deployment** — *1.5 marks* — On uneven surfaces, **outriggers or stabilizers must be deployed on firm, level pads** before elevation. The lift must not be raised on soft asphalt, mud, or sand without load-distribution pads. ANSI A92.20 requires a *level indicator* on the platform.

3. **No Over-Reach Beyond Platform Edge** — *1.5 marks* — Workers must keep their body *within the platform guardrails*. Leaning out multiplies the tipping moment. OSHA 1910.67(c)(2)(iii) prohibits using the guardrails as ladders or for lifting external loads.

4. **Wind and Weather Restrictions** — *1 mark* — Outdoor lifts must not be operated in winds exceeding **12.5 m/s (45 km/h)** or during thunderstorms/lightning. Per ANSI A92.24, a *wind-speed anemometer* must be available on-site.

5. **Capacity Plate and Load Monitoring** — *1.5 marks* — Every AWP must display a clearly legible **capacity plate** stating the maximum combined weight of personnel, tools, and materials. Overloading is a leading cause of tip-overs. Per ANSI A92.20, *load-sensing interlocks* are required on powered platforms exceeding **1000 lb (≈ 454 kg)** rated capacity.

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls**
> 1. **Forgetting the safety factor (1.25–1.5×)** on motor rating. A direct $P = Fv/\eta$ answer without the safety factor loses **1 mark**.
> 2. **Wrong units in the cylinder-pressure calculation.** Mixing bar, Pa, psi, or kg/cm² confuses the examiner. Always state the unit clearly.
> 3. **Not citing the standard.** KTU 2024 pattern requires mention of *EN 115-1*, *ANSI A92.20*, or *OSHA 1910*. A descriptive answer without a standard reference loses **1–2 marks**.
> 4. **Confusing "moving walk" with "escalator."** A common answer-eraser mistake. Moving walks are *primarily horizontal*; escalators are *inclined* (30°/35°).
> 5. **Skipping the Free-Body Diagram** in scissor-lift derivations. KTU award **0.5 mark** for a correct FBD in mechanical-engineering papers.
> 6. **Forgetting to include the weight of the platform itself** in addition to personnel+tools. This is a classic ½-mark deduction.
> 7. **Incorrect angle units in $\tan\theta$ calculation.** Use *degrees* in the calculator but state the calculation in radians for $\sin/\cos$ if the problem is rigorous.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **Last-Minute Revision Checklist (5-Minute Scan Before Exam)**

### ⭐ Core Concepts
- ☐ Moving walks = *people-conveyors*, mostly horizontal, max **12°** inclination, speed **0.5–0.8 m/s**.
- ☐ Man lifts = *Mobile Elevating Work Platforms (MEWPs)*, classified per **ANSI A92.20** into Group A (vertical/scissor) and Group B (boom).
- ☐ The **comb plate** is the single most safety-critical zone in a moving walk; the **outrigger/level surface** is the most critical for a man lift.
- ☐ Both systems are *personnel-carrying*; safety factor is **1.25–1.5×** for design margin.

### ⭐ Formula Mastery
- ☐ $F = W(\sin\alpha + \mu \cos\alpha)$ — tractive force for inclined moving walk
- ☐ $P = Fv / \eta$ — drive power (always include $\eta$)
- ☐ $h = L \sin\theta$ — scissor-lift height (single stage)
- ☐ $F_{cyl} = W_{total} / \tan\theta$ — scissor cylinder force
- ☐ $T_1/T_2 = e^{\mu\theta}$ — capstan / belt-wrap tension ratio (Eytelwein)

### ⭐ Standards to Quote
- ☐ **EN 115-1** (Moving walks — Europe/global)
- ☐ **IS 14665** (Indian standard for escalators & moving walks)
- ☐ **ANSI A92.20** (MEWP design)
- ☐ **OSHA 1910.67** (Vehicle-mounted man lifts)
- ☐ **OSHA 1910.68** (Dedicated man lifts)

### ⭐ Engineering Judgement
- ☐ Always check: *Can my calculated lift height meet the required working height?* If not, add stages or increase $\theta$.
- ☐ Always check: *Is the cylinder force within the rated capacity of the chosen hydraulic cylinder?* Include pressure relief.
- ☐ Always check: *Does the moving walk inclination exceed 12°?* If so, it must be classified as an escalator, not a moving walk.

### ⭐ Quick Numerical Shortcuts
- ☐ $\sin 8° \approx 0.139$ ; $\sin 12° \approx 0.208$ ; $\sin 30° = 0.500$ ; $\sin 70° \approx 0.940$
- ☐ $\tan 30° \approx 0.577$ ; $\tan 45° = 1.000$ ; $\tan 60° \approx 1.732$ ; $\tan 70° \approx 2.747$

> [!NOTE]
> **End of Module 4 Notes — Moving Walks & Man Lifts.** For cross-reference, revisit Module 3 (Conveyors & Cranes) and Module 5 (Industrial Trucks & AGVs) for the broader material-handling safety framework.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT522 - SAFETY IN MATERIAL HANDLING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Construction

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Introduction to Construction Safety in Material Handling

**Construction** in the context of industrial safety refers to the systematic discipline of planning, organizing, monitoring, and controlling activities at construction sites to prevent accidents, injuries, and fatalities during material handling operations. Construction is one of the most hazardous occupational sectors globally, with material handling being the single largest contributor to workplace injuries, accounting for approximately **25-30%** of all construction-related accidents.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Formal KTU Definition:** Construction safety in material handling encompasses the application of engineering principles, safety regulations, and best practices to ensure the safe storage, movement, lifting, and placement of building materials, tools, and equipment throughout the construction lifecycle — from site preparation to project completion.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Human Body Analogy

Imagine the human skeletal system: bones (materials), joints (connectors/fasteners), muscles (lifting equipment), and the nervous system (safety protocols). If any single element fails — a brittle bone, a weak joint, an overstrained muscle, or a delayed nerve signal — the entire system collapses, causing injury. Similarly, a construction site operates as an **interconnected biomechanical system** where materials, equipment, workers, and protocols must work in harmony. Just as we wear seatbelts (PPE) and follow traffic rules (safety procedures), construction workers must follow prescribed safety protocols for every lift, every scaffold, and every load.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Statistic (KTU Reference):** According to standard occupational safety data, the "**Fatal Four**" in construction are falls, struck-by objects, electrocutions, and caught-in/between incidents. Material handling directly contributes to **3 out of 4** of these categories.

## 1.3 Scope of Construction Material Handling

Construction material handling operations include:

- **Lifting Operations** — using cranes, hoists, and derricks
- **Horizontal Transport** — using forklifts, conveyors, and hand trucks
- **Vertical Transport** — using elevators, hoists, and cranes
- **Storage Operations** — stacking, racking, and warehousing on-site
- **Manual Handling** — lifting, carrying, pushing, and pulling by workers
- **Erection & Dismantling** — structural assembly using mechanical aids

### 1.4 Primary Hazards in Construction Material Handling

| Hazard Category | Description | Common Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Mechanical** | Failure of lifting gear | Crane hook snapping under overload |
| **Structural** | Collapse of temporary structures | Scaffolding failure during concrete pouring |
| **Human Factor** | Worker error or fatigue | Operator misjudging load weight |
| **Environmental** | Weather and site conditions | High winds affecting crane operation |
| **Electrical** | Contact with power lines | Boom crane contacting overhead lines |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Highlight:** The **Hierarchy of Controls** — Elimination → Substitution → Engineering Controls → Administrative Controls → PPE — forms the foundational decision framework for all construction material handling safety interventions.

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework of Construction Safety

The theoretical foundation of construction safety in material handling rests on three pillars:

### Pillar 1: **Load-Path Analysis**
Every material handling operation has a defined **load path** — the trajectory along which forces travel from the load to the ground. A safe operation maintains an **uninterrupted and rated load path**.

$$
\text{Load Path} = \{ \text{Load} \rightarrow \text{Sling} \rightarrow \text{Hook} \rightarrow \text{Block} \rightarrow \text{Boom} \rightarrow \text{Base} \rightarrow \text{Ground} \}
$$

**Every component in this path must have a Working Load Limit (WLL) ≥ the maximum anticipated load**, multiplied by a Safety Factor (SF).

### Pillar 2: **Center of Gravity (CG) Principles**
For any lifted object, the **center of gravity** is the point where the entire weight of the object can be considered to act. Safe lifting requires the **CG to lie directly below the lifting hook**.

**Conditions for safe vertical lift:**

$$
\sum F_z = 0 \quad \text{(Equilibrium in vertical direction)}
$$

$$
\sum M_x = 0 \quad \text{(No rotation about X-axis — CG aligned)}
$$

$$
\sum M_y = 0 \quad \text{(No rotation about Y-axis — CG aligned)}
$$

### Pillar 3: **Stability & Tipping Analysis**
For mobile cranes and forklifts, the **tipping condition** is governed by the principle of moments about the tipping edge.

## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following table contains all critical formulas for construction material handling safety problems. All symbols are explained beneath the table.

| # | Formula / Equation | Symbol Meaning | Typical Units | Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\text{SF} = \dfrac{\text{Ultimate Load}}{\text{Working Load}}$ | SF = Safety Factor | dimensionless | Rope, sling, hook rating |
| 2 | $W = m \cdot g$ | W = Weight, m = mass, g = 9.81 m/s² | N (Newtons) | Load weight calculation |
| 3 | $T = \dfrac{W}{n \cdot \sin\theta}$ | T = Sling tension, n = number of legs, θ = sling angle with horizontal | N | Multi-leg sling design |
| 4 | $F_{\text{tag}} = W \cdot (\sec\theta - 1)$ | F_tag = Tag-line force, θ = swing angle | N | Pendulum swing control |
| 5 | $M_{\text{tip}} = W \cdot d$ | M_tip = Tipping moment, d = horizontal distance from tipping edge | N·m | Crane stability |
| 6 | $\text{Capacity}_{75\%} = 0.75 \cdot \text{Capacity}_{100\%}$ | Outrigger capacity reduction | kg or tonnes | Outrigger pad sizing |
| 7 | $P_{\text{wind}} = 0.5 \cdot \rho \cdot v^2 \cdot C_d \cdot A$ | Wind pressure on load, ρ = air density, v = wind speed, C_d = drag coefficient, A = area | Pa (N/m²) | Wind load on suspended load |
| 8 | $\text{SWL}_{\text{derated}} = \text{SWL} \cdot f_{\text{angle}} \cdot f_{\text{reeving}}$ | Derated Safe Working Load | kg or tonnes | Crane chart corrections |
| 9 | $\sigma = \dfrac{F}{A} \le \sigma_{\text{yield}} / n$ | Allowable stress design | Pa (N/m²) | Lifting beam design |
| 10 | $\text{MHR} = 220 - \text{Age} - \text{RHR}$ | Maximum Heart Rate for fitness | bpm | Operator fitness assessment |

### 2.3 Detailed Explanation of Critical Formulas

#### **Formula 3: Sling Tension with Sling Angle**
This is arguably the **most-tested formula** in KTU examinations. The vertical component of each sling leg must support the entire load. When the sling angle (measured from horizontal) decreases, the tension **increases dramatically**.

**Engineering Insight:** As sling angle approaches 0° (horizontal), sling tension approaches **infinity** — a sling can never lift a load when horizontal. The **safe minimum sling angle is 60° from horizontal** (i.e., 30° from vertical).

#### **Formula 4: Tag-line Force**
Tag-lines are ropes used by workers to control swinging of suspended loads. The horizontal force required to control swing is derived from pendulum dynamics.

#### **Formula 5: Tipping Moment**
For mobile cranes, the moment about the tipping edge (typically the outrigger) must always be **resisted by the counterweight moment**:

$$
M_{\text{counterweight}} \ge M_{\text{load}} + M_{\text{wind}} + M_{\text{inertia}}
$$

## 2.4 Real-World Engineering Utility

These formulas find direct application in:

- **Tower crane selection** for high-rise construction sites in Kerala
- **Scaffolding design** for the NEP 2020-aligned KTU campus construction projects
- **Rigging studies** for pre-cast concrete element placement
- **Forklift route planning** in warehouse construction
- **Hoist selection** for multi-story material transport

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Sling Tension as a Function of Sling Angle

### Problem Setup
A load of weight **W** is suspended by **two sling legs** symmetrically placed, each making an angle **θ** with the horizontal. Determine the tension **T** in each sling leg.

### Step 1: Free Body Diagram Construction
The load W is in static equilibrium. Each sling leg exerts a force **T** along its length. The vertical components of these forces support the load, while horizontal components cancel each other (by symmetry).

### Step 2: Resolve Each Sling Force into Components

Vertical component of each leg:
$$
T_v = T \cdot \sin\theta
$$

Horizontal component of each leg:
$$
T_h = T \cdot \cos\theta
$$

### Step 3: Apply Vertical Equilibrium Condition

The sum of vertical components must equal the load weight:

$$
2 \cdot T \cdot \sin\theta = W
$$

### Step 4: Solve for T

$$
T = \dfrac{W}{2 \cdot \sin\theta}
$$

### Step 5: Generalize to n Legs

For **n** sling legs symmetrically arranged:

$$
T = \dfrac{W}{n \cdot \sin\theta}
$$

### Step 6: Critical Angle Analysis

To understand why sling angle matters, compute T/W ratio for different angles:

| Sling Angle θ (from horizontal) | sin θ | T/W (n=2) | Interpretation |
|---|---|---|---|
| 90° (vertical) | 1.000 | 0.500 W | **Ideal — minimum tension** |
| 60° | 0.866 | 0.577 W | **Recommended minimum (KTU)** |
| 45° | 0.707 | 0.707 W | Acceptable for light loads |
| 30° | 0.500 | 1.000 W | **Dangerous — tension equals full weight** |
| 15° | 0.259 | 1.932 W | **Extremely dangerous** |
| 5° | 0.087 | 5.739 W | **Near-horizontal — failure imminent** |

> [!NOTE]
> This derivation is the **foundation** for all rigging calculations in KTU examinations. Memorize the formula $T = \dfrac{W}{n \sin\theta}$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Mobile Crane Tipping Stability

### Problem Setup
A mobile crane with operating weight **W_c** has a counterweight at distance **d_c** from the tipping axis. The lifted load **W_L** is at horizontal distance **d_L** from the tipping axis. Determine the condition for stability.

### Step 1: Define the Tipping Axis
The tipping axis is the edge of the outrigger pad on the side opposite to the load.

### Step 2: Identify All Overturning Moments
The load **W_L** tries to tip the crane about the tipping axis:

$$
M_{\text{overturn}} = W_L \cdot d_L
$$

### Step 3: Identify All Resisting Moments
The crane's own weight provides the restoring moment:

$$
M_{\text{restore}} = W_c \cdot d_c
$$

### Step 4: Apply Stability Condition

For **static stability** (no wind, no dynamic effects):

$$
M_{\text{restore}} \ge M_{\text{overturn}}
$$

$$
W_c \cdot d_c \ge W_L \cdot d_L
$$

### Step 5: Introduce Stability Factor

The **Stability Factor (SF_stability)** is defined as:

$$
SF_{\text{stability}} = \dfrac{M_{\text{restore}}}{M_{\text{overturn}}} = \dfrac{W_c \cdot d_c}{W_L \cdot d_L}
$$

### Step 6: Apply KTU / Industry Standard

Per standard regulations (IS 4573, OSHA 1926.1402), the minimum stability factor is:

$$
SF_{\text{stability,min}} = 1.75 \quad \text{(without wind)}
$$

$$
SF_{\text{stability,min}} = 1.25 \quad \text{(with wind considered)}
$$

### Step 7: Solve Numerical Example

**Given:** A crane with W_c = 30 tonnes, d_c = 2.5 m, lifting W_L = 8 tonnes at d_L = 12 m. Check stability.

**Step 7.1: Calculate restoring moment**

$$
M_{\text{restore}} = 30 \times 1000 \times 9.81 \times 2.5 = 735{,}750 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Step 7.2: Calculate overturning moment**

$$
M_{\text{overturn}} = 8 \times 1000 \times 9.81 \times 12 = 941{,}760 \text{ N·m}
$$

**Step 7.3: Calculate stability factor**

$$
SF_{\text{stability}} = \dfrac{735{,}750}{941{,}760} = 0.781
$$

**Step 7.4: Verdict**

$$
0.781 < 1.75
$$

**The crane will tip over.** This load configuration is unsafe.

### Step 8: Determine Maximum Safe Load

Rearranging the stability equation for maximum W_L:

$$
W_{L,\text{max}} = \dfrac{W_c \cdot d_c}{d_L \cdot SF_{\text{stability,min}}}
$$

$$
W_{L,\text{max}} = \dfrac{30 \times 2.5}{12 \times 1.75} = 3.57 \text{ tonnes}
$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Student Mistake:** Forgetting to convert tonnes to kg, or omitting **g = 9.81 m/s²** when working in Newtons. The moment ratio is independent of g, but absolute moment values require consistent units.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Scaffolding Load Capacity

### Step 1: Define Standard Scaffold Classification
Per IS 4014 and BS 5973, scaffolds are classified into 6 duty classes:

| Class | Duty | Distributed Load (kN/m²) |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Inspection, very light | 0.75 |
| 2 | Light cleaning, painting | 1.50 |
| 3 | General construction, light material storage | 3.00 |
| 4 | Heavy material storage, brick handling | 4.50 |
| 5 | Masonry, concrete work | 6.00 |
| 6 | Heavy masonry, stone | 7.50 |

### Step 2: Calculate Total Load on Scaffold Platform

For a scaffold platform of area **A** and self-weight **W_s**:

$$
W_{\text{total}} = q \cdot A + W_s
$$

where **q** is the distributed load for the duty class.

### Step 3: Calculate Load per Standard (Vertical Member)

For **N_s** standards (vertical posts):

$$
P_{\text{per standard}} = \dfrac{W_{\text{total}}}{N_s}
$$

### Step 4: Check Buckling Capacity

Each standard must satisfy:

$$
P_{\text{per standard}} \le P_{\text{buckling}} = \dfrac{\pi^2 \cdot E \cdot I}{(K \cdot L)^2}
$$

where:
- **E** = Young's modulus of standard material (e.g., steel E = 200 GPa)
- **I** = Moment of inertia of cross-section
- **K** = Effective length factor (typically 1.0 for pinned ends, 0.7 for fixed)
- **L** = Unsupported length of standard

> [!NOTE]
> Scaffold safety depends on **3-1-1 rule**: 3 times the maximum intended load applied test, 1 design, 1 competent person inspection.

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Common KTU Problem Types

| Problem Type | Key Formula | Common Trap |
|---|---|---|
| Sling angle optimization | $T = \dfrac{W}{n \sin\theta}$ | Using angle from vertical instead of horizontal |
| Crane chart correction | $\text{SWL} \times f_{\text{angle}} \times f_{\text{reeving}}$ | Forgetting to apply both factors |
| Wind load on load | $P = 0.5 \rho v^2 C_d A$ | Using wrong drag coefficient |
| Forklift stability | Triangle of forces (load, weight, reaction) | Reversing tip axis direction |
| Manual lifting NIOSH | $\text{RWL} = LC \cdot HM \cdot VM \cdot DM \cdot AM \cdot FM \cdot CM$ | Confusing RWL with Recommended Weight Limit |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 4.5 Sling Configuration Schematics

### Table: Standard Sling Hitch Types and Their Applications

| Sling Hitch | Configuration | Capacity Factor | Best Application |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Vertical Hitch** | Single straight sling | 1.0 × SWL | Straight vertical lift |
| **Basket Hitch** | Sling passes under load | 2.0 × SWL (theoretical) | Rigid loads with smooth surfaces |
| **Choker Hitch** | Sling wraps around load | 0.75 × SWL | Bundled or cylindrical loads |
| **Bridle Hitch (2-leg)** | Two legs from one hook | 1.4 × SWL at 60° | Standard construction loads |
| **Bridle Hitch (4-leg)** | Four legs from one hook | 2.1 × SWL at 60° | Large structural elements |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 PART A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]**
Define the term **Working Load Limit (WLL)** in the context of construction material handling. How is it related to the Safety Factor?

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The **Working Load Limit (WLL)** is the maximum load that a piece of lifting equipment (sling, hook, shackle, crane) is rated to lift under normal service conditions. It is also called the Safe Working Load (SWL).

The relationship between WLL and Safety Factor is:

$$
\text{Safety Factor (SF)} = \dfrac{\text{Ultimate Breaking Load}}{\text{Working Load Limit (WLL)}}
$$

For example, a wire rope sling with a breaking strength of 10,000 kg and SF = 5 would have a WLL of 2,000 kg. Common safety factors in construction are: Wire rope slings = 5, Synthetic slings = 7, Chains = 4.

**(3 marks — 1 mark for definition, 1 mark for formula, 1 mark for example)**

### Question 2
**[KTU University Exam - Jun 2024]**
List any **six (6) hazards** associated with crane operations during construction material handling.

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

1. **Overloading** — exceeding the rated capacity of the crane
2. **Tipping/Overturning** — due to improper outrigger setup or soft ground
3. **Swinging loads** — pendulum effect causing struck-by injuries
4. **Two-blocking** — hook block contacting boom sheave
5. **Contact with power lines** — electrocution hazard
6. **Falling objects** — due to sling failure or improper rigging
7. **Operator error** — due to fatigue or inadequate training

**(3 marks — 0.5 marks each, any six points)**

## 5.2 PART B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023 | Module 4 Construction]**

**(a)** A steel beam weighing **2,500 kg** is to be lifted using a **4-leg wire rope sling**. Each sling leg makes an angle of **45°** with the horizontal. Determine:
   (i) The tension in each sling leg
   (ii) The minimum required breaking strength of each sling leg, assuming a safety factor of 5
   (iii) Comment on whether this sling configuration is safe for a typical construction site.

**(7 marks)**

**(b)** With the help of a neat diagram, explain the **Hierarchy of Controls** as applied to construction material handling safety. Provide **two construction-specific examples** for each level of the hierarchy.

**(7 marks)**

---

### SOLUTION TO QUESTION A

#### Part (a) — Sling Tension Calculation (7 marks)

**Given Data:**
- Load weight: W = 2,500 kg
- Number of sling legs: n = 4
- Sling angle with horizontal: θ = 45°
- Safety factor: SF = 5

**Step 1: Convert weight to Newtons**

$$
W = 2{,}500 \times 9.81 = 24{,}525 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate sin θ**

$$
\sin 45° = 0.7071
$$

**Step 3: Apply the sling tension formula**

$$
T = \dfrac{W}{n \cdot \sin\theta} = \dfrac{24{,}525}{4 \times 0.7071}
$$

$$
T = \dfrac{24{,}525}{2.8284} = 8{,}670.7 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 4: Calculate minimum breaking strength per leg**

$$
\text{Breaking Strength} = T \times SF = 8{,}670.7 \times 5 = 43{,}353.5 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 5: Express in convenient units**

$$
\text{Breaking Strength} = \dfrac{43{,}353.5}{9.81} = 4{,}418.6 \text{ kg} \approx 4.42 \text{ tonnes}
$$

**Step 6: Safety Comment**

The configuration is **marginally acceptable** because:
- 45° is below the recommended 60° minimum angle
- This results in higher tension (8,670 N vs 7,073 N at 60°)
- The sling legs carry 22.5% more load than at 60°

**Recommendation:** Use 60° sling angle for better safety margin.

**Mark Distribution:**
- 1 mark — Free body diagram and formula identification
- 2 marks — Correct numerical calculation
- 2 marks — Breaking strength calculation
- 1 mark — Unit conversions
- 1 mark — Safety comment

---

#### Part (b) — Hierarchy of Controls (7 marks)

The **Hierarchy of Controls** is a systematic approach to hazard control, ordered from most effective to least effective:

| Level | Control Type | Description | Construction Example 1 | Construction Example 2 |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Elimination** | Physically remove the hazard | Prefabricate components off-site to eliminate on-site lifting | Use pumped concrete instead of bucket lifting |
| 2 | **Substitution** | Replace with less hazardous option | Use lighter aluminum scaffolding instead of steel | Replace gasoline-powered equipment with electric |
| 3 | **Engineering Controls** | Design solutions to isolate workers | Install guardrails on all elevated platforms | Use automated conveyor systems for material transport |
| 4 | **Administrative Controls** | Change work procedures | Implement Permit-to-Work system for all lifts | Conduct daily toolbox talks and safety briefings |
| 5 | **PPE** | Personal protective equipment | Hard hats, safety harnesses, steel-toe boots | High-visibility vests and safety goggles |

**Key Points to Remember:**

- **Elimination is most effective** because it removes the hazard entirely
- **PPE is the last resort** — it protects the worker but does not eliminate the hazard
- **Multiple controls** are often applied simultaneously for layered protection
- The hierarchy should be applied in **top-down order** during risk assessment

**Mark Distribution:**
- 1 mark — Hierarchy pyramid diagram
- 3 marks — Description of each level (5 levels)
- 2 marks — Construction-specific examples
- 1 mark — Key principles and layered protection

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — ALTERNATIVE CHOICE
**[KTU University Exam - Jun 2024 | Module 4 Construction]**

**(a)** A mobile crane has the following specifications:
   - Total crane weight: **35 tonnes**
   - Counterweight center distance from tipping axis: **2.8 m**
   - Maximum load: **10 tonnes at 10 m radius**
   
   Determine:
   (i) The stability factor of the crane
   (ii) Whether the lift is safe as per IS standards (minimum SF = 1.75)
   (iii) The maximum safe load at 10 m radius

**(7 marks)**

**(b)** Explain the **safe sling angle** concept in rigging operations. What happens when the sling angle is reduced below 60° from horizontal? Discuss the implications for construction material handling safety.

**(7 marks)**

---

### SOLUTION TO QUESTION B

#### Part (a) — Crane Stability Calculation (7 marks)

**Given Data:**
- Crane operating weight: W_c = 35 tonnes = 35,000 kg
- Counterweight moment arm: d_c = 2.8 m
- Load weight: W_L = 10 tonnes = 10,000 kg
- Load radius: d_L = 10 m
- Minimum required SF = 1.75

**Step 1: Calculate restoring moment (in kg·m)**

$$
M_{\text{restore}} = W_c \times d_c = 35{,}000 \times 2.8 = 98{,}000 \text{ kg·m}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate overturning moment (in kg·m)**

$$
M_{\text{overturn}} = W_L \times d_L = 10{,}000 \times 10 = 100{,}000 \text{ kg·m}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate stability factor**

$$
SF_{\text{stability}} = \dfrac{M_{\text{restore}}}{M_{\text{overturn}}} = \dfrac{98{,}000}{100{,}000} = 0.98
$$

**Step 4: Verdict**

$$
SF_{\text{stability}} = 0.98 < 1.75
$$

**The lift is UNSAFE.** The crane will tip over. The load must be reduced.

**Step 5: Calculate maximum safe load at 10 m radius**

$$
W_{L,\text{max}} = \dfrac{W_c \times d_c}{d_L \times SF_{\text{min}}}
$$

$$
W_{L,\text{max}} = \dfrac{35{,}000 \times 2.8}{10 \times 1.75} = \dfrac{98{,}000}{17.5} = 5{,}600 \text{ kg} = 5.6 \text{ tonnes}
$$

**Step 6: Alternative — Reduce radius for full 10-tonne load**

$$
d_{L,\text{max}} = \dfrac{W_c \times d_c}{W_L \times SF_{\text{min}}} = \dfrac{98{,}000}{10{,}000 \times 1.75} = 5.6 \text{ m}
$$

The crane can lift 10 tonnes only at a maximum radius of 5.6 m.

**Mark Distribution:**
- 1 mark — Given data and formula identification
- 2 marks — Moment calculations
- 1 mark — Stability factor calculation
- 1 mark — Verdict with standard comparison
- 2 marks — Maximum safe load calculation

---

#### Part (b) — Safe Sling Angle Concept (7 marks)

**Definition:**
The **safe sling angle** is the angle that a sling leg makes with the horizontal plane, below which the tension in the sling becomes dangerously high relative to the load weight. Industry and KTU-recommended minimum sling angle is **60° from horizontal** (or 30° from vertical).

**Mathematical Basis:**

$$
T = \dfrac{W}{n \cdot \sin\theta}
$$

As θ decreases, sin θ decreases, and T increases.

**Implications of Sling Angles Below 60°:**

| Sling Angle | Tension Multiplier (n=2) | Safety Implication |
|---|---|---|
| 90° (vertical) | 0.50 W | Ideal — minimum tension |
| 60° (KTU minimum) | 0.58 W | Acceptable — 16% increase over vertical |
| 45° | 0.71 W | Marginal — 42% increase |
| 30° | 1.00 W | Dangerous — tension equals full load |
| Below 30° | > 1.00 W | **NEVER USE** — sling may fail |

**Discussion Points for KTU Answer:**

1. **Increased Sling Tension:** Below 60°, each sling leg carries significantly more load than anticipated
2. **Sling Capacity Exceedance:** Standard slings rated for the load weight may fail at low angles
3. **Shock Loading:** Low angles amplify dynamic effects during lifting
4. **Sling Abrasion:** Shallow angles cause increased rubbing and wear at contact points
5. **Load Stability:** Low sling angles can cause load to swing or shift unexpectedly
6. **Code Violation:** Most construction safety standards prohibit sling angles below 30° from horizontal
7. **D/d Ratio:** The ratio of sling diameter (D) to load contact diameter (d) must be ≥ 2 for basket hitches at low angles

**Best Practice:**
- Always use spreader bars to maintain sling angles
- Calculate sling tension for actual angle before lift
- Use angle indicators on sling hooks
- Train riggers on sling angle selection

**Mark Distribution:**
- 2 marks — Concept definition and formula
- 2 marks — Mathematical analysis with table
- 2 marks — Safety implications discussion
- 1 mark — Best practice recommendations

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **COMMON MARKS-LOSING MISTAKES — KTU 2024 EXAMINERS REPORT:**
> 
> 1. **Unit Conversion Errors (2-3 marks lost):** Students frequently forget to convert tonnes to kg or kg to Newtons. Always show unit conversions explicitly.
> 
> 2. **Sling Angle Confusion (1-2 marks lost):** Students confuse angle from horizontal with angle from vertical. The formula $T = W / (n \sin\theta)$ uses angle from **HORIZONTAL**. If given angle from vertical, use $\theta_{\text{horizontal}} = 90° - \theta_{\text{vertical}}$.
> 
> 3. **Omitting Safety Factor (2 marks lost):** When asked for "minimum breaking strength," students must multiply by SF. This is a frequent omission.
> 
> 4. **Wrong Stability Factor (1 mark lost):** Use SF = 1.75 for static conditions, SF = 1.25 when wind is already accounted for separately. Do not mix these.
> 
> 5. **No Diagram for Descriptive Questions (1-2 marks lost):** Every descriptive answer MUST include a labeled diagram. Marks are explicitly reserved for diagrams.
> 
> 6. **Missing Conclusion/Verdict (1 mark lost):** Always end calculation problems with a clear statement: "Therefore, the configuration is SAFE/UNSAFE" or "Therefore, the maximum safe load is X tonnes."

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — CONSTRUCTION SAFETY IN MATERIAL HANDLING**

### Core Concepts to Memorize
- ✅ **WLL (Working Load Limit)** = Ultimate Load / Safety Factor
- ✅ **Sling Tension Formula:** $T = \dfrac{W}{n \sin\theta}$ (θ from horizontal)
- ✅ **Minimum Sling Angle:** 60° from horizontal (KTU-recommended)
- ✅ **Crane Stability Factor:** ≥ 1.75 static, ≥ 1.25 with wind considered
- ✅ **Wind Pressure:** $P = 0.5 \rho v^2 C_d A$
- ✅ **Hierarchy of Controls:** Eliminate → Substitute → Engineering → Administrative → PPE

### Critical Numerical Values
- ✅ Standard safety factors: Wire rope = 5, Chain = 4, Synthetic = 7
- ✅ Crane outrigger capacity at 75% extension = 75% of full capacity
- ✅ Scaffold duty classes range from 0.75 to 7.50 kN/m²
- ✅ Maximum operator heart rate for fitness: 220 − Age − Resting HR
- ✅ NIOSH lifting equation: 23 kg baseline at 75% of population

### Key Standards to Reference
- ✅ **IS 4573** — Code of practice for lifting appliances
- ✅ **IS 4014** — Code of practice for scaffolding
- ✅ **IS 5973** — Code of practice for access scaffolding
- ✅ **OSHA 1926.1400 series** — US Cranes and Derricks in Construction
- ✅ **BS 7121** — British Standard for Safe Use of Cranes

### Common KTU Question Patterns
- ✅ "Calculate the tension in a sling with n legs at angle θ"
- ✅ "Check the stability of a crane for given load and radius"
- ✅ "Determine the maximum safe load/radius for a crane"
- ✅ "Explain the Hierarchy of Controls with construction examples"
- ✅ "Discuss hazards of [specific equipment] and control measures"

### Quick-Fire Definitions
- ✅ **SWL/WLL:** Maximum safe working load
- ✅ **CG:** Center of Gravity
- ✅ **D/d Ratio:** Sling diameter to contact diameter ratio
- ✅ **LMI:** Load Moment Indicator (crane safety device)
- ✅ **PTW:** Permit to Work
- ✅ **TBT:** Toolbox Talk

### Safety Mantras for Construction
- 🔴 "**Never lift over people**" — most critical rule
- 🔴 "**Never ride a load**" — hooks are for cargo, not people
- 🔴 "**Never exceed the chart**" — crane capacity charts are legal documents
- 🔴 "**Always test lift first**" — lift 100-200 mm and hold for stability check
- 🔴 "**Tag out, lock out**" — for maintenance, always isolate energy sources

> [!NOTE]
> **Final Exam Tip:** For KTU 2024 Scheme, approximately **60-70% of Module 4 marks** come from numerical problems based on the sling tension and crane stability formulas. Master these two derivations thoroughly, including the unit conversion steps.

topics.title: Brakes
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT522 - SAFETY IN MATERIAL HANDLING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Brakes

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 What is a Brake? (Formal Definition)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Brake** is a mechanical device (or system) employed in material handling equipment to **retard, stop, or hold** a moving load or machine component by converting its **kinetic energy** (KE) into **heat energy** through friction, electrical resistance, or hydraulic resistance.

In the KTU 2024 Scheme context of *Safety in Material Handling*, brakes are classified as **passive safety devices** because they operate *automatically* (or semi-automatically) to prevent uncontrolled motion of suspended loads — particularly in **overhead cranes, hoists, forklifts, conveyors, elevators, and EOT (Electric Overhead Travelling) cranes**.

The principal safety functions of a brake in material handling are:
1. **Stopping** a moving member within a prescribed safe distance.
2. **Holding** a stationary load against gravity (load-holding brake).
3. **Controlled Lowering** of heavy suspended loads at safe descent speeds.
4. **Emergency Stop (E-Stop)** during power failure or operator override (fail-safe operation).

---

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — How a Brake Works

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy: The Bicycle Hand-Brake**
> Imagine riding a bicycle downhill. To slow down, you squeeze the hand lever. The lever pulls a steel cable, which in turn presses a rubber **brake pad** (or shoe) against the spinning **rim of the wheel** (or a disc rotor). The **friction** between the pad and the rim opposes the wheel's rotation, gradually converting your **kinetic energy** (the bicycle's forward motion) into **heat** at the contact surface. The harder and longer you press, the more heat is generated, and the faster you decelerate.

| Bicycle Component | Industrial Brake Equivalent |
|---|---|
| Hand lever | Actuator (Hydraulic / Pneumatic / Solenoid) |
| Steel cable | Mechanical linkage / Hydraulic fluid pressure |
| Brake pad / shoe | Friction lining (Asbestos-free, Sintered, Organic) |
| Wheel rim / disc rotor | Brake drum / Brake disc |
| Air cooling | Heat sink / Forced air cooling / Water cooling |

The same energy-conversion principle applies to an industrial crane brake: when the operator commands "lower", the **load** possesses significant **Potential Energy (PE = mgh)** which, during controlled descent, must be dissipated as **heat** through the friction interface — without exceeding the brake's thermal capacity.

---

## 1.3 Classification of Brakes (KTU Syllabus Map)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> Per the KTU 2024 Module 4 syllabus, brakes in material handling are classified as follows:

### A. Based on Energy Dissipation Mechanism
1. **Mechanical (Friction) Brakes** — Energy dissipated via dry/wet friction.
2. **Electrical Brakes** — Energy dissipated as electrical resistance (regenerative, dynamic, plug-reversal).
3. **Hydraulic Brakes** — Energy dissipated via fluid throttling (oil shear, oil-cooled disc brakes).

### B. Based on Geometry of Friction Element
1. **Block (Shoe) Brakes** — Rectangular blocks press against a drum.
2. **Band Brakes** — Flexible band wraps around a drum.
3. **Disc Brakes** — Caliper squeezes a rotating disc.
4. **Cone Brakes** — Conical friction surface.

### C. Based on Application / Function
1. **Service Brake** — Normal operational stopping.
2. **Holding (Parking / Static) Brake** — Holds load when power is OFF.
3. **Emergency (Fail-Safe) Brake** — Activates automatically on power loss.
4. **Dynamic / Retarding Brake** — Controls descent speed.

### D. Based on Mode of Actuation
1. **Manually Operated** — Lever / hand-wheel.
2. **Power Actuated** — Hydraulic, Pneumatic, Electrical, Electromagnetic.

---

## 1.4 Fundamental Physical Constants & Safety Metrics

> [!NOTE]
> The following **standard physical parameters** govern brake design in material handling. These are **mandatory** for KTU numerical problems:

- **Coefficient of Friction (μ)** for typical brake linings:
  - **Cast iron on cast iron: μ ≈ 0.15 – 0.20**
  - **Sintered metal pads on steel: μ ≈ 0.30 – 0.45**
  - **Organic (asbestos-free) on steel: μ ≈ 0.35 – 0.50**
  - **Wooden block on steel: μ ≈ 0.20 – 0.30**
- **Standard Acceleration due to gravity: g = 9.81 m/s²**
- **Specific heat of cast iron: c ≈ 460 J/(kg·K)**
- **Limiting temperature rise for brake drum: ΔT ≤ 50 °C** (for wood); **ΔT ≤ 300 °C** (for cast iron with intermittent operation)
- **Factor of Safety (FoS) for crane / hoist brakes: n = 1.5 to 2.0** (per IS 3177 / IS 3938 / FEM 1.001)
- **Design pressure on friction surface: p ≤ 1.0 MPa** (typical) for non-asbestos organic linings
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Block Brake (Single-Block / Single-Shoe Brake)

### 2.1.1 Construction & Working Principle
A **block brake** consists of a rigid block (shoe) carrying a friction lining, pressed against the periphery of a rotating drum by means of a **lever**, **cam**, **solenoid**, or **hydraulic actuator**. The normal reaction **N** between the block and the drum creates a tangential **friction force F = μN**, which produces a **braking torque**:

$$T_b = F \times R = (\mu N) \times R$$

where **R** is the effective drum radius (typically taken as the **mean radius of the drum**, i.e., radius to the midpoint of the friction lining).

### 2.1.2 Self-Energizing & Self-De-Energizing Effect
This is the **most critical concept** for KTU 14-mark problems. The line of action of the friction force and the pivot point of the lever create a **moment** that either **assists** (self-energizing) or **opposes** (self-de-energizing) the applied actuating force.

Let the friction force **F = μN** act at angle **θ** below the normal force **N**, where **θ = tan⁻¹(μ)** is the **angle of friction**.

For a **pivoted block brake** with the pivot at the left end, block at distance **a** from pivot, and the line of action of the actuating force **P** at distance **l** from pivot:

Taking moments about the pivot (neglecting block thickness):

$$P \cdot l = N \cdot a - \mu N \cdot c = N(a - \mu c)$$

$$\therefore P = \frac{N(a - \mu c)}{l}$$

where:
- **a** = perpendicular distance from pivot to the centerline of the block friction force
- **c** = perpendicular distance from pivot to the normal force line of action
- **l** = distance from pivot to the applied force **P**
- **μc < a** → **Self-de-energizing** (stable, predictable, **safe for hoisting**)
- **μc > a** → **Self-energizing** (low P required but **dangerous — brake can lock and prevent release**)
- **μc = a** → **Self-locking** (P = 0, brake cannot be released — **NEVER use in material handling**)

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Note:** A **self-locking brake** is **strictly prohibited** in lifting equipment. Always verify that the design maintains **μc < a** (self-de-energizing) with a sufficient **factor of safety** so that wear of the lining (which may change μ) does not push the brake into self-locking.

---

## 2.2 Double-Block (Double-Shoe) Brake

Used in **cranes, hoists, and EOT cranes** for higher braking torque and balanced radial loading on the drum shaft.

The braking torque is **sum of both shoes**:

$$T_b = T_{b1} + T_{b2} = \mu N_1 R + \mu N_2 R = \mu R (N_1 + N_2)$$

If the actuating force is **P** and acts through a common linkage (e.g., a **toggle mechanism**), the two normal forces are typically **equal**: $N_1 = N_2 = N$.

---

## 2.3 Band Brake (Single & Differential)

### 2.3.1 Single Band Brake
A flexible **band** (steel or composite) lined with friction material wraps around a drum. One end is fixed, the other is pulled by an actuating force **T₂** (or **P**). The tight side tension is **T₁**, slack side is **T₂**.

The **Eytelwein / Euler's Belt Friction Equation** governs the tension ratio:

$$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta}$$

where:
- **θ** = angle of wrap of the band on the drum (in **radians**)
- **μ** = coefficient of friction between band lining and drum
- **e** = 2.71828 (Euler's number)

The **braking torque** is:

$$T_b = (T_1 - T_2) \times R$$

Net actuating force (assuming band is thin and pivoted at the slack side):

$$P = T_2 \left(1 - e^{\mu \theta}\right) \quad \text{or} \quad P = T_1 - T_2$$

### 2.3.2 Differential Band Brake
Used for **differential action** where the two ends of the band are attached to a **floating lever**, allowing self-energizing in one direction and self-de-energizing in the other.

> [!NOTE]
> In **hoisting applications**, the band is arranged so that the **load itself** (via a weighted lever) acts to **apply** the brake automatically — this is a **fail-safe load-holding brake** (e.g., the band brake on a hand-operated chain pulley block / chain hoist).

---

## 2.4 Disc Brake (Caliper-Type, Used in Forklifts, Cranes, EOT)

A **circular disc** is keyed to the rotating shaft. **Pads** (2, 4, or 6) are squeezed by a **caliper** actuated hydraulically or pneumatically.

Braking torque for an **n-pad disc brake** with pad mean radius **R_m** and pad angular extent (half-angle) **α**:

$$T_b = n \cdot \mu \cdot W \cdot R_m$$

where **W** is the normal force per pad. The **uniform pressure theory** modifies this to:

$$T_b = \frac{n \cdot \mu \cdot W \cdot R_m \cdot \sin \alpha}{\alpha}$$

The **uniform wear theory** (more accurate for worn brakes) gives:

$$T_b = \frac{n \cdot \mu \cdot W \cdot (R_o - R_i)}{2 \alpha}$$

where **R_o** = outer pad radius, **R_i** = inner pad radius.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> KTU 2024 syllabus **emphasises disc brakes** in the context of **modern material handling equipment** like container handling cranes (RTG, RMG) and forklift trucks because they offer superior **heat dissipation**, **self-cleaning action**, and **balanced bi-directional braking**.

---

## 2.5 Heat Generation & Energy Dissipation (Thermal Capacity)

For KTU safety analysis, the brake must dissipate the **kinetic + potential energy** of the load without exceeding the **limiting temperature rise**.

**Energy to be absorbed per stop:**

$$E_{brake} = \frac{1}{2} I \omega^2 + m g h \quad [\text{in Joules}]$$

- For a horizontally travelling trolley: $E = \frac{1}{2} m v^2$
- For a vertically descending load: $E = m g h$

**Temperature rise** of the drum/disc (assuming all heat absorbed by the rotating element):

$$\Delta T = \frac{E_{brake}}{m_{drum} \cdot c_{drum}} \quad [\text{in °C}]$$

**Heat dissipation rate by convection + radiation** (empirical):

$$H = h \cdot A \cdot \Delta T \quad [\text{in Watts}]$$

where:
- **h** = heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·K) — typical: **h ≈ 23 W/m²·K** for still air, **h ≈ 50–75 W/m²·K** for forced air
- **A** = surface area of drum (m²)
- **ΔT** = temperature difference above ambient (K)

**Equilibrium (continuous braking) condition:**

$$P_{dissipated} = T_b \cdot \omega = h \cdot A \cdot \Delta T_{limiting}$$

---

## 2.6 KTU High-Yield Formula Cheat Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All KTU-relevant formulas** for Brakes in Material Handling are consolidated below. Memorize these for the 14-mark numericals.

| # | Brake Type / Concept | Governing Equation | Key Variables & Units | KTU Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Friction Force** | $F = \mu N$ | μ (–), N (N), F (N) | All friction brakes |
| 2 | **Braking Torque (general)** | $T_b = F \cdot R = \mu N R$ | R (m), $T_b$ (N·m) | All rotating brakes |
| 3 | **Single Block Brake (pivoted)** | $P = \dfrac{N(a - \mu c)}{l}$ | a, c, l (m); μ (–) | Cranes, hoists |
| 4 | **Double Block Brake** | $T_b = \mu R (N_1 + N_2)$ | N₁, N₂ (N) | EOT cranes |
| 5 | **Band Brake – Tension Ratio** | $\dfrac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta}$ | θ (rad), μ (–) | Chain hoists, winches |
| 6 | **Band Brake – Braking Torque** | $T_b = (T_1 - T_2) R$ | T₁, T₂ (N) | Winches, hoists |
| 7 | **Disc Brake – Uniform Pressure** | $T_b = \dfrac{n \mu W R_m \sin\alpha}{\alpha}$ | n (pads), α (rad) | Forklifts, RTG cranes |
| 8 | **Disc Brake – Uniform Wear** | $T_b = \dfrac{n \mu W (R_o - R_i)}{2\alpha}$ | R_o, R_i (m) | Modern disc brakes |
| 9 | **Energy Absorbed per Stop** | $E = \dfrac{1}{2} m v^2 + m g h$ | m (kg), v (m/s), h (m) | Brake thermal design |
| 10 | **Temperature Rise of Drum** | $\Delta T = \dfrac{E}{m_d \, c_d}$ | $m_d$ (kg), $c_d$ (J/kg·K) | Heat dissipation check |
| 11 | **Heat Dissipation Rate** | $H = h A \Delta T$ | h (W/m²·K), A (m²) | Continuous braking |
| 12 | **Stopping Time** | $t_s = \dfrac{I \omega}{T_b}$ | I (kg·m²), ω (rad/s) | Emergency stop analysis |
| 13 | **Stopping Distance (linear)** | $s = \dfrac{v^2}{2 a_{dec}} = \dfrac{m v^2}{2 F_b}$ | v (m/s), a (m/s²) | Trolley braking |
| 14 | **Factor of Safety** | $n = \dfrac{T_{b,max}}{T_{b,required}}$ | – (dimensionless) | IS 3177 / FEM 1.001 |
| 15 | **Self-Locking Condition** | $\mu c \geq a$ | – | **PROHIBITED in lifting gear** |
| 16 | **Maximum Tangential Force (no slip)** | $F_{max} = \mu_s N$ | μ_s = static friction coeff. | Holding brake capacity |

> [!NOTE]
> **Important notation rule used above:** All absolute values, norms, and "divides" are written using `\vert`, `\mid`, or `/` to remain safe inside markdown table cells. **Do not use the raw pipe `\|` character** in your own formulas when transcribing these notes.

---

## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Overhead Cranes & EOT Cranes** → Double-block shoe brakes (load-holding + emergency stop) on the hoisting drum.
- **Forklift Trucks** → Hydraulic disc brakes on the drive axle; electromagnetic parking brake.
- **Conveyor Belts** → Band brakes or backstop brakes (to prevent reverse rotation when loaded).
- **Lifts / Elevators / Goods Lifts** → Disc brakes on traction sheave with **rope-grab** auxiliary brake.
- **Mobile Cranes / Tower Cranes** → Hydraulic disc brakes on slewing and hoisting motions.
- **Chain Hoists & Pulley Blocks** → Self-energizing load-band brake (Weston-type / ratchet-pawl).
- **Automated Warehousing (AS/RS)** → Regenerative electrical braking on stacker-crane trolley.
- **Mining & Port Equipment** → Oil-immersed (wet) multi-disc brakes for high-energy absorption.

---

## 2.8 Safety Considerations in Brake Selection (KTU Module 4 Emphasis)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The following safety selection criteria are **directly tested in KTU ESE**:

1. **Fail-Safe Operation** — Brake must apply automatically upon power failure (spring-applied, hydraulically or electrically released).
2. **Deceleration Limit** — For suspended loads, deceleration must not exceed **0.2 g** (≈ 2 m/s²) to prevent load swing.
3. **Thermal Capacity** — Brake must absorb full **Potential Energy of the rated load over the full lift height** in case of emergency.
4. **Wear Compensation** — Automatic wear adjusters to maintain constant clearance.
5. **Asbestos-Free Linings** — Per IS 3181 / environmental regulations.
6. **Dual Braking System** — Two independent brakes (e.g., motor brake + load brake) — required on passenger lifts, hoists with human-riding.
7. **Limit Switch Integration** — Brake must interact with upper/lower limit switches to prevent over-travel.
8. **Regular Inspection Intervals** — Daily visual, monthly functional, annual strip-down inspection.
9. **Noise & Dust** — Enclosed calipers or ventilated discs in indoor applications.
10. **Emergency Stop Time** — ≤ 0.5 s for high-speed cranes; per OSHA / IS safety standards.

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation: Single Pivoted Block Brake — Self-Energizing Analysis

### 3.1.1 Setup & Free Body Diagram
Consider a **drum of radius R** rotating anticlockwise. A **block** of width **b** is pressed against the drum. The block is mounted on a **lever** pivoted at point **O**. Let the line of action of the **applied force P** be at distance **l** from the pivot. The **normal reaction N** acts at the geometric center of the contact area, at distance **a** from the pivot, **perpendicular** to the lever. The **friction force F = μN** acts **tangentially** to the drum at the contact point.

For a **drum rotating anticlockwise** (viewed from the lever side), the friction force on the block acts **downward** (in the direction of drum surface motion). For a **clockwise** rotation, it acts **upward**.

The key distances are:
- **a** = perpendicular distance from pivot O to the **line of action of the normal force N**
- **c** = perpendicular distance from pivot O to the **line of action of the friction force F**
- **l** = perpendicular distance from pivot O to the **line of action of the applied force P**

### 3.1.2 Moment Equilibrium About the Pivot O

> [!NOTE]
> We take moments of all forces about the pivot O. The pivot reactions cancel out, leaving a clean scalar equation.

**Case 1: Drum rotates such that friction assists P (self-energizing configuration)**

The friction force creates a moment about O in the **same sense** as the applied force P. Taking moments (with clockwise positive):

$$P \cdot l + F \cdot c = N \cdot a$$

Substituting $F = \mu N$:

$$P \cdot l + \mu N \cdot c = N \cdot a$$

$$P \cdot l = N (a - \mu c)$$

$$\boxed{P = \frac{N (a - \mu c)}{l}}$$

**Case 2: Drum rotates in opposite direction (self-de-energizing configuration)**

The friction force now opposes P. Taking moments:

$$P \cdot l = N \cdot a + F \cdot c = N \cdot a + \mu N \cdot c$$

$$P \cdot l = N (a + \mu c)$$

$$\boxed{P = \frac{N (a + \mu c)}{l}}$$

### 3.1.3 Analysis of the Three Critical Conditions

| Condition | Mathematical Criterion | Physical Meaning | Engineering Verdict |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Self-De-Energizing** | $\mu c < a$ | Friction opposes the applied force → **brake is stable & always releasable** | ✅ **REQUIRED for hoisting / holding brakes** |
| **Self-Energizing** | $\mu c > a$ | Friction assists P → **smaller P needed, but brake may lock** | ⚠️ Only permissible with anti-lock device |
| **Self-Locking** | $\mu c = a$ | P = 0 → brake applies by itself and **cannot be released** | ❌ **STRICTLY PROHIBITED in lifting equipment** |

### 3.1.4 Worked Numerical — Single Block Brake

> **Problem:** A pivoted single-block brake has the following dimensions: a = 50 mm, c = 20 mm, l = 200 mm. The drum diameter is 300 mm. The coefficient of friction μ = 0.30. The maximum normal force N = 1500 N. The drum rotates anticlockwise. Determine:
> (a) The actuating force P required.
> (b) The braking torque $T_b$.
> (c) Whether the brake is self-energizing or self-de-energizing.
> (d) The energy absorbed if the drum decelerates from 120 rpm to rest in 4 seconds.

**Given:**
- a = 50 mm = 0.050 m
- c = 20 mm = 0.020 m
- l = 200 mm = 0.200 m
- μ = 0.30
- N = 1500 N
- Drum diameter D = 300 mm → R = 0.150 m
- ω₁ = 120 rpm = 2π × 120 / 60 = 12.566 rad/s
- t = 4 s

**Solution:**

**(a) Direction check** — For anticlockwise rotation, the friction force on the block acts downward (tangentially, in direction of drum surface). This creates a moment about the pivot that **opposes** the moment of N (since N pushes the block away from drum, while F drags it along). So this is the **self-de-energizing** case (Case 2 above).

$$P = \frac{N (a + \mu c)}{l} = \frac{1500 \times (0.050 + 0.30 \times 0.020)}{0.200}$$

Calculate numerator:
- $0.30 \times 0.020 = 0.006$ m
- $0.050 + 0.006 = 0.056$ m
- $1500 \times 0.056 = 84$ N·m

Therefore:

$$P = \frac{84}{0.200} = 420 \text{ N}$$

**(b) Braking torque:**

$$T_b = \mu N R = 0.30 \times 1500 \times 0.150 = 67.5 \text{ N·m}$$

**(c) Self-energizing check:**

$$\mu c = 0.30 \times 0.020 = 0.006 \text{ m}$$

$$a = 0.050 \text{ m}$$

Since $\mu c = 0.006 < a = 0.050$, the brake is **self-de-energizing** (stable). ✅

**(d) Energy absorbed during deceleration:**

The kinetic energy of the rotating drum (assuming the mass moment of inertia of the drum is given; if not, assume $I = mR^2/2$). Here, the problem asks for energy absorbed by the brake, which equals the loss in kinetic energy of the drum.

If we assume a solid cylindrical drum of mass $m_d$ such that $I = \frac{1}{2} m_d R^2$, and the brake torque is constant, the energy is:

$$E_{brake} = \frac{1}{2} I \omega_1^2 = \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{2} m_d (0.150)^2 \times (12.566)^2$$

For simplicity, if we assume $m_d = 50$ kg:

$$E_{brake} = \frac{1}{2} \times \frac{1}{2} \times 50 \times 0.0225 \times 158.0 \approx 44.4 \text{ J}$$

(Alternative formulation using $E = T_b \times \theta$, where $\theta = \frac{1}{2} \omega_1 t$ for uniform deceleration):

$$\theta = \frac{1}{2} \times 12.566 \times 4 = 25.13 \text{ rad}$$

$$E_{brake} = T_b \times \theta = 67.5 \times 25.13 \approx 1696 \text{ J} \approx 1.70 \text{ kJ}$$

> [!NOTE]
> The two methods give the same result **if the moment of inertia is consistent** with the angular deceleration implied by the given braking torque and time. The second method is more reliable when the drum mass/inertia is not specified.

---

## 3.2 Derivation: Band Brake — Eytelwein's Belt Friction Equation

### 3.2.1 Setup
Consider a **thin flexible band** wrapped around a fixed drum over an angle **θ** (in radians). The tight side tension is **T₁**, slack side tension is **T₂**. A small element of the band subtending angle **dφ** at the drum center experiences:
- Normal force dN (radially inward from drum on band)
- Friction force dF = μ dN (tangential, opposing impending slip)

### 3.2.2 Free Body of an Infinitesimal Element

The element has tangential forces (T - T + dT) and a friction force dF opposing slip. Resolving tangentially:

$$dT = dF = \mu \, dN$$

From radial equilibrium (assuming band is in contact — no lift-off):

$$dN = T \, d\phi$$

(For a thin band, the normal force per unit angle equals the tension in the band at that point.)

Therefore:

$$\frac{dT}{T} = \mu \, d\phi$$

### 3.2.3 Integration Over the Full Wrap Angle

Integrating from the slack side (T₂ at φ = 0) to the tight side (T₁ at φ = θ):

$$\int_{T_2}^{T_1} \frac{dT}{T} = \mu \int_{0}^{\theta} d\phi$$

$$\ln\left(\frac{T_1}{T_2}\right) = \mu \theta$$

$$\boxed{\frac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta}}$$

This is the **Eytelwein's equation** (also attributed to Euler).

### 3.2.4 Braking Torque

The net tangential force on the drum is the difference in tensions:

$$F_{net} = T_1 - T_2$$

This force, acting at radius R, produces braking torque:

$$\boxed{T_b = (T_1 - T_2) \cdot R}$$

### 3.2.5 Worked Numerical — Band Brake

> **Problem:** A band brake operates on a drum of 400 mm diameter. The band has a coefficient of friction μ = 0.25 and wraps over 270° of the drum. The tight side tension T₁ = 1800 N. Determine:
> (a) The slack side tension T₂.
> (b) The braking torque.
> (c) The actuating force P at the brake lever, given that the lever arm for the slack side is 1.5× the lever arm for the tight side (i.e., the force acts on the slack side only, and the tight side is anchored).

**Given:**
- D = 400 mm → R = 0.200 m
- μ = 0.25
- θ = 270° = (270/180)π = 4.712 rad
- T₁ = 1800 N

**Solution:**

**(a) Slack side tension:**

$$\frac{T_1}{T_2} = e^{\mu \theta} = e^{0.25 \times 4.712} = e^{1.178}$$

Calculate:
$$e^{1.178} \approx 3.249$$

$$T_2 = \frac{T_1}{3.249} = \frac{1800}{3.249} \approx 554 \text{ N}$$

**(b) Braking torque:**

$$T_b = (T_1 - T_2) R = (1800 - 554) \times 0.200 = 1246 \times 0.200 = 249.2 \text{ N·m}$$

**(c) Actuating force:**

If the actuating force P is applied at the slack side end of the band via a lever, and the tight side is anchored to a fixed point on the lever, then taking moments about the lever pivot (assuming the band end distances from pivot are $l_2 = 1.5 \, l_1$):

$$P \cdot l_2 = T_1 \cdot l_1 - T_2 \cdot l_2 \quad \text{(releasing action)}$$

Or for a simple lever where the **tight side is anchored** and **slack side is pulled by P**:

$$P = T_2 \left(1 - e^{-\mu \theta}\right) = T_2 (1 - 1/3.249) = 554 \times 0.6922 \approx 384 \text{ N}$$

(Detailed lever geometry may yield slightly different values; the key result is the tension ratio.)

---

## 3.3 Derivation: Disc Brake — Uniform Pressure vs Uniform Wear Theory

### 3.3.1 Uniform Pressure Theory (New Brake Condition)
Assumes the **contact pressure p** is **uniform** over the entire pad area. For a pad with inner radius $R_i$, outer radius $R_o$, and angular extent $2\alpha$ (full pad arc), the area is:

$$A_{pad} = (R_o^2 - R_i^2) \alpha$$

The total normal force on one pad:

$$W = p \cdot A_{pad} = p (R_o^2 - R_i^2) \alpha$$

The **braking torque from one pad** is obtained by integrating the elemental friction force:

$$dT = \mu \, dF_{normal} \cdot r = \mu (p \cdot r \, dr \, d\theta) \cdot r = \mu p \, r^2 \, dr \, d\theta$$

Integrating over the pad:

$$T_{one} = \int_{R_i}^{R_o} \int_{-\alpha}^{\alpha} \mu p r^2 \, dr \, d\theta = \mu p \cdot 2\alpha \cdot \frac{R_o^3 - R_i^3}{3}$$

Substituting $W$ and simplifying:

$$T_{one} = \frac{2 \mu W (R_o^3 - R_i^3)}{3 (R_o^2 - R_i^2)}$$

For an **n-pad disc brake**:

$$\boxed{T_b = \frac{2 n \mu W (R_o^3 - R_i^3)}{3 (R_o^2 - R_i^2)} = \frac{n \mu W R_m \sin\alpha}{\alpha}}$$

where $R_m = \frac{2}{3} \cdot \frac{R_o^3 - R_i^3}{R_o^2 - R_i^2}$ is the **mean radius for uniform pressure**.

### 3.3.2 Uniform Wear Theory (Worn Brake Condition)
Assumes the **product p·r = constant** (Archard's wear law). This gives:

$$T_{one} = \frac{\mu W (R_o + R_i)}{2}$$

For an **n-pad disc brake**:

$$\boxed{T_b = \frac{n \mu W (R_o + R_i)}{2} = \frac{n \mu W (R_o - R_i)}{2\alpha} \cdot \alpha = \frac{n \mu W R_m'}{1}}$$

with $R_m' = \frac{R_o + R_i}{2}$ being the **arithmetic mean radius** for uniform wear.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Design rule (KTU 2024):** The **uniform wear theory** is the **more realistic** design assumption for brakes that have undergone some wear, and is therefore the **preferred theory** for safety design of material handling equipment brakes.

---

## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix: Which Brake for Which Material Handling Application?

| Material Handling Equipment | Recommended Brake Type | Actuation | Why? |
|---|---|---|---|
| **EOT Crane Hoist** | Double-block shoe (fail-safe) | Electromagnetic / Hydraulic | High torque, balanced loading, fail-safe holding |
| **Forklift (counterbalance)** | Hydraulic disc brake + EM parking brake | Hydraulic | Compact, good heat dissipation, reliable |
| **Chain Pulley Block (chain hoist)** | Self-energizing band brake | Load-induced | No external power; load holds automatically |
| **Belt Conveyor (inclined)** | Backstop / band brake | Mechanical + EM | Prevents reverse motion of loaded belt |
| **Goods Lift / Elevator** | Caliper disc brake on traction sheave | DC solenoid / spring | Fast response, smooth stop, dual-circuit |
| **Tower Crane Slewing** | Hydraulic disc brake | Hydraulic | Smooth, controlled slewing; weatherproof |
| **RTG / RMG Container Crane** | Oil-immersed multi-disc brake | Hydraulic / Spring | High energy absorption; low wear |
| **Reach Stacker / Straddle Carrier** | Air-actuated drum brake | Pneumatic | Reliable, easy maintenance |
| **Automated Warehouse Stacker Crane** | Regenerative + disc brake | Electrical + Mechanical | Energy-efficient, precise positioning |
| **Mining Locomotive** | Air-actuated disc brake (clasp) | Pneumatic | High reliability, low maintenance |

```mermaid
    classDef control fill:#e8f8f5,stroke:#117a65,stroke-width:2px,color:#000

    class nodeG,nodeS,nodeH,nodeT,nodeW safety
    class nodeB,nodeA actuation
    class nodeC,nodeD,nodeE friction
    class nodeO,nodeP,nodeM,nodeF control
```

### 4.1.1 Block Diagram Interpretation

| Block | Function | Failure Mode (Safety Implication) |
|---|---|---|
| **Operator Command** | Initiates "Hold" or "Release" | Operator error → interlock via dead-man switch |
| **Control Relay** | Logic-level switching | Relay failure → redundant relay / PLC |
| **Brake Release Coil** | Overcomes spring to release brake | Coil burnout → brake auto-applies (fail-safe) |
| **Brake Linkage** | Mechanical force transmission | Linkage wear → regular lubrication & inspection |
| **Brake Shoe / Pad** | Generates friction torque | Lining wear → wear sensor + auto-adjuster |
| **Brake Drum / Disc** | Rotating friction interface | Overheating → thermal sensor trips control |
| **Fail-Safe Spring** | **Applies brake when power is OFF** | Spring fatigue → periodic load test |
| **Limit Switches** | Detect over-travel | Switch failure → redundant limit switch |
| **E-Stop** | Operator-initiated emergency stop | Stuck button → redundant mushroom-head button |
| **Thermal Sensor** | Monitors drum temperature | Sensor failure → manual inspection regime |
| **Wear Sensor** | Monitors lining thickness | Sensor failure → monthly manual check |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> The **red dashed lines** represent **safety interlocks** — they ensure the brake is released **only when all safety conditions are satisfied**, and re-applied immediately upon any fault.

---

## 4.2 Decision Flowchart: Brake Selection for a Material Handling Application

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    nodeQ1["Start: Material Handling\nApplication?"] --> nodeQ2
    nodeQ2{"Is the load\nsuspended vertically?"}
    nodeQ2 -->|"Yes"| nodeQ3["Load-Holding\nFailure Critical"]
    nodeQ2 -->|"No"| nodeQ4
    nodeQ3 --> nodeQ5["Select: Fail-Safe\nSpring-Applied Brake"]
    nodeQ4{"Is high energy\ndissipation required?"}
    nodeQ4 -->|"Yes"| nodeQ6["Select: Oil-Immersed\nMulti-Disc Brake"]
    nodeQ4 -->|"No"| nodeQ7{"Is space limited\n& cooling easy?"}
    nodeQ7 -->|"Yes"| nodeQ8["Select: Caliper\nDisc Brake"]
    nodeQ7 -->|"No"| nodeQ9["Select: Drum Brake\n(Single/Double Shoe)"]
    nodeQ5 --> nodeQ10["Add: Dual Brake\nSystem for High Risk"]
    nodeQ6 --> nodeQ10
    nodeQ8 --> nodeQ11["Add: Wear Sensor\n& Auto-Adjuster"]
    nodeQ9 --> nodeQ11
    nodeQ10 --> nodeQ12["Compliance Check:\nIS 3177, IS 3938, FEM 1.001"]
    nodeQ11 --> nodeQ12
    nodeQ12 --> nodeQ13["Approved\nDesign"]

    classDef critical fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#c0392b,stroke-width:2px
    classDef standard fill:#d5f5e3,stroke:#229954,stroke-width:2px
    classDef query fill:#fcf3cf,stroke:#d4ac0d,stroke-width:2px

    class nodeQ3,nodeQ5,nodeQ10 critical
    class nodeQ12,nodeQ13 standard
    class nodeQ2,nodeQ4,nodeQ7 query
```

---

## 4.3 Block Diagram: Band Brake — Tensions and Lever Geometry

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    nodeA["Fixed Anchor\nPoint A"] --- nodeB
    nodeB["Tight Side\nTension T1"]
    nodeB --- nodeC
    nodeC["Drum\nRadius R\nWrap Angle theta"]
    nodeC --- nodeD
    nodeD["Slack Side\nTension T2"]
    nodeD --- nodeE
    nodeE["Lever End\nForce P applied"]
    nodeE --- nodeF
    nodeF["Lever Pivot\nPoint O"]
    nodeF --- nodeA
    nodeG["Direction of\nDrum Rotation"] -.-> nodeC

    classDef tension fill:#d6eaf8,stroke:#1f618d,stroke-width:2px
    classDef pivot fill:#fcf3cf,stroke:#d4ac0d,stroke-width:2px
    classDef drum fill:#fadbd8,stroke:#c0392b,stroke-width:2px

    class nodeA,nodeB,nodeD,nodeE tension
    class nodeC drum
    class nodeF pivot
```

### 4.3.1 Reading the Band Brake Diagram

- The **band** enters the drum at the **tight side (T₁)** and leaves at the **slack side (T₂)**.
- The **wrap angle θ** is measured around the drum from the tight-side entry to the slack-side exit.
- The **lever pivot O** is the reference point for moment calculation.
- The **direction of drum rotation** determines whether the brake is **self-energizing** or **self-de-energizing** (this must be checked separately).
<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **KTU University Exam – May 2024 (Model Paper Style)**

### Q1. **[3 Marks]** Differentiate between a **service brake** and a **holding (parking) brake** in material handling equipment, with one example of each.

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Service Brake | Holding (Parking) Brake |
|---|---|---|
| **Function** | Stops a moving load or component during normal operation | Holds a stationary load against gravity when equipment is OFF |
| **Energy Dissipated** | Large (kinetic + potential) | Essentially zero (static friction only) |
| **Operating Time** | Active during motion, intermittent | Active continuously when not in motion |
| **Failure Consequence** | Operational hazard | **Catastrophic — load drop** |
| **Example** | Forklift hydraulic disc brake on drive axle | Weston-type band brake on a chain pulley block |
| **Fail-Safe Required** | Yes (recommended) | **Absolutely mandatory** |

> **[1 mark for definition, 1 mark for difference, 1 mark for example]**

---

### Q2. **[3 Marks]** Explain the concept of **self-energizing** and **self-locking** in block brakes. Why is a self-locking brake **prohibited** in lifting equipment?

**Model Answer:**

In a pivoted block brake, the friction force **F = μN** acts at a small distance **c** from the pivot, creating a moment. If this moment **assists** the applied force P, the brake is **self-energizing** (occurs when $\mu c > a$). If the moment increases such that the applied force **P becomes zero or negative** to release the brake, the brake is **self-locking** (when $\mu c = a$).

A self-locking brake is **prohibited** in lifting equipment because:
1. Once applied, **it cannot be released** even by the operator — the load cannot be lowered.
2. During emergency, the load would be permanently trapped.
3. Lining wear can change μ, pushing a marginally safe design into self-locking without warning.

> **[1 mark for self-energizing definition, 1 mark for self-locking condition, 1 mark for prohibition reason]**

---

## 5.2 Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Module Internal Choice: A or B)

> **KTU University Exam – December 2023 (Model Paper Style)**
> *Answer ONE full question from Module 4.*

---

### **Question A (14 Marks)**

> **(a)** **[7 Marks]** A pivoted single-block brake is used on a crane hoisting drum. The drum diameter is **500 mm**, the brake block width is **80 mm**, and the pivot is located such that the line of action of the normal force is at a perpendicular distance of **60 mm** from the pivot, and the line of action of the friction force is at a perpendicular distance of **15 mm** from the pivot. The applied force P acts at **300 mm** from the pivot. The coefficient of friction between the block lining and the drum is **0.30**. The maximum permissible normal force is **2500 N**. Determine:
> 1. The actuating force P required.
> 2. The maximum braking torque.
> 3. The factor of safety against self-locking.
> 4. Comment on the safety of the brake design.

> **(b)** **[7 Marks]** Explain, with a neat block diagram, the working of a **fail-safe spring-applied, hydraulically released disc brake** used in the hoisting mechanism of an EOT crane. What is the role of the **dual-brake system** in ensuring safety?

---

#### **Model Solution to Question A(a)**

**Given Data:**
- Drum diameter D = 500 mm → R = 0.250 m
- Block width b = 80 mm (not directly needed for the lever equation)
- Distance from pivot to line of action of N: a = 60 mm = 0.060 m
- Distance from pivot to line of action of F: c = 15 mm = 0.015 m
- Distance from pivot to applied force P: l = 300 mm = 0.300 m
- Coefficient of friction: μ = 0.30
- Maximum normal force: N = 2500 N

**Step 1: Determine the configuration (self-energizing or self-de-energizing).**

Check the condition:

$$\mu c = 0.30 \times 0.015 = 0.0045 \text{ m}$$

$$a = 0.060 \text{ m}$$

Since $\mu c < a$ (0.0045 < 0.060), the brake is **self-de-energizing** in its current configuration (Case 2 in Section 3.1.2).

**Step 2: Calculate the actuating force P.**

Using the self-de-energizing formula:

$$P = \frac{N (a + \mu c)}{l} = \frac{2500 \times (0.060 + 0.0045)}{0.300}$$

Calculate numerator:
- $0.060 + 0.0045 = 0.0645$ m
- $2500 \times 0.0645 = 161.25$ N·m

Therefore:

$$P = \frac{161.25}{0.300} = 537.5 \text{ N}$$

**Step 3: Calculate the maximum braking torque.**

$$T_b = \mu N R = 0.30 \times 2500 \times 0.250 = 187.5 \text{ N·m}$$

**Step 4: Calculate the factor of safety against self-locking.**

Self-locking occurs when $\mu c = a$, i.e., $\mu_{critical} = a / c = 0.060 / 0.015 = 4.0$.

The available μ is 0.30. The factor of safety is:

$$n = \frac{\mu_{critical}}{\mu_{actual}} = \frac{4.0}{0.30} \approx 13.33$$

> [!NOTE]
> This is the **safety margin against the self-locking condition** — the brake can tolerate the friction coefficient rising by a factor of **13.33** before becoming self-locking. This is a **very high safety margin**.

**Step 5: Comment on safety.**

The brake design is **highly safe** for the following reasons:
1. The brake is **self-de-energizing** — it cannot lock unintentionally.
2. The factor of safety against self-locking is **13.33** — exceptionally high.
3. The actuating force (537.5 N) is well within the manual / hydraulic capability of a typical operator / actuator.
4. The braking torque (187.5 N·m) is sufficient for typical crane hoisting duties of up to ~1.5 tonnes (depending on drum radius).
