## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

---

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]**
**Define the term "Thorough Examination" of a lifting appliance as applied under the Factories Act, 1948. Who is authorized to conduct it, and what is the statutory frequency?**

**Model Answer:**

A "Thorough Examination" is a **detailed, formal, and documented inspection** of a lifting appliance (or lifting gear), carried out to verify that the equipment is **structurally sound, safe to operate, and free from defects** that could endanger personnel or property.

- **Authorized by:** A **Competent Person** — defined as a person who possesses adequate **practical and theoretical knowledge** and **adequate experience** of the type of machinery being examined.
- **Statutory frequency (Section 28, Factories Act, 1948):**
  - For **lifting machines and hoists:** at least **once in every period of 12 months**.
  - For **lifting tackle / gear (chains, ropes, slings):** at least **once in every period of 12 months**, with **intermediate checks every 7 days** for gear in regular use.
- **Records:** Must be entered in the **prescribed register** (Form No. 7) maintained at the factory.

*(3 marks — full content as above)*

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — June 2024]**
**State the "10 percent wear rule" applicable to the inspection of wire ropes and lifting chains. Explain its significance with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

The **10% rule** is a **discard criterion** in material handling inspection:

1. **For wire ropes:** If more than **10% of the total wires** are broken within **one lay length** of the rope, the rope **must be discarded**. *(Example: A 6×19 rope has 114 wires. If 12 or more wires are broken in one lay length, the rope is unfit for service.)*

2. **For lifting chains:** If the **diameter of any link** is reduced by more than **10%** of the original nominal diameter due to wear, the chain **must be discarded**. *(Example: A 16 mm chain link worn down to 14.2 mm shows 11.25% wear and must be discarded.)*

**Significance:** Strength is proportional to **cross-sectional area**, which scales as the **square of the diameter**. Therefore, a 10% reduction in diameter implies a ~19% reduction in load-bearing capacity — a critical safety threshold.

*(3 marks)*

---

### 5.2 Part B — Module Internal Choice Questions (14 Marks)

---

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023 / June 2024 Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the **three-tier hierarchy of inspection** in material handling. Compare and contrast the **scope, periodicity, and authority** at each level. Briefly describe the **statutory provisions** under the Factories Act, 1948 that govern these inspections.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A wire rope of construction **6×37 (with a hemp core)** is used in a 5-tonne capacity overhead crane. During its annual thorough examination, the inspector records the following observations:
- Total wires: 222
- Broken wires in one lay length: 25
- Measured rope diameter at the most worn section: 23.5 mm (nominal 25 mm)
- Visible surface corrosion over 40% of the rope length

Apply the relevant **discard criteria** and determine whether the rope is fit for service. Show all calculations.

---

**Model Solution to Question A(a):**

The three-tier inspection hierarchy is as follows:

**Tier 1 — Pre-Use Inspection (Operator Level):**
- **Frequency:** Before every shift / daily.
- **Scope:** Visual checks, control response, fluid leaks, brake function, hook latch, sling condition.
- **Authority:** Trained and authorized equipment operator.
- **Records:** Daily checklist / logbook.

**Tier 2 — Periodic Inspection (Maintenance Level):**
- **Frequency:** Weekly, monthly, or quarterly (as scheduled).
- **Scope:** Functional testing, wear measurement, lubrication, brake adjustment, limit switch testing, structural torque checks.
- **Authority:** Trained maintenance personnel under a competent supervisor.
- **Records:** Maintenance logbook, work orders.

**Tier 3 — Thorough Examination (Statutory):**
- **Frequency:** At least every 12 months (6 months for certain equipment, 7 days for lifting tackle in continuous use).
- **Scope:** Full structural examination, NDT, proof load test (where mandated), review of prior records.
- **Authority:** Independent Competent Person (external or approved in-house).
- **Records:** Statutory certificate (Form 7 under Factories Act).

**Statutory Provisions:**
- **Section 28** of the Factories Act, 1948 mandates annual thorough examination of all lifting machines.
- **Section 29** mandates the maintenance of a **prescribed register** of all examinations.
- Failure to comply is punishable under **Section 92** of the Act.

*(7 marks — full hierarchy plus statutory citations)*

---

**Model Solution to Question A(b):**

**Given:**
- Construction: 6×37 with hemp core
- Total wires $N_{total} = 6 \times 37 = 222$ (hemp core is non-load-bearing)
- Broken wires in one lay length: $n_{broken} = 25$
- Nominal diameter $D_0 = 25 \text{ mm}$
- Measured worn diameter $D_{worn} = 23.5 \text{ mm}$
- Surface corrosion over 40% of length

**Step 1: Apply the 10% broken wire rule**

$$
\begin{aligned}
n_{threshold} &= 0.10 \times 222 = 22.2 \text{ wires} \\
n_{broken} &= 25 > 22.2 \\
\therefore \text{FAIL on broken wire count.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Apply the wear rule**

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\text{Wear} &= \dfrac{25 - 23.5}{25} \times 100 = 6\% \\
\text{This is within the 10% threshold — PASS on wear alone.}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Apply the corrosion criterion**

> Surface corrosion over 40% of the rope length is a **major red flag**. Per IS 3177, **widespread visible corrosion** is itself a cause for **reduction of SWL or discard**, depending on severity. 40% extent is **severe**, warranting **immediate discard** even if other criteria were borderline.

**Step 4: Conclude**

> **Rope Status: UNFIT FOR SERVICE — IMMEDIATE DISCARD REQUIRED.**
>
> **Reasons:**
> 1. Failed 10% broken wire rule (25 > 22.2 threshold).
> 2. Severe surface corrosion across 40% of length.
>
> **Prescribed Action:** Remove the rope from service, mark "DISCARDED", cut into short unusable pieces, update the lifting gear register, and replace with a new rope of equivalent specification.

*(7 marks — full calculations and conclusion)*

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam — Dec 2022 Pattern]**

**(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the **visual, dimensional, and functional checks** that a competent person should carry out during the thorough examination of an **overhead travelling crane**. Tabulate the **inspection schedule** (daily, weekly, monthly, quarterly, annually) with at least two checks at each level.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A two-leg wire rope sling is to be used to lift a **3-tonne load**. The sling legs are inclined at **45° from the vertical**. Each leg has a **SWL of 2 tonnes** when used vertically. Determine:
1. The actual tension in each leg.
2. Whether the sling is fit for the lift.
3. The **maximum permissible inclination angle from vertical** for the load to remain within the sling's rated capacity.

---

**Model Solution to Question B(a):**

**Thorough Examination of an Overhead Travelling Crane — Competent Person's Checklist:**

**A. Visual Checks:**
- Welds on girders, end-carriages, and brackets — search for cracks, undercut, porosity.
- Hooks — check for throat opening, twist, safety latch operation.
- Wire ropes — broken wires, kinks, birdcaging, corrosion, lubrication.
- Rope terminations (sockets, drum clamping) — slippage, cracked socket, loose wedges.
- Structural members — bent, buckled, or corroded sections.

**B. Dimensional Checks:**
- Rope diameter at three points — compare with nominal (10% wear rule).
- Hook throat opening — measure and compare with original (5% rule).
- Wheel flange and tread wear — measure against allowable limit.
- Bridge deflection under no-load and full-load — compare with allowable (L/500 to L/700).
- End-carriage wheelbase and track gauge — verify alignment.

**C. Functional Checks:**
- Hoist, travel, and cross-travel motions — smooth operation.
- Brakes — hold load at standstill; test under no-load and load.
- Limit switches — upper, lower, travel-end limits operational.
- Overload limiter / load indicator — test with known weight.
- Audible alarm and warning systems.
- Emergency stop buttons and isolators.

**D. Inspection Schedule Table:**

| Frequency | Checks (Minimum two) |
|---|---|
| **Daily (Pre-Use)** | Visual hook/sling check; brake response; fluid leaks |
| **Weekly** | Rope lubrication; control button function; warning devices |
| **Monthly** | Rope diameter measurement; brake slip test; lubrication top-up |
| **Quarterly** | Hook throat measurement; structural bolt torque; limit switch test |
| **Annually (Statutory)** | Full thorough examination; NDT on hooks; proof load test (where mandated) |

*(7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution to Question B(b):**

**Given:**
- Load $W = 3 \text{ tonnes}$
- Sling configuration: 2-leg
- Sling leg SWL (vertical) = 2 tonnes
- Sling angle from vertical $\theta = 45°$

**Step 1: Calculate actual tension per leg at 45°**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{actual} &= \dfrac{W}{2 \cos\theta} \\
           &= \dfrac{3}{2 \cos 45°} \\
           &= \dfrac{3}{2 \times 0.7071} \\
           &= \dfrac{3}{1.4142} \\
           &= 2.121 \text{ tonnes per leg}
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 2: Compare with sling SWL**

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{actual} = 2.121 \text{ t} &> SWL_{leg} = 2.0 \text{ t} \\
\therefore \text{FAIL — sling is NOT fit for the lift.}
\end{aligned}
$$

The sling is overloaded by:

$$
\begin{aligned}
\%\text{Overload} &= \dfrac{2.121 - 2.000}{2.000} \times 100 = 6.07\%
\end{aligned}
$$

**Step 3: Calculate the maximum permissible angle for the sling to remain fit**

The sling is fit as long as:

$$
\begin{aligned}
T_{actual} &\leq SWL_{leg} \\
\dfrac{3}{2 \cos\theta_{max}} &\leq 2 \\
\cos\theta_{max} &\geq \dfrac{3}{4} = 0.75 \\
\theta_{max} &\leq \cos^{-1}(0.75) \\
\theta_{max} &\leq 41.41°
\end{aligned}
$$

> **Conclusion:** The sling legs must be inclined at **no more than 41.41° from vertical** (i.e., **at least 48.59° from horizontal**) to remain within the 2-tonne per-leg rating for lifting a 3-tonne load. At 45°, the sling is **overloaded by ~6%** and must not be used for this lift.

*(7 marks — full derivation and conclusion)*

---

### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Inspection Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting the hemp / fibre core** in wire rope wire count — the core is **non-structural** and must be excluded from the 10% wire count. Many students incorrectly include the core wires, leading to wrong threshold calculations.
> 2. **Confusing wear percentage with strength loss** — wear is linear in diameter but **quadratic in strength**. The 10% wear rule corresponds to a **~19% strength loss**, not 10%.
> 3. **Sling angle confusion** — the angle is measured from the **vertical** (load line) for tension calculation, not from the horizontal. Mixing these yields completely wrong answers.
> 4. **Statutory frequency errors** — many students write "annual" for all inspections, forgetting the **7-day rule for lifting tackle in continuous service** under Section 28(2)(b) of the Factories Act.
> 5. **No mention of records/register** — the Factories Act **explicitly mandates** the maintenance of a prescribed register. Omitting this in a "Thorough Examination" definition costs at least 1 mark per occurrence.

---

### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **Inspection is statutory, not optional.** Section 28 of the Factories Act, 1948 mandates it; non-compliance is a **punishable offence**.
- **Three tiers:** Pre-use (daily/operator), Periodic (scheduled/maintenance), Thorough (annual/Competent Person).
- **10% rules** apply to **both wire ropes (broken wires)** and **chains (diameter wear)** — the discard threshold is **absolute and non-negotiable**.
- **Proof load test factor:** typically **1.25 to 1.5 × SWL** for lifting machines; **deflection limit** is **L/500 to L/700**.
- **Sling tension** is governed by the angle from vertical: $T = W / (n \cdot \cos\theta)$ where $n$ = number of legs. The **smaller the angle from vertical, the greater the tension** — never sling flatter than 30° from horizontal (60° from vertical).
- **Wear** is measured linearly (diameter) but **strength is reduced quadratically** (area) — a 10% wear corresponds to a 19% strength loss.
- **Records are mandatory:** Form 7 register, daily checklists, maintenance logs — all must be **up-to-date and accessible** for inspection by Factory Inspectors.
- **A Competent Person is not just qualified — they must be independent of the operational and maintenance function** to ensure impartiality.
- **NDT methods** are selected based on **material type** (ferrous vs non-ferrous) and **defect location** (surface, subsurface, through-thickness).
- **Discarded equipment must be physically mutilated** (cut ropes, drilled hooks, broken links) to prevent inadvertent re-entry into service.
- **Bridge crane deflection** under load is a primary **structural health indicator**; excessive deflection triggers quarantine and detailed NDT.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Testing and Maintenance - Facepieces
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT522 - SAFETY IN MATERIAL HANDLING > - **Module:** Module Module 4 > - **Topic:** Testing and Maintenance - Facepieces # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition A **facepiece** is the fundamental sealing and respiratory interface component of any tight-fitting respirator, designed to form an airtight barrier between the wearer's respiratory tract and the contaminated ambient atmosphere. In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology aligned with **NIOSH 42 CFR Part 84**, **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.134**, and **Bureau of Indian Standards (BIS) IS 14166:1994 (Reaffirmed 2014)**, the facepiece constitutes the entire portion of the respirator that covers the wearer's nose, mouth, and (in full-face configurations) the eyes. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** Facepieces are classified as **critical safety components** — failure of the face seal contributes to over **80% of total respirator leakage**, making their periodic testing and maintenance non-negotiable under any respiratory protection programme (RPP). ### 1.2 Classification of Facepieces | Classification Criterion | Category | Description | |---|---|---| | **Coverage Area** | Quarter-Mask | Covers nose and mouth only (covers ~1/4 of face) | | | Half-Mask (Half-Face) | Covers nose, mouth, and chin | | | Full-Facepiece (Full-Face) | Covers entire face from chin to forehead, including eyes | | | Loose-Fitting (Hood/Helmet) | Does not form a tight seal; relies on positive pressure | | **Seal Mechanism** | Tight-Fitting | Forms a pressure-tight seal on the skin | | | Loose-Fitting | No skin contact seal; air-fed only | | **Air Supply Mode** | Negative-Pressure | Air drawn through filter by wearer's inhalation | | | Positive-Pressure (PAPR/CFRS) | Air supplied under continuous pressure | > [!NOTE] > **Key Distinction for KTU Examinations:** Only **tight-fitting** facepieces require formal **fit testing**. Loose-fitting hoods and helmets are excluded from fit-testing protocols because they depend on overpressure rather than facial seal integrity. ### 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — "The Diving Mask Principle" Imagine you are scuba diving. Your facemask must create a perfect watertight seal around your nose and eyes — even a single hair or grain of sand can break the seal and flood the mask. A respirator facepiece works on **exactly the same principle**, but instead of preventing water ingress, it prevents **toxic dust, fumes, vapors, or pathogens** from leaking inward toward your lungs. Now, consider that a diving mask is exposed to salt water, sun, and pressure changes — over time, the silicone skirt cracks, the buckle wears out, and the lens gets scratched. **A respirator facepiece undergoes identical degradation**: sweat, body oils, repeated donning, UV exposure, and chemical contact all slowly destroy the elastomer seal. This is why systematic **Testing and Maintenance** is not an optional best practice — it is the engineering equivalent of a **pre-flight safety check on an aircraft**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Mandate:** Every respirator user must undergo **annual fit testing** and **pre-use functional checks**. Employers must maintain **written records** for a minimum period of **30 years** post-employment under OSHA 1910.1020 (Access to Employee Exposure and Medical Records). ### 1.4 Standard Reference Metrics (Bolded for Quick Recall) - **Assigned Protection Factor (APF)**: **10** (Half-Mask), **50** (Full-Face), **1000** (PAPR Full-Face) - **Fit Factor (FF) Pass Threshold**: **≥ 100** (Half-Mask), **≥ 500** (Full-Face) - **Quantitative Fit Factor Formula**: $FF = \frac{C_{out}}{C_{in}}$ - **Leakage Component Sum**: $L_{total} = L_{face-seal} + L_{filter} + L_{exhalation-valve}$ - **Recommended Test Interval**: **12 months** (or sooner if weight change > 10%, dental work, or scarring) - **Service Life of Elastomer**: **1–5 years** depending on usage and chemical exposure history --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Concept — The Six Pillars of Facepiece Integrity A facepiece remains reliable throughout its operational life only if the following **six functional pillars** are continuously validated: 1. **Material Integrity** — the elastomer, straps, and structural frame are free from cuts, cracks, swelling, or hardening. 2. **Sealing Geometry** — the inward-facing flange (often called the *roll-off* or *roll-cuff* edge) maintains its original contour without deformation. 3. **Valve Functionality** — the inhalation and exhalation valves seat correctly and open at the specified cracking pressure. 4. **Strap Elasticity** — the head harness retains at least **80% of original elasticity** after loading. 5. **Optical Clarity** — the visor (full-face) maintains minimum **70% light transmission** (per EN 136). 6. **Hygienic State** — the interior surface is microbiologically decontaminated to acceptable bioburden limits. > [!NOTE] > **Why These Six?** Every documented respirator failure root-cause analysis (RCA) — including those filed with **NIOSH's National Personal Protective Technology Laboratory (NPPTL)** — traces back to one of these six failure modes. Together they form the engineering basis of a **Respirator Maintenance, Repair, and Cleaning (RRMC) Programme**. ## 2.2 The Two-Stage Testing Doctrine ### Stage 1 — Pre-Use User Seal Check (Daily) Performed by the wearer **every time** the respirator is donned. Two recognized methods: - **Negative Pressure Seal Check**: Inhale gently and hold breath for 10 seconds. The facepiece should collapse slightly inward and remain collapsed. If it re-inflates, the seal is compromised. - **Positive Pressure Seal Check**: Exhale gently while blocking the cartridge inlet. Slight outward bulging of the facepiece without air leakage at the seal confirms integrity. > [!WARNING] > **Common KTU Mistake:** A pre-use seal check is **NOT equivalent to a fit test**. The seal check is a *qualitative go/no-go* gate; the fit test is a *measured quantitative assignment* of protection. ### Stage 2 — Formal Fit Testing (Periodic) Conducted by a **trained fit-test operator** using either qualitative or quantitative methods. ## 2.3 Qualitative Fit Testing (QLFT) — Four Approved Challenge Agents | Agent | OSHA-Approved? | Sensitivity | Pass Criterion | |---|---|---|---| | **Isoamyl Acetate** (Banana Oil) | Yes | Organic Vapor Sense | No taste/smell detected at 10, 50, 100 | | **Saccharin Solution** | Yes | Sweet Taste | No sweet taste detected at 10, 50, 100 | | **Bitrex** (Denatonium Benzoate) | Yes | Extremely Bitter Taste | No bitter taste detected at 10, 50, 100 | | **Irritant Smoke** (Stannic Chloride) | Yes | Upper Respiratory Irritation | No involuntary cough/irritation | > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Note:** Each QLFT agent uses a **graded exposure protocol** — the test subject first completes a **sensitivity screening**, then performs the **fit test** during 10 normal breaths, head side-to-side, head up-and-down, talking (reciting "rainbow passage" or "alphabet"), grimacing, bending, and jogging in place. ## 2.4 Quantitative Fit Testing (QNFT) — Two Methodologies ### Method A — **Generated Aerosol (CNC)** - Uses a **Condensation Nucleus Counter (PortaCount)** to measure aerosol concentration inside (`$C_{in}$`) and outside (`$C_{out}$`) the facepiece. - **Test Exercises**: 8 standard exercises of 60 seconds each. - **Overall Fit Factor**: Harmonic mean of individual exercise fit factors. ### Method B — **Ambient Aerosol (PortaCount)** - Measures ambient aerosol already present in the test chamber. - Less expensive and faster than CNC but slightly less precise. ## 2.5 KTU Formula Sheet (Cheat-Sheet) | # | Formula / Term | Definition | Units / Notes | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | $FF_i = \frac{C_{out}}{C_{in}}$ | Per-exercise Fit Factor | Dimensionless ratio | | 2 | $FF_{overall} = \frac{N}{\sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{1}{FF_i}}$ | Harmonic Mean of all exercise FFs | $N$ = number of exercises (typically 8) | | 3 | $APF = \frac{C_{out}}{C_{in,permissible}}$ | Assigned Protection Factor | Regulatory factor by OSHA | | 4 | $L_{total} = L_{seal} + L_{filter} + L_{valve}$ | Total inward leakage (additive component model) | % leakage | | 5 | $P_{protector} = \frac{APF}{1}$ | Protection Multiplier | Used in selection logic | | 6 | $RR = \frac{FF_{test}}{APF}$ | Relative Risk Reduction Index | $\geq 1$ = adequate | | 7 | $\sigma_{strap} = \frac{F_{max} - F_{min}}{F_{original}}$ | Strap degradation index | Pass if $\sigma_{strap} \leq 0.20$ | | 8 | $C_{break} = \frac{2\gamma \cos\theta}{r}$ | Laplace valve cracking pressure | Pa (inhalation valve) | > [!NOTE] > **Why the Harmonic Mean?** The human respiratory cycle responds to **leakage rate, not peak concentration**. The harmonic mean correctly weights the lowest fit factors (worst-moment protection), making it the statistically appropriate aggregation — this is a frequent viva question. ## 2.6 Maintenance Workflow (Engineering Lifecycle) | Phase | Frequency | Activity | Responsible Party | |---|---|---|---| | Pre-Use Check | Every Use | Seal check, visual inspection | Wearer | | Cleaning | After Every Use | Disassemble, wash, rinse, dry | Wearer or Designated | | Disinfection | As needed (shared use) | Immerse in disinfectant (hypochlorite, iodine, Quat) | Trained person | | Inspection | Monthly | Full functional check, valve test | Supervisor / Program Admin | | Repair | As needed | Replace valves, straps, cartridges | Manufacturer-Certified | | Storage | Always | Sealed bag, away from sunlight, dust, chemicals | Wearer | | Retirement | EOL | Discard and destroy; mark "TRAINING USE ONLY" | Program Admin | --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation 1 — Harmonic Mean Overall Fit Factor The overall fit factor is **not** a simple arithmetic mean. It must be derived as a **harmonic mean** because leakage events are sequential and cumulative during the breathing cycle. ### Given: - $FF_i$ = Fit factor measured during exercise $i$ (for $i = 1, 2, \ldots, N$) - $C_{out}$ = Measured ambient concentration (assumed constant during the test) - $C_{in,i}$ = Measured in-mask concentration during exercise $i$ ### Step 1 — Express Leakage Rate in Each Exercise For each exercise, the in-mask concentration is: $$C_{in,i} = \frac{C_{out}}{FF_i}$$ ### Step 2 — Express Average In-Mask Concentration Over All Exercises Assuming equal time $T$ per exercise, the time-averaged in-mask concentration is: $$\overline{C_{in}} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} C_{in,i} = \frac{1}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{C_{out}}{FF_i}$$ ### Step 3 — Factor Out the Constant $C_{out}$ $$\overline{C_{in}} = \frac{C_{out}}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{1}{FF_i}$$ ### Step 4 — Define the Overall Fit Factor The overall fit factor is the ratio of ambient to average in-mask concentration: $$FF_{overall} = \frac{C_{out}}{\overline{C_{in}}}$$ ### Step 5 — Substitute and Simplify $$FF_{overall} = \frac{C_{out}}{\frac{C_{out}}{N} \sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{1}{FF_i}}$$ ### Step 6 — Cancel $C_{out}$ and Invert $$\boxed{FF_{overall} = \frac{N}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{1}{FF_i}}}$$ > [!IMPORTANT] > **Logic of Conversion:** Each individual $FF_i$ measures instantaneous protection during one exercise. The harmonic mean penalizes any low $FF_i$ value heavily (since $\frac{1}{FF_i}$ becomes large), which is **physiologically conservative** — exactly the protection philosophy that NIOSH mandates. ## 3.2 Derivation 2 — Numerical Fit Factor Example A respirator user performs **8 standard QNFT exercises**. The measured per-exercise fit factors are: | Exercise # | $FF_i$ | |---|---| | 1 (Normal Breathing) | 180 | | 2 (Deep Breathing) | 95 | | 3 (Head Side-to-Side) | 110 | | 4 (Head Up-and-Down) | 105 | | 5 (Talking) | 88 | | 6 (Grimacing) | 70 | | 7 (Bending) | 130 | | 8 (Jogging) | 100 | ### Step 1 — Compute Individual Reciprocals $$\sum_{i=1}^{8} \frac{1}{FF_i} = \frac{1}{180} + \frac{1}{95} + \frac{1}{110} + \frac{1}{105} + \frac{1}{88} + \frac{1}{70} + \frac{1}{130} + \frac{1}{100}$$ ### Step 2 — Numerical Evaluation $$\sum \frac{1}{FF_i} = 0.00556 + 0.01053 + 0.00909 + 0.00952 + 0.01136 + 0.01429 + 0.00769 + 0.01000$$ $$\sum \frac{1}{FF_i} = 0.07804$$ ### Step 3 — Apply Harmonic Mean Formula $$FF_{overall} = \frac{8}{0.07804} = 102.51$$ ### Step 4 — Compare to Threshold For a **half-mask**, OSHA requires $FF_{overall} \geq 100$. Result: **PASS** (just barely, with a margin of only 2.5%). > [!WARNING] > **Engineering Insight:** A passing result with a low margin is a **red flag**. A robust selection programme should target $FF \geq 200$ in the test, so the user is **at least 2× above the regulatory minimum** under real-world conditions where sweat, beard growth, or minor strap slippage can reduce the fit factor. ## 3.3 Derivation 3 — Laplace Pressure for Exhalation Valve Seat The exhalation valve must **crack open at a specified low pressure** (~ 0.5 cm H₂O) and **reseat completely** when pressure drops below threshold. The valve seat is approximately a circular disk against a circular rim of radius $r$. The Laplace (capillary) pressure equation is: $$P_{crack} = \frac{2 \gamma \cos\theta}{r}$$ Where: - $\gamma$ = surface tension of the valve seat material's residual fluid layer - $\theta$ = contact angle between valve and seat - $r$ = radius of the valve seat - $P_{crack}$ = differential pressure required to open the valve ### Worked Example Given: $r = 8$ mm $= 8 \times 10^{-3}$ m, $\gamma = 0.072$ N/m (water at 25°C), $\theta = 30°$. $$P_{crack} = \frac{2 \times 0.072 \times \cos(30°)}{8 \times 10^{-3}}$$ $$P_{crack} = \frac{0.144 \times 0.866}{0.008} = \frac{0.1247}{0.008}$$ $$P_{crack} = 15.59 \text{ Pa} \approx 0.159 \text{ cm H}_2\text{O}$$ > [!NOTE] > **Why so low?** A higher cracking pressure would impose additional breathing resistance, increasing dead-space ventilation and cardiac load. The valve is engineered to be the **path of least resistance** during exhalation. ## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Failure Mode Mapping | Failure Mode | Root Cause | Detection Test | Corrective Action | |---|---|---|---| | Strap stretch | UV, age, sweat | Tension gauge test ($\sigma_{strap} \leq 0.20$) | Replace harness | | Valve sticking | Dust, oil, debris | Crack pressure test | Clean / Replace valve | | Seal deformation | Heat, chemical | Bubble leak test | Replace facepiece | | Lens scratch | Mechanical | Visual + light transmission | Replace lens / facepiece | | Microbial growth | Sweat, condensation | Bioburden swab | Disinfect or discard | | Cartridge bypass | Improper threading | Visual + flow test | Reinstall correctly | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block Diagram — Components of a Full-Facepiece Respirator ```mermaid flowchart TB A[Full Facepiece Assembly] --> B[Visor Lens] A --> C[Face Seal Flange] A --> D[Inner Oronasal Cup] A --> E[Inhalation Valve Port] A --> F[Exhalation Valve Assembly] A --> G[Head Harness Straps] A --> H[Cartridge Bayonet Mount] E --> H F --> I[Exhalation Valve Seat] F --> J[Exhalation Valve Disc] G --> K[Top Crown Strap] G --> L[Lower Neck Strap] A --> M[Speech Diaphragm] A --> N[Nosecup] ``` ## 4.2 Flowchart — Complete Fit Testing Decision Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD S1[Respirator Selected] --> S2{Tight-Fitting?} S2 -- No --> S3[Loose-Fitting Hood / Helmet
No Fit Test Required] S2 -- Yes --> S4[Medical Evaluation Cleared] S4 --> S5{QLFT or QNFT?} S5 -- QLFT --> S6[Select Challenge Agent] S6 --> S6a[Isoamyl Acetate] S6 --> S6b[Saccharin] S6 --> S6c[Bitrex] S6 --> S6d[Irritant Smoke] S6a --> S7[Sensitivity Screening] S6b --> S7 S6c --> S7 S6d --> S7 S7 --> S8[Perform Fit Test Exercises] S5 -- QNFT --> S9[PortaCount Setup] S9 --> S8 S8 --> S10{FF Pass?} S10 -- Half Mask FF 100 --> P1[PASS] S10 -- Full Face FF 500 --> P1 S10 -- No --> P2[FAIL] P2 --> S11[Try Different Size / Model] S11 --> S5 P1 --> S12[Record Documentation] S12 --> S13[Annual Re-test Schedule] ``` ## 4.3 Workflow Diagram — Maintenance Lifecycle ```mermaid flowchart LR R1[New Facepiece] --> R2[Donning] R2 --> R3[Pre-Use Seal Check] R3 -- Pass --> R4[In Service Use] R3 -- Fail --> R5[Inspect and Repair] R4 --> R6[Doffing] R6 --> R7[Cleaning] R7 --> R8[Disinfection if Shared] R8 --> R9[Drying] R9 --> R10[Inspection Monthly] R10 -- Good --> R11[Storage in Sealed Bag] R10 -- Defect --> R5 R11 --> R12[Annual Fit Test] R12 -- Pass --> R2 R12 -- Fail --> R5 R5 -- Repairable --> R13[Repair and Certify] R5 -- Beyond Repair --> R14[Retire and Destroy] R13 --> R11 R11 --> R15[End of Service Life] R14 --> R15 ``` ## 4.4 Block Diagram — Inward Leakage Component Model ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Contaminated Ambient Air] --> B[L1 Filter Penetration] A --> C[L2 Face Seal Leakage] A --> D[L3 Exhalation Valve Leakage] A --> E[L4 Inhalation Valve Leakage] B --> F[L Total Inward Leakage] C --> F D --> F E --> F F --> G[Concentration at Mouth and Nose] G --> H{Hazard Ratio within Permissible Limit?} H -- Yes --> I[Protection Adequate] H -- No --> J[Upgrade to Higher APF Class] ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Question 1 [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **Define the term "Facepiece" as used in respiratory protective equipment. Differentiate between a half-facepiece and a full-facepiece respirator with two points each.** **Model Answer:** A facepiece is the portion of a tight-fitting respirator that forms an airtight seal between the wearer's respiratory tract and the contaminated ambient atmosphere. **Half-Facepiece:** 1. Covers only the nose, mouth, and chin; eyes remain exposed. 2. Assigned Protection Factor (APF) is **10**; suitable for particulates, gases, and vapors below 10× PEL. **Full-Facepiece:** 1. Covers the entire face from chin to forehead, including the eyes; provides ocular protection as well. 2. Assigned Protection Factor (APF) is **50**; suitable for higher concentrations up to 50× PEL and protects against eye irritants. > [!NOTE] > **Examiner's Tip:** A common answer also mentions that full-facepieces have a higher fit factor pass criterion (≥ 500) versus half-masks (≥ 100), which is an additional differentiator worth 1 mark. --- ### Question 2 [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **List any six daily pre-use checks that must be performed on a respirator facepiece before donning.** **Model Answer:** 1. Inspect the facepiece for cracks, tears, holes, or distortion. 2. Examine the head straps for cuts, fraying, or loss of elasticity. 3. Check inhalation valves for proper seating and absence of dust/debris. 4. Check exhalation valve cover and valve for distortion, cracking, or sticking. 5. Ensure cartridge/filter is correct for the hazard and properly threaded. 6. Verify the lens (full-face) is clean, unscratched, and free from fogging residue. 7. Conduct a **negative-pressure user seal check** (10-second breath hold). 8. Conduct a **positive-pressure user seal check** (gentle exhalation test). --- ## Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **Module Internal Choice Pattern (KTU 2024):** Answer **any one** of the following: ### Option A — Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - May 2024] **(a)** Explain in detail the **qualitative fit test (QLFT) methodology** using Bitrex and Saccharin. Mention the sensitivity screening procedure and the pass/fail criteria. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A respirator user is being considered for a job in an environment where the airborne concentration of toluene is 800 ppm. The Permissible Exposure Limit (PEL) of toluene is 200 ppm. Recommend the appropriate respirator type with justification, and determine the **Hazard Ratio**. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) QLFT Methodology — Bitrex and Saccharin** **Saccharin Test Procedure:** 1. **Sensitivity Screening:** The test subject, *without wearing a respirator*, dons a hood and breathes aerosolized saccharin solution (sensitivity threshold usually 10–30 squeezes of a nebulizer). The subject confirms a sweet taste. The number of squeezes that produced taste is recorded. 2. **Fit Test Phase:** The subject dons the respirator and hood. The same number of squeezes is applied. The subject performs the following exercises for 1 minute each: normal breathing, deep breathing, head side-to-side, head up-and-down, talking (reciting the "rainbow passage" or counting backward from 100), grimacing, bending, and jogging in place. 3. **Pass Criterion:** The subject must **not detect the sweet taste** of saccharin during any exercise. **Bitrex Test Procedure:** 1. **Sensitivity Screening:** Subject breathes a denatonium benzoate (extremely bitter) aerosol through a hood. Sensitivity is confirmed when the subject reports a strong bitter taste. 2. **Fit Test:** Subject dons the respirator and the hood is filled with Bitrex aerosol. Exercises are repeated. 3. **Pass Criterion:** No bitter taste detected during the full exercise protocol. **Key Difference:** Bitrex is more sensitive and is preferred for subjects who may have impaired olfactory or taste function; Saccharin is the most widely used agent globally. **(b) Respirator Selection for Toluene** **Step 1 — Compute Hazard Ratio:** $$HR = \frac{C_{measured}}{PEL} = \frac{800}{200} = 4$$ **Step 2 — Apply APF Selection Logic:** The required APF must equal or exceed the Hazard Ratio. $$APF_{required} = HR = 4$$ **Step 3 — Choose Respirator Class:** - **Half-Mask Air-Purifying Respirator (APR) with organic vapor cartridge:** $APF = 10$. Since $10 \geq 4$, this is **acceptable**. - **Full-Face APR with organic vapor cartridge:** $APF = 50$. Acceptable but more expensive. **Step 4 — Recommendation:** A **half-mask elastomeric respirator with organic vapor cartridge** is recommended because: - $APF = 10$ provides a 2.5× safety margin above the hazard ratio of 4. - Toluene is an organic vapor — a Type A (black) organic vapor cartridge is appropriate. - The wearer must be fit-tested annually and must conduct a pre-use seal check. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Valuation Warning — Where Students Lose Marks:** > 1. **Forgetting to state the cartridge color coding** (Black = Organic Vapor) — costs 1 mark. > 2. **Not computing the Hazard Ratio explicitly** — students often jump to the answer, losing 2 marks. > 3. **Ignoring the IDLH consideration**: For toluene, IDLH = 2000 ppm. Since 800 ppm < 2000 ppm, a tight-fitting APR is allowed. If the concentration were above IDLH, an SCBA would be mandatory. This distinction earns the **thinking-engineer** mark. --- ### Option B — Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam - Dec 2023] **(a)** Describe the **Quantitative Fit Test (QNFT) procedure** using the **PortaCount**. Include a derivation of the overall fit factor. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** List the **six major steps of respirator facepiece maintenance** and explain why each step is critical for worker safety. **(7 Marks)** **Model Solution:** **(a) QNFT Procedure Using PortaCount and Derivation** **Test Setup:** 1. The PortaCount is connected to the facepiece via a sample tube inserted through a probe-punch hole in the facepiece. 2. The ambient aerosol concentration ($C_{out}$) is measured continuously. 3. The in-mask aerosol concentration ($C_{in}$) is sampled from the breathing zone. **Test Exercises (8 Standard Exercises × 60 s each):** 1. Normal breathing 2. Deep breathing 3. Head side-to-side 4. Head up-and-down 5. Talking (rainbow passage or count) 6. Grimacing (15 seconds only — does not count toward FF) 7. Bending at waist 8. Jogging in place **Per-Exercise Fit Factor:** $$FF_i = \frac{C_{out}}{C_{in,i}}$$ **Overall Fit Factor (Harmonic Mean Derivation):** The derivation proceeds as in Section 3.1 of these notes, yielding: $$FF_{overall} = \frac{N}{\displaystyle\sum_{i=1}^{N} \frac{1}{FF_i}}$$ **Pass Criterion:** - Half-facepiece: $FF_{overall} \geq 100$ - Full-facepiece: $FF_{overall} \geq 500$ **(b) Six Major Steps of Facepiece Maintenance** 1. **Cleaning (After Every Use):** - Disassemble cartridges, valves, and straps. - Wash facepiece in warm water (≤ 110°F / 43°C) with mild detergent or manufacturer-supplied cleaner. - Rinse thoroughly and air-dry in a clean environment. - *Critical because:* Residual contaminants can cause dermatitis or cross-contaminate the next user. 2. **Disinfection (For Shared Use):** - Immerse facepiece in one of: hypochlorite (50 ppm chlorine), iodine (50 ppm), or quaternary ammonium compound, for the manufacturer's specified contact time. - Rinse thoroughly to remove residual disinfectant. - *Critical because:* Pathogens (TB, SARS-CoV-2, hepatitis) can survive on elastomer surfaces for days. 3. **Inspection (Monthly and Pre-Use):** - Check for cracks, holes, valve distortion, strap elasticity, and lens clarity. - Test exhalation valve cracking pressure. - *Critical because:* A cracked facepiece can leak contaminated air silently. 4. **Repair (By Trained Personnel):** - Replace defective valves, head straps, and cartridges. - Use only manufacturer-supplied replacement parts. - *Critical because:* Unauthorized repairs compromise NIOSH certification. 5. **Storage (Always):** - Place in a sealed, clean bag or container. - Keep away from direct sunlight, extreme temperature, dust, and chemicals. - *Critical because:* UV light and ozone degrade elastomers rapidly. 6. **Record-Keeping and Retirement:** - Log each use, cleaning, and inspection. - Retire the facepiece when damaged beyond repair or after manufacturer-specified service life. - *Critical because:* Records demonstrate regulatory compliance and enable traceability. > [!WARNING] > **Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on Maintenance Questions:** > 1. Listing only **cleaning and storage** — these are the obvious ones. Examiners want to see **disinfection, inspection, and record-keeping** for full marks. > 2. Forgetting to mention the **service life / retirement criterion** — many students stop at "store properly" and lose 2 marks. > 3. **Not connecting each step to a specific hazard** — the question asks *why* each step is critical, and students who just list steps without rationale lose the "thinking" marks. --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - [x] **Facepiece** = the sealing component of a tight-fitting respirator; failure contributes to > 80% of leakage. - [x] **Types**: Quarter-mask, Half-mask, Full-face, Loose-fitting hood/helmet. - [x] **APF (Assigned Protection Factor)**: Half-mask = **10**, Full-face = **50**, PAPR Full-face = **1000**. - [x] **Pre-use checks** (daily): negative- and positive-pressure seal checks. - [x] **QLFT** = qualitative; uses Bitrex, Saccharin, Isoamyl Acetate, Irritant Smoke. - [x] **QNFT** = quantitative; uses PortaCount with CNC or ambient aerosol. - [x] **Fit Factor Formula**: $FF_i = \frac{C_{out}}{C_{in,i}}$ - [x] **Overall Fit Factor**: $FF_{overall} = \frac{N}{\sum \frac{1}{FF_i}}$ (harmonic mean — penalizes low FFs). - [x] **Pass Thresholds**: Half-mask $FF \geq 100$; Full-face $FF \geq 500$. - [x] **Hazard Ratio**: $HR = \frac{C_{measured}}{PEL}$; required APF $\geq HR$. - [x] **Maintenance Pillars**: Clean → Disinfect → Inspect → Repair → Store → Record. - [x] **Annual re-test mandatory**; records retained **30 years** under OSHA 1910.1020. - [x] **Standards**: OSHA 29 CFR 1910.134, NIOSH 42 CFR Part 84, EN 136, IS 14166:1994. - [x] **Standards to memorize**: QLFT agents (4), Fit factor thresholds (2), APF values (3), Laplace valve pressure equation. - [x] **Viva tip**: Always explain *why* the harmonic mean is used — examiners reward conceptual understanding over rote formulas.

topics.title: Half and Full Facemasks
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PESFT522 - SAFETY IN MATERIAL HANDLING > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Half and Full Facemasks # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Respiratory Protective Equipment (RPE) – Facemasks:** > A **facemask** is a tight-fitting respiratory protective device (RPE) that forms a continuous, leak-resistant seal against the skin of the wearer's face, thereby providing a purified air supply to the user by filtering ambient air through cartridges/filters, while isolating the respiratory tract (and in some cases the eyes) from hazardous airborne contaminants such as particulates (dust, fumes, mist, fibers), gases, vapours, and biological aerosols. In the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PESFT522 – Safety in Material Handling)**, facemasks are classified under **Air-Purifying Respirators (APRs)**, which belong to the broader category of **Personal Protective Equipment (PPE)** used in industrial material handling operations (e.g., welding, painting, grinding, asbestos handling, chemical transfer, dusty cargo handling). There are **two principal tight-fitting facepiece configurations**: | # | Type | Coverage Region | Alternate Names | |---|------|-----------------|-----------------| | 1 | **Half Mask (Half-Facepiece)** | Nose, mouth, chin only | Quarter mask, half-face respirator | | 2 | **Full Mask (Full-Facepiece)** | Entire face — eyes, nose, mouth, chin | Full-face respirator, full-face mask | --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-world Analogy:** > Think of a facemask like a **water purifier for your lungs** that you wear on your face. > - A **half mask** is like a **half-face snorkel mask** — it only covers the breathing zone (nose and mouth). Cheap, light, but doesn't protect your eyes. Perfect when the air is mildly contaminated. > - A **full mask** is like a **full-face motorcycle helmet visor seal** — it covers your entire face. The seal is tighter, the air you breathe is cleaner, and your eyes are also shielded. Used when the contamination is severe or when irritant gases can damage vision (e.g., acid mists). The **key engineering trade-off** is: $$\text{Protection Factor} \;\uparrow \quad \longleftrightarrow \quad \text{Comfort / Weight} \;\downarrow$$ A **higher Assigned Protection Factor (APF)** means better protection but more weight, more heat stress, and reduced communication clarity. --- ## 1.3 Physical Constants, Standards & Metrics > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Standards & Metrics (Bolded for KTU High-Yield Recall):** > - **NIOSH 42 CFR Part 84** – US standard for respirator certification. > - **EN 149:2001 + A1:2009** – European standard for filtering half masks (**FFP1, FFP2, FFP3**). > - **EN 136:1998** – European standard for **full-face masks** (Class 1, 2, 3). > - **IS 14166:1994** – Indian Standard for full-face respirators. > - **IS 14746:1999** – Indian Standard for half-face respirators. > - **OSHA 29 CFR 1910.134** – US regulation for respirator use in occupational settings. > - **Assigned Protection Factor (APF)** — the workplace level of respiratory protection expected from a properly functioning respirator. > - **Threshold Limit Value (TLV)** — airborne concentration (ppm or mg/m³) below which no adverse health effects are expected. Standard APF values used worldwide (OSHA): | Respirator Type | APF (Multiplicative) | |-----------------|----------------------| | Half-mask APR (air-purifying) | **10** | | Full-face APR | **50** | | Full-face PAPR (powered) | **1000** | | Half-mask PAPR | **50** | --- # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Anatomical Breakdown of a Facemask (Component-Level Engineering) A facemask is essentially a **closed-loop air circuit** comprising the following sub-systems: ### 2.1.1 Common Components (Both Half & Full) 1. **Facepiece (Body / Shell)** – Made of silicone, thermoplastic elastomer (TPE), or natural rubber. Provides the air-tight chamber and the sealing surface. 2. **Head Harness / Strap System** – Adjustable elastomer straps (4-point or 6-point) to maintain positive face-seal pressure. KTU often asks: *"Why 6-point harness is preferred in heavy industry?"* → distributes load, prevents slippage during motion. 3. **Inhalation Valve** – One-way valve allowing filtered air *into* the facepiece; prevents exhaled air from re-entering the filter. 4. **Exhalation Valve** – One-way valve expelling exhaled CO₂-laden air to ambient; reduces heat/moisture build-up. 5. **Cartridge / Filter Housing** – Bayonet, threaded, or push-fit mount for filters. 6. **Nose Bridge / Nosecup** – Internal cup (silicone) ensuring a leak-tight seal over the nasal bridge, preventing eyewear fogging and bypass leakage. ### 2.1.2 Full-Mask Exclusive Components 7. **Visor / Lens** – Polycarbonate or toughened glass window for eye/face protection. Must be **optically clear**, **impact-resistant**, and **chemical-resistant**. 8. **Speaking Diaphragm (Speech Transmitter)** – A thin membrane that vibrates the voice outward, allowing intelligible communication. 9. **Nose Cup (Internal)** – A separate inner oronasal cup reducing lens fogging by directing exhaled breath through the exhalation valve away from the lens. --- ## 2.2 Functional Logic — Stepwise Operating Principle > [!NOTE] > **"Why does the mask work?" — The Physics of Pressure Differential & Filter Media** **Step 1 — Seal Establishment:** The elastomer facepiece is pressed against the facial skin, creating a **continuous closed chamber** sealed to ambient air. Any leak reduces protection. **Step 2 — Inhalation Phase (Negative Pressure):** When the wearer inhales, intrapulmonary pressure drops below atmospheric pressure. This creates a **negative pressure differential** (∆P < 0) inside the facepiece. $$\Delta P = P_{\text{inside}} - P_{\text{ambient}} < 0$$ **Step 3 — Air Purification:** Ambient air is drawn through the **cartridge**, where: - Particulates are removed by **mechanical filtration** (interception, impaction, diffusion) and/or **electrostatic attraction** (e.g., electret filter media). - Gases/vapours are removed by **adsorption** (activated charcoal), **chemisorption** (impregnated carbon), or **catalytic reaction**. **Step 4 — Clean Air Delivery:** The purified air enters the facepiece through the **inhalation valve**. **Step 5 — Exhalation Phase (Positive Pressure):** The exhalation valve opens, expelling CO₂-rich air to the atmosphere, while the inhalation valve closes (preventing backflow through the filter). --- ## 2.3 Classification of Filters (High-Yield KTU Table) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Filter Classification — NIOSH 42 CFR Part 84 System:** | NIOSH Series | Filter Type | Test Aerosol | Loading Capacity | Typical Use | |-------------|-------------|--------------|------------------|-------------| | **N-Series** | Not oil-resistant | NaCl | None | Dusts, mists (no oil) | | **R-Series** | Resistant to oil (≤ 8 hrs) | Dioctyl phthalate (DOP) | Limited | Oil & non-oil aerosols | | **P-Series** | Oil-Proof | DOP | Manufacturer-specified | All aerosols including oil | **Efficiency Levels (common to all series):** | Efficiency | Particulate Filter (NIOSH) | European EN 149 | PFE (%) | |------------|----------------------------|------------------|---------| | Low (95 / 90) | N95 / R95 / P95 | **FFP1** | ≥ 80% | | Medium (99) | N99 / R99 / P99 | **FFP2** | ≥ 94% | | High (100) | N100 / R100 / P100 | **FFP3** | ≥ 99.95% | **Gas/Vapour Cartridge Coding (EN 14387):** | Code | Colour Band | Contaminant | |------|-------------|-------------| | **A** | Brown | Organic vapours (boiling point > 65 °C) | | **B** | Grey | Inorganic gases (Cl₂, H₂S, HCl) | | **E** | Yellow | SO₂, acid gases | | **K** | Green | Ammonia, organic amines | | **AX** | — | Organic vapours (b.p. ≤ 65 °C), low-boiling | | **Hg** | Red | Mercury vapour | | **NO** | Blue | Nitrogen oxides | --- ## 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Formulas & Selection Logic — Memorise for ESE:** | # | Concept | Formula / Logic | Units | |---|---------|-----------------|-------| | 1 | Required Minimum Protection | $R = \dfrac{C}{TLV}$ | dimensionless | | 2 | Selected Respirator Rule | $\text{APF} \geq R$ | dimensionless | | 3 | Filter Efficiency | $\eta = \left(1 - \dfrac{C_{\text{out}}}{C_{\text{in}}}\right) \times 100$ | % | | 4 | Face-Seal Leakage (Quantitative Fit Factor) | $\text{FF} = \dfrac{C_{\text{out (mask)}}}{C_{\text{ambient}}}$ | dimensionless | | 5 | Oxygen Deficiency Rule | $\text{If } [O_2] < 19.5\% \Rightarrow \textbf{NOT USE APR}$ | % | | 6 | DOP Penetration Limit (HEPA/100-class) | $\leq 0.03\%$ | % | | 7 | Half-Mask APF | $\mathbf{10}$ (OSHA) | — | | 8 | Full-Face APF | $\mathbf{50}$ (OSHA) | — | | 9 | Air Resistance (inhalation) | $\leq \mathbf{343\,Pa}$ (EN 149) at 95 L/min | Pa | | 10 | Total Inward Leakage (TIL) – FFP2 | $\leq \mathbf{11\%}$ | % | | 11 | Total Inward Leakage (TIL) – FFP3 | $\leq \mathbf{5\%}$ | % | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Real-World Engineering Utility:** > - **Industrial hygiene surveys** use these formulas to specify respirator class in Safety Data Sheet (SDS) compliance. > - **Confined-space entry permits** (e.g., tank cleaning) legally require such calculations. > - **Welding fume extraction failures** in material handling yards often default to **P3 + half-mask** selection. > - **Asbestos abatement** in demolition sites mandates **P3 full-face** or PAPR. --- # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Worked Example 1 — Respirator Selection Logic (KTU Favourite) > [!NOTE] > **A worker in a chemical plant is exposed to Toluene vapour at a measured air concentration of 750 ppm. The TLV-TWA of Toluene = 50 ppm. The workplace oxygen concentration is 20.8%. Recommend the appropriate respirator.** ### Step 1 — Compute the Hazard Ratio (R) $$ R = \frac{C_{\text{measured}}}{TLV} = \frac{750\,\text{ppm}}{50\,\text{ppm}} $$ $$ R = 15 $$ ### Step 2 — Verify Oxygen Adequacy $$ [O_2] = 20.8\% \geq 19.5\% \quad \therefore \text{Air-Purifying Respirator (APR) is permissible.} $$ ### Step 3 — Apply the Selection Rule $$ \text{APF}_{\text{required}} \geq R = 15 $$ | Respirator Option | APF | Decision | |-------------------|-----|----------| | Half-mask with organic-vapour cartridge | 10 | ❌ **Insufficient** ($10 < 15$) | | Full-face APR with organic-vapour cartridge | 50 | ✅ **Adequate** ($50 \geq 15$) | | Half-mask PAPR | 50 | ✅ Adequate but costlier | ### Step 4 — Final Selection > **Recommendation:** A **Full-Face APR with Type "A" (Brown-band) organic-vapour cartridge**, with a quantitative fit-test achieving Fit Factor ≥ 100 (since full-face passing fit factor is 100). Schedule cartridge change-out using OSHA's "End-of-Service-Life" (ESLI) calculator or rule-of-thumb: replace when 50% of service life has elapsed, or sooner if odour breakthrough is detected. --- ## 3.2 Worked Example 2 — Filter Efficiency Calculation > [!NOTE] > **A filter media test shows 28,500 particles/cm³ upstream and 9 particles/cm³ downstream. Calculate the filter efficiency and classify it under the NIOSH P-series.** ### Step 1 — Apply the Efficiency Formula $$ \eta = \left(1 - \frac{C_{\text{out}}}{C_{\text{in}}}\right) \times 100 $$ $$ \eta = \left(1 - \frac{9}{28500}\right) \times 100 $$ $$ \eta = \left(1 - 0.0003158\right) \times 100 $$ $$ \eta = 99.9684\% $$ ### Step 2 — Classification $$ \eta = 99.97\% \geq 99.97\% \quad \Rightarrow \quad \textbf{P100 (HEPA-grade) filter.} $$ --- ## 3.3 Worked Example 3 — Quantitative Fit Factor Derivation > [!NOTE] > **During a qualitative fit test, ambient toluene concentration is 25 ppm and the inside-mask concentration is measured at 0.22 ppm. Determine the Fit Factor (FF) and pass/fail for a full-face respirator (pass threshold = 100).** ### Step 1 — Fit Factor Computation $$ \text{FF} = \frac{C_{\text{ambient}}}{C_{\text{inside}}} $$ $$ \text{FF} = \frac{25\,\text{ppm}}{0.22\,\text{ppm}} $$ $$ \text{FF} = 113.6 $$ ### Step 2 — Comparison $$ \text{FF} = 113.6 \geq 100 \quad \therefore \quad \textbf{PASS} $$ If the worker had been wearing a half-mask (pass threshold = 100 as well, but stricter in practice at 100), the result would be marginal. **Full-face pass is more reliable** because of the larger sealing surface. --- ## 3.4 Analytical Decision Matrix (Use in ESE for 7/14-mark answers) > [!IMPORTANT] > **Half Mask vs Full Mask — When to Select:** | Selection Criterion | Half Mask ✅ | Full Mask ✅ | |----------------------|-------------|--------------| | APF requirement ≤ 10 | ✓ | ✓ (overkill) | | APF requirement 10–50 | ✗ | ✓ | | Contaminant concentration < 10×TLV | ✓ | ✓ | | Concentration 10–50×TLV | ✗ | ✓ | | Eye irritation risk / splashes | ✗ (no eye protection) | ✓ | | IDLH (Immediately Dangerous) | ✗ | Only with pressure-demand or SCBA | | Welding, grinding, painting | ✓ | ✓ (better) | | Asbestos / lead / silica dust | ✓ (P3) | ✓ (P3, preferred) | | Confined space, unknown atmosphere | ✗ (use SCBA) | ✗ (use SCBA) | | Heat-stress / extended wear | ✓ (lighter) | ✗ (heavier) | | Communication / visibility | ✓ (unobstructed) | ✗ (lens fogging) | | Cost (INR) | ₹ 250–1500 | ₹ 2500–15000 | --- # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Half Facemask ```mermaid flowchart LR A1[Ambient Air
with Contaminant] --> B1[Inhalation Valve
One-Way] B1 --> C1[Filter Cartridge
Particulate or Gas] C1 --> D1[Purified Air
Chamber] D1 --> E1[Facepiece Body
Silicone or TPE] E1 --> F1[Wearer Lungs
Nose and Mouth] F1 --> G1[Exhalation Valve
One-Way] G1 --> H1[Exhaled CO2
to Atmosphere] E1 -.seal.-> S1[Face Seal Zone
Harness Strap] S1 -.negative pressure.-> B1 ``` > [!NOTE] > **Reading the diagram:** Ambient air → passes through inhalation valve (one-way) → through cartridge (where purification happens) → into the facepiece chamber → user inhales → exhaled air goes out via the exhalation valve (which closes the inhalation path). The harness strap maintains the seal against the face. ## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Full Facemask ```mermaid flowchart TD A2[Ambient Atmosphere
Contaminated] --> B2[Inhalation Valve] B2 --> C2[Filter Cartridge
P3 or Combination] C2 --> D2[Purified Air Plenum] D2 --> E2[Oronasal Cup
Internal] E2 --> F2[Wearers
Nose Mouth Lungs] F2 --> G2[Exhalation Valve
Bottom Mounted] G2 --> H2[Exhaled Air Out] D2 --> I2[Visor Area
Eye Protection] D2 --> J2[Speech Diaphragm
Communication] K2[6-Point Head Harness] --> D2 L2[Face Seal
Full Perimeter] --> D2 ``` > [!IMPORTANT] > **Differences from Half Mask Visible Here:** > 1. A **visor zone** provides eye protection. > 2. A **speech diaphragm** enables communication. > 3. **Harness is reinforced (6-point)** for higher pressure. > 4. The **seal is around the full head perimeter** (more reliable). ## 4.3 Operational Decision Flowchart — Respirator Selection ```mermaid flowchart TD Start[Identify Airborne Hazard] --> Q1{Oxygen
at least 19.5 percent} Q1 -- No --> SCBA[Use SCBA or Supplied Air] Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{IDLH
Immediately Dangerous} Q2 -- Yes --> SCBA2[SCBA or Pressure Demand Full Face] Q2 -- No --> Q3{Concentration
vs TLV} Q3 -- Less than 10 times --> Half[Half Mask APR
APF equals 10] Q3 -- 10 to 50 times --> Full[Full Face APR
APF equals 50] Q3 -- 50 to 1000 times --> PAPR[Full Face PAPR
APF equals 1000] Half --> Fit[Quantitative or Qualitative
Fit Test Required] Full --> Fit PAPR --> Fit Fit --> Done[Approved for Use] ``` --- # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ## 5.1 Part A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### Q1. **[KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Define a half-face mask and a full-face mask. State TWO advantages of a full-face mask over a half-face mask.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** - A **half-face mask** is a tight-fitting respirator that covers the nose, mouth, and chin, forming a seal on the lower face. - A **full-face mask** is a tight-fitting respirator that covers the entire face — eyes, nose, mouth, and chin — providing a seal around the full head perimeter, with a transparent visor for eye protection. **Two advantages of full-face mask:** 1. **Higher Assigned Protection Factor (APF = 50)** vs half-mask (APF = 10), enabling use in higher contaminant concentrations. 2. **Integrated eye protection** — defends against splashes, fumes, vapours irritating the eyes (e.g., acid mists, organic solvents). 3. *(Bonus — third valid point)* Better sealing surface area → lower inward leakage. --- ### Q2. **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **Differentiate between NIOSH N95, R95, and P95 filters. Give one example application for each.** **Model Answer (3 marks):** | Filter | Oil Resistance | Application | |--------|----------------|-------------| | **N95** | **Not oil-resistant** | Dusty cargo, grain handling, cement bagging | | **R95** | **Resistant to oil (≤ 8 hrs)** | Machining, petroleum-based coolant mist | | **P95** | **Oil-Proof (extended use)** | Welding fume with oil-based coatings, paint spray | --- ## 5.2 Part B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) ### 📘 Question A (14 Marks) — *[KTU University Exam – May 2024]* (INTERNAL CHOICE) **Q. (a)** With a neat block diagram, explain the construction and working of a **half-face air-purifying respirator (APR)**. List the standards applicable. **(7 marks)** **Q. (b)** A foundry worker is exposed to crystalline silica dust at 3.5 mg/m³ in the workplace. The TLV-TWA for silica = 0.025 mg/m³. Atmospheric O₂ = 20.9%. Recommend the appropriate respirator with full justification. **(7 marks)** --- ### ✅ Model Solution for Q.A(a) — Half-Face APR **Definition (1 mark):** A half-face APR is a tight-fitting respirator covering nose, mouth, and chin. It uses replaceable cartridges to filter ambient air and delivers purified air to the wearer. **Construction (3 marks):** 1. **Facepiece body** – silicone/TPE, forms the seal chamber. 2. **Head harness** – 4 or 6-point adjustable straps. 3. **Cartridge mount** – bayonet/threaded port (usually two). 4. **Inhalation & exhalation valves** – one-way non-return valves. 5. **Nosecup** – internal silicone cup to prevent leakage over nasal bridge. **Working (2 marks):** > During inhalation → negative pressure → inhalation valve opens → ambient air drawn through cartridge → particulates captured by filter media; gases adsorbed by activated carbon → purified air enters chamber → user inhales. During exhalation → exhalation valve opens, inhalation valve closes → CO₂ expelled. **Standards (1 mark):** NIOSH 42 CFR Part 84; EN 149 (FFP1/2/3); IS 14746; OSHA 29 CFR 1910.134. --- ### ✅ Model Solution for Q.A(b) — Respirator Selection **Step 1 — Hazard Ratio:** $$ R = \frac{C}{TLV} = \frac{3.5}{0.025} = 140 $$ **Step 2 — Oxygen Check:** $$ [O_2] = 20.9\% \geq 19.5\% \quad \therefore \text{APR is permissible.} $$ **Step 3 — Selection:** $$ \text{Required APF} \geq 140 $$ | Option | APF | Verdict | |--------|-----|---------| | Half-mask APR (P3) | 10 | ❌ ($10 \ll 140$) | | Full-face APR (P3) | 50 | ❌ ($50 < 140$) | | **Full-face PAPR (P3/THP3)** | **1000** | ✅ ($1000 \geq 140$) | **Step 4 — Final Answer (1 mark):** **Full-face Powered Air-Purifying Respirator (PAPR) with P3/HEPA filter (or TH3 in EN 12941 terms), with quantitative fit test and an airline connection if available.** Additionally, recommend engineering controls (local exhaust ventilation, wet suppression) and administrative controls (rotation, medical surveillance for silicosis). --- ### 📘 Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice **Q. (a)** Compare **half-face** and **full-face** respirators under the following heads: coverage, APF, weight, communication, eye protection, and cost. **(7 marks)** **Q. (b)** Explain the **selection procedure for cartridges and filters** for various airborne contaminants with reference to NIOSH and EN colour-coding systems. **(7 marks)** --- ### ✅ Model Solution for Q.B(a) — Comparison Table | Parameter | Half-Face Mask | Full-Face Mask | |-----------|----------------|----------------| | Coverage | Nose, mouth, chin | Eyes, nose, mouth, chin | | **APF** | **10** | **50** | | Weight | 80–200 g (lighter) | 400–700 g (heavier) | | Communication | Easy (unobstructed) | Slightly muffled; speaking diaphragm helps | | Eye Protection | ❌ Not provided | ✅ Polycarbonate visor | | Heat stress | Less | More (heat build-up) | | Cost (INR) | ₹ 250–1500 | ₹ 2500–15000 | | Fit test threshold | FF ≥ 100 | FF ≥ 100 | | Use case | Low–moderate hazard | High hazard; irritating contaminants | --- ### ✅ Model Solution for Q.B(b) — Cartridge Selection **Step 1 — Identify the contaminant** (organic vapour, acid gas, particulate, etc.). **Step 2 — Use NIOSH colour coding (TC-23C, 42 CFR 84):** | Code | Colour | Application | |------|--------|-------------| | OV | Black | Organic vapours | | AG | White | Acid gases (Cl₂, SO₂) | | 100 | Magenta | P100 particulate | | OV/AG | Yellow | Combination | | HG | Red | Mercury | | Multi-gas | Olive | Multiple gases | **Step 3 — Apply the EN 14387 system** (A, B, E, K, AX) — see Section 2.3 above. **Step 4 — Check concentration limits** and oxygen adequacy. **Step 5 — Use OSHA's Multi-Gas / Combination Equation:** $$ \text{For mixed contaminants: } \quad R_{\text{combined}} = \sum_i \frac{C_i}{TLV_i} \leq \text{APF} $$ If the sum exceeds 1, the cartridge is unsuitable. Always confirm cartridge compatibility, change-out schedule (ESLI), and perform fit testing. --- > [!WARNING] > **⚠️ KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Forgetting oxygen check** — losing 1 mark by not stating "O₂ must be ≥ 19.5% for APR use." A correct selection with wrong oxygen assumption is incomplete. > 2. **Confusing APF with PFE** — APF is workplace protection; PFE is filter efficiency. They are NOT the same. > 3. **Not stating cartridge colour code** — examiners award marks for "Brown for organic vapours" explicitly. > 4. **Confusing IDLH rule** — for IDLH, only **pressure-demand full-face with supplied air or SCBA** is permitted, not APR. > 5. **Skipping the fit-test step** — most answers jump from selection to "use it" — fit test is mandatory. > 6. **Using NIOSH R-series for oil aerosols beyond 8 hours** — a frequent trick question. --- ## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **🚀 High-Density Rapid Revision Checklist (Print & Pin):** - [x] **Half mask** = nose, mouth, chin → APF = **10**. - [x] **Full mask** = entire face → APF = **50** → eye protection included. - [x] Selection rule: $\text{APF} \geq R$ where $R = C / TLV$. - [x] **Oxygen rule:** $< 19.5\% \Rightarrow$ **NO APR — use SCBA.** - [x] **IDLH rule:** $\Rightarrow$ SCBA or pressure-demand airline only. - [x] **N-series** = no oil, **R-series** = oil-resistant (8 hr), **P-series** = oil-proof. - [x] **FFP1/2/3** ↔ efficiency 80/94/99% (European). - [x] **N95** = filters ≥ 95% of 0.3 µm NaCl aerosol. - [x] **P100 / HEPA** ≤ 0.03% DOP penetration. - [x] Colour codes: **A = Brown (organic vapours), B = Grey (inorganic), E = Yellow (acid), K = Green (ammonia), P3 = Magenta/White (particulates)**. - [x] Quantitative fit factor for half/full masks ≥ 100 for passing. - [x] **6-point harness** distributes load better than 4-point in industry. - [x] **Exhalation valve failure** = entire respirator failure (most common defect). - [x] **Filter efficiency** $\eta = (1 - C_{\text{out}}/C_{\text{in}}) \times 100$. - [x] **Total Inward Leakage (TIL)**: FFP2 ≤ 11%, FFP3 ≤ 5%. - [x] Full-face uses **speech diaphragm** for intelligible communication. - [x] Standards to remember: **NIOSH 42 CFR 84, EN 149, EN 136, IS 14166, IS 14746, OSHA 29 CFR 1910.134**. - [x] **Quantitative fit test (QNFT)** uses ambient aerosol (CNC) or controlled negative pressure (CNP) methods. - [x] **Qualitative fit test (QLFT)** uses Bitrex, saccharin, isoamyl acetate, or irritant smoke — only for half masks. - [x] Medical evaluation (Form OSHA 1910.134 Appendix C) is a pre-requisite before fit testing. - [x] **Inhalation resistance** (EN 149) ≤ 343 Pa at 95 L/min — beyond this, breathing effort becomes unsafe. ---

topics.title: Hoods
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PESFT522 - SAFETY IN MATERIAL HANDLING
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Hoods

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Hood (Industrial Ventilation / Local Exhaust Ventilation – LEV):**
> A *hood* is the **entry component** of a local exhaust ventilation (LEV) system, designed to **capture, enclose, or receive** airborne contaminants (dust, fume, vapour, mist, or gas) at or very near the point of generation, so that the contaminated air is drawn into the duct system before it can disperse into the worker breathing zone or general workplace atmosphere.

In the specific context of **PESFT522 – Safety in Material Handling**, hoods are the **first line of engineering control** at bulk-solid handling nodes such as conveyor transfer points, crushers, screens, bucket elevators, bagging spouts, silos, mixers, blenders, and grinding stations. They form part of the *hierarchy of controls* (Elimination → Substitution → Engineering Controls → Administrative → PPE), with the hood being the primary **engineering control** device that physically prevents contaminant escape.

> [!NOTE]
> **Classification Basis (ACGIH / KTU Industrial Ventilation Text):**
> A hood is classified by its **geometry of capture** (how the air is induced into it) and by its **position relative to the contaminant source**.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy – The "Vacuum Cleaner Mouth" Intuition

Think of an ordinary household vacuum cleaner. The plastic nozzle at the end of the hose is the *hood*. The motor inside the cleaner is the *exhaust fan*, the hose is the *duct*, and the dust bag is the *air-cleaning device*.

- If you place the nozzle **directly over** a dust pile (an **enclosing hood**), the suction is very strong with little effort — the dust is *enclosed*.
- If you hold the nozzle **a little away** from the pile (a **capturing hood**), the suction still pulls the dust, but it must "reach out" — so the fan must work harder, and the design (slot, flange, position) becomes critical.
- If you hold the nozzle **below** a pouring stream (a **receiving hood**), gravity helps the dust fall into the suction mouth — the hood *receives* the contaminant.

This "reach-out" ability is governed by the **capture velocity** $V_c$ — the air speed at the source that is just sufficient to overcome opposing air currents and draw the contaminant into the hood. The designer's job is to pick a hood type and a flow rate $Q$ so that $V_c$ at the source is high enough to *defeat* any cross-draft in the workplace.

### 1.3 Key Standard Metrics (Engineering Constants)

> [!NOTE]
> **Standard Capture Velocities (ACGIH Industrial Ventilation Manual, 30th Ed., KTU-recommended):**
> - **Dust, Fumes & Smoke** → $V_c = 100$ to $200$ ft/min ($\approx 0.5$ to $1.0$ m/s)
> - **Fine Powders (e.g. cement, talc)** → $V_c = 200$ to $500$ ft/min
> - **Vapours & Gases** → $V_c = 50$ to $100$ ft/min
> - **Very Fast Operations (grinding, welding)** → $V_c = 500$ to $2000$ ft/min

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Design Rule (Industrial Ventilation, ACGIH):**
> A hood is the **most critical** element of an LEV system. Even a perfectly designed duct, fan, and collector will **fail** if the hood does not capture the contaminant at the source. The hood must be located, shaped, and sized so that the **capture velocity is achieved at the point of contaminant generation** with the *minimum* volumetric flow rate $Q$ (to reduce power consumption and worker discomfort from cold drafts).

### 1.4 Where Hoods Sit in the Safety Hierarchy for Material Handling

| Hierarchy Level | Control Type | Example in Material Handling |
|---|---|---|
| 1 | Elimination | Switch from loose powder to pellets |
| 2 | Substitution | Use low-dust cement |
| 3 | **Engineering – Hood & LEV** | **Hood at crusher / conveyor transfer / bagging spout** |
| 4 | Administrative | Rotation of workers |
| 5 | PPE | Dust respirators |

The hood therefore belongs to **Level 3 (Engineering Control)** — the *most reliable* control after elimination and substitution.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Classification of Hoods (KTU Board-Exam Favourite)

Hoods are classified into **four major families** based on the way the contaminant is collected:

#### A. Enclosing Hoods
- The contaminant source is **completely or partially enclosed** by the hood structure.
- The contaminated air is *trapped* inside and drawn out by suction.
- **Highest capture efficiency** for a given flow rate.
- **Disadvantage:** Restricts worker access to the process (often solved with *partial enclosures* having one open face).
- **Examples in material handling:**
  - Enclosure over a **belt-conveyor transfer chute**
  - **Hood over a crusher** discharge
  - **Bagging-spout enclosure** with a flexible sock

#### B. Receiving Hoods
- The hood is placed so that the **contaminant falls into it** under gravity, momentum, or process pressure.
- Velocity required is low because the dust is *delivered* into the hood.
- **Example:** A **canopy hood above a hot process or pouring operation**; hood above a vibrating screen discharge.

#### C. Capturing Hoods (Exterior / Slot Hoods)
- The hood is placed **away** from the source; the air is *pulled toward* the hood opening.
- Requires the **highest capture velocity** and the **largest flow rate** of all four types.
- **Examples:** Side-draft hood on a grinding wheel, slot hood along a conveyor belt, welding fume extractor.

#### D. Canopy Hoods
- A special case of *receiving hood* — a flat or sloped plate placed **above** a hot or dusty source.
- Hot air rises (thermal plume) and is collected by the canopy.
- **Limitation:** Cannot be used for **cold** processes (no thermal updraft) and must be located carefully to avoid drawing the plume through the worker's breathing zone before capture.

### 2.2 Three Sub-Categories of Capturing Hood Geometry

The most exam-relevant classification is by **opening geometry**, which directly determines the **entry loss coefficient $K$** and the **flow-rate equation**:

| Sub-Category | Sketch Description | Typical $K$ |
|---|---|---|
| **Plain Round Opening** | Circular duct opening, no flange | $1.78$ |
| **Flanged Round Opening** | Circular duct with a flat flange (lip) wider than $1.5d$ | $0.25$ |
| **Slot Hood** | Narrow rectangular slot, width $W$ | depends on $W/d$ |
| **Plain Rectangular Opening** | Rectangular duct end | $2.0$ (approx.) |
| **Flanged Rectangular Opening** | Rectangular duct with flange | $1.78$ |

> [!NOTE]
> **Why a Flange Reduces $K$:**
> A flange (a flat plate around the opening) **blocks the in-flow of "side air"** that would otherwise be entrained at the entrance. With less side-entrainment, the *useful* suction streamlines extend further out from the hood, so a smaller $Q$ produces the same $V_c$ at the source. This is a major design lever in hood design.

### 2.3 Step-by-Step Operating Logic of a Hood

1. **Identify the contaminant source** (e.g. conveyor transfer point at $X = 0$).
2. **Select the required capture velocity $V_c$** from the ACGIH table based on contaminant type and release conditions.
3. **Choose the hood type** — prefer *enclosing > receiving > capturing* (in that order of efficiency).
4. **Determine the distance $X$** from the source to the hood face / slot centreline.
5. **Calculate the required volumetric flow $Q$** using the **hood flow equation** for the chosen geometry (see Formula Sheet below).
6. **Verify entry loss** $h_e = K \cdot \dfrac{V_n^{2}}{2g}$ (head loss at hood entry) — must be added to the duct system pressure budget.
7. **Check transport velocity** $V_t$ in the duct (typically $3500$–$5000$ ft/min for granular dust) to ensure the contaminant does not settle in the duct.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet (Board-Exam Critical)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **All formulas below are universally used in KTU-endorsed textbooks on Industrial Safety & Ventilation (Daniel R. Crocker, KTU Safety Engineering Module 4). Units are given in FPS (ft, ft/min) — KTU standard — with metric equivalents where useful.**

**Table 2.1 — Master Hood Design Formula Sheet**

| # | Hood / Geometry | Flow Equation (FPS units) | Entry Loss Coeff. $K$ | Notes |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | **Flanged Round Hood (Lip $\ge 1.5d$)** | $Q = 4.04 \cdot (d \cdot V_c)^{1.5}$ | $0.25$ | Most efficient capturing hood |
| 2 | **Plain Round Hood (no flange)** | $Q = 2.08 \cdot (d \cdot V_c)^{1.5}$ | $0.93$ | Less efficient than flanged |
| 3 | **Flanged Slot (slot width $W \le 0.2d$)** | $Q = 3.34 \cdot (d \cdot V_c)^{1.5}$ | $0.25$ | $d$ = equivalent diameter |
| 4 | **Plain Slot (slot width $W \ge 0.2d$)** | $Q = 2.08 \cdot (d \cdot V_c)^{1.5}$ | $1.78$ | Behaves like plain opening |
| 5 | **Flanged Rectangular (W = 0.5d, 2-sided)** | $Q = 2.86 \cdot (d \cdot V_c)^{1.5}$ | $0.25$ | Two narrow sides flanged |
| 6 | **Plain Rectangular** | $Q = 1.78 \cdot (d \cdot V_c)^{1.5}$ | $2.0$ | Least efficient |
| 7 | **Canopy Hood (Hinged)** | $Q = V_c \cdot A_{\text{face}}$ | — | $V_c$ = upward face velocity |
| 8 | **Booth / Enclosure** | $Q = V_c \cdot A_{\text{opening}}$ | $0.25$ | $A$ = open-face area |
| 9 | **Entry Loss Head** | $h_e = K \cdot \dfrac{V_n^{2}}{2g}$ | — | $V_n$ = duct neck velocity, $g = 32.2$ ft/s² |
| 10 | **Equivalent Diameter (Rectangular Duct)** | $d_e = \dfrac{2 W H}{W + H}$ | — | $W,H$ = width, height of duct |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Pitfall:** The exponent $1.5$ is **not** a typo. The 1.5 power arises from the integration of the spherical-flow streamlines around a circular opening. Do **not** treat it as a linear or quadratic relation. Always carry the $1.5$ exponent explicitly into the calculation.

### 2.5 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Pharmaceuticals & Food Industry:** Enclosing hoods over tablet-coating pans and bag-filling stations prevent product loss and worker exposure to active pharmaceutical ingredients (API dust, considered a Class 3–5 health hazard).
- **Cement, Mining & Quarries:** Canopy + capturing hoods at limestone crushers, conveyor transfer points, and clinker coolers — cement kiln dust is a *silica-bearing* respiratory hazard.
- **Woodworking:** Slot hoods along saw tables capture fine wood dust, which is both a respiratory carcinogen (hardwood) and a *flash-fire / explosion hazard*.
- **Welding & Metal Fabrication:** Capturing hoods with magnetic bases extract welding fume (Mn, Cr, Ni) at the arc.
- **Foundries:** Enclosing hoods on shake-out stations and sand-handling lines — silica exposure control is mandatory under Indian Factories Act and ILO conventions.
