# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

> **[KTU University Exam – May 2024 Model Question]**
>
> **Q1. Define "Proof Load" and state its statutory relationship with Safe Working Load (SWL) for a lifting machine as per Indian Standard 3177.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> Proof Load is the *test load* applied to a lifting machine to verify its structural integrity and confirm that the equipment can withstand loads above its normal operating capacity without permanent deformation. As per **IS 3177**, a new lifting machine shall be subjected to a **static proof load of 1.25 × SWL** (i.e., 125 percent of the Safe Working Load), held for a minimum of 5 minutes. The test confirms elastic behaviour, brake holding ability, and overall fitness for service. (3 marks)

> **[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023 Model Question]**
>
> **Q2. Distinguish between "Preventive Maintenance" and "Predictive Maintenance" with one example each.**
>
> **Model Answer (3 marks):**
> **Preventive Maintenance (PM)** is *time-based* or *usage-based* maintenance carried out at fixed intervals regardless of equipment condition — for example, changing gearbox oil every 3000 hours. **Predictive Maintenance (PdM)** is *condition-based* maintenance triggered by real-time monitoring of equipment health parameters — for example, replacing a bearing when vibration FFT analysis shows characteristic outer race defect frequencies exceeding ISO 10816 Zone B limits. PM is schedule-driven; PdM is data-driven. (3 marks)

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions — Module Internal Choice (14 Marks)

> > [!WARNING]
> > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout:**
> > - Students **commonly lose 2–3 marks** by stating "**SWL is tested at 125%**" without mentioning the **5-minute hold duration** and the requirement to check for **permanent deformation**. Always write: *"hold for 5 min, lower, check for permanent set."*
> > - Another frequent mistake is writing **"PM is better than BM"** without qualifying. Examiners expect you to state **when** each is appropriate.
> > - In NDT questions, students often **omit the method** for non-magnetic materials. Always state: *"Use DPI or ET for aluminium, MPT for steel."*
> > - Always cite the **specific Indian Standard** (IS 3177, IS 2266, IS 4573) for full marks.

---

### **Question A — 14 Marks (Choice 1)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4 — Dec 2024]**
>
> **(a) [7 marks]** A 15-tonne capacity overhead crane in a steel plant is to undergo its annual statutory test. Describe in detail the step-by-step procedure for the annual thorough examination and load test as per the Factories Act, 1948 and IS 3177. What documents must be issued on successful completion?
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** With a neat sketch, explain the **Bathtub Curve** of equipment failure. How does it guide the selection of maintenance intervals?

---

#### Model Answer — Question A(a) [7 marks]

**Step 1: Pre-Test Preparation**
- Notify the Factory Inspector and Chief Inspector at least **7 days in advance** (per Rule 62 of Indian Factories Rules).
- Apply **Lockout-Tagout (LOTO)** to isolate the crane from power supply.
- Clean the crane thoroughly, especially the hook block, wire ropes, brakes, and hoist mechanism.
- Review the **previous test certificate**, maintenance log, and operator complaint register.

**Step 2: Visual and Functional Examination**
- Inspect **wire ropes** for broken wires, kinks, corrosion, and reduction in diameter (discard if > 5% broken in one rope lay).
- Inspect **hooks** for throat opening elongation (> 5% = scrap per IS 3815).
- Inspect **sheaves, drums, and bearings** for wear.
- Check **brake linings, limit switches, overload relays, and safety catches** for proper operation.
- Test **no-load running** to detect abnormal sound, vibration, or oil leakage.

**Step 3: Non-Destructive Testing (NDT)**
- Conduct **Magnetic Particle Testing (MPT)** on the hook shank and load-bearing welds.
- Conduct **Ultrasonic Testing (UT)** on the crane girders and welded joints at critical stress zones.
- Conduct **Liquid Penetrant Inspection (DPI)** on lifting lugs and pin connections.

**Step 4: Static Proof Load Test**
- Calculate the test load:

$$
P_{test} = 1.25 \times \text{SWL} = 1.25 \times 15 \text{ tonnes} = 18.75 \text{ tonnes}
$$

- Use **calibrated test weights** (preferably dead weights, not hydraulic).
- Lift the test load **slightly clear of the ground** and **hold for 5 minutes**.
- Check for any **permanent deformation, cracking, or yielding** in the structure.
- Measure the deflection of the crane girder and verify it returns to zero after load removal.

**Step 5: Dynamic and Brake Test**
- Operate the crane through **one complete cycle** (hoist, traverse, cross-traverse) at the test load.
- Test the **brake holding** by lifting the test load and stopping the hoist mid-air; brake should hold without slippage.
- Test the **limit switches** by lifting the hook to the upper limit; the hoist must auto-stop.

**Step 6: Post-Test Documentation**
- Issue **Certificate of Fitness** in **Form No. 12** of Indian Factories Rules, signed by the **Competent Person**.
- Update the **Crane Register** with test date, results, and next due date.
- File the NDT reports and load test certificate in the equipment history file.

> *(7 marks: 1 mark preparation, 1 mark visual, 1 mark NDT, 2 marks load test procedure with calculation, 1 mark dynamic test, 1 mark documentation)*

---

#### Model Answer — Question A(b) [7 marks]

The **Bathtub Curve** is a reliability engineering concept that plots the **failure rate** of equipment against time. It has three distinct phases:

**Phase 1 — Infant Mortality (Burn-in Period) [2 marks]**
- Failure rate is **high** initially and decreases with time.
- Failures are caused by **manufacturing defects, improper installation, or substandard materials**.
- Examples: bad bearing installation, improperly torqued bolts, defective welds.
- **Mitigation:** Run-in test, burn-in test, commissioning trials, vendor inspection.

**Phase 2 — Useful Life (Normal Operation) [2 marks]**
- Failure rate is **low and constant**.
- Failures are **random** and independent of time (acts of God, operator error, random stress).
- Examples: power surge, foreign object damage, accidental impact.
- **Mitigation:** Preventive Maintenance (PM), operator training, condition monitoring.

**Phase 3 — Wear-out (End of Life) [2 marks]**
- Failure rate **increases rapidly** with time.
- Failures are caused by **fatigue, wear, corrosion, and material degradation**.
- Examples: bearing wear-out, rope fatigue, gear pitting.
- **Mitigation:** Planned replacement, overhaul, refurbishment, or scrap.

**Maintenance Interval Selection [1 mark]**
The optimal PM interval lies at the **inflection point of Phase 2** (just before wear-out begins), determined using reliability data, Weibull analysis, or manufacturer recommendations. The Bathtub Curve is also a **decision tool** for warranty engineering — most products are warranted to cover Phase 1 (infant mortality).

---

### **Question B — 14 Marks (Choice 2)**

> **[KTU University Exam – Model Paper, Module 4 — May 2024]**
>
> **(a) [7 marks]** Compare the **five types of maintenance strategies** (Breakdown, Preventive, Predictive, Corrective, and RCM) in a tabular form. State the conditions under which each strategy is most suitable for material handling equipment.
>
> **(b) [7 marks]** A 10-tonne EOT crane is fitted with a $6 \times 37$ wire rope of **28 mm diameter**. The ultimate tensile strength of the wire is **1770 N/mm²**. Calculate the **Factor of Safety** of the rope. State whether the rope is suitable for **(i)** goods lifting and **(ii)** personnel-carrying applications, citing relevant IS code provisions.

---

#### Model Answer — Question B(a) [7 marks]

**Comparison Table (5 marks):**

| Strategy | Trigger | When to Use | Example in MHE |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Breakdown Maintenance (BM)** | Equipment failure | Non-critical, low-cost, redundant equipment; consumable items | Conveyor idler rollers, light bulbs, small bearings |
| **Preventive Maintenance (PM)** | Time or usage interval | Critical rotating equipment with predictable wear | Gearbox oil change, motor greasing, brake adjustment |
| **Predictive Maintenance (PdM)** | Condition monitoring data exceeds threshold | High-cost, critical, continuous-process equipment | Motor vibration analysis, thermography of bearings |
| **Corrective Maintenance (CM)** | Defect identified during inspection (before failure) | Equipment with periodic inspection regime | Replacing a worn sheave discovered during monthly check |
| **Reliability-Centered Maintenance (RCM)** | FMEA-driven consequence analysis | Safety-critical, regulatory, high-capital equipment | EOT cranes, nuclear fuel handling, aerospace cranes |

**Selection Guidelines (2 marks):**
- For **cranes carrying molten metal** in a steel plant → RCM + PdM (safety critical, continuous operation).
- For **forklifts in a warehouse** → PM + Corrective (medium criticality, scheduled services).
- For **portable hand pallet trucks** → BM (low cost, easy replacement).

---

#### Model Answer — Question B(b) [7 marks]

**Given:**
- SWL (W) = 10 tonnes = 10,000 kg
- Rope construction: $6 \times 37$
- Rope diameter: d = 28 mm
- Wire UTS: $\sigma_{uts}$ = 1770 N/mm²

**Step 1: Convert SWL to Newtons (1 mark)**

$$
W = 10{,}000 \times 9.81 = 98{,}100 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 2: Calculate metallic area of rope (1 mark)**
For $6 \times 37$ construction, fill factor $\approx 0.38$:

$$
A_{metal} = 0.38 \times \dfrac{\pi}{4} \times (28)^2
$$

$$
A_{metal} = 0.38 \times 615.75 = 233.99 \text{ mm}^2
$$

**Step 3: Calculate minimum breaking force (2 marks)**
For $6 \times 37$, breaking efficiency factor = 0.85:

$$
F_{MBF} = 0.85 \times \sigma_{uts} \times A_{metal}
$$

$$
F_{MBF} = 0.85 \times 1770 \times 233.99
$$

$$
F_{MBF} = 0.85 \times 414{,}162
$$

$$
F_{MBF} = 352{,}038 \text{ N}
$$

**Step 4: Calculate Factor of Safety (1 mark)**

$$
\text{FoS} = \dfrac{F_{MBF}}{W} = \dfrac{352{,}038}{98{,}100} = 3.59
$$

**Step 5: Compliance Check (2 marks)**

- **For goods lifting:** IS 2266 / IS 6594 specifies **minimum FoS ≥ 2.0**. Since 3.59 > 2.0, the rope is **SAFE for goods lifting**. ✓
- **For personnel carrying:** IS 2266 specifies **minimum FoS ≥ 5.0** for personnel-carrying hoists. Since 3.59 < 5.0, the rope is **NOT SUITABLE for personnel-carrying applications**. ✗

**Step 6: Recommendation (1 mark)**
For personnel-carrying applications, the rope diameter must be increased to obtain FoS ≥ 5.0. The required breaking force is:

$$
F_{required} = 5.0 \times 98{,}100 = 490{,}500 \text{ N}
$$

The required metallic area is:

$$
A_{required} = \dfrac{490{,}500}{0.85 \times 1770} = 326.0 \text{ mm}^2
$$

The required rope diameter is:

$$
d_{required} = \sqrt{\dfrac{4 \times 326.0}{\pi \times 0.38}} = 33.04 \text{ mm}
$$

Use a **34 mm or 36 mm** rope for personnel-carrying applications.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!TIP]
> **RAPID REVISION CHECKLIST — Testing and Maintenance (Module 4)**

### Statutory Framework
- [ ] Factories Act **Section 28 & 29** — Lifting Machines and Lifting Tackle
- [ ] Indian Factories Rules — Register of Lifting Machines (Form 12, 13, 14)
- [ ] **IS 3177** — Code for inspection of cranes and hoists
- [ ] **IS 2266** — Wire ropes for cranes
- [ ] **IS 3815** — Lifting hooks
- [ ] **IS 4573** — Slings
- [ ] **IS 6594** — Overhead cranes
- [ ] **IS 2062** — Structural steel

### Load Test Multipliers (MUST MEMORIZE)
- [ ] Lifting Machine Annual Test → **125% of SWL**, hold **5 minutes**
- [ ] Lifting Tackle (chains, slings) → **200% of SWL**
- [ ] Hooks → Static test at **200% of SWL** for 2 minutes
- [ ] New Lifting Machine (Pre-commissioning) → **125% of SWL**

### Inspection Intervals (MUST MEMORIZE)
- [ ] Lifting Machine → **Annually** (12 months)
- [ ] Lifting Tackle → **Annually** + visual before each use
- [ ] Wire Rope → **Daily visual** + annual thorough check
- [ ] Cranes → Internal examination every **4 years**
- [ ] Forklifts → Daily pre-use + **annual LOLER inspection**

### NDT Method Selection (MUST MEMORIZE)
- [ ] **Steel + Surface crack** → MPT (Magnetic Particle Testing)
- [ ] **Steel + Sub-surface** → UT (Ultrasonic Testing)
- [ ] **Aluminium + Surface** → DPI (Liquid Penetrant)
- [ ] **Internal volumetric defect** → RT (Radiography)
- [ ] **Wire rope broken wires** → EM / Eddy Current

### Wire Rope Discard Criteria (MUST MEMORIZE)
- [ ] **> 10% broken wires** in 8 × diameter length
- [ ] **> 5% broken wires** in one strand
- [ ] **Diameter reduction > 10%** due to internal wear
- [ ] Any **bird-cage, kink, or severe corrosion**

### Hook Discard Criteria (MUST MEMORIZE)
- [ ] **Throat opening > 5%** of original dimension
- [ ] **Wear on load-bearing surfaces > 10%** of original dimension
- [ ] Any **crack, nick, or weld repair** is cause for rejection
- [ ] Hook must be replaced, **never re-heat-treated in field**

### Maintenance Types (MUST MEMORIZE — 5 Types)
- [ ] **BM** — Breakdown Maintenance (run-to-failure)
- [ ] **PM** — Preventive Maintenance (time/usage based)
- [ ] **PdM** — Predictive Maintenance (condition based)
- [ ] **CM** — Corrective Maintenance (repair after defect identification)
- [ ] **RCM** — Reliability-Centered Maintenance (FMEA-driven)

### Key Formulas (MUST MEMORIZE)
- [ ] $P_{test} = 1.25 \times \text{SWL}$ (lifting machine)
- [ ] $P_{test} = 2.0 \times \text{SWL}$ (lifting tackle)
- [ ] $\text{FoS} = \dfrac{\text{Ultimate Load}}{\text{Working Load}}$
- [ ] $\text{Availability} = \dfrac{\text{MTBF}}{\text{MTBF} + \text{MTTR}}$
- [ ] $R(t) = e^{-\lambda t}$, where $\lambda = \dfrac{1}{\text{MTBF}}$

### "Never Forget" Golden Rules
- [ ] **NEVER** operate a crane without a valid Certificate of Fitness
- [ ] **NEVER** exceed SWL even for testing — use calibrated dead weights for proof load
- [ ] **NEVER** weld a cracked hook — always replace
- [ ] **NEVER** use a wire rope with bird-caging
- [ ] **ALWAYS** apply LOTO before maintenance
- [ ] **ALWAYS** file NDT and load test reports in the equipment history
- [ ] **ALWAYS** have a Competent Person sign the Certificate of Fitness
- [ ] **ALWAYS** inform the Factory Inspector 7 days before annual test

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Final Exam Tip:** The most common 14-mark question combines **(a) Test procedure with statutory citations** + **(b) A numerical on FoS or test load calculation**. Practice both — they appear in **every KTU University Exam** under Module 4.
